topics.title: Separation factor and its dependence on process variable; equilibrium and rate governed process; classification of separation process: conventional methods like sedimentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT527 - SEPARATION PROCESSES IN FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Separation factor and its dependence on process variable; equilibrium and rate governed process; classification of separation process: conventional methods like sedimentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Separation Factor (α) — The Yardstick of Separation

The **separation factor (α)** is a dimensionless parameter that quantifies the *degree of separation* achievable between two components (A and B) in a given separation operation. It is formally defined as the **ratio of the composition ratios** of the two key components in two coexisting phases (or streams).

The general mathematical expression is:

$$
\alpha_{A,B} \;=\; \frac{\left(\dfrac{x_A}{x_B}\right)_{\text{phase 1}}}{\left(\dfrac{x_B}{x_B}\right)_{\text{phase 2}}}
$$

In its most commonly used form (for a feed/product comparison):

$$
\alpha_{A,B} \;=\; \frac{\left(\dfrac{y_A}{y_B}\right)}{\left(\dfrac{x_A}{x_B}\right)}
$$

where $x$ denotes composition in the feed/raffinate, and $y$ denotes composition in the product/extract.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Interpretation Rules for α:**
> - If $\alpha = 1$ → **No separation is possible** (both components distribute identically between the two phases).
> - If $\alpha > 1$ → Component A is preferentially concentrated in phase 1.
> - The **farther α is from 1**, the **easier the separation** (e.g., α = 10 is far easier than α = 1.1).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Color Marble Jar"

Imagine you have a jar containing **100 red and 100 blue marbles** mixed uniformly. Now, you have a sieve that tends to let red marbles fall through faster.

- If the sieve lets **all marbles through equally** → α = 1 (no separation).
- If the sieve lets **red marbles fall twice as fast** as blue → α = 2 (moderate separation).
- If the sieve **only lets red marbles through** → α → ∞ (perfect separation).

The separation factor α is simply a **number that tells you how picky your separation tool is** between two components.

### 1.3 Dependence of α on Process Variables

The separation factor is **not a fixed material constant** — it varies with operating and thermodynamic variables:

| Process Variable | Effect on α |
|---|---|
| **Temperature (T)** | Strong effect in distillation, extraction, crystallization. Increasing T often **decreases α** in distillation (relative volatility falls near the critical point). |
| **Pressure (P)** | Influences α in gas-phase separations (distillation, gas absorption) and membrane processes. |
| **Composition** | α can vary with composition, especially in non-ideal mixtures (e.g., azeotropes form when α = 1). |
| **Choice of solvent/membrane** | In extraction, α depends on the solvent; in membrane processes, on membrane selectivity. |
| **pH** | Critical in ion-exchange, liquid-liquid extraction of ionizable species. |

### 1.4 Equilibrium vs. Rate-Governed Processes — Quick Definition

> [!NOTE]
> - **Equilibrium-Governed (Stage) Process:** The extent of separation is dictated by **thermodynamic equilibrium** between phases. Mass transfer is assumed instantaneous, and the design is based on the **number of theoretical stages** (e.g., distillation plate calculation).
> - **Rate-Governed Process:** The extent of separation is dictated by **mass transfer kinetics** and diffusion rates. The design relies on **mass transfer coefficients, contact time, and area** (e.g., membrane filtration, absorption with finite liquid-side resistance).

### 1.5 Classification of Separation Processes

Separation processes are classified into **two broad families**:

1. **Conventional (Mechanical) Separations** — Based on physical properties like size, density, and state.
   - Sedimentation, Filtration, Centrifugation, Sieving, Flotation, Evaporation.

2. **Advanced (Diffusional/Equilibrium) Separations** — Based on mass transfer between phases.
   - Distillation, Extraction, Absorption, Adsorption, Membrane Separation, Crystallization, Ion Exchange.

### 1.6 Sedimentation — The Oldest Separation Method

**Sedimentation** is the process whereby suspended solid particles in a liquid settle under the influence of **gravity (or centrifugal force)**. It is one of the most ancient and economically important separation methods in the food industry.

Examples: Clarification of fruit juices, settling of starch, purification of cane juice in sugar industry, treatment of effluents in dairy plants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Sedimentation falls under *conventional (mechanical) separation* and is governed by **physical equilibrium** between the gravitational body force and the drag force — making it a **rate-governed** separation (settling velocity is rate-controlled).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Separation Factor — Detailed Mathematical Framework

The separation factor arises naturally from **phase equilibrium relations**. For a binary mixture of A and B distributed between Phase I and Phase II:

$$
\alpha_{A,B} \;=\; \frac{K_A}{K_B}
$$

where $K_A$ and $K_B$ are the **equilibrium distribution coefficients** of A and B, defined as:

$$
K_A \;=\; \frac{y_A}{x_A} \qquad K_B \;=\; \frac{y_B}{x_B}
$$

**Why does this form arise?** Because at equilibrium, each component partitions between the two phases according to its own distribution coefficient. The *ratio* $K_A / K_B$ measures how differently the two components distribute — which is the very essence of separation.

#### 2.1.1 Special Case: Distillation (Relative Volatility)

In distillation, the separation factor is called the **relative volatility** ($\alpha_{AB}$):

$$
\alpha_{AB} \;=\; \frac{K_A}{K_B} \;=\; \frac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}}
$$

for an ideal binary system, where $P^{sat}$ is the saturation vapor pressure. Since $P^{sat}$ depends strongly on temperature:

$$
\ln P^{sat} \;=\; A \;-\; \frac{B}{T} \quad \text{(Antoine Equation form)}
$$

Therefore, **α in distillation is a strong function of T** — typically decreasing as T rises toward the critical point.

#### 2.1.2 Special Case: Liquid–Liquid Extraction

In solvent extraction, the separation factor is:

$$
\alpha_{A,B} \;=\; \frac{(y_A/x_A)}{(y_B/x_B)} \;=\; \frac{K_{A,\text{extract}} / K_{B,\text{extract}}}{1}
$$

where $y$ refers to the extract phase and $x$ to the raffinate. **α depends on solvent choice, temperature, and sometimes pH.**

#### 2.1.3 Special Case: Membrane Separation

For membranes, the separation factor is defined in terms of **permeabilities**:

$$
\alpha_{A,B} \;=\; \frac{P_{A}^{m}}{P_{B}^{m}}
$$

where $P^{m}$ is the membrane permeability. It also depends on T, P, and the membrane material.

### 2.2 Why α Depends on Process Variables — The "Why" Behind the Math

- **Temperature** alters vapor pressure, solubility, and diffusion coefficients — so K-values change.
- **Pressure** changes fugacity coefficients (gas-phase) and liquid-phase activity coefficients at high pressure.
- **Composition** affects activity coefficients in non-ideal mixtures (e.g., azeotrope formation when γ changes sign with composition).
- **Solvent/Membrane Selection** changes the K-values directly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** If α = 1, *no number of stages* can achieve separation by an equilibrium method. The engineer must then switch technologies (e.g., extractive distillation, pressure-swing, or membrane).

### 2.3 Equilibrium-Governed vs. Rate-Governed Processes — Structured Comparison

| Feature | Equilibrium-Governed | Rate-Governed |
|---|---|---|
| **Driving force** | Departure from thermodynamic equilibrium | Concentration / pressure gradient |
| **Design basis** | Number of equilibrium stages (N) | Mass transfer coefficient (k), area (A), time (t) |
| **Limiting case** | Infinitely fast mass transfer | Equilibrium *never quite* reached |
| **Examples** | Distillation, Extraction (ideal), Crystallization, Adsorption equilibrium | Membrane separation, Absorption, Sedimentation, Drying |
| **Key equation** | McCabe–Thiele, Kremser equation | Fick's law, Stokes' law, mass transfer correlations |
| **Product purity** | Limited by equilibrium (azeotropes) | Limited by kinetics & selectivity |

### 2.4 Sedimentation — Physics and Operating Principles

Sedimentation is a **rate-governed mechanical separation**. The fundamental physics is the balance of three forces on a single particle:

1. **Gravitational (body) force:** $F_g = \dfrac{\pi \, d_p^3}{6} \, (\rho_p - \rho) \, g$
2. **Buoyancy:** Already included via $(\rho_p - \rho)$.
3. **Drag force:** $F_D = C_D \cdot \dfrac{\pi \, d_p^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\rho \, v^2}{2}$

At **terminal settling velocity**, the net force = 0:

$$
F_g = F_D
$$

For low Reynolds number ($Re_p < 0.1$), the drag coefficient $C_D = 24 / Re_p$. Substituting gives the famous **Stokes' Law**:

$$
v_s \;=\; \frac{d_p^2 \, (\rho_p - \rho) \, g}{18 \, \mu}
$$

where $v_s$ = settling velocity, $d_p$ = particle diameter, $\rho_p$ = particle density, $\rho$ = fluid density, $\mu$ = fluid viscosity, $g$ = gravitational acceleration.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Separation Factor (general) | $\alpha = \dfrac{(y_A / y_B)}{(x_A / x_B)}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Separation Factor (K-form) | $\alpha = K_A / K_B$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Relative volatility (distillation) | $\alpha = P_A^{sat} / P_B^{sat}$ | Dimensionless; ideal binary |
| 4 | Stokes' Law (settling velocity) | $v_s = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho) g}{18 \mu}$ | m/s; valid for $Re_p < 0.1$ |
| 5 | Particle Reynolds Number | $Re_p = \dfrac{d_p \, v_s \, \rho}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Drag coefficient (Stokes regime) | $C_D = 24 / Re_p$ | $Re_p < 0.1$ |
| 7 | Drag coefficient (Newton regime) | $C_D = 0.44$ | $Re_p > 10^3$ |
| 8 | Settling velocity (Newton) | $v_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 d_p (\rho_p - \rho) g}{3 C_D \rho}}$ | m/s; turbulent regime |
| 9 | Stokes criterion | $v_s \propto d_p^2$ | Doubling particle size quadruples $v_s$ |
| 10 | Critical diameter for Stokes regime | $d_p < \sqrt{\dfrac{3.3 \mu^2}{\rho (\rho_p - \rho) g}}$ | Approximate cutoff |
| 11 | Minimum fluidization velocity (related) | $u_{mf} = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho) g}{1650 \mu}$ | m/s; in fluidized beds |
| 12 | Mass transfer rate | $N_A = k \cdot (C_A^* - C_A)$ | kg/(m²·s); fundamental rate law |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility of the Separation Factor

- **Process selection:** If α is too close to 1, conventional equilibrium methods are uneconomical → switch to advanced methods.
- **Energy and equipment sizing:** Higher α → fewer stages → smaller, cheaper columns.
- **Feasibility check:** α is the **first** parameter calculated when screening a separation problem (the *King's method* in process synthesis).
- **Solvent/membrane screening:** For extraction and membrane processes, screening reduces to finding a system with high α.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** When asked about α, always state the *operational definition with reference to specific phases or streams*. A bare formula without context is incomplete and will be penalized.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Separation Factor from Phase Equilibrium

**Starting Point:** Consider a binary mixture of components A and B in equilibrium between two phases — Phase I (e.g., vapor or extract) and Phase II (e.g., liquid or raffinate).

**Step 1 — Write the equilibrium distribution relation for each component:**

At thermodynamic equilibrium, the chemical potentials of each component are equal in both phases. For component i:

$$
\mu_i^{I} \;=\; \mu_i^{II}
$$

For dilute/ideal systems, this reduces to a linear distribution:

$$
y_i \;=\; K_i \, x_i
$$

where $y_i$ is the mole fraction in Phase I, $x_i$ is the mole fraction in Phase II, and $K_i$ is the **equilibrium distribution coefficient** (a function of T, P, and composition).

**Step 2 — Write the relations for both components:**

$$
y_A = K_A \, x_A \qquad y_B = K_B \, x_B
$$

**Step 3 — Take the ratio $y_A / y_B$ and divide by $x_A / x_B$:**

$$
\alpha_{A,B} \;=\; \frac{(y_A / y_B)}{(x_A / x_B)}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the equilibrium relations from Step 2:**

$$
\alpha_{A,B} \;=\; \frac{K_A \, x_A / (K_B \, x_B)}{x_A / x_B} \;=\; \frac{K_A}{K_B} \cdot \frac{x_A / x_B}{x_A / x_B}
$$

**Step 5 — The composition terms cancel (crucial result):**

$$
\boxed{\;\alpha_{A,B} \;=\; \frac{K_A}{K_B}\;}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The separation factor is simply the **ratio of distribution coefficients**, and it is *independent of the actual compositions* — though $K_i$ themselves may depend on composition for non-ideal systems.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Stokes' Law (Terminal Settling Velocity)

**Starting Point:** A spherical particle of diameter $d_p$ and density $\rho_p$ falling under gravity through a fluid of density $\rho$ and viscosity $\mu$.

**Step 1 — Identify the forces acting on the particle:**

- Net gravitational force (gravity minus buoyancy):

$$
F_g \;=\; \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \, (\rho_p - \rho) \, g
$$

- Drag force (resistance of the fluid):

$$
F_D \;=\; C_D \cdot \frac{\pi d_p^2}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — At terminal velocity, acceleration is zero (steady state):**

$$
F_g = F_D
$$

$$
\frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} (\rho_p - \rho) g \;=\; C_D \cdot \frac{\pi d_p^2}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho v_s^2}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $v_s^2$:**

$$
v_s^2 \;=\; \frac{4 \, d_p \, (\rho_p - \rho) \, g}{3 \, C_D \, \rho}
$$

$$
v_s \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{4 \, d_p \, (\rho_p - \rho) \, g}{3 \, C_D \, \rho}}
$$

**Step 4 — Assume Stokes regime ($Re_p < 0.1$), where $C_D = 24 / Re_p$:**

Substitute $C_D$:

$$
v_s \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{4 \, d_p \, (\rho_p - \rho) \, g}{3 \cdot (24/Re_p) \cdot \rho}}
$$

Substitute $Re_p = d_p v_s \rho / \mu$:

$$
v_s \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{4 \, d_p \, (\rho_p - \rho) \, g \cdot d_p \, v_s \, \rho}{72 \, \rho \, \mu}}
$$

$$
v_s \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{4 \, d_p^2 \, (\rho_p - \rho) \, g \, v_s}{72 \, \mu}}
$$

Square both sides:

$$
v_s^2 \;=\; \frac{4 \, d_p^2 \, (\rho_p - \rho) \, g \, v_s}{72 \, \mu}
$$

Divide by $v_s$:

$$
v_s \;=\; \frac{4 \, d_p^2 \, (\rho_p - \rho) \, g}{72 \, \mu}
$$

Simplify the constant:

$$
\boxed{\;v_s \;=\; \frac{d_p^2 \, (\rho_p - \rho) \, g}{18 \, \mu}\;}
$$

**Interpretation:** Settling velocity is **directly proportional to the square of the particle diameter** and the density difference, and **inversely proportional to the fluid viscosity**. This is why smaller particles settle much more slowly and why heating (lowering μ) accelerates sedimentation.

---

### 3.3 Verification of Dimension Homogeneity (Stokes' Law)

$$
[v_s] \;=\; \frac{[L^2] \cdot [M L^{-3}] \cdot [L T^{-2}]}{[M L^{-1} T^{-1}]} \;=\; \frac{M \cdot L \cdot T^{-2}}{M \cdot L^{-1} \cdot T^{-1}} \;=\; L \, T^{-1}
$$

Confirms that $v_s$ has units of velocity (m/s). ✓

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Operating Regimes in Sedimentation

| Regime | $Re_p$ range | $C_D$ | $v_s$ Equation | Validity in Food Industry |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Stokes (laminar)** | $Re_p < 0.1$ | $C_D = 24/Re_p$ | $v_s = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho) g}{18 \mu}$ | Very fine particles (silt, bacteria, colloidal matter in juice clarification) |
| **Intermediate** | $0.1 < Re_p < 10^3$ | Empirical (e.g., Schiller–Naumann) | Iterative | Medium-sized food particles (e.g., cocoa solids, yeast cells) |
| **Newton (turbulent)** | $Re_p > 10^3$ | $C_D \approx 0.44$ | $v_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{3 d_p (\rho_p - \rho) g}{\rho}}$ | Coarse particles (e.g., grain, sugar crystals in slurry) |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Separation Factor in a Distillation Column

**Problem:** A binary mixture of benzene (A) and toluene (B) is distilled at 1 atm. At column top, the liquid composition is $x_A = 0.90$ and the vapor in equilibrium is $y_A = 0.95$. Calculate the separation factor.

**Step 1 — Compute composition ratios:**

$$
\frac{y_A}{y_B} \;=\; \frac{0.95}{1 - 0.95} \;=\; \frac{0.95}{0.05} \;=\; 19
$$

$$
\frac{x_A}{x_B} \;=\; \frac{0.90}{1 - 0.90} \;=\; \frac{0.90}{0.10} \;=\; 9
$$

**Step 2 — Apply definition:**

$$
\alpha \;=\; \frac{y_A / y_B}{x_A / x_B} \;=\; \frac{19}{9} \;=\; 2.11
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

Since $\alpha = 2.11 > 1$, benzene is preferentially vaporized. The value is typical for benzene–toluene, indicating a **moderately easy separation** achievable in a finite number of stages.

---

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Stokes' Settling Velocity

**Problem:** A spherical starch granule ($d_p = 50 \, \mu m$, $\rho_p = 1500 \, kg/m^3$) settles in water ($\rho = 1000 \, kg/m^3$, $\mu = 10^{-3} \, Pa \cdot s$) under gravity ($g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$). Verify Stokes regime and calculate $v_s$.

**Step 1 — Calculate $v_s$ assuming Stokes regime:**

$$
v_s \;=\; \frac{(50 \times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot (1500 - 1000) \cdot 9.81}{18 \cdot 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
v_s \;=\; \frac{(2.5 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot 500 \cdot 9.81}{1.8 \times 10^{-2}}
$$

$$
v_s \;=\; \frac{1.226 \times 10^{-5}}{1.8 \times 10^{-2}} \;=\; 6.81 \times 10^{-4} \, m/s
$$

**Step 2 — Verify $Re_p$:**

$$
Re_p \;=\; \frac{d_p v_s \rho}{\mu} \;=\; \frac{(50 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (6.81 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot 1000}{10^{-3}} \;=\; 0.034
$$

Since $Re_p = 0.034 < 0.1$, **Stokes regime is valid**. ✓

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** The starch granule settles at approximately 0.68 mm/s. In a 1 m tall clarifier, it would take about 24.5 minutes to settle completely.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification of Separation Processes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Separation Processes]:::root --> B1[Equilibrium Governed]
    A0 --> B2[Rate Governed]
    A0 --> B3[Mechanical Conventional]

    B1 --> C1[Distillation]
    B1 --> C2[Liquid Liquid Extraction]
    B1 --> C3[Adsorption]
    B1 --> C4[Crystallization]
    B1 --> C5[Absorption]

    B2 --> D1[Membrane Separation]
    B2 --> D2[Sedimentation]
    B2 --> D3[Drying]
    B2 --> D4[Leaching]
    B2 --> D5[Chromatography]

    B3 --> E1[Sedimentation]
    B3 --> E2[Filtration]
    B3 --> E3[Centrifugation]
    B3 --> E4[Sieving]
    B3 --> E5[Flotation]

    E1 --> F1[Gravity Settling Tank]
    E1 --> F2[Thickener]
    E1 --> F3[Clarifier]
    E1 --> F4[Hydrocyclone]

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b1f3a,stroke-width:2px
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The diagram shows a **three-way classification** of separation processes used in food technology.
- **Equilibrium-governed** processes rely on thermodynamic equilibrium between phases.
- **Rate-governed** processes are limited by mass/heat transfer kinetics.
- **Mechanical/conventional** processes exploit differences in physical properties (size, density).
- Sedimentation appears in *both* the rate-governed and the mechanical-conventional categories, reflecting its dual nature.

---

### 4.2 Process Flow Architecture — Batch Sedimentation in Food Industry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw[Raw Feed Slurry]:::input --> mix[Mixing and Pre-conditioning]:::unit
    mix --> chem[Coagulant or Flocculant Addition]:::unit
    chem --> tank[Sedimentation Tank]:::main
    tank --> clar[Clarified Liquid Overflow]:::output
    tank --> sludge[Concentrated Sludge Underflow]:::output
    sludge --> filt[Filter Press or Centrifuge]:::unit
    filt --> cake[Dewatered Solids Cake]:::output
    filt --> water[Recovered Water Recycle]:::output

    classDef input fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    classDef unit fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    classDef main fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:3px
    classDef output fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600
```

**Interpretation of the flow:**
- Raw slurry (e.g., fruit juice with pulp, dairy whey with casein) is pre-conditioned with coagulants or flocculants.
- The stream enters a **sedimentation tank** where gravity settling occurs over a residence time.
- Clarified liquid overflows the top; concentrated sludge is removed from the bottom.
- Sludge is further dewatered in a filter press or centrifuge to recover solids and water.
- This is the standard architecture for **juice clarification, sugar refining, and dairy effluent treatment**.

---

### 4.3 Force-Balance Schematic on a Settling Particle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Spherical Particle]:::particle --> P2[Forces Acting]:::forces
    P2 --> F1[Gravitational Force Fg]:::fdown
    P2 --> F2[Buoyancy Force Fb]:::fup
    P2 --> F3[Drag Force FD opposing motion]:::fdrag
    P2 --> P4[Net Force Condition]:::condition
    P4 --> P5[If Fg gt Fb plus FD, particle accelerates]:::cond1
    P4 --> P6[At terminal velocity, Fg equals Fb plus FD, v equals vs]:::cond2
    P5 --> P7[Transient Settling]:::phase
    P6 --> P8[Steady State Settling]:::phase

    classDef particle fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#5c3a6e,stroke-width:2px
    classDef forces fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    classDef fdown fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
    classDef fup fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    classDef fdrag fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#7f0000
    classDef condition fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cond1 fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600
    classDef cond2 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600
    classDef phase fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#5c3a6e
```

**Interpretation of the force diagram:**
- A single settling particle experiences three principal forces: gravity (downward), buoyancy (upward), and drag (opposing motion).
- During the **transient phase**, the net force is non-zero, and the particle accelerates.
- At **terminal velocity**, the forces balance, and the particle settles at a constant $v_s$ given by Stokes' law.
