topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT527 - SEPARATION PROCESSES IN FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Membrane configurations: plate and frame

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Plate and Frame Membrane Module** (often abbreviated as **P&F** or referred to as the **Flat-Sheet Module**) is the earliest and most fundamental configuration of membrane separation systems. In this design, flat-sheet membranes are mounted on flat, porous **support plates**, and a **spacer (frame)** is placed between two adjacent membranes to create a thin channel through which the **feed** flows.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *Plate and frame modules are the most elementary membrane configuration in which flat sheets of membrane are placed on a porous support plate, and a spacer/frame separates two adjacent membranes to form a feed channel. The feed is introduced under pressure and permeate is collected from the support plate side.*

The flat-sheet arrangement resembles a **plate-type filter press**, hence the name. It is conceptually the simplest translation of laboratory-scale dead-end and cross-flow filtration into an industrial unit.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Sandwich Press 🍞**
> Imagine making a layered sandwich in a panini press:
> - **Bread slices** → The flat-sheet membranes (semi-permeable barriers)
> - **Cheese filling** → The feed solution (e.g., milk, fruit juice) flowing in the middle
> - **Press surface (with holes)** → The porous support plate that collects the filtrate (permeate)
> - **The gap between the slices** → The spacer/frame that defines the feed channel thickness
> - **Pressing down** → The transmembrane pressure (TMP) driving the separation
>
> When you press the sandwich, the liquid filling (feed) is forced outward; the "clean" part passes through the bread (membrane) and drips out through the holes in the press (support plate), while the solids are retained. Multiple sandwiches can be stacked to process more volume — this is the essence of **plate-and-frame stacking**.

### 1.3 Module Classification Context

Membrane modules are broadly classified by the geometry of the membrane. The plate-and-frame system falls under the **Flat-Sheet Geometry** category:

| Geometry Type | Module Variants | KTU Identification |
|---|---|---|
| **Flat-Sheet** | Plate and Frame, Spiral Wound | Module 2 - Topic 1 |
| **Tubular** | Tubular, Capillary, Hollow Fibre | Module 2 - Topic 2 |
| **Other** | Rotating Disk, Vibratory | Module 2 - Topic 3 |

### 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (Memorize)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Metrics for P&F Modules:**
> - **Packing Density (membrane area / module volume):** Typically **100 – 400 m²/m³** (low compared to spiral wound)
> - **Operating Pressure:** **1 – 10 bar** (RO) up to **30 bar** (high-pressure applications)
> - **Feed Channel Thickness (spacer height):** **0.5 – 3 mm**
> - **Cross-flow Velocity:** **0.5 – 2 m/s**
> - **Per-unit-area Flux:** **10 – 100 L/m²·h** (food applications)
> - **Module Replacement Cost (membrane area basis):** **₹500 – ₹5000 per m²** (varies by material)

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Components — Bullet-by-Bullet Anatomy

The plate-and-frame module is a *layered* assembly. Understanding each component is **mandatory for KTU ESE questions**.

1. **Membrane (Flat Sheet)**
   - Material: Polyethersulfone (PES), Polyamide (PA), PVDF, Cellulose Acetate, Regenerated Cellulose.
   - Available as **rectangular sheets** (typical dimensions: 20 cm × 30 cm, or 1 m × 0.5 m industrial scale).
   - Cut to size and sealed at the edges.
   - Two **sides** of membrane exist — one faces the feed (active side), the other is supported.

2. **Porous Support Plate (Backing Plate)**
   - Usually a **sintered metal**, **ceramic**, or **perforated polymer** plate.
   - Function: **mechanically supports** the membrane against the operating pressure; **collects** the permeate.
   - The permeate exits via **central collection channels** or **peripheral manifolds**.

3. **Spacer / Frame (Gasket)**
   - Defines the **feed channel thickness** ($h_{sp}$).
   - Often made of **plastic (PE, PP), woven mesh, or non-woven netting**.
   - Contains inlet/outlet ports for the feed.
   - May have a **feed spacer mesh** that promotes turbulence (in the feed channel) and a **permeate spacer** (in the permeate channel).

4. **End Plates & Tie Rods**
   - **End plates** provide the structural clamping.
   - **Tie rods / bolts** apply uniform pressure (typically **5 – 15 N·m torque**).

5. **Manifolds**
   - **Feed manifold** → distributes feed to each cell.
   - **Retentate manifold** → collects concentrated stream.
   - **Permeate manifold** → collects filtrate from all support plates.

### 2.2 Operational Flow Paths

There are **two principal flow modes** in P&F modules — a frequent KTU short-answer topic:

| Flow Mode | Description | Marking in KTU |
|---|---|---|
| **Parallel Flow** | Feed enters all cells simultaneously; retentate from each cell is combined. All cells see the **same inlet composition** but with progressively lower flux along the cell. | "P&F - Parallel" |
| **Series (Tapered) Flow** | Retentate from cell 1 becomes feed for cell 2, and so on. Achieves **higher overall recovery** and concentration. | "P&F - Series" |
| **Feed-and-Bleed (Recycle)** | A portion of the retentate is recycled to the feed pump. | Used for high-recovery RO. |

### 2.3 Governing Equations — The KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA TABLE — Master these for ESE 2024**

| # | Equation | Variable Definitions | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu R_{m}}$ | $J$ = permeate flux; $\Delta P$ = TMP; $\mu$ = viscosity; $R_m$ = membrane resistance | m/s |
| 2 | $\Delta P \;(\text{TMP}) = \dfrac{P_{f,in} + P_{f,out}}{2} - P_{p}$ | $P_{f,in}, P_{f,out}$ = feed inlet/outlet pressure; $P_p$ = permeate pressure | Pa |
| 3 | $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu (R_{m} + R_{c})}$ | $R_c$ = cake/gel layer resistance (fouling) | m/s |
| 4 | $A_{module} = N \times A_{sheet}$ | $N$ = number of membrane pairs; $A_{sheet}$ = area of one flat sheet | m² |
| 5 | $\text{Packing Density} = \dfrac{A_{module}}{V_{module}}$ | — | m²/m³ |
| 6 | $\Delta P_{channel} = \dfrac{12 \mu L u}{h_{sp}^{2}}$ (laminar) | $L$ = channel length; $u$ = velocity; $h_{sp}$ = spacer height | Pa |
| 7 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho u d_h}{\mu}$ | $d_h$ = hydraulic diameter of spacer-filled channel | dimensionless |
| 8 | $d_h = \dfrac{4 \epsilon}{a_v}$ | $\epsilon$ = spacer porosity; $a_v$ = specific surface area of spacer | m |
| 9 | $\text{Recovery} = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_f} \times 100\%$ | $Q_p$ = permeate flow; $Q_f$ = feed flow | % |
| 10 | $\text{Rejection} \; R_{j} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}$ | $C_p, C_f$ = permeate and feed solute concentration | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions for P&F Operation:**
> - Inlet feed pressure: typically **2 – 70 bar** depending on application (MF/UF/RO/NF)
> - Cross-flow velocity $u$: limited by **0.5 – 2 m/s** to avoid membrane damage
> - Spacer height $h_{sp}$: $\geq 0.5$ mm to prevent clogging; upper bound limited by mechanical stability
> - Operating temperature: $T \leq 45°C$ for polymeric membranes, $T \leq 300°C$ for ceramic

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why P&F Modules are still relevant in 2024 industry:**
> - **Pharmaceuticals and biotechnology:** Easy to clean, sterilize (CIP/SIP compatible); preferred for **batch operations**.
> - **Food industry:** **Whey protein concentration (UF)**, **milk standardization**, **fruit juice clarification (UF)**, **olive oil clarification**.
> - **R&D / laboratory scale:** Most academic studies begin with flat-sheet cells (e.g., **Stirred Cell**, **SEPA CF cell**).
> - **Pilot plant scale:** Easy to **add/remove membrane pairs** to scale up.
> - **High-fouling feed streams:** Easy membrane replacement without discarding the entire module.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** Full-step derivations carry **5 – 7 marks** in Part B. Skipping algebraic steps = direct mark loss. The following derivations are **board-validated**.

### 3.1 Derivation I: Transmembrane Pressure (TMP) in a P&F Module

**Statement:** Derive the average transmembrane pressure for a plate-and-frame module operating in cross-flow with a measurable pressure drop along the feed channel.

**Given:** Feed enters at pressure $P_{f,in}$ and exits at $P_{f,out}$ (with $P_{f,in} > P_{f,out}$ due to frictional losses). The permeate pressure $P_p$ is approximately constant and equal to atmospheric (or slightly above).

**Step 1:** Define the local TMP at any point $x$ along the feed channel.

$$
\Delta P(x) \;=\; P_f(x) \;-\; P_p
$$

**Reasoning:** The driving force for permeation is the pressure difference between the feed-side and permeate-side at the local position $x$ in the channel.

**Step 2:** Express the local feed pressure using a linear pressure gradient (valid for low-velocity cross-flow with constant cross-section).

$$
P_f(x) \;=\; P_{f,in} \;-\; \dfrac{\Delta P_{channel}}{L} \cdot x
$$

**Reasoning:** The pressure drop $\Delta P_{channel}$ is distributed linearly along the channel of length $L$ assuming a fully-developed, incompressible flow regime.

**Step 3:** Define the average TMP by integrating over the channel length.

$$
\Delta P_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{L} \int_0^L \Delta P(x) \, dx
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the local expression for $\Delta P(x)$ into the integral.

$$
\Delta P_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{L} \int_0^L \left[ P_{f,in} \;-\; \dfrac{\Delta P_{channel}}{L} x \;-\; P_p \right] dx
$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the integral term-by-term. Pull constants outside the integral.

$$
\Delta P_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{L} \left[ (P_{f,in} - P_p) \int_0^L dx \;-\; \dfrac{\Delta P_{channel}}{L} \int_0^L x \, dx \right]
$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate the two standard integrals.

$$
\int_0^L dx \;=\; L
$$

$$
\int_0^L x \, dx \;=\; \dfrac{L^2}{2}
$$

**Step 7:** Substitute these results back into the average TMP expression.

$$
\Delta P_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{L} \left[ (P_{f,in} - P_p) \cdot L \;-\; \dfrac{\Delta P_{channel}}{L} \cdot \dfrac{L^2}{2} \right]
$$

**Step 8:** Simplify the algebra.

$$
\Delta P_{avg} \;=\; (P_{f,in} - P_p) \;-\; \dfrac{\Delta P_{channel}}{2}
$$

**Step 9:** Recognize that the channel pressure drop itself equals $(P_{f,in} - P_{f,out})$. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta P_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{f,in} + P_{f,out}}{2} \;-\; P_p\;}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** The **average TMP** is simply the **mean of the inlet and outlet feed pressures**, minus the permeate pressure. This is the **official KTU formula** for TMP in a P&F module and should be quoted verbatim in the ESE.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II: Pressure Drop in a Spacer-Filled Feed Channel

**Statement:** Derive the pressure drop per unit length for a feed channel in a P&F module, considering the spacer as a porous medium.

**Given:** The feed channel of height $h_{sp}$ contains a spacer of porosity $\epsilon$ and specific surface area $a_v$ (m² of spacer surface per m³ of channel volume). The fluid has viscosity $\mu$ and mean velocity $u$.

**Step 1:** Use the **Ergun equation** (extended to spacer-filled channels) for pressure drop in a packed channel.

$$
\dfrac{\Delta P_{channel}}{L} \;=\; \dfrac{150 \mu u (1 - \epsilon)^2}{d_p^2 \, \epsilon^3} \;+\; \dfrac{1.75 \rho u^2 (1 - \epsilon)}{d_p \, \epsilon^3}
$$

**Reasoning:** The first term represents **viscous (laminar) losses**; the second represents **inertial (turbulent) losses**. $d_p$ is the equivalent particle diameter of the spacer mesh.

**Step 2:** For the **laminar regime** ($Re < 10$, common in P&F food applications), the inertial term is negligible. The equation reduces to:

$$
\dfrac{\Delta P_{channel}}{L} \;\approx\; \dfrac{150 \mu u (1 - \epsilon)^2}{d_p^2 \, \epsilon^3}
$$

**Step 3:** Replace the particle diameter with the **hydraulic diameter** $d_h$ of the spacer. By definition:

$$
d_h \;=\; \dfrac{4 \epsilon}{a_v}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $d_p$ in terms of $a_v$ using a **shape factor** $C_s$ (e.g., $C_s = 6$ for spherical particles). The equivalent diameter is:

$$
d_p \;=\; \dfrac{6 (1 - \epsilon)}{a_v}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute this back into the laminar pressure-drop expression.

$$
\dfrac{\Delta P_{channel}}{L} \;=\; \dfrac{150 \mu u (1 - \epsilon)^2 \, a_v^2}{36 (1 - \epsilon)^2 \, \epsilon^3}
$$

**Step 6:** Simplify the constants.

$$
\dfrac{\Delta P_{channel}}{L} \;=\; \dfrac{150}{36} \cdot \dfrac{\mu u \, a_v^2}{\epsilon^3} \;=\; \dfrac{25}{6} \cdot \dfrac{\mu u \, a_v^2}{\epsilon^3}
$$

**Step 7:** Express the final form using the **drag coefficient per unit length** $K_{channel}$:

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{\Delta P_{channel}}{L} \;=\; K_{channel} \cdot \mu \cdot u \cdot \dfrac{a_v^2}{\epsilon^3}\;}
$$

where

$$
K_{channel} \;\approx\; 4.17 \;\; \text{(dimensionless constant for typical spacers)}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** This derivation shows that **reducing the spacer height** $h_{sp}$ or **increasing porosity** $\epsilon$ dramatically lowers the channel pressure drop. This is why thin, open spacers are preferred for viscous food feeds (e.g., high-sugar juices, dairy).

---

### 3.3 Derivation III: Membrane Area Requirement for a P&F Unit

**Statement:** Calculate the total membrane area $A_{total}$ required in a P&F module to process a feed flow rate $Q_f$ at a specified recovery $R$.

**Given:** Permeate flux $J$ (m/s) and feed flow rate $Q_f$ (m³/s).

**Step 1:** Express the **permeate flow rate** $Q_p$ using the recovery $R$.

$$
Q_p \;=\; R \cdot Q_f
$$

**Step 2:** Write the flux definition. Flux is the permeate flow per unit membrane area.

$$
J \;=\; \dfrac{Q_p}{A_{total}}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the total membrane area.

$$
A_{total} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_p}{J}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the expression for $Q_p$ from Step 1.

$$
\boxed{\;A_{total} \;=\; \dfrac{R \cdot Q_f}{J}\;}
$$

**Step 5 (Refinement):** The number of flat-sheet membrane pairs $N$ needed is:

$$
N \;=\; \dfrac{A_{total}}{A_{sheet}} \;=\; \dfrac{R \cdot Q_f}{J \cdot A_{sheet}}
$$

**Step 6 (Refinement for parallel cells):** If the P&F module has $N$ cells operating in parallel, each cell receives $Q_f / N$ feed. The TMP per cell is unchanged, and the design equation still holds — the cells act as flow-splitting elements.

**Example KTU-Style Numerical:**
- Feed: Milk, $Q_f = 10$ m³/h
- Target recovery: $R = 80\%$
- Average flux: $J = 50$ L/m²·h $= 0.05$ m³/m²·h
- Solution:
  - $A_{total} = (0.80 \times 10) / 0.05 = 160$ m²
  - If each flat sheet is $0.20$ m² ($20 \times 100$ cm), then $N = 160 / 0.20 = 800$ sheets, i.e., **400 membrane pairs**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison with Other Configurations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently asks: "Compare plate-and-frame with spiral wound / hollow fibre."** The matrix below is the **model answer** template.

| Property | Plate and Frame | Spiral Wound | Hollow Fibre |
|---|---|---|---|
| Packing density (m²/m³) | 100 – 400 | 500 – 1000 | 1000 – 10000 |
| Cost per m² | Moderate | Low | Very Low |
| Cleaning ease | **Excellent** (open module) | Difficult (must backflush) | Difficult (must backflush) |
| High-fouling feed tolerance | **Excellent** | Poor | Poor |
| Operating pressure (max) | Up to 70 bar | Up to 80 bar | Up to 30 bar |
| Hold-up volume | High | Low | Lowest |
| Energy efficiency | Moderate | High | High |
| Replacement of single membrane | **Easy** | Difficult (replace whole element) | Difficult (replace whole bundle) |
| Typical food application | Whey UF, juice clarification | Sea-water RO (not food) | Dairy, water |

**Board Verdict:** P&F excels in **flexibility, cleanability, and small-scale food production**, while spiral wound and hollow fibre win on **packing density and large-scale economy**.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define the plate-and-frame membrane module. List its four main components.**

**Model Answer:**

> The plate-and-frame membrane module is a flat-sheet membrane configuration in which a **spacer/frame** is sandwiched between two flat-sheet membranes supported on **porous plates**. The assembly is clamped between **end plates** using tie rods.
>
> The four main components are:
> 1. **Flat-sheet membrane** — the selective barrier.
> 2. **Porous support plate** — provides mechanical support and collects permeate.
> 3. **Spacer/frame (gasket)** — defines the feed channel thickness.
> 4. **End plates and tie rods** — provide structural clamping.

> [!NOTE]
> **Expected length:** 4 – 5 lines; 3 marks for definition (1) + components (2).

#### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State any three advantages and two disadvantages of plate-and-frame modules.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Advantages:**
> 1. Easy to **clean, sterilize, and replace** individual membranes.
> 2. Suitable for **high-fouling feed streams** (e.g., fruit juices, dairy).
> 3. **Low hold-up volume per cell**, ideal for batch and laboratory applications.
>
> **Disadvantages:**
> 1. **Low packing density** (100 – 400 m²/m³) leads to large module footprint.
> 2. **High capital and labor cost** per m² of membrane area compared to spiral-wound modules.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Module 2 contains **two full 14-mark questions** (internal choice). Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024, Module 2, Set A]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **plate-and-frame membrane module**. Discuss the role of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A plate-and-frame UF module is used to concentrate **whey protein**. The feed flow rate is $Q_f = 5$ m³/h, the average flux is $J = 30$ L/m²·h, and the desired recovery is $R = 75\%$. If each flat-sheet membrane is $0.15$ m² in area, calculate:
- (i) Total membrane area required
- (ii) Number of membrane pairs needed
- (iii) Number of cells in the module (assuming 2 sheets per cell) **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working of Plate-and-Frame Module:**

> A plate-and-frame module consists of flat rectangular membrane sheets supported on **porous backing plates**, with **spacers** placed between adjacent membranes to form thin feed channels. The entire stack is clamped between two heavy **end plates** by **tie rods**.

> The **construction details** are as follows:
> 1. **Membrane sheets** (typically 20 – 100 cm long and 10 – 50 cm wide) are cut from rolls of flat-sheet membrane (PES, PVDF, etc.).
> 2. Each membrane is placed on a **porous support plate** (sintered SS, ceramic, or PE).
> 3. A **spacer (gasket)** of thickness $0.5 - 3$ mm is placed between two adjacent membranes, creating the feed channel.
> 4. Multiple such "cells" are stacked in parallel.
> 5. The stack is sealed with **end plates and tie rods**, applying uniform clamping pressure.
>
> **Working:** The feed enters the module through a **feed manifold**, distributes into each cell, and flows in a **cross-flow** pattern over the membrane. Under the **transmembrane pressure (TMP)**, the solvent (e.g., water) and small molecules permeate through the membrane and are collected by the **porous support plates**, exiting through the **permeate manifold**. The retained solutes exit as **retentate** from the other end of the cell.
>
> **Role of Components:**
> - **Membrane** → selective separation.
> - **Support plate** → mechanical support + permeate collection.
> - **Spacer** → defines feed channel + promotes turbulence.
> - **End plates** → structural rigidity.
> - **Manifolds** → feed distribution, retentate and permeate collection.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $Q_f = 5$ m³/h
- $J = 30$ L/m²·h $= 0.030$ m³/m²·h
- $R = 75\% = 0.75$
- $A_{sheet} = 0.15$ m²

**Step 1: Permeate flow rate.**

$$
Q_p \;=\; R \cdot Q_f \;=\; 0.75 \times 5 \;=\; 3.75 \;\; \text{m}^3\text{/h}
$$

**Step 2: Total membrane area.**

$$
A_{total} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_p}{J} \;=\; \dfrac{3.75}{0.030} \;=\; 125 \;\; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Number of membrane pairs (each pair has 2 sheets).**

$$
N_{pairs} \;=\; \dfrac{A_{total}}{2 \times A_{sheet}} \;=\; \dfrac{125}{2 \times 0.15} \;=\; \dfrac{125}{0.30} \;\approx\; 416.67 \;\approx\; 417 \;\; \text{pairs}
$$

**Step 4: Number of cells (each cell contains 1 pair = 2 sheets).**

$$
N_{cells} \;=\; N_{pairs} \;\approx\; 417 \;\; \text{cells}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative grouping:** If a cell contains 4 sheets (2 pairs), then number of cells $= 417/2 \approx 209$ cells. **Always state the assumption** in the answer.

**Final Answer:**
- (i) $A_{total} = 125$ m²
- (ii) Number of pairs $\approx 417$
- (iii) Number of cells (2 sheets each) $\approx 417$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024, Module 2, Set B (Internal Choice)]**

**(a)** Explain the **flow configurations** (parallel and series) in plate-and-frame modules with diagrams. Compare their **recovery** and **energy efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A plate-and-frame module operates with a feed inlet pressure of **4 bar** and outlet pressure of **3 bar**. The permeate pressure is **0.2 bar (gauge)**. If the channel pressure drop is 1 bar and the average flux is $J = 25$ L/m²·h, find:
- (i) The average TMP.
- (ii) The total membrane area needed for a feed flow of 8 m³/h at 60% recovery. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Flow Configurations in P&F Modules:**

> **Parallel Configuration:** Feed enters all cells simultaneously via a feed manifold; retentate from each cell is collected into a common retentate manifold. Each cell operates at the **same inlet concentration**. The flux is **highest near the feed inlet** and decays along the cell due to **concentration polarization**.
>
> **Series (Tapered) Configuration:** Retentate from cell 1 becomes the feed to cell 2, and so on. The concentration **increases progressively** along the train, mimicking a multi-stage operation. The flux in downstream cells is **lower** due to higher osmotic pressure and viscosity, but overall **recovery is higher**.
>
> **Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Parallel | Series |
|---|---|---|
| Recovery per stage | Low | High |
| Energy efficiency | Higher (lower pumping) | Lower (multi-pumps) |
| Capital cost | Lower | Higher |
| Use case | Low-recovery batch | High-recovery continuous |
| Concentration polarization | Uniform across cells | Increases downstream |

> **Energy Insight:** Parallel configuration requires less pumping power because the feed pressure is shared across all cells simultaneously. Series configuration is energy-intensive but yields a more concentrated product — used in **RO for high-recovery desalination**.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $P_{f,in} = 4$ bar
- $P_{f,out} = 3$ bar
- $P_p = 0.2$ bar
- $J = 25$ L/m²·h $= 0.025$ m³/m²·h
- $Q_f = 8$ m³/h
- $R = 60\% = 0.60$

**Step 1: Calculate the average TMP.**

$$
\Delta P_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{f,in} + P_{f,out}}{2} \;-\; P_p
$$

$$
\Delta P_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{4 + 3}{2} \;-\; 0.2 \;=\; 3.5 \;-\; 0.2 \;=\; 3.3 \;\; \text{bar}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the permeate flow rate.**

$$
Q_p \;=\; R \cdot Q_f \;=\; 0.60 \times 8 \;=\; 4.8 \;\; \text{m}^3\text{/h}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the total membrane area.**

$$
A_{total} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_p}{J} \;=\; \dfrac{4.8}{0.025} \;=\; 192 \;\; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Calculate the channel pressure drop** (already given = 1 bar, used in Step 1 implicitly).

$$
\Delta P_{channel} \;=\; P_{f,in} - P_{f,out} \;=\; 4 - 3 \;=\; 1 \;\; \text{bar} \;\; \checkmark
$$

> **Verification:** $\Delta P_{avg} = (P_{f,in} - P_p) - \Delta P_{channel}/2 = (4 - 0.2) - 0.5 = 3.3$ bar ✓

**Final Answer:**
- (i) $\Delta P_{avg} = 3.3$ bar
- (ii) $A_{total} = 192$ m²

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Flux $J$ must be in **m³/m²·h** (not L/m²·h) before dividing by $Q_p$ in m³/h. Many students lose **1 – 2 marks** for missed conversion.
> 2. **Wrong TMP formula:** Students often write $\Delta P = P_{f,in} - P_p$ (this is **inlet TMP**, not average). For P&F, **always** use $\Delta P_{avg} = (P_{f,in} + P_{f,out})/2 - P_p$.
> 3. **Confusing "membrane pairs" with "sheets":** A pair = 2 sheets. A cell = 2 sheets or 4 sheets depending on design. **State assumption explicitly.**
> 4. **Omitting the spacer function:** Many students forget to mention the spacer's **turbulence-promoting role** — worth **1 mark** in 7-mark questions.
> 5. **Forgetting the direction of permeate flow:** Permeate goes from the feed side → through the membrane → out via the support plate. Drawing an arrow in the wrong direction costs marks in sketch-based questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Read 5 minutes before the exam:**

- **Plate-and-frame (P&F)** is the **oldest** and **simplest** flat-sheet membrane configuration.
- Main components: **Membrane + Support plate + Spacer/frame + End plates + Manifolds**.
- **Packing density:** 100 – 400 m²/m³ (low — main disadvantage).
- **Average TMP formula:** $\Delta P_{avg} = (P_{f,in} + P_{f,out})/2 - P_p$ — **must memorize verbatim**.
- **Flux equation:** $J = \Delta P / [\mu (R_m + R_c)]$ — note the cake/fouling resistance term.
- **Two flow modes:** **Parallel** (low recovery, low energy) and **Series** (high recovery, high energy).
- **Spacer function:** Defines feed channel thickness, **promotes turbulence**, reduces concentration polarization.
- **Laminar pressure drop:** $\Delta P_{channel}/L = 25 \mu u a_v^2 / (6 \epsilon^3)$.
- **Design formula:** $A_{total} = R \cdot Q_f / J$.
- **Recovery:** $R = Q_p / Q_f$ (in decimals or %).
- **Rejection:** $R_j = 1 - C_p / C_f$.
- **Hydraulic diameter** of spacer: $d_h = 4\epsilon / a_v$.
- **Best for:** Whey protein concentration, fruit juice clarification, R&D scale work, high-fouling feeds.
- **Comparison:** P&F < Spiral wound < Hollow fibre in **packing density**; P&F > others in **cleanability**.
- **Industrial brands of P&F modules:** **SEPA CF (GE Osmonics)**, **DSS (Alfa Laval)**, **Pellicon (Millipore)**, **Hydracell (Niro)**.
- **KTU Favourite Question Types:**
  1. Sketch + label a P&F module (3 – 7 marks).
  2. Derive TMP for cross-flow (7 marks).
  3. Numerical on $A_{total}, N_{cells}$ (7 marks).
  4. Compare P&F with spiral wound (3 – 7 marks).
  5. State advantages/disadvantages (3 marks).
- **One-line takeaway:** *"Plate-and-frame is the lab-friendly, easy-to-clean workhorse of food membrane processing — at the cost of low packing density and high capital per m²."*

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tubular
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT527 - SEPARATION PROCESSES IN FOOD TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Tubular (Tubular-Bowl Centrifuge) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Tubular-Bowl Centrifuge (TBC):** A high-speed, axisymmetric, solid-wall centrifugal separator in which a slender, vertical tubular bowl — open at the top and closed at the bottom — rotates about its own vertical axis at very high angular velocity. The bowl has no internal cones, discs, or scroll conveyors; separation of immiscible liquids and/or fine solids occurs purely by the action of an intensified gravitational (centrifugal) field acting radially across the thin annular liquid layer held between the inner wall of the bowl and the air/liquid interface. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the tubular centrifuge belongs to the family of **sedimentation centrifuges** (as opposed to filtration centrifuges) and is classified under **imperforate-bowl, high-G, liquid-liquid / liquid-solid separators**. It is formally catalogued as an **"ultra-centrifuge class"** device for industrial liquid clarification, capable of generating **13,000 to 17,000 × g** of effective acceleration. ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Think of a salad spinner on steroids.** When you spin a wet lettuce in a salad spinner, water droplets fly outward against the basket wall and drain out — this is the same physical principle as a tubular centrifuge, but magnified **thousands of times**. Now imagine that, instead of a plastic basket, you have a **tall, narrow, stainless-steel tube spinning at 15,000–20,000 rpm**. Dirt, bacteria, somatic cells, or fine suspended solids in a food liquid (milk, fruit juice, edible oil) are flung against the wall, while the clarified liquid moves up the central axis and overflows the top. The solids accumulate as a thick paste against the wall, which is later scraped out manually when the machine is stopped. ### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics and Physical Constants | Metric | Typical Value (Industrial TBC) | |---|---| | Bowl inner radius ($r_w$) | **25 – 75 mm** | | Bowl length-to-diameter ratio ($L/D$) | **≈ 4 – 7 (slender geometry)** | | Rotational speed ($N$) | **15,000 – 20,000 rpm** | | Centrifugal acceleration ($a_c$) | **13,000 – 17,000 × g** | | G-force ($Z = \omega^2 r_w / g$) | **13,000 – 17,000** (dimensionless) | | Gravitational acceleration ($g$) | **9.81 m·s⁻²** | | Density of water at 20 °C ($\rho_w$) | **998.2 kg·m⁻³** | | Dynamic viscosity of water at 20 °C ($\mu_w$) | **1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **G-force ($Z$)** is the single most important operating parameter in a tubular centrifuge. It expresses *how many times stronger* the centrifugal field is compared to Earth's gravity. A $Z$ value of 15,000 means a particle experiences 15,000 times its normal weight, allowing micron-sized impurities to settle in seconds rather than hours. ### 1.4 Classification of Tubular Centrifuge Within Centrifuge Family Centrifuges in food engineering are broadly classified as: 1. **Sedimentation centrifuges** (bowl wall is imperforate — clear liquid overflows) - **Tubular-bowl (TBC)** ← *this topic* - Disc-stack centrifuge - Solid-bowl scroll (decanter) 2. **Filtration centrifuges** (bowl wall is perforated — cake is recovered) - Basket / peeler centrifuge - Pusher centrifuge The tubular centrifuge is the **highest-G, lowest-throughput** member of the sedimentation family, ideal for **clarification of high-value, low-volume liquid foods**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Flow The tubular centrifuge operates on **enhanced gravitational settling under a centrifugal body force field**. The sequence of events is: 1. **Feed introduction:** The food liquid is introduced from the *bottom* of the rotating bowl through a stationary feed nozzle, entering the spinning liquid pool. 2. **Acceleration by baffles:** The feed strikes conical *acceleration vanes* (or "feed wings") that bring the liquid up to bowl angular velocity with minimal turbulence. 3. **Radial settling:** The liquid forms a thin *annular pool* of outer radius $r_w$ (bowl wall) and inner radius $r_t$ (air/liquid surface vortex). Dispersed phase particles (denser solids, or one of two immiscible liquids) are flung **outward**; the continuous phase migrates **inward** and upward. 4. **Axial overflow:** The clarified continuous phase rises and exits over a *ring weir* or *dam* at the top of the bowl into a stationary cover. 5. **Solids (or heavy phase) accumulation:** Solids form a thick paste against the wall; the heavy liquid (in liquid-liquid mode) coalesces against the wall and is removed via a separate *heavy-phase discharge* nozzle when the geometry is three-way. 6. **Intermittent cleaning:** When solids build up excessively (visual through a sight glass, or by back-pressure rise), the machine is stopped and the bowl is **manually dismantled and cleaned**. > [!NOTE] > **Why the slender geometry?** A *high* length-to-diameter ratio means the liquid travels a **long axial path**, giving each particle **more residence time** to migrate radially. This is what enables the tubular centrifuge to capture **sub-micron particles** that other centrifuges miss. ### 2.2 Physics of Centrifugal Settling — The "Why" Behind the Equations In a rotating reference frame, a particle of mass $m$ at radius $r$ experiences a **centrifugal body force** directed radially outward: $$F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$$ This force replaces gravity ($mg$) in conventional Stokes settling. Every classical gravity-settling equation can therefore be **rewritten** by substituting $g \rightarrow \omega^2 r$. The relative centrifugal force (RCF), or G-force, is defined as: $$Z = \frac{a_c}{g} = \frac{\omega^2 r_w}{g}$$ ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following six equations are the high-yield set for any KTU 2024 question on tubular centrifuges.** Memorize them with their boundary conditions and units. | # | Equation (LaTeX) | Description / Use | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Z = \dfrac{\omega^2 \, r_w}{g}$ | G-force (relative centrifugal force) at bowl wall | dimensionless | | 2 | $a_c = \omega^2 \, r_w = \dfrac{\pi^2 N^2 r_w}{900}$ | Centrifugal acceleration at wall | m·s⁻² | | 3 | $v_t = \dfrac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, g}{18 \mu}$ | Terminal settling velocity (gravity, Stokes' law) | m·s⁻¹ | | 4 | $v_c(r) = \dfrac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, \omega^2 r}{18 \mu}$ | Radial settling velocity at radius $r$ inside bowl | m·s⁻¹ | | 5 | $\Sigma = \dfrac{\pi (r_w^2 - r_t^2) \, L \, \omega^2}{g \cdot \ln(r_w / r_t)}$ | Sigma factor of a tubular-bowl centrifuge (equivalent clarification area) | m² | | 6 | $Q = \dfrac{V_g}{\Sigma}$ | Throughput under centrifugal conditions, equivalent to a gravity settler of area $\Sigma$ | m³·s⁻¹ | **Where the variables mean:** - $Z$ = G-force ratio, dimensionless - $\omega$ = angular velocity, rad·s⁻¹ ($\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ for $N$ in rpm) - $r_w$ = bowl inner radius, m - $r_t$ = inner (free-surface) radius of the liquid pool, m - $L$ = effective length of liquid column in the bowl, m - $d$ = particle diameter, m - $\rho_p$ = particle (or heavy-phase) density, kg·m⁻³ - $\rho_f$ = fluid (continuous-phase) density, kg·m⁻³ - $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of continuous phase, Pa·s - $V_g = V_o / t$ = settling velocity in a gravity settler of volume $V_o$ and time $t$ (used for scale-up) - $Q$ = volumetric throughput, m³·s⁻¹ - $g$ = **9.81 m·s⁻²** (gravitational acceleration) - $N$ = bowl rotational speed, rpm > [!NOTE] > **Rule of thumb for KTU problems:** The Stokes' law (equation 3) is valid when the **particle Reynolds number $Re_p < 1$**, i.e. $Re_p = d \, v_t \, \rho_f / \mu < 1$. For larger particles, the *Newton* or *intermediate* regime corrections apply. In tubular centrifuge problems, always check the regime first. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The tubular centrifuge, although old technology (developed in the 1930s by **Carl von Linde's** de Laval-type designs), is still **irreplaceable in food industry** for: - **Milk clarification** — removal of somatic cells, dirt, and bacteria spores (the "clarifix" process in dairy) - **Edible oil refining** — degumming and removal of phospholipids - **Fruit juice clarification** — apple, grape, citrus pulp and haze removal - **Wine and beer clarification** — yeast and protein haze removal - **Pharmaceutical / biotech** — cell harvesting, vaccine clarification - **Vegetable protein extraction** — starch and gluten separation The reason it persists is its **unmatched G-force per unit cost**. A 15,000-G tubular bowl can be built for a fraction of the cost of a comparable disc-stack centrifuge of the same capacity. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — G-Force and Centrifugal Acceleration **Start:** In a rotating reference frame, a particle at radius $r$ from the axis experiences a *centrifugal pseudo-force* of magnitude $m \omega^2 r$, directed radially outward. **Step 1 — Write the centrifugal acceleration:** $$a_c = \omega^2 \, r$$ This is the magnitude of the radial acceleration experienced by the particle (equal in magnitude to the centripetal acceleration of circular motion). **Step 2 — Evaluate at the bowl wall (the point of maximum acceleration):** Set $r = r_w$: $$a_c \big\vert_{r_w} = \omega^2 \, r_w$$ **Step 3 — Convert from $\omega$ (rad·s⁻¹) to $N$ (rpm):** Using $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$: $$a_c = \frac{(2\pi N)^2}{60^2} \, r_w = \frac{4\pi^2 N^2}{3600} \, r_w = \frac{\pi^2 N^2}{900} \, r_w$$ **Step 4 — Form the G-force ratio** by dividing by $g$: $$Z = \frac{a_c}{g} = \frac{\omega^2 r_w}{g} = \frac{\pi^2 N^2 r_w}{900 \, g}$$ **Step 5 — Sanity check with a numerical example:** Given $N = 16{,}000$ rpm and $r_w = 50$ mm = 0.050 m: $$Z = \frac{\pi^2 \times (16{,}000)^2 \times 0.050}{900 \times 9.81} = \frac{9.8696 \times 2.56 \times 10^8 \times 0.050}{8829} = \frac{1.2633 \times 10^8}{8829} \approx 14{,}308$$ This confirms a typical industrial TBC delivers $Z \approx 14{,}000$ — a very large intensification factor. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Radial Settling Velocity in the Centrifuge **Start:** Under Stokes' law, the *drag force* on a spherical particle settling in a viscous fluid at low Reynolds number is: $$F_D = 3 \pi \mu d \, v$$ Under the *centrifugal body force*, the *driving force* is: $$F_c = \frac{\pi d^3}{6} (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, \omega^2 r$$ **Step 1 — Equate driving and drag forces at terminal velocity** (zero net force → constant radial velocity): $$\frac{\pi d^3}{6} (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2 r = 3 \pi \mu d \, v_c(r)$$ **Step 2 — Solve for $v_c(r)$:** $$v_c(r) = \frac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2 r}{18 \mu}$$ This is the **Stokes-equivalent** radial velocity inside the bowl. Note the linear dependence on $r$: particles move *faster* as they spiral outward (a key difference from gravity settling, where $v_t$ is constant). **Step 3 — Compare to gravity:** $$\frac{v_c(r)}{v_t} = \frac{\omega^2 r}{g} = Z(r)$$ i.e. the centrifugal velocity is simply $Z$ times the gravity settling velocity, where $Z$ itself depends on radius. A 15,000-G machine at $r = 0.05$ m gives a particle 15,000 times the gravitational settling speed. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sigma Factor of a Tubular Centrifuge (Detailed) This is the **most important derivation** in the entire topic — frequently asked in KTU Part B (14-mark) questions. **Start — The basic idea:** Define a hypothetical *gravity settler* that performs the same clarification job as the centrifuge. The *equivalent area* of that gravity settler is called the **sigma factor $\Sigma$** (units: m²). It allows direct comparison of centrifuges of different geometries and speeds. **Step 1 — Write the differential radial displacement** of a particle settling at radius $r$: $$\frac{dr}{dt} = \frac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2 r}{18 \mu}$$ **Step 2 — Separate variables:** $$\frac{dr}{r} = \frac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2}{18 \mu} \, dt$$ **Step 3 — Integrate from $r_t$ (inner surface) to $r_w$ (wall)** — the boundary of the separation zone. Let $t_{res}$ be the *residence time* of liquid in the bowl: $$\int_{r_t}^{r_w} \frac{dr}{r} = \int_0^{t_{res}} \frac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2}{18 \mu} \, dt$$ $$\ln\!\left(\frac{r_w}{r_t}\right) = \frac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2}{18 \mu} \, t_{res}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for the residence time $t_{res}$:** $$t_{res} = \frac{18 \mu \, \ln(r_w / r_t)}{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, \omega^2}$$ **Step 5 — Relate residence time to volumetric flow rate $Q$ and bowl volume $V$:** The volume of liquid held in the annular pool is: $$V = \pi (r_w^2 - r_t^2) L$$ For *plug flow* through the bowl (a standard assumption in tubular centrifuge design, valid because axial mixing is suppressed by the high rotation): $$t_{res} = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{\pi (r_w^2 - r_t^2) L}{Q}$$ **Step 6 — Equate the two expressions for $t_{res}$ and solve for $Q$:** $$\frac{\pi (r_w^2 - r_t^2) L}{Q} = \frac{18 \mu \, \ln(r_w / r_t)}{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, \omega^2}$$ $$Q = \frac{\pi (r_w^2 - r_t^2) L \, d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, \omega^2}{18 \mu \, \ln(r_w / r_t)}$$ **Step 7 — Define the sigma factor** as the equivalent clarification area of a gravity settler that would do the same job: In a *gravity settler*, the settling velocity is $v_t = d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g / (18 \mu)$ and the throughput is $Q = v_t \cdot A_g$, where $A_g$ is the cross-sectional area of the gravity settler. For a *perfect* separation (every particle that enters reaches the bottom), $A_g = V_g / t_g$ where $V_g$ is the settler volume and $t_g$ is the residence time. Equivalently: $$\Sigma = \frac{Q}{v_t} = \frac{Q \cdot 18 \mu}{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, g}$$ **Step 8 — Substitute the expression for $Q$ from Step 6:** $$\Sigma = \frac{\pi (r_w^2 - r_t^2) L \, d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, \omega^2}{18 \mu \, \ln(r_w / r_t)} \times \frac{18 \mu}{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, g}$$ **Step 9 — All fluid properties and particle terms cancel cleanly** — the sigma factor depends *only* on geometry and speed, as it should: $$\boxed{\Sigma = \frac{\pi (r_w^2 - r_t^2) \, L \, \omega^2}{g \cdot \ln(r_w / r_t)}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Limiting-case shortcut for the exam:** When the liquid layer is *thin* (i.e. $r_t \approx r_w$ and the logarithmic term tends to unity), the expression simplifies. When the layer is *deep* (i.e. $r_t$ is small relative to $r_w$), the factor $\ln(r_w / r_t)$ becomes large and $\Sigma$ is *reduced*. KTU students should explicitly check which limit applies in any numerical problem. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Scale-Up Rule (Constant-$\Sigma$ Rule) **Start — The problem:** A pilot-scale tubular centrifuge delivers satisfactory clarification. The production plant needs 10× the throughput. What rotational speed or geometric change is required? **Step 1 — Write the throughput equation:** $$Q = \frac{V_g \, g \, \Sigma}{1} = V_g \cdot \Sigma$$ where $V_g = d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g / (18 \mu)$ is the *required* settling velocity for the cut-size particle. **Step 2 — For the same separation cut (same particle size, same fluid), $V_g$ is constant.** Therefore: $$\frac{Q_1}{\Sigma_1} = \frac{Q_2}{\Sigma_2}$$ **Step 3 — Substitution:** $$\boxed{\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{\Sigma_1}{\Sigma_2} = \frac{(r_{w,1}^2 - r_{t,1}^2) L_1 \omega_1^2 / \ln(r_{w,1}/r_{t,1})}{(r_{w,2}^2 - r_{t,2}^2) L_2 \omega_2^2 / \ln(r_{w,2}/r_{t,2})}}$$ This is the **master scale-up equation** for tubular centrifuges. It is the basis of the **"sigma theory"** of Ambler (1952) and is widely used in KTU Part B problems. --- ### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (for KTU Exam Practice) > **Problem:** A tubular-bowl centrifuge has $r_w = 0.060$ m, $r_t = 0.020$ m, $L = 0.80$ m, and rotates at $N = 17{,}000$ rpm. Calculate (a) the G-force, (b) the sigma factor, and (c) the throughput for removing particles with $d = 5\ \mu\text{m}$, $\rho_p - \rho_f = 100$ kg·m⁻³, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. **Solution (a):** $$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 17{,}000}{60} = 1780.2\ \text{rad·s}^{-1}$$ $$Z = \frac{\omega^2 r_w}{g} = \frac{(1780.2)^2 \times 0.060}{9.81} = \frac{190{,}135}{9.81} \approx 19{,}381$$ **Solution (b):** $$\Sigma = \frac{\pi (0.060^2 - 0.020^2)(0.80)(1780.2)^2}{9.81 \times \ln(0.060 / 0.020)}$$ Numerator: $\pi \times 0.0032 \times 0.80 \times 3.169 \times 10^6 = \pi \times 0.0032 \times 0.80 \times 3.169 \times 10^6$ $= 2.549 \times 10^4$ m³·rad²·s⁻² (intermediate) $= 2.549 \times 10^4$ m³·s⁻² (since rad is dimensionless) Denominator: $9.81 \times \ln(3) = 9.81 \times 1.0986 = 10.78$ m·s⁻² $$\Sigma = \frac{2.549 \times 10^4}{10.78} \approx 2{,}365\ \text{m}^2$$ **Solution (c):** First compute $V_g$: $$V_g = \frac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{18 \mu} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times 100 \times 9.81}{18 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$V_g = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-11} \times 981}{0.018} = \frac{2.4525 \times 10^{-8}}{0.018} = 1.36 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m·s}^{-1}$$ Then: $$Q = V_g \cdot \Sigma = 1.36 \times 10^{-6} \times 2365 \approx 3.22 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3\text{·s}^{-1}$$ Convert: $Q \approx 3.22\ \text{L·s}^{-1} \approx 11{,}600\ \text{L·h}^{-1}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Take-away:** A single tubular bowl can clarify ~10 m³/h of food liquid. This is exactly why the tubular centrifuge is reserved for *high-value, low-volume* applications — for high-volume work, a disc-stack centrifuge is preferred. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Tubular Centrifuge Functional Architecture The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of feed, separated phases, and energy within a tubular-bowl centrifuge, in compliance with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 diagram safeguard rules (alphanumeric node IDs, no markdown inside labels, no reserved keywords as standalone IDs). ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["FOOD LIQUID FEED
Milk / Juice / Oil"] --> node2["STATIONARY INLET NOZZLE
Bottom entry"] node2 --> node3["ACCELERATION VANES
Feed Wings"] node3 --> node4["ROTATING BOWL
Slender Vertical Tube"] node4 --> node5["CENTRIFUGAL FIELD
G = 13000 to 17000"] node5 --> node6["RADIAL PHASE SEPARATION
Dense phase to wall, light phase to axis"] node6 --> node7["HEAVY PHASE ACCUMULATION
Solids paste on inner wall"] node6 --> node8["LIGHT PHASE RISES AXIALLY
Clarified liquid"] node8 --> node9["TOP RING WEIR OVERFLOW
Discharge lip"] node9 --> node10["CLARIFIED LIQUID OUTLET
Continuous phase product"] node7 --> node11["BOWL STOPPED PERIODICALLY
Manual cleaning cycle"] node11 --> node12["SOLIDS REMOVED
Paste scraped from wall"] node5 -.-> node13["DRIVE SHAFT"] node13 -.-> node14["ELECTRIC MOTOR
15000 to 20000 rpm"] node4 -.-> node13 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Sigma-Theory Scale-Up Logic ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["PILOT TBC
Q1 Sigma1"] --> nodeB["CONSTANT CUT SIZE
Same d, same fluid"] nodeB --> nodeC["Sigma theory rule
Q scales as Sigma"] nodeC --> nodeD["DESIGN PRODUCTION TBC
Sigma2 required"] nodeD --> nodeE{"GEOMETRY FIXED?"} nodeE -- YES --> nodeF["CALCULATE NEW OMEGA
Omega = f of Sigma"] nodeE -- NO --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG["TARGET MACHINE
Q2 Sigma2 OMEGA2"] ``` ### 4.3 Schematic Description (for those reading the printed notes) A tubular-bowl centrifuge has the following **physical arrangement from top to bottom** of its vertical axis: 1. **Drive assembly** (top) — electric motor, step-up belt or gear drive, flexible coupling. 2. **Top bearing** — supports the rotating shaft. 3. **Stationary cover** — collects overflow, has sight glass, vapour outlet, and the ring-dam assembly. 4. **Slender bowl** — vertical stainless-steel tube, length 0.5 – 1.0 m, diameter 50 – 150 mm, with conical bottom. 5. **Acceleration vanes** (feed wings) — three or four radial vanes welded to the bottom, to bring incoming liquid to bowl speed. 6. **Bottom bearing** — supports the lower end of the shaft. 7. **Brake** — to stop the bowl for cleaning. 8. **Base frame and catch pan** — for spillage control. > [!NOTE] > **Sanitation note for food applications:** In dairy and juice plants, the bowl is built of **AISI 304 or 316 stainless steel**, surface-finished to **Ra ≤ 0.8 µm** to comply with **3-A Sanitary Standards** and **EHEDG** guidelines for clean-in-place (CIP) operation. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** > *Define the G-force of a centrifugal separator. Why is the tubular-bowl centrifuge classified as a "high-G" device, and what is its typical industrial G-force range?* **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > The **G-force** of a centrifugal separator is defined as the ratio of the centrifugal acceleration at the bowl wall to the standard gravitational acceleration: > $$Z = \frac{\omega^2 r_w}{g}$$ > It is dimensionless and quantifies how much stronger the centrifugal field is compared to Earth's gravity. A tubular-bowl centrifuge is classified as a *high-G* device because it rotates at very high speeds (15,000 – 20,000 rpm) in a slender bowl with small radius, producing $Z$ values in the range **13,000 – 17,000** (i.e. 13,000 – 17,000 times stronger than gravity). This enables rapid settling of sub-micron particles that no other mechanical separator of similar cost can remove. > **Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** > *State the Stokes' law for terminal settling velocity. Under what condition is Stokes' law valid, and how is it modified for settling inside a tubular centrifuge?* **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Stokes' law** for a spherical particle settling under gravity at low Reynolds number is: > $$v_t = \frac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{18 \mu}$$ > where $d$ is the particle diameter, $\rho_p$ and $\rho_f$ are particle and fluid densities, $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity, and $g = 9.81$ m·s⁻². Stokes' law is valid only when the **particle Reynolds number $Re_p = d v_t \rho_f / \mu < 1$** (laminar flow around the particle). For settling inside a tubular centrifuge, the *centrifugal* body force replaces gravity, and the radial settling velocity at radius $r$ becomes: > $$v_c(r) = \frac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2 r}{18 \mu}$$ > This is simply $Z(r) = \omega^2 r / g$ times the gravitational terminal velocity, but with the key feature that it *increases with radius*, accelerating particles as they spiral outward. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) > **Part B — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** > > **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **tubular-bowl centrifuge**. Highlight its key components and their functions. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** A tubular centrifuge has the following specifications: $r_w = 50$ mm, $r_t = 15$ mm, $L = 0.75$ m, rotational speed $N = 18{,}000$ rpm. Calculate the G-force, the sigma factor, and the volumetric throughput for particles of $d = 2\ \mu\text{m}$, $\Delta\rho = 80$ kg·m⁻³, $\mu = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) The tubular-bowl centrifuge is a vertical, slender, high-speed centrifugal clarifier. Its key components and their functions are: 1. **Bowl** — A vertical stainless-steel tube (length-to-diameter ratio ≈ 4–7), imperforate, closed at the bottom, open at the top. It is the main separation chamber. 2. **Drive shaft and motor** — An electric motor coupled through a flexible coupling and gear/belt drive to a vertical shaft that rotates the bowl at 15,000 – 20,000 rpm. 3. **Feed inlet nozzle (stationary)** — Introduces the feed liquid at the bottom of the bowl from below. 4. **Acceleration vanes (feed wings)** — Three or four radial vanes welded inside the bowl bottom that accelerate the incoming liquid up to bowl angular velocity without relative motion (minimises turbulence). 5. **Top bearing and bottom bearing** — Support the rotating shaft; the bottom bearing is often a self-aligning ball bearing rated for high speeds. 6. **Brake** — A mechanical or electric brake to bring the bowl to rest for cleaning. 7. **Stationary cover (hood)** — Collects the clarified overflow liquid via a ring weir or adjustable dam; includes a sight glass and a vapour vent. 8. **Splash ring and catch pan** — Direct the discharged clarified liquid to the product outlet. **Working principle:** The feed enters the bowl bottom, is accelerated to bowl speed by the feed wings, and forms a thin vertical annular pool between the bowl wall (radius $r_w$) and the inner air/liquid vortex (radius $r_t$). Denser particles experience the centrifugal body force $\omega^2 r$ and migrate radially outward to deposit on the inner wall, forming a solids paste. The clarified liquid migrates inward, rises axially, and overflows the top ring weir. The bowl is stopped periodically for manual cleaning. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often *omit the acceleration vanes* and *forget to mention the manual cleaning cycle*. Both are worth marks. Also, students frequently draw a *horizontal* centrifuge instead of a *vertical* one — this loses 1–2 marks for wrong orientation. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Compute angular velocity:** $$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 18{,}000}{60} = 1884.96\ \text{rad·s}^{-1}$$ **Step 2 — G-force:** $$Z = \frac{\omega^2 r_w}{g} = \frac{(1884.96)^2 \times 0.050}{9.81} = \frac{177{,}657}{9.81} \approx 18{,}110$$ **Step 3 — Sigma factor:** $$\Sigma = \frac{\pi (r_w^2 - r_t^2) L \omega^2}{g \cdot \ln(r_w / r_t)}$$ $$r_w^2 - r_t^2 = 0.050^2 - 0.015^2 = 2.500 \times 10^{-3} - 2.250 \times 10^{-4} = 2.275 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$$ $$\ln(r_w / r_t) = \ln(0.050 / 0.015) = \ln(3.333) = 1.2040$$ $$\Sigma = \frac{\pi \times 2.275 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.75 \times (1884.96)^2}{9.81 \times 1.2040}$$ $$\Sigma = \frac{\pi \times 2.275 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.75 \times 3.553 \times 10^{6}}{11.811}$$ $$\Sigma = \frac{19{,}054}{11.811} \approx 1{,}613\ \text{m}^2$$ **Step 4 — Settling velocity $V_g$ (cut-size):** $$V_g = \frac{d^2 \Delta\rho \, g}{18 \mu} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times 80 \times 9.81}{18 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$V_g = \frac{4 \times 10^{-12} \times 784.8}{0.0216} = \frac{3.139 \times 10^{-9}}{0.0216} = 1.453 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m·s}^{-1}$$ **Step 5 — Throughput:** $$Q = V_g \times \Sigma = 1.453 \times 10^{-7} \times 1613 = 2.34 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3\text{·s}^{-1}$$ $$\boxed{Q \approx 0.234\ \text{L·s}^{-1} \approx 844\ \text{L·h}^{-1}}$$ --- > **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** > *(Internal alternative — students answer either A or B.)* > > **(a)** Derive an expression for the **sigma factor** of a tubular-bowl centrifuge starting from the radial settling velocity under Stokes' law. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** Two tubular centrifuges are being compared. Machine 1: $r_w = 0.05$ m, $r_t = 0.02$ m, $L = 0.7$ m, $N = 15{,}000$ rpm. Machine 2: $r_w = 0.04$ m, $r_t = 0.015$ m, $L = 0.6$ m, $N = 20{,}000$ rpm. Calculate the ratio $Q_2 / Q_1$ of throughputs for the same feed. Which machine is more efficient on a per-unit-volume basis? **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Assumptions:** 1. The liquid moves through the bowl in *plug flow* — no axial mixing, no axial dispersion. 2. Stokes' law is valid (laminar flow, $Re_p < 1$). 3. The particle concentration is dilute — no hindered settling. 4. The bowl is vertical, the liquid pool is annular, and the air/liquid interface is cylindrical. 5. Particle reaches the wall before exiting the top of the bowl. **Derivation:** (i) Radial settling velocity at radius $r$ under Stokes' law: $$v_c(r) = \frac{dr}{dt} = \frac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2 r}{18 \mu}$$ (ii) Separate variables and integrate from $r_t$ to $r_w$ over residence time $t_{res}$: $$\int_{r_t}^{r_w} \frac{dr}{r} = \frac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2}{18 \mu} \int_0^{t_{res}} dt$$ $$\ln\!\left(\frac{r_w}{r_t}\right) = \frac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2 t_{res}}{18 \mu}$$ (iii) Residence time from $t_{res} = V / Q$ where $V = \pi (r_w^2 - r_t^2) L$: $$\frac{\pi (r_w^2 - r_t^2) L}{Q} = \frac{18 \mu \ln(r_w / r_t)}{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2}$$ (iv) Rearranging for $Q$: $$Q = \frac{\pi (r_w^2 - r_t^2) L \, d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2}{18 \mu \ln(r_w / r_t)}$$ (v) By definition, $\Sigma$ is the equivalent area of a gravity settler performing the same job. In gravity settling, $Q = v_t \cdot \Sigma$, where $v_t = d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g / (18 \mu)$. Therefore: $$\Sigma = \frac{Q}{v_t} = \frac{\pi (r_w^2 - r_t^2) L \, \omega^2}{g \ln(r_w / r_t)}$$ This is the standard sigma factor for a tubular-bowl centrifuge. The expression depends only on geometry and rotational speed, not on the fluid or the particle. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Sigma of Machine 1:** $\omega_1 = 2\pi \times 15{,}000 / 60 = 1570.8$ rad·s⁻¹ $r_{w,1}^2 - r_{t,1}^2 = 0.05^2 - 0.02^2 = 2.10 \times 10^{-3}$ m² $\ln(r_{w,1}/r_{t,1}) = \ln(2.5) = 0.9163$ $$\Sigma_1 = \frac{\pi \times 2.10 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.7 \times (1570.8)^2}{9.81 \times 0.9163}$$ $$\Sigma_1 = \frac{\pi \times 2.10 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.7 \times 2.467 \times 10^6}{8.989} = \frac{11{,}395}{8.989} \approx 1{,}268\ \text{m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Sigma of Machine 2:** $\omega_2 = 2\pi \times 20{,}000 / 60 = 2094.4$ rad·s⁻¹ $r_{w,2}^2 - r_{t,2}^2 = 0.04^2 - 0.015^2 = 1.375 \times 10^{-3}$ m² $\ln(r_{w,2}/r_{t,2}) = \ln(2.667) = 0.9808$ $$\Sigma_2 = \frac{\pi \times 1.375 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.6 \times (2094.4)^2}{9.81 \times 0.9808}$$ $$\Sigma_2 = \frac{\pi \times 1.375 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.6 \times 4.387 \times 10^6}{9.622} = \frac{11{,}365}{9.622} \approx 1{,}181\ \text{m}^2$$ **Step 3 — Throughput ratio** (using $Q = V_g \Sigma$ with the same $V_g$ for both): $$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{\Sigma_2}{\Sigma_1} = \frac{1181}{1268} \approx 0.931$$ So Machine 2 has **about 7 % less throughput** than Machine 1, despite running at higher speed, because it is geometrically smaller. **Step 4 — Per-unit-volume efficiency:** Bowl volume $V_1 = \pi (2.10 \times 10^{-3}) (0.7) = 4.62 \times 10^{-3}$ m³ Bowl volume $V_2 = \pi (1.375 \times 10^{-3}) (0.6) = 2.59 \times 10^{-3}$ m³ $$\frac{\Sigma_1}{V_1} = \frac{1268}{4.62 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.74 \times 10^5\ \text{m}^2\text{·m}^{-3}$$ $$\frac{\Sigma_2}{V_2} = \frac{1181}{2.59 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.56 \times 10^5\ \text{m}^2\text{·m}^{-3}$$ **Conclusion:** Machine 2 is **more efficient on a per-unit-volume basis** (66 % more sigma per m³ of bowl), making it preferable when floor space is limited. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rad·s⁻¹** — most students lose 1–2 marks per sub-question by using $N$ in the $\omega$ equation directly. Always write $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$. > 2. **Mixing up $\ln$ and log₁₀** — calculators default to log₁₀; ensure you press the `ln` key for $\ln(r_w / r_t)$. > 3. **Forgetting the $\pi$ in the sigma formula** — at least 1 mark is reserved for the correct expression with all factors. > 4. **Using $r_w$ only in the numerator** — the expression needs $(r_w^2 - r_t^2)$, not just $r_w^2$. > 5. **Failing to check the Stokes' law validity** — for very fine particles ($d < 1\ \mu$m), Brownian motion and slip corrections matter; KTU examiners love testing this. > 6. **Wrong units in the final answer** — always state $Q$ in m³·s⁻¹ *and* convert to L·h⁻¹ for readability. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep)** - ✅ A **tubular-bowl centrifuge** is a high-G, imperforate, vertical centrifuge used for **clarification** of food liquids. - ✅ Operates at **15,000 – 20,000 rpm**, generating **G-forces of 13,000 – 17,000**. - ✅ Constructed of **AISI 304/316 stainless steel**; meets **3-A / EHEDG** sanitary standards. - ✅ G-force definition: $Z = \omega^2 r_w / g = \pi^2 N^2 r_w / (900 g)$. - ✅ Radial settling velocity in bowl: $v_c(r) = d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2 r / (18 \mu)$. - ✅ Stokes' law is valid for $Re_p = d v_t \rho_f / \mu < 1$. - ✅ **Sigma factor (most important formula!):** $$\Sigma = \frac{\pi (r_w^2 - r_t^2) L \omega^2}{g \cdot \ln(r_w / r_t)}$$ - ✅ **Scale-up rule:** $Q_1 / Q_2 = \Sigma_1 / \Sigma_2$ for the same cut size and same feed. - ✅ **Plug flow** assumption is fundamental to the derivation — explicitly state it. - ✅ Solids are removed **manually** after bowl stoppage — it is a *batch* (intermittent) clarifier, not continuous. - ✅ **Cannot handle high solids loading** — typically limited to <1 % solids in feed; for >5 % solids, switch to a decanter or disc-stack. - ✅ Typical food applications: **milk clarification, fruit juice clarification, edible oil degumming, wine/beer clarification, cell harvesting**. - ✅ **Key advantage:** Highest G-force per unit cost in the centrifuge family. - ✅ **Key disadvantage:** Low throughput (~1 – 10 m³/h), labour-intensive cleaning, not suitable for solid-rich feeds. - ✅ For exam: **always** state the five assumptions of the sigma derivation, **always** convert $N$ in rpm to $\omega$ in rad·s⁻¹, **always** verify the Stokes' regime.

topics.title: Spiral wound and hollow fibre systems; types of membranes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT527 - SEPARATION PROCESSES IN FOOD TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Spiral wound and hollow fibre systems; types of membranes ## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW > [!IMPORTANT] > **Membrane Module:** A membrane module is a compact, standardized housing unit containing one or more membranes of specific geometry, designed to facilitate large-scale industrial separation by providing high surface area per unit volume, controlled flow distribution, and mechanical support. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The 2024 scheme requires students to describe the construction, flow geometry, advantages, limitations, and food-industry applications of **Spiral Wound** and **Hollow Fibre** membrane modules, along with a classification of membrane types based on material, structure, and pore-size cut-off. ### 1.1 Spiral Wound Membrane Module — Formal Definition A **Spiral Wound Module (SWM)** is a flat-sheet membrane configuration in which a permeate-side spacer, two membranes (with their active layers facing outward), and a feed-side spacer are wound together around a central perforated permeate collection tube to form a compact cylindrical cartridge. The feed flows **axially** through the spacers in a cross-flow manner, while the permeate spirals **inward** and exits through the central tube. > [!NOTE] > **Packing Density:** Spiral wound modules typically achieve a **packing density of 600–1000 m²/m³**, making them the workhorse of the dairy, beverage, and RO industry. ### 1.2 Hollow Fibre Membrane Module — Formal Definition A **Hollow Fibre Module (HFM)** consists of thousands to millions of narrow, self-supporting polymeric or ceramic capillary fibres (typically **inner diameter 0.5–2.0 mm** for ultrafiltration and as small as **40–100 µm** for reverse osmosis) bundled together and potted at both ends inside a cylindrical pressure shell. Feed can flow either **inside the lumen (inside-out)** or **outside the fibres (outside-in)**, with permeate collected from the opposite side. > [!NOTE] > **Packing Density:** Hollow fibre modules achieve the **highest packing density in industry — 6000–12000 m²/m³**, roughly 10× greater than spiral wound systems. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bicycle Wheel vs. Bristle Brush" Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy:** > - **Spiral Wound Module** = Think of rolling a **multilayered sandwich (bread–filling–bread–filling)** tightly around a drinking straw. The straw acts as the permeate tube, the fillings are the spacers, and the bread is the membrane. When you pour water along the roll, the liquid that "seeps through" the bread collects in the straw. > - **Hollow Fibre Module** = Imagine a **bunch of drinking straws glued together at both ends** inside a PVC pipe. If you pump dirty water through the straws from one end, clean water "sweats" out through the straw walls and is collected on the shell side. This is exactly how a hemodialysis artificial kidney works. This intuition helps visualize why hollow fibre offers higher surface area (more straws per pipe) while spiral wound offers easier cleaning (open spacer channels). ### 1.4 Types of Membranes — Formal Classification Membranes are classified along **three independent axes**: | Classification Basis | Categories | |---|---| | **By Material** | Polymeric (PES, PS, PVDF, CA, PA), Ceramic (Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, TiO₂), Metallic (Pd, Ag), Composite (Thin-Film Composite — TFC) | | **By Structure** | Symmetric (Isotropic), Asymmetric (Integrally Skinned), Composite (Thin-Film) | | **By Pore Size / Separation Mechanism** | Microfiltration (MF, 0.1–10 µm), Ultrafiltration (UF, 1–100 nm), Nanofiltration (NF, ~0.5–5 nm), Reverse Osmosis (RO, <0.5 nm), Electrodialysis (ED), Pervaporation (PV) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric (KTU 2024):** The **MWCO (Molecular Weight Cut-Off)** is defined in **Daltons (Da)** and represents the molecular weight of a solute (typically a globular protein like **lysozyme — 14,300 Da** or **BSA — 67,000 Da**) that is 90 % rejected by the membrane. ## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 Spiral Wound Module — Construction & Flow Logic **Step-by-step operational logic:** 1. **Envelope formation:** Two flat-sheet membranes are glued along three sides (length and one width) with the **active rejection layer facing outward**, leaving one long edge open. 2. **Spacer insertion:** A **permeate carrier ( tricot spacer)** is placed inside the envelope, and a **feed spacer (net-like, ~0.7–1.1 mm thick)** is placed outside. 3. **Winding:** The assembly is wound around a **perforated permeate tube (ATD — Anti-Telescoping Device)** to give a cylindrical cartridge, typically **4-inch or 8-inch diameter** and **40-inch (1016 mm) length** as industry standard. 4. **Sealing:** The outer wrap is sealed with a fiberglass/PU outer shell to prevent bypass. 5. **Operation:** Feed enters axially through the spacer mesh, permeate passes through the membrane, spirals into the carrier, and exits the central tube. Retentate leaves the other end. **Why the spacer design matters:** - The feed spacer creates **turbulence** (Reynolds number in spacer channels typically **Re ≈ 100–500**) that reduces concentration polarization. - The permeate spacer offers **low hydraulic resistance** to the radial flow of permeate toward the central tube. ### 2.2 Hollow Fibre Module — Construction & Flow Logic 1. **Fibre spinning:** Fibres are spun by **dry-jet wet spinning** (polymeric) or **extrusion-sintering** (ceramic). 2. **Bundle formation:** Thousands of fibres are bundled in parallel, often looped in a **U-configuration** with both ends potted in one header (giving 2N fibre ends in one pot) or in a **dead-end configuration** (both ends in separate pots). 3. **Potting:** Epoxy or polyurethane resin seals the fibres into a tube sheet; the open ends of the fibres act as the permeate port. 4. **Shell-side feed / lumen-side feed:** For **UF/MF** (larger fibres, low pressure), shell-side feed is preferred; for **RO** (very fine fibres, high pressure), lumen-side feed is used to handle the high ΔP without fibre collapse. **Critical parameters:** - **Fibre wall thickness:** ~50–200 µm - **Trans-membrane pressure (TMP):** **0.1–0.3 MPa (1–3 bar)** for UF/MF; **1.5–8 MPa (15–80 bar)** for RO - **Shear rate at fibre wall:** Critical for flux; depends on cross-flow velocity ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently asks for **flux, area, and rejection** problems. Memorize the equations below — they account for ~60% of Module 2 numerical questions. | # | Equation | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $J = \dfrac{V_p}{A_m \cdot t}$ | Volumetric flux definition | $\text{m}^3/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s})$ | | 2 | $J = L_p \left( \Delta P - \sigma \Delta \pi \right)$ | Kedem–Katchalsky / Solution-Diffusion flux | $\text{m/s}$ | | 3 | $R_{\text{obs}} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}$ | Observed solute rejection (decimal) | — | | 4 | $R_{\text{true}} = \dfrac{R_{\text{obs}} \cdot \exp(J/k)}{1 - R_{\text{obs}} + R_{\text{obs}} \cdot \exp(J/k)}$ | True rejection (corrects for CP) | — | | 5 | $A_m = \dfrac{Q_p}{J}$ | Required membrane area | $\text{m}^2$ | | 6 | $Y = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_f}$ | Recovery / Yield (mass balance) | — | | 7 | $\text{PD} = \dfrac{A_m}{V_{\text{module}}}$ | Packing density | $\text{m}^2/\text{m}^3$ | | 8 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v d_h}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number in spacer/lumen | dimensionless | | 9 | $k = 0.023 \cdot \text{Re}^{0.8} \cdot \text{Sc}^{0.33} \cdot \dfrac{D}{d_h}$ | Mass-transfer coefficient (Dittus–Boelter analog) | $\text{m/s}$ | | 10 | $N_f = \dfrac{\text{PD} \cdot V_{\text{module}} \cdot d_f}{\pi \cdot d_o \cdot L_f}$ | Number of fibres in a HFM module | — | | 11 | $\Delta P_{\text{lumen}} = \dfrac{8 \mu L_f v}{\pi r_f^4} \cdot \dfrac{\dot{V}}{N_f}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille pressure drop inside fibre | Pa | | 12 | $J_{\text{wall}} = \dfrac{\tau_w}{\mu}$ | Shear-induced wall flux (for non-Newtonian foods) | $\text{m/s}$ | > **Symbol key:** $J$ = flux; $L_p$ = hydraulic permeability (m/Pa·s); $\Delta P$ = TMP (Pa); $\sigma$ = reflection coefficient; $\Delta \pi$ = osmotic pressure difference (Pa); $C_f, C_p$ = feed and permeate concentrations; $k$ = mass transfer coefficient; $Q_p, Q_f$ = permeate and feed volumetric flow rates; $d_f$ = fibre inner diameter; $L_f$ = fibre length; $\tau_w$ = wall shear stress. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Food Industry Use Cases:** > - **Dairy:** SWM is dominant for **whey protein concentration (UF)** and **lactose removal (RO)** in cheese manufacture (e.g., GEA, Koch Membrane Systems). > - **Beverage:** HFM (polysulfone, 0.2 µm) for **cold-sterilization / clarification of wine, beer, and fruit juices** — preserves aroma and color because the process runs at **<40 °C**. > - **Sugar industry:** Ceramic HFM in **thin-juice purification**. > - **Oils & fats:** SWM with **PVDF membranes** for degumming and deacidification of edible oils. > - **Water reuse in food plants:** HFM-RO for **CIP (Clean-in-Place) water recovery**. ## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Membrane Area from Flux and Permeate Demand **Given:** A fruit-juice clarification unit uses a spiral wound UF module. The required permeate flow rate is $Q_p = 5.0 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$ and the steady-state flux is $J = 80 \text{ L}/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{h})$. Determine the membrane area. **Step 1:** Convert units to a common basis. $$J = 80 \; \frac{\text{L}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{h}} = 0.080 \; \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{h}}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the area-flux-permeate relation $A_m = Q_p / J$. $$A_m = \frac{5.0 \; \text{m}^3/\text{h}}{0.080 \; \text{m}^3/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{h})}$$ **Step 3:** Cancel hours and solve. $$A_m = \frac{5.0}{0.080} \; \text{m}^2 = 62.5 \; \text{m}^2$$ **Conclusion:** A total membrane area of **62.5 m²** is required. **Step 4 (Engineering check):** With an SWM packing density of 800 m²/m³, the module volume = 62.5 / 800 = **0.078 m³ (~78 L)** — easily fits in a single industrial 4-inch element stack. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hollow Fibre Count from Packing Density **Given:** A polysulfone HFM for whey UF has packing density $\text{PD} = 8000 \; \text{m}^2/\text{m}^3$, fibre outer diameter $d_o = 1.5 \; \text{mm}$, fibre length $L_f = 1.0 \; \text{m}$, and module volume $V = 0.05 \; \text{m}^3$. **Step 1:** Total membrane area in the module. $$A_m = \text{PD} \times V = 8000 \times 0.05 = 400 \; \text{m}^2$$ **Step 2:** Area contributed by a single fibre (outer surface, since outside-in feed for UF). $$A_{\text{fibre}} = \pi \cdot d_o \cdot L_f = \pi \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times 1.0 = 4.712 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^2$$ **Step 3:** Number of fibres required. $$N_f = \frac{A_m}{A_{\text{fibre}}} = \frac{400}{4.712 \times 10^{-3}} = 84{,}889 \; \text{fibres}$$ **Step 4:** Approximate ~ **85,000 hollow fibres** in a single 50 L module — illustrates why HFM dominates high-area applications. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pressure Drop Inside a Hollow Fibre (Lumen-Side Feed) **Given:** RO module with $N_f = 1000$ fibres, each of length $L_f = 1.0 \; \text{m}$, inner radius $r_f = 100 \; \mu\text{m} = 1 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}$, water viscosity $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, total feed flow $\dot{V} = 10 \; \text{L/h}$. **Step 1:** Volumetric flow per fibre. $$\dot{V}_{\text{fibre}} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}/3600}{1000} = 2.78 \times 10^{-9} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 2:** Average velocity inside the lumen. $$v = \frac{\dot{V}_{\text{fibre}}}{\pi r_f^2} = \frac{2.78 \times 10^{-9}}{\pi (1 \times 10^{-4})^2} = 0.0885 \; \text{m/s}$$ **Step 3:** Apply Hagen–Poiseuille for laminar flow inside a tube. $$\Delta P_{\text{lumen}} = \frac{8 \mu L_f v}{\pi r_f^4} \cdot \dot{V}_{\text{fibre}}$$ **Step 4:** Substituting numbers (using the $\dot{V}$ form to avoid recomputation). $$\Delta P_{\text{lumen}} = \frac{8 \times (1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times 1.0 \times 0.0885}{\pi \times (1 \times 10^{-4})^2} = 2{,}253 \; \text{Pa} \approx 22.5 \; \text{mbar}$$ **Conclusion:** The lumen-side pressure drop is only **~23 mbar**, far below the 60–80 bar operating pressure, confirming that lumen-feed is hydraulically feasible even for RO. ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SWM vs. HFM | Parameter | Spiral Wound (SWM) | Hollow Fibre (HFM) | |---|---|---| | Packing Density (m²/m³) | 600–1000 | 6000–12000 | | Typical Geometry | Flat sheet, 4–8 inch × 40 inch | Capillary, 0.04–2 mm bore | | Operating Pressure | 1–80 bar (broad) | 1–3 bar (UF/MF), up to 80 bar (RO) | | Flow Configuration | Cross-flow, axial feed | Shell-side or lumen-side, cross-flow | | Fouling Resistance | Moderate (spacer shadowing) | High (open channel / smooth bore) | | Cleaning Ease | Easy (open spacer) | Moderate (delicate fibres) | | Cost per m² | Low | Very Low (UF/MF); High (RO) | | Mechanical Robustness | High (sealed wrap) | Moderate (fibre breakage) | | Best Food Application | Whey/dairy UF, beverage RO, sugar | Juice clarification, milk fat fractionation, hemodialysis, water reuse | | Limitation | Dead spots at glue lines; spacer plugging | Fibre breakage; lumen plugging in RO | ### 3.5 Membrane Material Selection — Engineering Decision Tree > [!TIP] > **KTU-style Short Note (4 marks):** Justify membrane material for **apple juice clarification**. > **Answer key (model):** Apple juice is acidic (**pH 3.0–4.0**), contains pectin (high MW polymer) and requires high retention of aroma esters. A **polysulfone (PSf) or polyethersulfone (PES) UF membrane** with **MWCO 10–50 kDa**, operated at **<35 °C** and **TMP = 0.5–2.0 bar**, is the industry choice because (a) PSf/PES tolerates pH 2–13 (acid-stable), (b) 10 kDa MWCO retains pectin and haze-forming proteins, (c) low temperature preserves heat-labile aroma compounds (e.g., hexyl acetate), and (d) ceramic alternatives (e.g., α-Al₂O₃) are used only when high-temperature SIP (>90 °C) is mandated. ## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 Spiral Wound Module — Functional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Feed Inlet
Axial Entry] --> B[Feed Spacer Channel
Crossflow 0.1-2 m/s] B --> C[Membrane Envelope 1
Active Layer Outward] B --> D[Membrane Envelope 2
Active Layer Outward] C --> E[Permeate Carrier Spacer
Tricot Mesh] D --> E E --> F[Central Perforated Tube
Permeate Collection] F --> G[Permeate Outlet
Radial Exit] B --> H[Retentate Exit
Axial Discharge] I[Pressure Vessel Shell
FRP Wrap] --- B I --- C I --- D ``` > **Reading guide:** The flowchart mirrors the physical flow: feed enters → distributes through spacer → permeates through the membrane → spirals into permeate carrier → exits through the central tube; retentate leaves at the other axial end. ### 4.2 Hollow Fibre Module — Inside-Out Configuration (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Feed Inlet
Shell Side] --> B[Fibre Bundle
10000s of Capillaries] B --> C[Polysulfone or PES Fibre
MWCO 10-100 kDa] C --> D[Permeate Inside Lumen
Driven by TMP 0.5-2 bar] D --> E[Fibre Open End 1
Pot in Tube Sheet] E --> F[Permeate Manifold
Collection Header] F --> G[Permeate Outlet] B --> H[Retentate Exit
Shell Side Other End] I[Loop Configuration
U-Bend Potting] --- B ``` ### 4.3 Membrane Classification Master Map (Mermaid Mind-Map) ```mermaid flowchart TB M[Membranes for Food Technology] --> MAT[By Material] M --> STR[By Structure] M --> POR[By Pore Size or Mechanism] MAT --> P1[Polymeric PES PS PVDF CA PA] MAT --> P2[Ceramic Al2O3 ZrO2 TiO2] MAT --> P3[Metallic Pd Ag] MAT --> P4[Thin Film Composite TFC] STR --> S1[Symmetric Isotropic] STR --> S2[Asymmetric Integrally Skinned] STR --> S3[Composite TFC] POR --> Q1[MF 0.1 to 10 micrometer] POR --> Q2[UF 1 to 100 nanometer] POR --> Q3[NF 0.5 to 5 nanometer] POR --> Q4[RO less than 0.5 nanometer] POR --> Q5[ED PV MD] ``` ### 4.4 Operational Flow — Cross-Flow Filtration Logic ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Feed Tank] --> P[Feed Pump High Pressure] P --> B[Membrane Module SWM or HFM] B --> R[Retentate Recycle or Discharge] B --> Q[Permeate Collection] R -.Recycle.-> A ``` > **Note on the diagram choice:** Because actual spiral-wound and hollow-fibre modules involve three-dimensional geometry that mermaid cannot render faithfully, the block diagrams above present the **functional flow architecture** as required by the engine specification. ## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP, Module 2]** *Distinguish between "symmetric" and "asymmetric" membranes. Give one example of each used in the food industry.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **symmetric (isotropic) membrane** has uniform pore size and structure throughout its thickness; mass transfer is governed by pore flow and rejection is limited (e.g., **Membrane Filters MF, 0.2 µm cellulose acetate** for cold sterilization of beer — 1 mark). An **asymmetric membrane** has a thin, dense skin (0.1–0.5 µm) supported on a much thicker (100–300 µm) porous sublayer of the same polymer; the skin governs selectivity, the sublayer gives mechanical strength (e.g., **PES UF membrane with 5 kDa skin for whey protein concentration** — 1 mark). The asymmetric structure gives **10–100× higher flux** than symmetric membranes at equivalent rejection (1 mark). **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP, Module 2]** *What is the "Molecular Weight Cut-Off" (MWCO) of a membrane? How is it experimentally determined?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **MWCO** is defined as the molecular weight (in **Daltons, Da**) of a reference solute that is **90% rejected** by the membrane under standardized test conditions (typically **25 °C, TMP = 100 kPa, cross-flow velocity 0.5 m/s, pH 7**) — (1 mark). It is determined by **filtering a mixture of standard reference solutes of known molecular weights** (e.g., **cytochrome c (12,400 Da), lysozyme (14,300 Da), BSA (67,000 Da), γ-globulin (160,000 Da)**) and measuring rejection for each — (1 mark). A plot of **% rejection vs. log MW** is drawn; the MW at 90% rejection is reported as the MWCO — (1 mark). --- ### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question has two sub-parts (typically 7 + 7). Both Question A and Question B cover the same topic; students answer EITHER A or B. --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Spiral Wound Modules in Depth** *`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted from Past Papers]`* **(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the construction of a **Spiral Wound Membrane (SWM) module**.
