List its **advantages and limitations** for food-industry applications. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** *Construction (4 marks):* 1. **Membrane envelope** — Two flat-sheet membranes (typical length 1.0 m, width equivalent to module diameter) are sealed along three edges using polyurethane or epoxy adhesive, with the **active rejection layer facing outward**. 2. **Permeate carrier** — A **tricot-knit or spacer fabric** is placed between the two membranes to provide a low-resistance flow path. 3. **Feed spacer** — A **diamond or ladder-shaped net** (~0.7–1.1 mm thick) is glued on the outside of the envelope to create cross-flow channels and induce turbulence. 4. **Winding** — The envelope is wound around a **perforated central permeate tube (PVC or ABS)** equipped with ATDs (anti-telescoping devices) at both ends to prevent the roll from shifting under pressure. 5. **Outer shell** — The rolled cartridge is wrapped with **fiberglass-reinforced tape and shrink-wrap** for mechanical strength. 6. **Standard dimensions** — Industry standard: **4-inch (101.6 mm) or 8-inch (203.2 mm) diameter × 40-inch (1016 mm) length**, fitted inside a **stainless steel or FRP pressure vessel**. *Advantages (1.5 marks):* - High **packing density (600–1000 m²/m³)** → compact plant footprint. - **Low cost per m²** of membrane area. - Can be operated at **high pressure (up to 80 bar)** → suitable for RO/NF of milk, whey, and sugar. - **Easy to clean** because of the open feed spacer. - **Modular and scalable** — elements are simply added in series or parallel. *Limitations (1.5 marks):* - Feed spacer **induces particle shadowing and biofouling** at the spacer-membrane contact. - **Dead spots at glue lines** cause localized fouling. - The narrow spacer channel gives **high pressure drop** and limits recovery per stage. - Not ideal for **highly viscous feeds** (e.g., concentrated fruit purees). --- **(b)** A dairy plant wants to concentrate **10,000 L/h of skim milk** (TDS ≈ 12%, osmotic pressure 1.8 MPa) from **9 % to 20 % total solids** using a **spiral wound RO system**. Assume: $L_p = 4 \times 10^{-12}$ m/(Pa·s), $\sigma = 0.95$, permeate is essentially pure water, target recovery $Y = 0.75$. Calculate: **(i)** required membrane area, **(ii)** number of 4-inch elements needed (each provides 30 m²), and **(iii)** required feed pressure. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Mass balance to find permeate volume (1 mark):** $$V_p = Y \cdot V_f = 0.75 \times 10{,}000 = 7{,}500 \; \text{L/h} = 7.5 \; \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$ **Required flux at $\Delta P = 30$ bar (1.5 bar = 0.3 MPa osmotic) (1 mark):** $$J = L_p (\Delta P - \sigma \Delta \pi) = 4 \times 10^{-12} \times (30 \times 10^5 - 0.95 \times 1.8 \times 10^6)$$ $$J = 4 \times 10^{-12} \times (3.0 \times 10^6 - 1.71 \times 10^6) = 4 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.29 \times 10^6$$ $$J = 5.16 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m/s} = 18.6 \; \text{L}/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{h})$$ **Required membrane area (1 mark):** $$A_m = \frac{V_p}{J} = \frac{7500 \; \text{L/h}}{18.6 \; \text{L}/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{h})} = 403 \; \text{m}^2$$ **(ii) Number of 4-inch elements (1 mark):** $$N_{\text{elements}} = \frac{403}{30} \approx 14 \; \text{elements}$$ **Practical answer: 14 spiral-wound RO elements, arranged in a 2-stage tapered array (e.g., 8 + 6) for high recovery.** **(iii) Required feed pressure (1 mark):** $$P_{\text{feed}} = \Delta P + P_{\text{permeate}} = 30 \; \text{bar} + 0 \approx 30 \; \text{bar (gauge)}$$ --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Hollow Fibre Modules in Depth** *`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted from Past Papers]`* **(a)** Compare **inside-out** and **outside-in** flow configurations in **Hollow Fibre Modules**. Under what circumstances is each preferred? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** *Inside-Out (Lumen-Side Feed) (3.5 marks):* - Feed flows **inside the fibre lumen**; permeate passes through the wall and is collected on the shell side. - **Mechanical support:** The internal feed pressure keeps the fibre **inflated**, making it suitable for **high-pressure operations (RO, NF) at 30–80 bar**. - **Cleanliness:** The lumen is **easily cleaned by reverse flushing**; this configuration is the **industry standard for RO brackish and sea-water desalination**. - **Disadvantage:** **High lumen-side pressure drop** (Hagen–Poiseuille, $\Delta P \propto 1/r^4$) restricts fibre length to typically **≤ 1.5 m**. *Outside-In (Shell-Side Feed) (3.5 marks):* - Feed is distributed across the **outer surface of the fibre bundle**; permeate is collected from the **lumen**. - **High cross-flow velocity** (1–3 m/s) achievable on the shell side with low pressure drop → suitable for **UF/MF where flux is limited by concentration polarization and fouling**. - **Larger fibre diameter** (1–2 mm) is permissible because the lumen is on the permeate (low-pressure) side. - **Preferred for:** dairy whey UF, juice clarification, plasma fractionation, haemodialysis. - **Disadvantage:** Non-uniform shell-side flow distribution can cause **channelling** and bypass; **air-binding** is a common operational issue. *Selection rule of thumb (built into the 0.5 mark rationale):* **Lumen feed = high pressure + clean feed**; **Shell feed = low pressure + fouling-prone feed**. --- **(b)** A winery uses a **polysulfone Hollow Fibre UF module** (MWCO 100 kDa, $L_p = 8 \times 10^{-11}$ m/Pa·s) to clarify **2,000 L/h of grape must** (viscosity 1.2 mPa·s, density 1050 kg/m³). The fibres have $d_i = 1.0 \; \text{mm}$, $L_f = 1.2 \; \text{m}$, $N_f = 12{,}000$, and the target flux is $J = 60 \; \text{L}/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{h})$ at $\Delta P = 1.5 \; \text{bar}$. Calculate: **(i)** the Reynolds number in the lumen, **(ii)** the mass-transfer coefficient $k$ using $k = 0.023 \, \text{Re}^{0.8} \, \text{Sc}^{0.33} \, D/d_h$ with $D = 5 \times 10^{-11} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}$, and **(iii)** the wall shear stress. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Reynolds Number (2 marks):** *Per-fibre volumetric flow:* $$\dot{V}_{\text{fibre}} = \frac{V_p}{N_f} = \frac{2{,}000 \times 0.9}{12{,}000} = 0.15 \; \text{L/h per fibre} = 4.17 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$ *Velocity in lumen:* $$v = \frac{\dot{V}_{\text{fibre}}}{\pi r_i^2} = \frac{4.17 \times 10^{-8}}{\pi \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3})^2} = 0.0531 \; \text{m/s}$$ *Reynolds number:* $$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho v d_i}{\mu} = \frac{1050 \times 0.0531 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} = 46.5$$ → **Laminar flow** (Re < 2,300), as expected for UF. **(ii) Schmidt number and k (3 marks):** $$\text{Sc} = \frac{\mu}{\rho D} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{1050 \times 5 \times 10^{-11}} = 22{,}857$$ $$k = 0.023 \times (46.5)^{0.8} \times (22{,}857)^{0.33} \times \frac{5 \times 10^{-11}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$k = 0.023 \times 22.4 \times 28.7 \times 5 \times 10^{-8} = 7.4 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{m/s}$$ **(iii) Wall shear stress (2 marks):** $$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot d_h}{4 L_f} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^5 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 1.2} = 31.25 \; \text{Pa}$$ > **Cross-check using $\tau_w = 8 \mu v / d$:** $$\tau_w = \frac{8 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.0531}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.51 \; \text{Pa (apparent)}$$ The discrepancy arises because the **flux-derived pressure** (1.5 bar over 1.2 m) gives the **total** ΔP including entrance effects, not just friction; in design, **friction is computed from velocity alone** and gives a much lower τ_w — students should report both and comment. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Module 2 — SWM & HFM):** > 1. **Unit mismatch:** Many students mix L/(m²·h) and m/s without converting. Always convert flux to **SI (m/s)** before substituting into $J = L_p(\Delta P - \sigma \Delta \pi)$. > 2. **Forgetting the osmotic pressure term in RO:** For RO of milk or juice, $\sigma \Delta \pi$ can be **1–8 MPa** and dominates the equation; ignoring it gives a 200–400% error in flux. > 3. **Confusing packing density and area:** A module rated "30 m²" has a *membrane area*, not a *footprint area*; the *footprint* (vessel volume) is **30 m² / 800 m²/m³ = 0.0375 m³**. > 4. **Spelling "Hollow Fibre" with one "l"** — KTU evaluator preference is the **British "Hollow Fibre" (double l)**. > 5. **Spacer vs. permeate carrier confusion:** The **feed spacer** is *outside* the membrane envelope; the **permeate carrier** is *inside*. Many diagrams are drawn backwards. > 6. **MWCO units:** Always state MWCO in **kDa or Da**, not µm — they are different metrics (size vs. molecular weight). > 7. **Forgetting membrane material's pH/temperature limits:** PES tolerates pH 2–13 and 95 °C; cellulose acetate tolerates only pH 3–7 and 35 °C. Wrong material recommendation = **−2 marks**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: SWM, HFM, and Membrane Types** - ☐ **Spiral Wound** = envelope wound around central tube; **packing density 600–1000 m²/m³**; workhorse of dairy/beverage RO. - ☐ **Hollow Fibre** = capillary bundle potted in shell; **packing density 6,000–12,000 m²/m³**; best for UF/MF clarification and high-area RO. - ☐ **Lumen feed** → high pressure (RO/NF); **Shell feed** → low pressure (UF/MF, fouling-prone). - ☐ **Flux equation** $J = L_p(\Delta P - \sigma \Delta \pi)$ — always include $\sigma \Delta \pi$ for RO/NF. - ☐ **MWCO** = MW of solute rejected 90 %; expressed in **Da or kDa**. - ☐ **Membrane materials:** Polymeric (cheap, broad pH), Ceramic (expensive, high T/pH), Metallic (specialty — H₂ separation), TFC (RO standard — polyamide on polysulfone). - ☐ **Structures:** Symmetric (uniform), Asymmetric (thin skin + porous sublayer), TFC (skin ≠ sublayer material). - ☐ **Pore-size spectrum:** **MF (0.1–10 µm) > UF (1–100 nm) > NF (0.5–5 nm) > RO (<0.5 nm)** — each one order tighter in rejection. - ☐ **Concentration polarization** reduces true rejection → use the **K/CP correction** equation. - ☐ **Reynolds number** inside a hollow fibre lumen is typically **< 100** (laminar) → use **Hagen–Poiseuille** for ΔP. - ☐ **Permeate carrier** is *inside* the envelope; **feed spacer** is *outside* — common exam trap. - ☐ **Hagen–Poiseuille pressure drop** scales as $1/r^4$ — small RO fibres give huge pressure drop, limiting length to ~1.5 m. - ☐ **Standard SWM size** is **4-inch or 8-inch × 40-inch**; elements are arranged in **tapered arrays** (e.g., 8:4:2) for high recovery without fouling. - ☐ **Food-industry mantra:** Cold operation preserves aroma → **membrane technology is "thermal-friend" of the food industry**.

topics.title: Materials for membrane
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT527 - SEPARATION PROCESSES IN FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Materials for membrane

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Separation Processes in Food Technology**, a **membrane material** is defined as a specialized, semi-permeable barrier phase (typically fabricated as a thin film, hollow fiber, or tubular structure) that selectively restricts the transport of specific chemical species, molecules, ions, or suspended solids under a defined driving force (pressure, concentration, temperature, or electrical potential gradient). The selection of membrane material fundamentally governs the **permeability**, **selectivity**, **mechanical integrity**, **chemical compatibility**, and **fouling behavior** of the entire membrane separation process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> For a Food Technology context, the membrane material must satisfy **food-grade regulatory compliance** (e.g., **FDA Title 21 CFR**, **EU Regulation 1935/2004**, **BIS standards**), ensuring no toxic leaching, no adverse odor/flavor transfer, and biocompatibility with the food matrix.

### 1.2 Classification of Membrane Materials

Membrane materials are broadly classified into four major families based on their chemical origin and physical structure:

| Category | Examples | Primary Use in Food Tech |
|----------|----------|--------------------------|
| **Polymeric (Organic)** | Cellulose Acetate, Polysulfone, PVDF, Polyamide | Dairy, juice clarification, whey processing |
| **Ceramic (Inorganic)** | Alumina (Al₂O₃), Zirconia (ZrO₂), Titania (TiO₂) | High-temperature sterilization, CIP-cleanable systems |
| **Metallic** | Stainless steel, Palladium alloys | Specialized gas separation, extreme pH/temperature |
| **Composite / Hybrid** | Thin-Film Composite (TFC), Mixed Matrix Membranes (MMM) | Nanofiltration, reverse osmosis desalination |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Definition – Asymmetric Membrane:**
> A membrane possessing a thin, dense top "skin" layer (0.1–1 µm) supported by a much thicker porous sub-layer (100–200 µm). The skin governs selectivity; the sub-layer provides mechanical strength.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> **The Coffee Filter Analogy** ☕
>
> Imagine a **sieve with adjustable mesh sizes**:
> - The **material** of the sieve is the *membrane material*.
> - The **mesh size** represents the *pore size rating* (Microfiltration, Ultrafiltration, Nanofiltration, Reverse Osmosis).
> - The **sieve frame** represents the *mechanical support* (substrate).
> - The **material coating** (e.g., non-stick Teflon vs. sticky nylon) represents *hydrophilicity vs. hydrophobicity*.
>
> Just as you would not use a rusted metal sieve for brewing tea, you cannot use a non-food-grade polymer for processing milk. The material dictates *what passes*, *what is rejected*, and *how easily it cleans*.

### 1.4 Ideal Property Requirements for Membrane Materials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A "perfect" membrane material should simultaneously satisfy the following engineering criteria:

1. **High Selectivity** – Sharp molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) discrimination.
2. **High Permeability** – Maximized flux (L/m²·h) at low transmembrane pressure.
3. **Mechanical Strength** – Withstand operating pressures up to **0.1–10 MPa** without compaction.
4. **Chemical Resistance** – Stable against acids, bases, sanitizers (NaOCl, H₂O₂, HNO₃).
5. **Thermal Stability** – Operate within **0–95 °C** for most polymeric systems; up to **400 °C** for ceramics.
6. **Hydrophilic Balance** – Anti-fouling surface energy to minimize protein/pigment adsorption.
7. **Food-Grade Inertness** – No migration of toxic monomers, plasticizers, or additives.
8. **Processability** – Capable of being cast, spun, sintered, or phase-inverted.
9. **Cost-Effectiveness** – Affordable membrane area per m² (target: **< ₹5,000/m²** for food-grade UF/MF).
10. **Long Service Life** – Minimum **2–5 years** operational lifespan under CIP (Clean-In-Place) cycles.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Polymeric Membrane Materials (Most Common in Food Industry)

#### 2.1.1 Cellulose Acetate (CA)
- **Chemical Nature:** Acetyl ester of cellulose; degree of substitution 2.4–2.9.
- **Properties:** Moderately hydrophilic, low fouling, good selectivity.
- **Limitation:** Poor chemical resistance (pH 2–8 only), hydrolyzes at extreme pH.
- **Food Applications:** Reverse osmosis of fruit juices, whey demineralization, vinegar clarification.

#### 2.1.2 Polysulfone (PS) and Polyethersulfone (PES)
- **Chemical Nature:** Aromatic polymers with –SO₂– and –O– linkages.
- **Properties:** Excellent thermal stability (T_g ≈ **190 °C**), high mechanical strength, broad pH tolerance (1–13).
- **Limitation:** Inherently **hydrophobic** → high protein fouling. Often blended with hydrophilic additives (PVP, PEG).
- **Food Applications:** Ultrafiltration of milk, beer clarification, enzyme recovery.

#### 2.1.3 Polyvinylidene Fluoride (PVDF)
- **Chemical Nature:** –(CH₂–CF₂)ₙ– repeat unit.
- **Properties:** Outstanding chemical resistance, mechanically robust, hydrophobic.
- **Food Applications:** Membrane Bioreactors (MBR), protein fractionation, vegetable oil filtration.

#### 2.1.4 Polyamide (PA) and Polyimide (PI)
- **Chemical Nature:** Amide (–CONH–) linkages.
- **Properties:** High selectivity, good thermal stability, used in **Thin-Film Composite (TFC)** form.
- **Food Applications:** Nanofiltration for sugar decolorization, lactose concentration, salt removal.

#### 2.1.5 Other Notable Polymers

| Polymer | Abbreviation | Key Feature | Typical Food Use |
|---------|--------------|-------------|-----------------|
| Polyacrylonitrile | PAN | Hydrophilic, solvent-resistant | UF of protein solutions |
| Polyethylene | PE | Cheap, flexible | Microfiltration, pre-filters |
| Polypropylene | PP | Hydrophobic, steam-sterilizable | Wine/beer sterile filtration |
| Polytetrafluoroethylene | PTFE | Extreme chemical inertness | Aggressive CIP environments |
| Polyvinyl chloride | PVC | Low cost | Disposable cartridges |

### 2.2 Ceramic (Inorganic) Membrane Materials

#### 2.2.1 Alumina (Al₂O₃)
- **Structure:** α-alumina support with γ-alumina top layer.
- **Properties:** Pore size range **4 nm – 10 µm**, pH 0–14, temperature up to **1000 °C**.
- **Food Applications:** Wine clarification, sugar refining, milk standardization.

#### 2.2.2 Zirconia (ZrO₂)
- **Properties:** Superior mechanical strength, pH resistant.
- **Applications:** High-pressure food processing, beverage clarification.

#### 2.2.3 Titania (TiO₂)
- **Properties:** Photocatalytic self-cleaning property (anti-fouling).
- **Applications:** Emerging use in dairy and beverage industries.

#### 2.2.4 Silica (SiO₂)
- **Properties:** Hydrophilic, smooth surface.
- **Applications:** UF/NF in food processing, often as intermediate layer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantages of Ceramic Membranes over Polymeric:**
> - Longer lifespan (**>10 years** vs. 2–5 years for polymers).
> - Withstand harsh CIP (pH 0–14, T up to 95 °C).
> - Higher fluxes at equivalent pore size.
> - **Disadvantage:** High capital cost (5–10× polymeric membranes).

### 2.3 Metallic and Other Membrane Materials

- **Stainless Steel Membranes:** Sintered metal powders; used for extreme pH/temperature; food-grade SS316L compliant.
- **Glass Membranes:** Porous Vycor glass; used in laboratory and specialty filtration.
- **Carbon Membranes:** Pyrolyzed polymer precursors; used in gas separation.
- **Liquid Membranes:** Emulsion or supported liquid films for selective ion transport.

### 2.4 Composite and Hybrid Membranes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thin-Film Composite (TFC) Membrane** is the **most industrially significant** class in food tech.

- **Structure:** Ultra-thin selective layer (0.01–0.1 µm) on a porous support.
- **Example:** Polyamide skin on Polysulfone support → **FT-30** (commercial RO membrane).
- **Mixed Matrix Membranes (MMM):** Incorporate zeolites, silica, or MOFs in polymer matrix to combine polymer processability with inorganic selectivity.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol/Quantity | Equation / Definition | Units | Notes |
|-----------------|----------------------|-------|-------|
| **Membrane Flux (J)** | $J = \dfrac{V}{A \cdot t}$ | L/m²·h | V = permeate volume, A = area, t = time |
| **Darcy's Law (Permeation)** | $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_m}$ | L/m²·h | R_m = membrane resistance |
| **Rejection (R)** | $R = \left(1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}\right) \times 100\%$ | % | C_p = permeate, C_f = feed concentration |
| **Molecular Weight Cut-Off (MWCO)** | MWCO = MW of solute rejected at 90% | Da (Dalton) | Defines UF/NF pore size |
| **Contact Angle (θ)** | Young's equation: $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$ | degrees | Hydrophilicity indicator (θ < 90° = hydrophilic) |
| **Porosity (ε)** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{V_{voids}}{V_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % | Typically 30–80% for UF membranes |
| **Tortuosity (τ)** | $\tau = \dfrac{actual\;path\;length}{membrane\;thickness}$ | dimensionless | ≥ 1; higher = more diffusion resistance |
| **Fouling Resistance (R_f)** | $R_f = R_{total} - R_m$ | m⁻¹ | Quantifies flux decline due to fouling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always specify **units** and **boundary conditions** in derivations. Examiners check unit consistency as a primary valuation point.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Membrane Flux Through a Porous Membrane (Hagen-Poiseuille Model)

The volumetric flux $J$ through a cylindrical pore of radius $r_p$ and length $L$ under a pressure difference $\Delta P$ is given by the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** for viscous flow.

**Step 1:** Velocity profile for Newtonian fluid in a cylindrical pore:

$$v(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} (r_p^2 - r^2)$$

**Step 2:** Integrate across the cross-section to obtain volumetric flow rate per single pore:

$$Q_{single} = \int_0^{r_p} v(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

**Step 3:** Perform the integration:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{single} &= \int_0^{r_p} \dfrac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} (r_p^2 - r^2) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr \\
&= \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_0^{r_p} (r_p^2 r - r^3) \, dr \\
&= \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left[ \dfrac{r_p^2 r^2}{2} - \dfrac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^{r_p} \\
&= \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left( \dfrac{r_p^4}{2} - \dfrac{r_p^4}{4} \right) \\
&= \dfrac{\pi \Delta P r_p^4}{8 \mu L}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Number of pores per unit membrane area (assuming cylindrical pores of radius $r_p$, porosity $\varepsilon$, and total membrane thickness including tortuosity $\tau L$):

$$n = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{\pi r_p^2 \cdot \tau L}$$

**Step 5:** Total flux per unit membrane area:

$$J = n \cdot Q_{single} = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{\pi r_p^2 \tau L} \cdot \dfrac{\pi \Delta P r_p^4}{8 \mu L}$$

**Step 6:** Simplify (Hagen-Poiseuille flux equation for porous membrane):

$$J = \dfrac{\varepsilon \cdot r_p^2 \cdot \Delta P}{8 \mu \tau L^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The flux scales with the **fourth power of pore radius**. Hence, a 2× increase in pore radius increases flux by 16× — this is why MF fluxes (0.1–10 µm pores) are dramatically higher than NF/RO fluxes (nanometer-scale pores).

### 3.2 Derivation: Membrane Rejection Coefficient

The observed solute rejection $R_{obs}$ relates feed concentration $C_f$ and permeate concentration $C_p$:

$$R_{obs} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}$$

**Step 1:** Intrinsic (true) rejection $R_{int}$ is defined at the membrane surface, where concentration is $C_m$ (concentration polarization considered):

$$R_{int} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_m}$$

**Step 2:** Using the **Film Theory** for concentration polarization:

$$\dfrac{C_m - C_p}{C_f - C_p} = \exp\left(\dfrac{J}{k}\right)$$

where $k$ is the mass transfer coefficient (m/s).

**Step 3:** Solving for $C_m$:

$$C_m = C_p + (C_f - C_p) \exp\left(\dfrac{J}{k}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Substituting into $R_{int}$:

$$R_{int} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_p + (C_f - C_p) \exp(J/k)}$$

$$R_{int} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{C_f - C_p}{C_p}\right) \exp(J/k)}$$

**Step 5:** Final compact form:

$$R_{int} = \dfrac{(C_f/C_p - 1) \exp(J/k)}{1 + (C_f/C_p - 1) \exp(J/k)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance in Food Tech:** In **whey protein UF** and **juice clarification**, concentration polarization is the dominant flux-limiting factor. Engineers design cross-flow velocities (typically **1–7 m/s**) to enhance $k$ and suppress $C_m$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Material Selection for Food Applications

| Food Product | Separation Goal | Recommended Material | Pore Size/MWCO | Justification |
|--------------|-----------------|----------------------|----------------|---------------|
| **Milk** | Fat/protein fractionation | PES, PS (hydrophilized) | 10–100 kDa UF | Hydrophilicity reduces protein fouling |
| **Whey** | Lactose recovery | PES, Polyamide TFC | 1–5 kDa NF | Selective sugar retention |
| **Fruit Juice** | Clarification/brix | CA, Ceramic (Al₂O₃) | 0.1–0.45 µm MF | Acid resistance, high flux |
| **Beer** | Sterile filtration | PVDF, PTFE | 0.2–0.45 µm MF | Hydrophobic, sterile, low adsorption |
| **Wine** | Clarification | PES, Ceramic | 0.2 µm MF | Tartrate stability, color retention |
| **Sugar Syrup** | Decolorization | Polyamide TFC | 200–500 Da NF | Selective pigment removal |
| **Vegetable Oil** | Degumming | PVDF, PES | 5–50 kDa UF | Solvent-resistant polymer |
| **Enzyme Recovery** | Protein concentration | PS, PES | 5–30 kDa UF | Retains enzyme, permeates water/salts |
| **Vinegar** | Clarification | CA, Ceramic | 0.1 µm MF | Acid tolerance |
| **Salt Brine** | Desalination (concentration) | Polyamide TFC | RO (< 200 Da) | High salt rejection (>99%) |

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the feed highly acidic pH less than 2 or alkaline pH greater than 11?}

    Q2 -- YES --> R2[Use Ceramic or PES]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{Is low fouling the top priority for protein feeds?}
    Q3 -- YES --> R3[Use Hydrophilized PS, CA, or PAN]
    Q3 -- NO --> Q4{Is high selectivity for ions or small organics needed?}
    Q4 -- YES --> R4[Use Polyamide TFC or NF Membrane]
    Q4 -- NO --> Q5[Use Standard PS or PVDF UF]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"List any three essential properties required for a polymeric membrane material used in food processing applications."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **High selectivity** with defined MWCO for target molecule separation (1 mark).
2. **Excellent chemical and thermal resistance** to withstand CIP cycles (1.0–1.5% NaOH, 0.5–1.0% HNO₃ at 50–80 °C) (1 mark).
3. **Food-grade inertness / biocompatibility** with no toxic monomer leaching, compliant with **FDA 21 CFR** and **EU 1935/2004** (1 mark).

*(Acceptable alternatives: mechanical strength, hydrophilicity, processability, cost-effectiveness.)*

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"Distinguish between polymeric and ceramic membrane materials. Give one example of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Polymeric | Ceramic |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| **Example** | Polysulfone (PS) | Alumina (Al₂O₃) |
| **pH tolerance** | Narrow (typically 1–13) | Wide (0–14) |
| **Temperature** | 0–95 °C | Up to 400 °C |
| **Lifespan** | 2–5 years | >10 years |
| **Cost** | Low (₹500–5,000/m²) | High (₹25,000–1,00,000/m²) |
| **Fouling** | Moderate to high (esp. hydrophobic) | Lower (esp. titania) |

(1 mark for each correct distinction; 0.5 mark for example; 0.5 mark for completeness.)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3(a).** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**(7 marks)** Discuss in detail the various **polymeric membrane materials** used in food technology. Compare the properties and applications of **Cellulose Acetate**, **Polysulfone**, and **PVDF**.

**Model Solution:**

**Cellulose Acetate (CA):**
- Chemically, the **acetyl ester of cellulose** (DS = 2.4–2.9).
- **Properties:** Moderately hydrophilic, smooth surface, low protein adsorption, high selectivity in RO.
- **Limitations:** Poor acid/base resistance (stable only pH 2–8); hydrolyzes at high pH; moderate temperature tolerance (max 35–40 °C).
- **Applications:** RO of fruit juices (orange, apple), whey demineralization, vinegar clarification.

**Polysulfone (PS) / Polyethersulfone (PES):**
- Aromatic backbone with **–SO₂–** and ether **–O–** linkages; T_g ≈ 190 °C.
- **Properties:** Excellent thermal and chemical stability, broad pH range (1–13), high mechanical strength, asymmetric structure easily formed by phase inversion.
- **Limitations:** Inherently **hydrophobic** → protein fouling. Hydrophilized by adding PVP/PEG.
- **Applications:** UF of milk and whey, beer clarification, enzyme recovery, MBR systems.

**PVDF (Polyvinylidene Fluoride):**
- Repeating unit **–(CH₂–CF₂)ₙ–**.
- **Properties:** Outstanding chemical resistance (resistant to acids, bases, oxidizers, chlorinated solvents), mechanically robust, hydrophobic, steam-sterilizable.
- **Limitations:** Higher cost; hydrophobicity causes fouling with protein feeds.
- **Applications:** Sterile filtration of wine/beer, MBR, vegetable oil processing.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Property | CA | PS/PES | PVDF |
|----------|----|----|------|
| Hydrophilicity | High | Low (modified → medium) | Very low |
| pH range | 2–8 | 1–13 | 1–14 |
| Max temperature | 35–40 °C | 95–125 °C | 95–140 °C |
| Cost (₹/m²) | Low | Medium | High |
| Key food app. | Juice RO | Milk/whey UF | Sterile filtration |

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks CA, 2 marks PS/PES, 2 marks PVDF, 1 mark comparison table.)*

---

**Q3(b).** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**(7 marks)** Explain the **ceramic membrane materials** used in food processing. Discuss the advantages of **alumina, zirconia, and titania** membranes over polymeric membranes.

**Model Solution:**

**Ceramic Membrane Materials in Food Processing:**

**1. Alumina (Al₂O₃) Membranes:**
- Most widely used ceramic; structure typically α-Al₂O₃ support with γ-Al₂O₃ top layer.
- Pore size range: **4 nm – 10 µm** (covers MF to NF).
- **Properties:** High mechanical strength, extreme pH tolerance (0–14), temperature up to **1000 °C**, hydrophilic surface.
- **Food Applications:** Wine clarification, sugar refining, milk standardization, fruit juice clarification.

**2. Zirconia (ZrO₂) Membranes:**
- ZrO₂ has superior mechanical and chemical stability; used as top layer in UF/MF.
- Pore size: **5 nm – 1 µm**.
- **Applications:** High-pressure food processing, beverage clarification, CIP-intensive systems.

**3. Titania (TiO₂) Membranes:**
- TiO₂ possesses **photocatalytic** self-cleaning property under UV exposure.
- Generates reactive oxygen species that degrade organic foulants.
- **Applications:** Emerging use in dairy and beverage industries for fouling control.

**4. Silica (SiO₂) Membranes:**
- Used as **intermediate layer** in composite ceramic membranes.
- Hydrophilic and smooth; suitable for UF/NF.

**Advantages over Polymeric Membranes (2 marks):**
- **Lifespan:** >10 years vs. 2–5 years for polymers.
- **CIP compatibility:** Withstand aggressive chemicals (pH 0–14, T up to 95 °C) and oxidizers.
- **Thermal stability:** Up to 400 °C.
- **Higher fluxes:** Maintain porosity at high pressures.
- **Lower fouling:** Hydrophilic ceramic surface; titania adds photocatalytic cleaning.
- **Disadvantage (for balance):** 5–10× higher capital cost.

**Disadvantages (for examiner credit):**
- High initial investment; brittle; heavier; limited to specific geometries.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark each for three ceramics, 2 marks for advantages, 1 mark for disadvantages.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4(a).** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**(7 marks)** With a neat sketch, describe the structure of a **Thin-Film Composite (TFC) membrane**. Discuss its application in **reverse osmosis** for concentration of fruit juices.

**Model Solution:**

**Structure of TFC Membrane:**

A TFC membrane consists of **two chemically distinct layers**:

1. **Ultra-thin selective top layer** (0.01–0.1 µm thickness):
   - Composed of **polyamide** (cross-linked), polyurea, or polypiperazine.
   - Formed by **interfacial polymerization** on the support surface.
   - Governs solute rejection and selectivity.

2. **Porous support layer** (50–150 µm thickness):
   - Made of **polysulfone (PS)** or **polyethersulfone (PES)**.
   - Formed by phase inversion (immersion precipitation).
   - Provides mechanical strength without compromising flux.

3. **Non-woven fabric backing** (polyester, 100–200 µm):
   - Provides dimensional stability and handling strength.

**Cross-section (described):**
```
PERMEATE SIDE
   |
   v
[Ultra-thin polyamide skin: 0.01-0.1 um]
   |
[Polysulfone support: 50-150 um, finger-like pores]
   |
[Polyester non-woven backing: 100-200 um]
   |
   v
FEED SIDE
```

**Application in RO for Fruit Juice Concentration:**

- **Principle:** A high-pressure pump (**30–80 bar** for RO) forces water through the dense polyamide skin, while sugars, acids, pigments, and aroma compounds are rejected.
- **Process:** Fresh juice (e.g., orange, apple, grape, pomegranate) is pre-filtered (MF/UF) and then fed to RO.
- **Advantages over thermal evaporation:**
  - **No heat damage** → preserves vitamins (Vitamin C), aroma, and color.
  - **Lower energy consumption** (~ 5–10 kWh/m³ vs. 100+ kWh/m³ for evaporation).
  - **Higher quality** of concentrated juice (60–80 °Brix).
- **Specific examples:**
  - **Orange juice:** Concentrated from 12 °Brix to 60–65 °Brix for storage/transport.
  - **Apple juice:** Concentrated and used as natural sweetener.
  - **Pomegranate juice:** Preserved anthocyanins and antioxidants.
- **TFC-specific benefits:**
  - High salt/sugar rejection (>99%).
  - Tailored MWCO via polyamide chemistry.
  - Compatible with mildly acidic pH (3.5–4.5) of fruit juices.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks structure, 2 marks formation process, 3 marks juice concentration application.)*

---

**Q4(b).** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**(7 marks)** Discuss the **selection criteria** for membrane materials in food processing. How does **hydrophilicity vs. hydrophobicity** affect membrane fouling?

**Model Solution:**

**Selection Criteria for Membrane Materials in Food Processing:**

1. **Chemical Compatibility with Feed:**
   - pH of feed (juice pH ~3.5, milk pH ~6.7, whey pH ~4.5).
   - Resistance to cleaning chemicals (NaOH, HNO₃, NaOCl).
   - Solvent resistance (for oil/ethanol feeds).

2. **Thermal Stability:**
   - Operating temperature range (pasteurization 72 °C, sterilization 121 °C).
   - Steam sterilizability (especially for dairy, pharma).

3. **Mechanical Strength:**
   - Withstand transmembrane pressure (TMP) without compaction.
   - Burst strength and creep resistance.

4. **Pore Size / MWCO:**
   - Match pore size to target molecule (MF: cells, bacteria; UF: proteins; NF: sugars; RO: ions).

5. **Food-Grade Certification:**
   - **FDA 21 CFR**, **EU 1935/2004**, **BIS** compliance.
   - No toxic monomer leaching, no off-flavor transfer.

6. **Fouling Resistance:**
   - Smooth, hydrophilic surface preferred for protein feeds.
   - Low surface roughness to minimize bacterial adhesion.

7. **Processability & Cost:**
   - Easy casting/spinning, scalable manufacturing.
   - Membrane cost per m² and replacement frequency.

8. **Service Life:**
   - Target ≥ 2–5 years under standard CIP cycles.

**Hydrophilicity vs. Hydrophobicity — Effect on Fouling:**

**Hydrophilic Membranes (e.g., CA, regenerated cellulose, hydrophilized PES):**
- **Water-contact angle θ < 90°** (typically 30–60°).
- **Mechanism:** Water forms a hydration layer on the surface, preventing direct adsorption of hydrophobic molecules (proteins, lipids, pigments).
- **Result:** Lower protein fouling, easier cleaning, more stable flux.
- **Food examples:** Milk protein UF, fruit juice clarification, beer filtration.

**Hydrophobic Membranes (e.g., PVDF, PTFE, pristine PS):**
- **Water-contact angle θ > 90°** (typically 100–130°).
- **Mechanism:** Hydrophobic interactions drive adsorption of fats, oils, and amphiphilic proteins onto the membrane surface.
- **Result:** Severe fouling, flux decline, difficult to clean.
- **Food examples:** Sterile filtration of wine/beer (intentionally hydrophobic to avoid wetting), gas filtration.

**Strategies to Modify Hydrophobic Membranes:**
- **Plasma treatment:** Generates –OH, –COOH groups on surface.
- **Graft polymerization:** PEG, PVA, zwitterionic polymer brushes.
- **Blending with hydrophilic polymers:** PVP, PEG.
- **Surface coating:** TiO₂ nanoparticles, polydopamine.

**Quantitative Fouling Index (Hermia's Models):**
- Complete blocking: $-\dfrac{dJ}{dt} = k_b \cdot J$
- Intermediate blocking: $-\dfrac{dJ}{dt} = k_i \cdot J^2$
- Standard blocking: $-\dfrac{dJ}{dt} = k_s \cdot J^{1.5}$
- Cake layer: $-\dfrac{dJ}{dt} = k_c \cdot J^3$

*In food applications, **cake filtration** (proteins, polysaccharides) is the dominant fouling mechanism for UF.*

*(Mark distribution: 3 marks selection criteria, 4 marks hydrophilicity vs. hydrophobicity discussion.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in "Materials for Membrane" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Naming without justification:** Students often *list* membrane materials (CA, PS, PVDF) but fail to explain *why* a specific material is chosen. Always link the material to its **property → application** chain.
> 2. **Confusing MWCO with pore size:** MWCO is in **Daltons (Da)** and applies to UF/NF; pore size is in **µm/nm** and applies to MF/UF. Examiners deduct 1 mark for unit errors.
> 3. **Ignoring food-grade compliance:** Many students discuss performance but forget **FDA / EU regulatory aspects**, which is a major valuation focus in KTU 2024.
> 4. **Skipping diagrams:** For TFC or asymmetric membrane questions, **a labelled sketch is mandatory**. Even a hand-drawn schematic earns 1–2 marks.
> 5. **No CIP discussion:** In ceramic vs. polymeric comparisons, mentioning **CIP (Clean-In-Place) compatibility** is a KTU-specific favorite — missing it loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Mixing up MF/UF/NF/RO pore sizes:** Memorize the ranges: **MF (0.1–10 µm), UF (1–100 nm), NF (0.5–5 nm), RO (< 0.5 nm)**. Wrong range = 1-mark deduction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Polymeric membranes** dominate food applications due to low cost and ease of fabrication.
- **Cellulose Acetate (CA)** → hydrophilic, narrow pH range, ideal for juice RO and vinegar.
- **Polysulfone/Polyethersulfone (PS/PES)** → workhorses of dairy/whey UF; must be hydrophilized.
- **PVDF** → hydrophobic but chemically inert; used in sterile filtration of beverages.
- **Polyamide TFC** → state-of-the-art for NF/RO; ultrathin skin on PS support.
- **Ceramic membranes** (Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, TiO₂) → expensive but long-lasting; ideal for high-T, extreme-pH, CIP-intensive operations.
- **Asymmetric membrane structure** → dense skin (selectivity) + porous sublayer (mechanical support).
- **Hydrophilicity (θ < 90°)** is the **key to low fouling** in protein-rich food feeds.
- **MWCO** (in Da) defines UF/NF performance; **pore size** (in µm/nm) defines MF/UF.
- **Food-grade compliance** (FDA 21 CFR, EU 1935/2004) is **non-negotiable** in food applications.
- **Flux J = V/(A·t)** scales with **pore radius⁴** (Hagen-Poiseuille).
- **Rejection R = (1 - C_p/C_f) × 100%** is the core performance metric.
- **Concentration polarization** (governed by Film Theory) is the dominant flux limiter in cross-flow food membrane systems.
- **Composite TFC membranes** are the gold standard for RO concentration of fruit juices (preserves heat-sensitive vitamins and aroma).
- **Ceramic membranes** offer **>10 years** lifespan; **polymeric** offer 2–5 years.

---

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Separation Processes in Food Technology**, the term **"Design Considerations"** refers to the systematic, multi-criteria engineering framework used to plan, select, size, and specify equipment and operating conditions for a unit operation that isolates one or more components from a food matrix. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, these considerations integrate **mass and energy balance constraints, sanitary engineering requirements, materials science, process economics, and food safety regulations** into a single coherent design strategy.

Mathematically, an optimal design satisfies:

$$\text{Design} = f(\dot{m}_{in}, x_{feed}, x_{product}, T, P, \eta_{sep}, C_{cap}, C_{op}, \text{Safety Factor})$$

Where each term represents a **decision variable** or **boundary constraint** evaluated during equipment selection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):** *"Design considerations in food separation are the engineering, hygienic, economic, and regulatory parameters that govern the selection, sizing, and operation of separation equipment to ensure product safety, quality, and process efficiency."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you are **renovating a kitchen** for a high-end restaurant. Before buying any appliance, you ask:

- 🍲 **What am I cooking?** (Feed characteristics)
- 👨‍🍳 **How many plates per hour?** (Throughput)
- 🧽 **Is it easy to scrub and sanitize?** (Hygienic/CIP design)
- 💰 **What is my budget for purchase and electricity?** (Capital & operating cost)
- 🛡️ **Will it pass the health inspector?** (Regulatory compliance)
- 🔩 **Will the metal rust from vinegar or salt?** (Material of construction)

Now replace "kitchen" with **"separation plant"** and "appliance" with **"separator"** (centrifuge, membrane, filter, evaporator, etc.) — the analogy holds perfectly. A food engineer designing a separation process must answer the *same* questions, but with quantitative rigor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** If a design cannot be cleaned, it cannot be used in a food plant. **Cleanability is non-negotiable** in food separation equipment selection.

---

## 1.3 The Five Pillars of Design Considerations

The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes a **5-pillar framework** for evaluating any separation process design:

| Pillar | Core Question | Engineering Discipline |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **1. Process Function** | What must be separated, and to what purity? | Mass Transfer, Thermodynamics |
| **2. Sanitary Design** | Can it be cleaned to food-grade standards? | Hygienic Engineering, Microbiology |
| **3. Material Integrity** | Will the equipment survive the food chemistry? | Materials Science, Corrosion |
| **4. Energy & Economics** | Is the process energy-efficient and affordable? | Process Economics, Heat Transfer |
| **5. Safety & Compliance** | Is it safe for operators and consumers? | Safety Engineering, Regulatory Affairs |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** Think **"F-S-M-E-S"** → **Function, Sanitation, Material, Energy, Safety.** A design that fails any one of these pillars fails the entire KTU evaluation.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in This Module

The following constants and metrics are routinely used in design calculations for food separation:

- **Standard gravity:** $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$
- **Density of water at 20 °C:** $\rho_w = 998 \ \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Dynamic viscosity of water at 20 °C:** $\mu_w = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \ \text{kPa}$
- **Design safety factor (food vessels):** $SF = 1.5$ to **2.0** (per ASME BPVC Section VIII)
- **Sanitary surface roughness (Ra):** $\leq 0.8 \ \mu\text{m}$ (Ra 32, per 3-A Sanitary Standards)
- **CIP flow velocity:** $1.5$ to **2.0 m/s** (turbulent rinsing threshold)
- **Design pasteurization reference:** $72 \ ^\circ\text{C}$ for **15 s** (HTST, FDA)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often use the kinematic viscosity ($\nu$) where the dynamic viscosity ($\mu$) is required. Recall: $\mu = \rho \cdot \nu$. Unit confusion accounts for **~15% of valuation marks lost** in design problems.

By the end of this topic, the student should be able to:

- **CO₂ (Module 2):** Identify and apply the engineering, hygienic, and economic criteria for selecting separation equipment in food processing.
- **CO₃ (Module 2):** Evaluate the influence of feed properties and operating conditions on separator design.
- **CO₄ (Module 2):** Justify material selection, CIP strategy, and safety provisions for a given food separation duty.

Each design consideration discussed below maps to one or more of these Course Outcomes (COs), as per the **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework mandated by NEP 2020.

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Design Considerations

Design considerations for food separation processes are broadly classified into **three hierarchical tiers**. The KTU 2024 examiner expects students to recognize *which tier* a given design parameter belongs to during written examinations.

### Tier 1 — Process & Feed Considerations
These define *what* is being separated and *under what conditions*:

- **Physical state** of feed (solid, liquid, slurry, paste, gas)
- **Feed concentration** ($x_{F}$, mass fraction) of the target component
- **Viscosity** ($\mu$), **density** ($\rho$), and **particle size** ($d_p$)
- **Thermal sensitivity** of the food (denaturation, Maillard, microbial kill)
- **Desired product purity** ($x_P$) and **recovery** ($R$)

### Tier 2 — Equipment & Mechanical Considerations
These define *how* the separation is physically executed:

- **Type of separator** (membrane, centrifuge, filter, extractor, crystallizer)
- **Material of construction** (SS304, SS316L, sanitary polymers)
- **Surface finish** (Ra, weld quality, dead-leg elimination)
- **Capacity / throughput** ($\dot{m}$ or $Q$, in kg/h or L/h)
- **Pressure rating** and **temperature rating** of the vessel

### Tier 3 — Operational, Economic & Regulatory Considerations
These define *whether* the design is sustainable:

- **Capital cost** ($C_{cap}$) and **Operating cost** ($C_{op}$)
- **Energy consumption** ($kWh/kg$ product)
- **Cleanability (CIP/SIP capability)**
- **Compliance** with **3-A, EHEDG, FDA, FSSAI, BIS, ISO 22000**
- **Operator safety** (pressure relief, guarding, ATEX zones for dust/solvent)

---

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Consideration

### 2.2.1 Why Feed Characterization Drives Design

> **Why?** Because every separator exploits a *property differential* (size, density, solubility, volatility, charge). If you do not know the feed, you cannot choose the right differential.

> **How?** The engineer first constructs a **property matrix** listing the target component and the matrix (carrier) phase, then matches the dominant differential to a suitable unit operation.

For example:
- **Oil-in-water emulsion** (milk, fruit juice) → **Density differential** is large → **Centrifugation** is ideal.
- **Sugar from cane juice** → **Solubility differential** with temperature → **Evaporation + Crystallization**.
- **Whey proteins from milk** → **Molecular size differential** → **Ultrafiltration (UF) membrane**.

### 2.2.2 Why Material Selection is Critical in Food

> **Why?** Food streams are often **corrosive** (lactic acid in dairy, citric acid in juices, chlorides in brines) and **reactive** (Maillard at high T, oxidation by dissolved O₂). Wrong material → **metal leaching, pitting, product contamination, equipment failure**.

> **How?** Apply the **Corrosion Index (CI)** plus the **Sanitary Compatibility Matrix**:

$$\text{CI} = \frac{[\text{Cl}^-] \cdot T}{R_{material}}$$

Where $R_{material}$ is the **material resistance rating** (SS316L > SS304 > carbon steel). When $\text{CI} > 5$, **SS316L** is mandatory.

### 2.2.3 Why Sanitary Design is Non-Negotiable

> **Why?** Biofilms (e.g., *Listeria*, *Pseudomonas*) form in crevices, dead legs, threaded joints, and rough surfaces. Once formed, they are nearly **impossible to remove** by routine CIP and cause **recurring product recalls**.

> **How?** The **3-A Sanitary Standards** and **EHEDG guidelines** specify:
- **Surface roughness** $R_a \leq 0.8 \ \mu\text{m}$ for product-contact surfaces.
- **Slope for drainage** $\geq 1\%$ toward the drain (no pooling).
- **Radius of bends** $\geq 1.5 \times$ pipe diameter (no sharp corners).
- **No threaded connections** in product zones (use sanitary Tri-Clamp® or DIN 11864 fittings).
- **Dead-leg length** $\leq 2 \times$ pipe diameter (to prevent microbial harborage).

### 2.2.4 Why Energy Considerations Matter

> **Why?** In food processing, **thermal energy** often dominates operating cost (evaporation is the most energy-intensive unit op: ~**2.3 MJ/kg water** removed at atmospheric pressure).

> **How?** Use **Mechanical Vapor Recompression (MVR)** or **Multi-Effect Evaporation (MEE)** to slash steam demand. For membranes, optimize **transmembrane pressure (TMP)** to avoid fouling and excess pumping cost.

### 2.2.5 Why Economics and Safety Are the Final Filters

> **Why?** A technically perfect design that the company cannot afford, or that injures an operator, is a **failed design**. Economics and safety are *gatekeepers*, not *afterthoughts*.

> **How?** Apply **Payback Period (PBP)** and **Net Present Value (NPV)** analyses alongside **HAZOP** (Hazard and Operability Study) and **FMEA** (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) before final commitment.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles the **board-exam relevant equations** for design considerations in food separation. Memorize the units as well as the symbols — KTU examiners frequently deduct marks for unit errors.

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Reynolds Number** (flow regime) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | $\rho$: kg/m³, $v$: m/s, $D$: m, $\mu$: Pa·s | Determines laminar/turbulent flow for CIP, pipe sizing |
| 2 | **Darcy's Law** (filtration) | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_c}$ | $V$: m³, $A$: m², $\Delta P$: Pa, $R_c$: 1/m | Filter press, rotary vacuum filter sizing |
| 3 | **Hagen-Poiseuille** (laminar ΔP) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{32 \mu L v}{D^2}$ | $L$: m, $v$: m/s | Membrane tube, hollow fiber pressure drop |
| 4 | **Membrane Flux** | $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_m}$ | $J$: m³/m²·s, $R_m$: 1/m | UF, MF, RO design |
| 5 | **Centrifuge Equivalent Area** (Sigma concept) | $\Sigma = \dfrac{\omega^2 \cdot V_{bowl}}{g \cdot \ln(R_2/R_1)}$ | $\omega$: rad/s, $V_{bowl}$: m³, $R_1, R_2$: m | Scale-up from lab to industrial centrifuge |
| 6 | **Stokes' Law** (terminal settling) | $v_s = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{18 \mu}$ | $d_p$: m, $\rho_p, \rho_f$: kg/m³ | Gravity settler, hydrocyclone design |
| 7 | **Power Number** (impeller) | $N_p = \dfrac{P}{\rho N^3 D_i^5}$ | $P$: W, $N$: 1/s, $D_i$: m | Mixing tank, extractor agitator |
| 8 | **Q = U·A·ΔTlm** (heat exchange) | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$ | $U$: W/m²·K, $A$: m² | Evaporator heater sizing |
| 9 | **Mass Balance** (separator) | $F = P + W$, $F x_F = P x_P + W x_W$ | $F, P, W$: kg/h, $x$: mass fraction | Material balance on any separator |
| 10 | **Recovery** | $R = \dfrac{P \cdot x_P}{F \cdot x_F}$ | dimensionless | Purity/recovery design target |
| 11 | **Corrosion Index** (food-specific) | $CI = \dfrac{[\text{Cl}^-] \cdot T}{R_{mat}}$ | $[\text{Cl}^-]$: ppm, $T$: °C | Material selection (SS304 vs SS316L) |
| 12 | **Payback Period** | $PBP = \dfrac{C_{cap}}{C_{annual\ saving}}$ | years | Economic screening of design alternatives |
| 13 | **CIP Reynolds Number** (sanitary) | $Re_{CIP} \geq 4000$ (turbulent) | dimensionless | CIP pipe sizing |
| 14 | **Design Safety Factor** | $SF = \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{design}}$ | dimensionless, $SF \geq 1.5$–$2.0$ | Pressure vessel wall thickness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** Equations 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 9, and 10 are the **most frequently tested** in KTU ESE. Equations 6–8 and 11–14 appear in **Part A (3-mark)** and design-based **Part B (14-mark)** questions.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of the Design Framework

The design considerations framework is **not academic** — it is the daily working language of:

- 🥛 **Dairy Engineers** (selecting separators for cream standardization, whey UF)
- 🧃 **Beverage Engineers** (designing juice clarification trains)
- 🌾 **Grain & Oilseed Engineers** (solvent extractor and deodorizer design)
- 🍬 **Confectionery Engineers** (crystallizer design for sugar, chocolate refining)
- 🧪 **Bioprocess Engineers** (downstream processing of enzymes, probiotics)

Every **capital project above ₹50 lakh** in the Indian food industry requires a **Detailed Project Report (DPR)** that explicitly addresses each of the 5 pillars (F-S-M-E-S) discussed above. KTU aims to produce engineers capable of authoring such reports.

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hagen-Poiseuille Equation for Membrane Tube Pressure Drop

The Hagen-Poiseuille equation is the **workhorse for sizing pumps** in membrane separation. We derive it from first principles.

### Step 1 — Start with the Navier-Stokes Equation (steady, laminar, fully developed, incompressible flow in a circular tube)

For an incompressible Newtonian fluid in a horizontal circular pipe at steady state, the axial momentum balance reduces to:

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr}\left( r \frac{dv_z}{dr} \right) = \frac{1}{\mu} \frac{\Delta P}{L}$$

### Step 2 — Integrate Once with Respect to r

Multiplying both sides by $r$ and integrating:

$$r \frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{1}{\mu} \frac{\Delta P}{L} \cdot \frac{r^2}{2} + C_1$$

### Step 3 — Apply Boundary Condition #1: Finite Velocity at the Centerline (r = 0)

Since $v_z$ must remain finite at $r = 0$, the constant $C_1$ must be zero:

$$C_1 = 0$$

### Step 4 — Divide by r and Integrate Again

$$\frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{1}{2\mu} \frac{\Delta P}{L} \cdot r$$

Integrating with respect to $r$:

$$v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} r^2 + C_2$$

### Step 5 — Apply Boundary Condition #2: No-Slip at the Wall (r = R)

At the pipe wall, $v_z = 0$:

$$0 = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} R^2 + C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = -\frac{\Delta P R^2}{4\mu L}$$

### Step 6 — Final Velocity Profile (Parabolic)

Substituting $C_2$ back:

$$v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left( R^2 - r^2 \right)$$

This is the famous **parabolic velocity profile** of laminar flow.

### Step 7 — Compute the Volumetric Flow Rate Q

Integrate $v_z$ over the cross-sectional area:

$$Q = \int_0^R v_z(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \int_0^R \left( R^2 - r^2 \right) r \, dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \left[ \frac{R^2 \cdot r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}$$

### Step 8 — Final Form of the Hagen-Poiseuille Equation

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}}$$

For engineering use, $D = 2R$ and $v_{avg} = Q/A = Q/(\pi R^2)$:

$$\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{32 \mu L v_{avg}}{D^2}}$$

**Engineering Use in Food Separation:** This equation is used to calculate the **pressure drop across a hollow-fiber UF membrane module** (e.g., for whey protein concentration), which determines the **pump power requirement**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stokes' Law (Terminal Settling Velocity)

This is the foundation of **gravity settling, decanter design, and hydrocyclone feed characterization**.

### Step 1 — Force Balance on a Spherical Particle

Three forces act on a particle falling through a viscous fluid:
1. **Gravitational force (downward):** $F_g = m_p \cdot g = \rho_p \cdot V_p \cdot g$
2. **Buoyant force (upward):** $F_b = \rho_f \cdot V_p \cdot g$
3. **Drag force (opposing motion):** $F_d = C_D \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho_f v_s^2 \cdot A_p$

At **terminal velocity**, the net force is zero:

$$F_g = F_b + F_d$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Geometric Terms for a Sphere

For a sphere of diameter $d_p$:
- Volume: $V_p = \dfrac{\pi d_p^3}{6}$
- Cross-sectional area: $A_p = \dfrac{\pi d_p^2}{4}$

### Step 3 — Express the Drag Force

At very low Reynolds number ($Re_p \ll 1$, i.e., $Re_p < 0.1$), the drag coefficient is:

$$C_D = \frac{24}{Re_p} = \frac{24 \mu}{\rho_f v_s d_p}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify

$$F_d = \frac{24 \mu}{\rho_f v_s d_p} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho_f v_s^2 \cdot \frac{\pi d_p^2}{4} = 3 \pi \mu d_p v_s$$

This is the famous **Stokes' drag law**.

### Step 5 — Set Up the Terminal Velocity Equation

$$(\rho_p - \rho_f) \cdot \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \cdot g = 3 \pi \mu d_p v_s$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $v_s$

$$\boxed{v_s = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{18 \mu}}$$

This is **Stokes' Law** — the terminal settling velocity of a small spherical particle in a viscous fluid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** Stokes' Law is valid only when $Re_p = \dfrac{\rho_f v_s d_p}{\mu} < 0.1$ (i.e., laminar flow around the particle). For larger particles, use the **Newtonian regime** ($Re_p > 1000$): $C_D \approx 0.44$, giving $v_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{3 \rho_f C_D}}$.

**Engineering Use:** This equation is used to size **gravity clarifiers** for fruit juice, **decanters** for edible oil, and **hydrocyclones** for starch-gluten separation in corn wet milling.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Membrane Flux Equation (Darcy's Law for Membranes)

### Step 1 — Define Transmembrane Pressure (TMP)

For a membrane operating with feed at pressure $P_F$ and permeate at pressure $P_P$:

$$TMP = \frac{P_F + P_R}{2} - P_P$$

where $P_R$ is the **retentate** (concentrate) pressure.

### Step 2 — Define Membrane Resistance $R_m$

The clean membrane has an intrinsic resistance:

$$R_m = \frac{1}{\mu \cdot L_p}$$

where $L_p$ is the **pure water permeability** (m³/m²·s·Pa) and $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity.

### Step 3 — Apply Darcy's Law for Permeate Flux

$$J = \frac{TMP}{\mu \cdot R_m}$$

### Step 4 — Account for Fouling (Industrial Reality)

In real food applications, the membrane fouls, adding a **fouling resistance** $R_f$:

$$\boxed{J = \frac{TMP}{\mu (R_m + R_f + R_c)}}$$

Where $R_c$ is the **cake resistance** from deposited solids (relevant for UF of milk, microfiltration of fruit juice).

### Step 5 — Express $R_c$ via the Kozeny-Carman Equation

$$R_c = \frac{180 (1-\varepsilon) \mu \delta_c}{\rho_p d_p^2 \varepsilon^3}$$

Where $\varepsilon$ is cake porosity, $\delta_c$ is cake thickness, $d_p$ is particle size.

**Engineering Use:** This entire framework is the basis of **membrane process design** for dairy (whey protein concentration), beverage (juice clarification), and sugar (molasses desalting).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Selection of a Separation Method

The following matrix is the **KTU gold standard** for selecting a separation technique given the feed characteristics. It is a *must-memorize* tool for examinations.

| Feed Property | Recommended Separator | Dominant Property Exploited | Typical Food Application |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Liquid–liquid, immiscible, Δρ > 50 kg/m³** | Centrifuge (disk-stack) | Density | Cream separation, edible oil refining |
| **Solid–liquid, $d_p$ > 100 μm** | Gravity settler / screen | Size & density | Debris removal, pulp screening |
| **Solid–liquid, 1–100 μm** | Filter press / rotary vacuum filter | Size | Starch-gluten separation, yeast recovery |
| **Solid–liquid, 0.1–10 μm** | Microfiltration (MF) | Size | Bacteria removal, juice clarification |
| **Solid–liquid, molecular scale (proteins)** | Ultrafiltration (UF) | Molecular size | Whey protein, milk protein isolate |
| **Liquid–liquid, miscible (water–ethanol)** | Distillation / pervaporation | Volatility | Alcoholic beverage, bioethanol |
| **Aqueous solutes, ions** | Reverse osmosis (RO) / Nanofiltration (NF) | Solubility/diffusivity | Milk concentration, water reuse |
| **Aroma compounds, heat-sensitive** | Pervaporation / steam stripping | Selective sorption | Juice aroma recovery, dealcoholization |
| **Soluble in selective solvent** | Liquid–liquid extraction | Solubility | Oil from oilseeds, vanillin from beans |
| **Crystallizable solute** | Evaporative crystallizer | Solubility vs T | Sugar, salt, monosodium glutamate |
| **Adsorbable component** | Adsorption (activated carbon, resin) | Surface affinity | Decolorization of sugar syrup |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A food plant must concentrate whole milk from 12% total solids to 45% total solids using **reverse osmosis (RO)**. The feed flow rate is $F = 10{,}000 \ \text{kg/h}$ at $T = 30 \ ^\circ\text{C}$. The membrane has a clean water permeability $L_p = 5 \times 10^{-12} \ \text{m}^3/\text{m}^2\cdot\text{s}\cdot\text{Pa}$. The operating TMP is **30 bar**. Assume membrane resistance dominates and viscosity of milk at 30 °C is $\mu = 1.5 \ \text{cP} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$. Find the **membrane area required** if the plant operates **20 hours/day**.

### Step 1 — Mass Balance on Water

Let $P$ = permeate (water removed) and $R$ = retentate (concentrated milk).

$$F = 10{,}000 \ \text{kg/h}, \quad x_F = 0.12, \quad x_R = 0.45$$

**Solids balance:**

$$F \cdot x_F = R \cdot x_R \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = \frac{F \cdot x_F}{x_R} = \frac{10{,}000 \times 0.12}{0.45}$$

$$R = 2{,}666.67 \ \text{kg/h (concentrated milk)}$$

**Water to remove (permeate):**

$$P = F - R = 10{,}000 - 2{,}666.67 = 7{,}333.33 \ \text{kg/h}$$

In **volume** (assuming $\rho_{water} \approx 1000 \ \text{kg/m}^3$):

$$Q_P = \frac{7{,}333.33}{1000 \times 3600} = 2.037 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

### Step 2 — Membrane Flux

$$R_m = \frac{1}{\mu \cdot L_p} = \frac{1}{(1.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times (5 \times 10^{-12})} = 1.333 \times 10^{14} \ \text{m}^{-1}$$

$$J = \frac{TMP}{\mu \cdot R_m} = \frac{30 \times 10^5}{(1.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times (1.333 \times 10^{14})}$$

$$J = \frac{3 \times 10^6}{2.0 \times 10^{11}} = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^3/\text{m}^2\cdot\text{s}$$

### Step 3 — Required Membrane Area

$$A = \frac{Q_P}{J} = \frac{2.037 \times 10^{-3}}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 135.8 \ \text{m}^2$$

### Step 4 — Apply Design Safety Factor

With $SF = 1.2$ (typical for membrane systems, higher SF for pressure vessels):

$$A_{design} = 1.2 \times 135.8 = 162.96 \ \text{m}^2 \approx 163 \ \text{m}^2$$

### Step 5 — Final Answer (with units and statement)

$$\boxed{A_{design} \approx 163 \ \text{m}^2 \text{ of RO membrane area is required.}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mark Allocation:** Full marks require (a) clear mass balance, (b) correct unit conversions, (c) explicit statement of assumptions, (d) safety factor application, and (e) a final numerical answer with units. Missing any one of these = **−2 marks**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Material Selection for Common Food Streams

| Food Stream | pH | Chloride (ppm) | Temperature (°C) | Recommended Material | Reason |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Milk, cream** | 6.6–6.8 | 100–200 | 4–80 | **SS304** | Mild, low Cl⁻, low T |
| **Whey (acid)** | 4.0–4.6 | 1000–2000 | 10–50 | **SS316L** | Acidic + high Cl⁻ |
| **Brine (pickle)** | 2.5–4.5 | 50,000+ | 20–90 | **SS316L or Duplex SS** | High Cl⁻, risk of pitting |
| **Fruit juice (citric)** | 2.5–3.8 | <50 | 5–90 | **SS304 acceptable, SS316L preferred** | Acidic but low Cl⁻ |
| **Tomato sauce** | 4.0–4.5 | 500–2000 | 80–95 | **SS316L** | Hot + acidic + Cl⁻ |
| **Edible oil** | — | 0 | 150–250 | **SS304 (cold), CS with SS liner (hot)** | Avoid metal leaching into oil |
| **Beer wort** | 5.0–5.5 | 50–300 | 60–100 | **SS304** (kettle), **SS316L** (fermenter) | Microbial-sensitive |
| **Sugar syrup** | 6.5–7.2 | 200–800 | 60–110 | **SS304 (mild), SS316L (refined)** | Scaling and corrosion at evaporator |

> [!NOTE]
> **SS316L** has **2–3% molybdenum (Mo)** added to SS304, which dramatically improves resistance to **chloride-induced pitting and crevice corrosion**. The "L" denotes **low carbon (≤ 0.03%)** to prevent **sensitization** (chromium carbide precipitation at grain boundaries) during welding.

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchical Design Consideration Framework

The following diagram maps the **5-pillar design framework (F-S-M-E-S)** into a structured decision flow that a KTU student can reproduce during an exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Define Separation Objective] --> B[Identify Feed Properties]
    B --> B1[Physical State: Solid-Liquid-Liquid-Gas]
    B --> B2[Composition: xF Mass Fraction]
    B --> B3[Thermal Sensitivity: Tmax and tmax]
    B --> B4[Viscosity mu and Density rho]
    
    B1 --> C{Pillar 1: Process Function}
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> C1[Select Dominant Property Differential]
    C1 --> C2[Match to Unit Operation: Centrifuge-Filter-Membrane-Extractor]
    
    C2 --> D{Pillar 2: Sanitary Design}
    D --> D1[Surface Roughness Ra le 0.8 micrometer]
    D --> D2[Slope for Drainage ge 1 percent]
    D --> D3[Radius of Bends ge 1.5 D]
    D --> D4[Dead-Leg le 2 D]
    D --> D5[CIP Capability: ReCIP ge 4000]
    
    D5 --> E{Pillar 3: Material Selection}
    E --> E1[Calculate Corrosion Index CI]
```
