# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: List the five major categories of design considerations that govern the selection of separation equipment in food processing industries.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The five major categories of design considerations in food separation are:

1. **Process and Feed Considerations** – physical state, composition, viscosity, thermal sensitivity, and desired product purity.
2. **Sanitary / Hygienic Design** – surface finish (Ra ≤ 0.8 μm), drainage slope, dead-leg elimination, and CIP compatibility.
3. **Material of Construction** – corrosion resistance (SS304, SS316L), chloride compatibility, and weld quality.
4. **Energy and Economic Considerations** – capital cost, operating cost, energy per kg of product, and payback period.
5. **Safety and Regulatory Compliance** – pressure relief, ATEX zones, FDA/FSSAI/3-A/EHEDG standards, and operator safety.

> [!TIP]
> For full **3 marks**, the student must list all five with at least **one descriptor each** (e.g., "Sanitary: Ra ≤ 0.8 μm"). A bare list without descriptors = **−1 mark**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between SS304 and SS316L as materials of construction for food separation equipment. State two specific food applications where SS316L is mandatory.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | SS304 | SS316L |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Composition** | 18% Cr, 8% Ni | 16% Cr, 10% Ni, **2–3% Mo** |
| **Carbon content** | ≤ 0.08% | ≤ 0.03% ("L" = low carbon) |
| **Chloride resistance** | Moderate | **High** (Mo addition) |
| **Pitting corrosion** | Susceptible | **Resistant** |
| **Weldability** | Good | **Superior** (low-C prevents sensitization) |
| **Cost** | Lower | ~25–40% higher |

**Two food applications where SS316L is mandatory:**
1. **Hot brine handling** (pickle, cheese) — high Cl⁻ + high T causes pitting in SS304.
2. **Whey ultrafiltration / RO** — high Cl⁻ + acidic pH + protein fouling demands Mo-stabilized grade.

> [!TIP]
> For full marks, explicitly mention the **role of molybdenum (Mo)** in chloride resistance. Examiners specifically look for this keyword.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, each Part B question features **internal choice** between **Question A** and **Question B**. Both questions below carry 14 marks with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q: A dairy plant plans to concentrate 15,000 kg/h of skim milk (8.5% total solids, balance water) to 30% total solids using a multi-effect evaporator (MEE).**

**(a) [7 marks]** Draw and explain the **process flow diagram** of a **forward-feed triple-effect evaporator**. State the **assumptions** used in design. Identify the dominant **design considerations** (at least five) specific to this evaporator application in the dairy industry.

**(b) [7 marks]** Calculate the **steam economy** and **total steam required per hour** if the feed enters at 40 °C, the first effect boils at 80 °C, the second at 65 °C, and the third at 50 °C. Assume the **enthalpy of vaporization is approximately constant at 2300 kJ/kg** and the **specific heat of milk is 3.9 kJ/kg·K**. Use the **simple material and energy balance** approach.

---

### ✅ Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) [7 marks]

**Process Flow Diagram (Description):**

In a **forward-feed triple-effect evaporator**, both the feed and the steam/vapor flow in the **same direction** — from Effect 1 → Effect 2 → Effect 3. The live steam enters the calandria (steam chest) of Effect 1, boils the milk, and the vapor produced ("secondary vapor") is used as the heating medium for Effect 2, and so on.

**Key Equipment in the Train:**

- **Feed preheater** (uses flash vapor to preheat incoming milk)
- **Effect 1 calandria** (steam-heated, T₁ = 80 °C)
- **Effect 1 vapor separator** (removes entrained droplets)
- **Effect 2 calandria** (heated by Effect 1 vapor, T₂ = 65 °C)
- **Effect 3 calandria** (heated by Effect 2 vapor, T₃ = 50 °C)
- **Condenser + vacuum pump** (at the end of Effect 3)
- **Product discharge pump** (concentrated milk at ~30% TS)
- **CIP loop** (clean-in-place piping)
- **PRV on each calandria** (pressure relief at 1.1 × design pressure)

**Assumptions in Design:**

1. **Boiling-point elevation (BPE)** is negligible for skim milk (a reasonable assumption for low-solids dairy; in reality, ~1–2 °C BPE applies at 30% TS).
2. **Latent heat of vaporization is constant** at 2300 kJ/kg across all effects.
3. **Heat losses to surroundings are negligible** (well-insulated calandrias).
4. **Equal heat transfer area** in all three effects (A₁ = A₂ = A₃).
5. **No solids in vapor** (perfect separation in vapor separators).
6. **Feed enters above bubble point** (sub-cooled liquid feed at 40 °C).
7. **Negligible sensible heat changes in vapor** compared to latent heat.

**Five Dominant Design Considerations (specific to dairy MEE):**

1. **Thermal Sensitivity of Milk** — Whey proteins denature above 75 °C; casein can foul heat surfaces. **Multi-effect design lowers T in later effects** to mitigate damage.
2. **Material of Construction** — SS316L for evaporator bodies and tubes; eliminates metal-catalyzed off-flavors and resists lactic acid corrosion.
3. **CIP Capability** — Every effect must be drainable (slope ≥ 1%), with spray balls for internal cleaning, dead-legs ≤ 2D, Ra ≤ 0.8 μm.
4. **Vacuum Operation** — Last effect operates under vacuum (≈ 12 kPa abs at 50 °C); requires vacuum pump, condenser, and PRV on each effect.
5. **Steam Economy vs. Capital Cost** — More effects → higher steam economy (3-effect ≈ 2.5–3.0 kg water evaporated per kg steam) but higher capital cost and lower ΔT per effect → larger heat transfer area. **Optimization needed.**

**Additional considerations:** Vapour-liquid separation efficiency (entrainment separators to prevent product loss), condensate recovery for boiler feedwater, and energy integration (MVR/MEE hybrid).

---

#### Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $F = 15{,}000 \ \text{kg/h}$ skim milk
- $x_F = 0.085$ (8.5% TS)
- $x_P = 0.30$ (30% TS)
- $T_{feed} = 40 \ ^\circ\text{C}$
- $T_1 = 80 \ ^\circ\text{C}$, $T_2 = 65 \ ^\circ\text{C}$, $T_3 = 50 \ ^\circ\text{C}$
- $\lambda \approx 2300 \ \text{kJ/kg}$ (constant)
- $C_p = 3.9 \ \text{kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$

**Step 1 — Total Water to be Evaporated**

Solids balance:

$$P \cdot x_P = F \cdot x_F$$

$$P = \frac{15{,}000 \times 0.085}{0.30} = 4{,}250 \ \text{kg/h (concentrate)}$$

Total water evaporated:

$$W_{total} = F - P = 15{,}000 - 4{,}250 = 10{,}750 \ \text{kg/h}$$

**Step 2 — Energy to Heat Milk to First Boiling Point (40 °C → 80 °C)**

$$Q_{heat} = F \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T = 15{,}000 \times 3.9 \times (80 - 40)$$

$$Q_{heat} = 15{,}000 \times 3.9 \times 40 = 2{,}340{,}000 \ \text{kJ/h}$$

**Step 3 — Energy to Vaporize Water (all three effects)**

$$Q_{vap} = W_{total} \cdot \lambda = 10{,}750 \times 2300 = 24{,}725{,}000 \ \text{kJ/h}$$

**Step 4 — Total Energy Required**

$$Q_{total} = Q_{heat} + Q_{vap} = 2{,}340{,}000 + 24{,}725{,}000 = 27{,}065{,}000 \ \text{kJ/h}$$

**Step 5 — Theoretical Steam Economy of a Triple-Effect Evaporator**

For an ideal *n*-effect evaporator, the steam economy is:

$$E = \frac{W_{total}}{S} \approx n \cdot \eta$$

For a real triple-effect evaporator:

$$E_{real} \approx 0.85 \times 3 = 2.55 \ \text{kg water} / \text{kg steam}$$

(Here, 0.85 is a typical thermodynamic efficiency factor accounting for BPE, heat losses, and sensible heat.)

**Step 6 — Steam Required**

$$S = \frac{W_{total}}{E_{real}} = \frac{10{,}750}{2.55} = 4{,}215.7 \ \text{kg/h}$$

**Step 7 — Final Answer**

$$\boxed{S \approx 4{,}216 \ \text{kg steam/h}, \quad E_{real} \approx 2.55 \ \text{kg water/kg steam}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Mark Scheme (Part b):**
> - Correct mass balance → **2 marks**
> - Correct energy balance → **2 marks**
> - Steam economy formula + calculation → **2 marks**
> - Final answer with units + statement → **1 mark**

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**Q: A beverage company processes 8,000 L/h of clarified apple juice (ρ = 1050 kg/m³, μ = 1.2 cP at 20 °C) through a **plate-and-frame microfiltration (MF) membrane system** to remove residual haze particles (target turbidity < 5 NTU).**

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **key design considerations** for selecting (i) membrane material, (ii) pore size, (iii) transmembrane pressure, and (iv) material of construction for the membrane housing. Justify each choice with reference to apple juice chemistry and food safety regulations.

**(b) [7 marks]** During pilot trials, the membrane flux declines from an initial $J_0 = 80 \ \text{L/m}^2\cdot\text{h}$ (LMH) to a steady-state $J_{ss} = 45 \ \text{L/m}^2\cdot\text{h}$ after 4 hours. Calculate the **membrane area required** for the full-scale plant, the **fouling resistance ratio** $R_f / R_m$, and recommend a **cleaning protocol** (chemicals, temperature, and frequency).

---

### ✅ Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) [7 marks]

**(i) Membrane Material: Polyvinylidene Fluoride (PVDF) or Polysulfone (PSf) — Hydrophilized**

- **Justification:** Apple juice contains organic acids (malic, citric at pH 3.5–4.0) and pectin, which foul hydrophobic membranes rapidly. **PVDF** is mechanically robust, tolerates pH 1–13 and temperatures up to 95 °C, and resists chlorine-based cleaning agents. **Polysulfone** offers higher flux but lower chlorine tolerance. For acidic fruit juice, **hydrophilized PVDF** is the KTU-recommended choice.

**(ii) Pore Size: 0.2 μm (sterilizing-grade MF)**

- **Justification:** Apple juice haze particles are typically 0.5–5 μm (pectin–protein complexes). A **0.2 μm pore** guarantees removal of haze particles **and** all bacteria (*Lactobacillus*, *Acetobacter*) and yeast (*Saccharomyces*). This allows the juice to be called "cold-pasteurized" or "fresh" while meeting **FSSAI microbial limits** (TPC < 100 CFU/mL for fruit juice).

**(iii) Transmembrane Pressure (TMP): 1.0–2.0 bar**

- **Justification:** Apple juice is **non-Newtonian at high pectin concentration** and forms a compressible cake. Above TMP ≈ 2.5 bar, the cake compacts and flux *decreases* ("flux collapse"). Operating at **1.5 bar** is the industry sweet spot — high enough for throughput, low enough to avoid pectin gel compaction. The feed velocity should be **1–2 m/s** in the feed channel to keep particles in suspension (high shear).

**(iv) Material of Construction: SS316L for Housing, EPDM or Silicone for Gaskets**

- **Justification:**
  - **SS316L** — Apple juice has pH 3.5 (mild acid) and may contain chloride from water sources. SS316L's Mo content prevents pitting in chloride-bearing aqueous streams.
  - **EPDM gaskets** — Food-grade, FDA-compliant, temperature-resistant up to 150 °C, no dead-legs when properly seated.
  - **No threaded connections** in the product zone — use **Tri-Clamp®** or **DIN 11864** sanitary fittings only.
  - **Surface finish** Ra ≤ 0.8 μm to prevent biofilm formation, per **3-A Sanitary Standard 18-01** (membrane systems).

---

#### Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Volumetric Feed Rate in m³/s**

$$Q_F = 8{,}000 \ \text{L/h} = 8 \ \text{m}^3/\text{h} = \frac{8}{3600} = 2.222 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Use Steady-State Flux to Size Membrane Area**

At steady state, $J_{ss} = 45 \ \text{LMH} = 45 \ \text{L/m}^2\cdot\text{h} = \dfrac{45 \times 10^{-3}}{3600} \ \text{m}^3/\text{m}^2\cdot\text{s}$

$$J_{ss} = 1.25 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^3/\text{m}^2\cdot\text{s}$$

$$A = \frac{Q_F}{J_{ss}} = \frac{2.222 \times 10^{-3}}{1.25 \times 10^{-5}} = 177.78 \ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Apply Design Safety Factor (1.15 for membrane plants)**

$$A_{design} = 1.15 \times 177.78 = 204.4 \ \text{m}^2 \approx 205 \ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the Fouling Resistance Ratio**

The flux decline follows the **resistance-in-series model**:

$$\frac{1}{J} = \frac{\mu (R_m + R_f)}{TMP} = \frac{1}{J_0} + \frac{\mu R_f}{TMP}$$

So:

$$\frac{1}{J_{ss}} - \frac{1}{J_0} = \frac{\mu R_f}{TMP} - 0$$

$$\frac{1}{J_0} = \frac{\mu R_m}{TMP} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_m = \frac{TMP}{\mu \cdot J_0}$$

Assume $TMP = 1.5 \ \text{bar} = 1.5 \times 10^5 \ \text{Pa}$, $\mu = 1.2 \ \text{cP} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$.

Convert fluxes to SI:
- $J_0 = \dfrac{80 \times 10^{-3}}{3600} = 2.222 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^3/\text{m}^2\cdot\text{s}$
- $J_{ss} = 1.25 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^3/\text{m}^2\cdot\text{s}$

$$R_m = \frac{1.5 \times 10^5}{(1.2 \times 10^{-3}) \times (2.222 \times 10^{-5})} = 5.625 \times 10^{12} \ \text{m}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{J_{ss}} = \frac{\mu (R_m + R_f)}{TMP}$$

$$R_m + R_f = \frac{TMP}{\mu \cdot J_{ss}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^5}{(1.2 \times 10^{-3}) \times (1.25 \times 10^{-5})} = 1.0 \times 10^{13} \ \text{m}^{-1}$$

$$R_f = 1.0 \times 10^{13} - 5.625 \times 10^{12} = 4.375 \times 10^{12} \ \text{m}^{-1}$$

**Fouling Resistance Ratio:**

$$\boxed{\frac{R_f}{R_m} = \frac{4.375 \times 10^{12}}{5.625 \times 10^{12}} = 0.778 \approx 0.78}$$

This means **fouling contributes ~44% of the total resistance** — a typical result for apple juice MF.

**Step 5 — Recommended Cleaning Protocol**

| Step | Chemical | Concentration | Temperature | Duration |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **1. Alkaline wash** | NaOH | 1.0–2.0% w/w | 70–80 °C | 30 min |
| **2. Intermediate rinse** | Potable water | — | 50 °C | 10 min |
| **3. Acid wash** | HNO₃ or citric acid | 0.5–1.0% w/w | 50–60 °C | 20 min |
| **4. Final rinse** | Potable water | — | Ambient | Until pH neutral |
| **5. Sanitization** (optional) | NaOCl | 100–200 ppm free Cl | Ambient | 15 min |
| **6. Final rinse** | Potable water | — | Ambient | 5 min |

**Cleaning Frequency:** Every **6–8 hours** of operation, or when flux drops below **80%** of $J_{ss}$.

**Justification:**
- **NaOH** hydrolyzes and dissolves pectin, proteins, and polyphenols (the dominant apple juice foulants).
- **Acid wash** removes mineral scales (calcium oxalate from apple).
- **NaOCl sanitization** (if used) keeps biofilm counts low.
- **CIP velocity:** Maintain $Re \geq 4000$ in all product pipes.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Mark Scheme (Part b):**
> - Correct area calculation → **2 marks**
> - Safety factor applied → **1 mark**
> - Fouling resistance model setup → **2 marks**
> - Final $R_f/R_m$ value → **1 mark**
> - Cleaning protocol with correct chemicals & justification → **1 mark**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks on "Design Considerations" Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing mass and mole fractions** — Always use mass fractions ($x$) unless explicitly asked for mole fractions ($y$). Mixing them up = **−2 marks**.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the design safety factor** — A "clean" calculated area or wall thickness is *not* the final answer. KTU expects you to multiply by **SF = 1.2 (membranes) to 2.0 (pressure vessels)**. Forgetting this = **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 
> 3. **Ignoring viscosity unit conversions** — Viscosity in centipoise (cP) **must** be converted to Pa·s: $1 \ \text{cP} = 10^{-3} \ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$. A common silent error.
> 
> 4. **Listing design considerations without grouping** — Merely listing "temperature, pressure, material" in a flat list loses marks. The KTU marker expects the **5-pillar framework (F-S-M-E-S)** or **3-tier hierarchy** for full credit.
> 
> 5. **Writing "SS316" instead of "SS316L"** — The "L" (low carbon) is *specifically* required for welded food-grade equipment to prevent sensitization. KTU examiners are trained to spot this.
> 
> **Bonus pitfall:** Writing "P&ID" without explaining what it stands for ("Piping and Instrumentation Diagram") may cost 0.5 marks. Always expand acronyms on first use.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> Use this as a **rapid revision checklist** the night before the exam.

### 🎯 The Big Picture
- **Design considerations** = the *engineering criteria* that govern the choice, size, and operation of a food separator.
- The **5-pillar framework** is **F-S-M-E-S** → **Function, Sanitation, Material, Energy, Safety**.
- The **3-tier hierarchy** is → **Process/Feed → Equipment/Mechanical → Operational/Economic/Regulatory**.

### 🧠 Must-Memorize Equations
- Reynolds number: $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$
- Hagen-Poiseuille: $\Delta P = \dfrac{32 \mu L v}{D^2}$
- Darcy's law (filtration/membrane): $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu (R_m + R_f + R_c)}$
- Stokes' settling: $v_s = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{18 \mu}$
- Centrifuge equivalent area: $\Sigma = \dfrac{\omega^2 V_{bowl}}{g \ln(R_2/R_1)}$
- Mass balance: $F = P + W$; $F x_F = P x_P + W x_W$
- Recovery: $R = \dfrac{P x_P}{F x_F}$
- Corrosion index: $CI = \dfrac{[\text{Cl}^-] \cdot T}{R_{mat}}$
- Payback period: $PBP = \dfrac{C_{cap}}{C_{annual\ saving}}$
- Design safety factor: $SF \geq 1.5$–$2.0$ (vessels); $1.2$ (membranes)

### 🧹 Sanitary Design Must-Knows
- **Surface finish** $R_a \leq 0.8 \ \mu\text{m}$ (3-A)
- **Drainage slope** $\geq 1\%$
- **Bend radius** $\geq 1.5 D$
- **Dead-leg** $\leq 2 D$
- **No threads** in product zone — use Tri-Clamp® or DIN fittings
- **CIP Reynolds** $Re \geq 4000$ (turbulent)
- **CIP sequence:** pre-rinse → alkali (1–2% NaOH, 70–80 °C) → rinse → acid (0.5–1% HNO₃) → rinse → sanitize (NaOCl 100–200 ppm) → final rinse

### 🛠️ Material Selection
- **SS304** — standard food grade, mild streams
- **SS316L** — high Cl⁻, acidic, welded equipment (**always specify the "L"!**)
- **Duplex SS** — hot concentrated brine
- **Polymers (PTFE, EPDM, silicone)** — gaskets and seals

### ⚖️ Economic & Safety
- Apply **PBP** and **NPV** for design alternatives
- Apply **HAZOP** and **FMEA** for safety
- Always include **PRVs** (set at 1.1 × design pressure)
- Compliance: **3-A, EHEDG, FDA, FSSAI, BIS, ISO 22000, HACCP**

### 🔁 Common Separator Selection Triggers
- Cream from milk → **Disk-stack centrifuge** (Δρ)
- Whey protein → **UF membrane** (molecular size)
- Apple juice clarity → **MF (0.2 μm PVDF)** (particle size + microbiology)
- Sugar refining → **Evaporative crystallizer** (solubility vs T)
- Bioethanol purification → **Distillation** (volatility)
- Edible oil refining → **Centrifuge + adsorption** (Δρ + color)

### 🎓 One-Line Summary for Viva
> *"Design considerations in food separation are an integrated evaluation of feed properties, hygienic design, material compatibility, energy economics, and safety regulations to ensure that the chosen separator produces safe, high-quality food at an acceptable cost."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance of membrane
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT527 - SEPARATION PROCESSES IN FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Performance of membrane

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Membrane Performance** refers to the quantitative and qualitative measure of how effectively a semipermeable membrane accomplishes a given separation task under specific operating conditions (transmembrane pressure, temperature, feed concentration, cross-flow velocity). In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, membrane performance is benchmarked using two principal engineering parameters:

1. **Permeate Flux ($J$)** — the volumetric (or mass) rate of solvent/solute passage per unit membrane area per unit time.
2. **Rejection Coefficient ($R$)** — the membrane's ability to retain a specific solute while allowing the solvent to permeate.

Mathematically, the performance envelope of any membrane is described by the triad:

$$\{J,\, R,\, \tau\} \quad \text{where } \tau = \text{membrane service lifetime/operating stability}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In food technology contexts, membrane performance is never quoted in isolation — it is always reported *alongside* the **product integrity metrics** (color retention, vitamin retention, microbial log reduction, aroma profile preservation).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **tea-strainer with a calibrated mesh size** placed over a cup. The strainer physically blocks tea leaves (the "retained solute") while letting the brewed tea liquor (the "permeate") drip through. The **drip rate** is your *flux*; the **clarity of the liquid** (no leaves escaped) is your *rejection*. Now imagine that as you keep pouring, fine leaf particles start *clogging* the mesh — the drip rate slows, and that's analogous to **fouling**. Finally, if you push the tea too hard (high pressure), some leaves squeeze through — that drop in rejection is the **concentration polarization + compaction effect**.

**Key Performance Metrics in Bold:**
- **Permeate Flux ($J$):** Typically **10–500 L/m²·h (LMH)** in food UF/MF applications.
- **Rejection ($R$):** Expressed as a **dimensionless fraction (0 to 1)** or **percentage (0–100%)**.
- **Transmembrane Pressure (TMP):** Standard operating range **0.5–10 bar** for MF/UF, **10–80 bar** for RO.
- **Cross-Flow Velocity ($u$):** Typically **0.5–3 m/s** in food industry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Permeate vs. Retentate:**
> - **Permeate (P):** The portion of feed that *passes through* the membrane (purified solvent or low-MW fraction).
> - **Retentate (R):** The portion that is *retained* by the membrane and recycled/concentrated (the concentrate stream).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework

The performance of a membrane is governed by **three sequential resistance layers** that solvent molecules must traverse:

1. **Membrane Intrinsic Resistance ($R_m$)** — Inherent resistance offered by the dense skin layer (a function of pore size, porosity, tortuosity, and thickness).
2. **Fouling Resistance ($R_f$)** — Additional resistance offered by deposited material on the membrane surface (irreversible, time-dependent).
3. **Concentration Polarization Layer Resistance ($R_{cp}$)** — Resistance offered by the build-up of retained solute near the membrane surface (reversible by adjusting cross-flow).

**Governing Logic Flow:**

- Solvent flux is driven by the **net driving force** (chemical potential gradient), which for pressure-driven processes reduces to **transmembrane pressure ($\Delta P$)**.
- The flux is **inversely proportional** to the sum of all resistances.
- As the **operating time** increases, $R_f$ and $R_{cp}$ typically increase, causing **flux decline** — a primary indicator of degrading membrane performance.
- **Rejection** is governed by **solute size, shape, charge**, and **membrane pore size distribution**.
- Recovery (yield) of the process is determined by the **volumetric concentration factor (VCF)**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbols & Units | Physical Meaning | Applicable Process |
|---|---------|-----------------|------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $J = \dfrac{V_p}{A_m \cdot t}$ | $J$ = flux (L/m²·h), $V_p$ = permeate volume (L), $A_m$ = membrane area (m²), $t$ = time (h) | Volumetric throughput per unit area | All membrane processes |
| 2 | $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_{total}}$ | $\Delta P$ = TMP (Pa), $\mu$ = viscosity (Pa·s), $R_{total}$ = total resistance (m⁻¹) | Darcy's law for membrane flux | MF, UF |
| 3 | $R_{total} = R_m + R_f + R_{cp}$ | $R_m, R_f, R_{cp}$ = membrane, fouling, CP resistances (m⁻¹) | Resistance-in-series model | All pressure-driven |
| 4 | $R = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}$ | $R$ = rejection, $C_p$ = permeate concentration, $C_f$ = feed concentration | Solute rejection coefficient | All |
| 5 | $R_{obs} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_b}$ | $C_b$ = bulk feed concentration | Observed (apparent) rejection | UF/RO accounting for CP |
| 6 | $R_{true} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_w}$ | $C_w$ = wall (membrane surface) concentration | Intrinsic membrane rejection | All |
| 7 | $\dfrac{C_w - C_p}{C_b - C_p} = \exp\left(\dfrac{J}{k}\right)$ | $k$ = mass transfer coefficient (m/s) | Film theory equation (CP) | UF, RO |
| 8 | $\text{Recovery} = \dfrac{V_p}{V_f} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{\text{VCF}}$ | $V_f$ = feed volume, VCF = volumetric concentration factor | Yield / fractionation efficiency | All |
| 9 | $k = \dfrac{D}{\delta} = 0.023 \cdot \left(\dfrac{D \cdot \rho \cdot u}{d_h \cdot \mu}\right)^{0.5} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\mu}{\rho \cdot D}\right)^{1/3}$ | $D$ = diffusivity, $\delta$ = boundary layer thickness, $d_h$ = hydraulic diameter | Dittus-Boelter / Lévêque mass transfer correlation | Cross-flow modules |
| 10 | $\text{MDVR} = -\dfrac{d(\ln J)}{dt}$ | $dJ/dt$ = flux decline rate | Membrane decay rate | Lifetime studies |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions:**
> - For an **ideal membrane**: $R = 1$ (100% rejection of solute) and $J = \text{const}$ (constant flux).
> - For a **fully porous MF membrane**: $R \to 0$ for solutes smaller than the largest pore.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the food industry, membrane performance dictates:
- **Dairy**: Standardization of milk protein concentration, whey fractionation (UF).
- **Fruit juice**: Clarification (UF), concentration (RO), deacidification (NF).
- **Beverages**: Cold sterilization of wine and beer (MF), water reuse (RO).
- **Sugar refining**: Demineralization of cane juice (ED/RO hybrid).
- **Enzyme processing**: Recovery and concentration of industrial enzymes (UF).

The economic viability of these processes hinges on **sustaining high flux** (capital cost = membrane area) and **maintaining high rejection** (product quality).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Resistance-in-Series Flux Equation

**Starting Point:** Solvent volumetric flow through a porous medium follows Darcy's law.

$$J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_{total}}$$

**Step 1 — Identify the driving force:**
For a pressure-driven membrane process, the net driving force per unit area is the **transmembrane pressure (TMP)**, defined as:

$$\Delta P = \frac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_p$$

where $P_{in}$, $P_{out}$, $P_p$ are inlet, outlet, and permeate side pressures respectively.

**Step 2 — Express the total resistance as a sum of three contributions:**

$$R_{total} = R_m + R_f + R_{cp}$$

**Step 3 — Define each resistance term:**

- $R_m = \dfrac{\Delta P_0}{\mu \cdot J_0}$ (from clean water flux $J_0$ measurement at TMP $\Delta P_0$)
- $R_f$ obtained by water flux measurement after removing the CP layer
- $R_{cp} = R_{total} - R_m - R_f$ (by difference)

**Step 4 — Substitute back into Darcy's law:**

$$\boxed{J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot (R_m + R_f + R_{cp})}}$$

**Conversion logic:** Each resistance corresponds to a hypothetical "additional thickness" of membrane that the solvent must effectively traverse. Fouling is irreversible; CP is reversible; $R_m$ is constant for a given membrane.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Film Theory and Concentration Polarization

**Step 1 — Set up a steady-state mass balance at the membrane surface:**

At steady state, the convective flux of solute toward the membrane equals the sum of solute permeating through the membrane and the back-diffusive flux away from the membrane:

$$J \cdot C_b = J \cdot C_p + D \cdot \frac{dC}{dy}\bigg|_{y=0}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate across the boundary layer** (thickness $\delta$, from $y=0$ at the wall to $y=\delta$ in the bulk):

$$J \cdot (C_b - C_p) = -D \cdot \frac{dC}{dy}$$

Rearranging:

$$J \cdot dy = -D \cdot \frac{dC}{C_b - C_p}$$

Integrating from $y=0$ ($C = C_w$) to $y=\delta$ ($C = C_b$):

$$J \cdot \delta = D \cdot \ln\left(\frac{C_w - C_p}{C_b - C_p}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Define the mass transfer coefficient** $k = D / \delta$:

$$J = k \cdot \ln\left(\frac{C_w - C_p}{C_b - C_p}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the wall concentration** (a critical performance metric):

$$\boxed{C_w = C_p + (C_b - C_p) \cdot \exp\left(\frac{J}{k}\right)}$$

**Conversion logic:** As $J$ increases (higher pressure), $C_w$ rises exponentially, leading to:
- **Osmotic pressure increase** → reduces effective driving force in RO
- **Gel layer formation** → limits flux in UF (gel-polarization model: $J_{lim} = k \cdot \ln(C_g / C_b)$)
- **Increased fouling rate**

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Membrane Recovery and Volumetric Concentration Factor

**Step 1 — Define the recovery** (also called yield or conversion):

$$Y = \frac{V_p}{V_f}$$

**Step 2 — Mass balance on the solute (retained species):**

$$C_f \cdot V_f = C_r \cdot V_r + C_p \cdot V_p$$

For a high-rejection membrane, $C_p \ll C_f$, so:

$$C_f \cdot V_f \approx C_r \cdot V_r$$

**Step 3 — Volume balance:**

$$V_f = V_r + V_p \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_r = V_f - V_p$$

**Step 4 — Define the Volumetric Concentration Factor (VCF):**

$$\text{VCF} = \frac{V_f}{V_r} = \frac{C_r}{C_f} \quad \text{(for high rejection)}$$

**Step 5 — Express recovery in terms of VCF:**

$$\boxed{Y = 1 - \frac{1}{\text{VCF}}}$$

**Conversion logic:** A VCF of 10 means the feed volume is reduced 10-fold, giving 90% recovery as permeate. In whey UF for protein concentration, industrial targets are typically VCF = 10–20.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Performance Parameters vs. Process Type

| Performance Metric | MF | UF | NF | RO |
|-------------------|----|----|----|-----|
| **Pore size / MWCO** | 0.1–10 µm | 1–100 kDa | 200–1000 Da | <200 Da |
| **Typical Flux (LMH)** | 50–500 | 10–150 | 5–50 | 5–40 |
| **Operating TMP (bar)** | 0.1–2 | 0.5–5 | 5–25 | 10–80 |
| **Dominant Resistance** | $R_m$, $R_f$ | $R_{cp}$, $R_f$ | $R_m$, osmotic | $R_m$, osmotic |
| **Rejection Driver** | Size exclusion | Size exclusion | Size + charge | Solution-diffusion |
| **Concentration Polarization** | Moderate | Severe (gel layer) | High | High (osmotic) |
| **Typical $R$ for target solute** | >0.99 (bacteria) | 0.95–0.99 (proteins) | 0.5–0.95 (salts) | >0.99 (NaCl) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concentration Polarization — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Feed[Feed Stream Cb] -->|Convective flux J*Cb| BoundaryLayer[Concentration Boundary Layer]
    BoundaryLayer -->|Back-diffusion D*dCdy| BulkMix[Bulk Mixing Region]
    BoundaryLayer -->|Permeation J*Cp| Membrane[Semipermeable Membrane Skin]
    Membrane -->|Pure solvent J| Permeate[Permeate Stream Cp]
    
    Membrane -.->|Rejection R| Retentate[Retentate Stream Cr]
    
    BoundaryLayer -->|Concentration rises from Cb to Cw| WallConc[Wall Concentration Cw greater than Cb]
    WallConc -->|Osmotic pressure pi| FluxReduction[Effective Driving Force Reduced]
    
    FluxReduction -->|Flux decline| Jcurve[J vs t decay curve]
    
    style Feed fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Permeate fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Retentate fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Membrane fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style WallConc fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Jcurve fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Resistance-in-Series Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TMP[Transmembrane Pressure Delta P] --> Series{Series Resistance Network}
    Rm[Membrane Resistance Rm - Constant] --> Series
    Rf[Fouling Resistance Rf - Irreversible] --> Series
    Rcp[Concentration Polarization Rcp - Reversible] --> Series
    Series --> Flux[Permeate Flux J]
    
    CrossFlow[Cross Flow Velocity u] -->|Shear reduces| Rcp
    Cleaning[Periodic CIP] -->|Removes| Rf
    MembraneSel[Membrane Material Selection] -->|Determines| Rm
    
    Flux --> Monitor[Performance Monitoring]
    Monitor -->|Track J decline| FluxDecay
    Monitor -->|Track Cp/Cf| RejectionCheck
    
    style TMP fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style Flux fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style Rm fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style Rf fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style Rcp fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style FluxDecay fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style RejectionCheck fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Process Flow Architecture of a Cross-Flow Membrane System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FeedTank[Feed Tank] --> Pump[Feed Pump]
    Pump --> InletP[Inlet Pressure Pin]
    InletP --> MembraneModule[Membrane Module Cross Flow]
    MembraneModule --> OutletP[Outlet Pressure Pout]
    MembraneModule --> PermeateOut[Permeate Collection]
    OutletP --> BackPRV[Back Pressure Valve]
    BackPRV --> Recycle[Recycle to Feed Tank]
    
    InletP --> TMPcalc{TMP Calculation}
    OutletP --> TMPcalc
    PermeateOut --> TMPcalc
    TMPcalc --> Controller[Process Controller]
    Controller -->|Adjusts| Pump
    Controller -->|Adjusts| BackPRV
    
    style FeedTank fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style MembraneModule fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style PermeateOut fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style Controller fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define permeate flux and rejection coefficient in membrane separation processes. Write their mathematical expressions.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Permeate Flux ($J$):** The volume of permeate passing through a unit area of membrane per unit time. Expressed as:

$$J = \frac{V_p}{A_m \cdot t} \quad \text{[units: L/m}^2\cdot\text{h, or LMH]}$$

- **Rejection Coefficient ($R$):** The ability of a membrane to retain a specific solute while allowing solvent to pass. Expressed as:

$$R = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f}$$

where $C_p$ and $C_f$ are permeate and feed solute concentrations respectively. $R$ ranges from 0 (no retention) to 1 (complete retention).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is concentration polarization? How does it affect membrane performance?**

**Model Answer:**
**Concentration polarization (CP)** is the accumulation of retained solute on the membrane surface, creating a concentration gradient from the membrane wall ($C_w$) to the bulk feed ($C_b$).

**Effects on performance:**
- Increases **wall concentration** $C_w > C_b$, raising **osmotic pressure** and reducing effective driving force.
- Leads to **gel layer formation** in UF, causing flux to plateau at a limiting value.
- Promotes **fouling** by increasing local precipitation/scaling propensity.
- Reduces **observed rejection** (since $C_w$ rises above $C_b$).
- Controlled by **increasing cross-flow velocity** and **using turbulence promoters**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### **Option A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the **resistance-in-series model** for membrane flux. Explain each resistance term with its physical significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In an **ultrafiltration process**, the following data were obtained for whey protein concentration:
- Clean water flux $J_0 = 250$ LMH at $\Delta P_0 = 2$ bar
- Process flux $J = 80$ LMH at $\Delta P = 2$ bar, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- After removing the CP layer by high cross-flow, flux = 120 LMH at $\Delta P = 2$ bar

Calculate the membrane resistance, fouling resistance, and concentration polarization resistance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:** (See Section 3.1)

$$J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot (R_m + R_f + R_{cp})}$$

- $R_m$: intrinsic to membrane (pore size, porosity, skin thickness)
- $R_f$: from irreversible deposition of foulants
- $R_{cp}$: from reversible build-up of retained solute

**Part (b) — Calculations:**

Convert units: $\Delta P = 2 \text{ bar} = 2 \times 10^5$ Pa, $J_0 = 250$ LMH $= 250 / 3600 / 10^{-3}$... Let me use consistent units.

Convert: $J_0 = 250$ L/m²·h $= 6.944 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/m²·s = $6.944 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s
$J_{process} = 80$ L/m²·h = $2.222 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s
$J_{no\_cp} = 120$ L/m²·h = $3.333 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s
$\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s

**Step 1: Membrane resistance $R_m$** (from clean water flux):

$$R_m = \frac{\Delta P_0}{\mu \cdot J_0} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{(1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times (6.944 \times 10^{-5})}$$

$$R_m = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{6.944 \times 10^{-8}} = 2.88 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 2: Total resistance under process conditions:**

$$R_{total} = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot J} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{(1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times (2.222 \times 10^{-5})}$$

$$R_{total} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{2.222 \times 10^{-8}} = 9.00 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 3: Resistance without CP layer** (membrane + fouling only):

$$R_{m+f} = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot J_{no\_cp}} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{(1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times (3.333 \times 10^{-5})} = 6.00 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 4: Solve for individual resistances:**

$$R_{cp} = R_{total} - R_{m+f} = 9.00 \times 10^{12} - 6.00 \times 10^{12} = 3.00 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

$$R_f = R_{m+f} - R_m = 6.00 \times 10^{12} - 2.88 \times 10^{12} = 3.12 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{R_m = 2.88 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-1}, \quad R_f = 3.12 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-1}, \quad R_{cp} = 3.00 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-1}}$$

**Interpretation:** $R_{cp}$ is significant but reversible (controlled by cross-flow); $R_f$ is comparable in magnitude — frequent cleaning is essential.

---

### **Option B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of the **film theory model**, derive the relationship between wall concentration, bulk concentration, and permeate concentration in membrane separation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An RO membrane is processing orange juice concentrate. The bulk feed concentration of soluble solids is 12°Brix, and the permeate is 0.5°Brix. The mass transfer coefficient is $k = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s, and the operating flux is $J = 18$ LMH. Calculate the **wall concentration** and comment on its implications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:** (See Section 3.2)

$$\frac{C_w - C_p}{C_b - C_p} = \exp\left(\frac{J}{k}\right)$$

**Part (b) — Calculations:**

Convert flux: $J = 18$ LMH $= 18 / (3600 \times 10^{-3})$ m/s $= 5.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m/s

**Step 1: Calculate the dimensionless group $J/k$:**

$$\frac{J}{k} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-6}}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.333$$

**Step 2: Compute exponential term:**

$$\exp(0.333) = 1.396$$

**Step 3: Solve for $C_w$:**

$$C_w = C_p + (C_b - C_p) \times 1.396$$

$$C_w = 0.5 + (12 - 0.5) \times 1.396 = 0.5 + 11.5 \times 1.396$$

$$C_w = 0.5 + 16.05 = 16.55 \text{ °Brix}$$

**Step 4: Implications:**

$$\boxed{C_w = 16.55 \text{ °Brix}}$$

**Implications:**
- The wall concentration is **~38% higher** than bulk (16.55 vs 12), a clear sign of significant CP.
- Such high wall concentration promotes **sugar supersaturation** → risk of **scaling/crystallization** on the membrane surface (fouling).
- Osmotic pressure at the wall is elevated, **reducing the effective TMP** and therefore the net driving force.
- **Recommendation:** Increase cross-flow velocity, lower flux, or use pulsed feed to disrupt the boundary layer.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Converting $J$ in LMH to m/s without showing the conversion ($-1$ to $-2$ marks).
> 2. **Forgetting to convert bar to Pa** in resistance calculations — a frequent error.
> 3. **Mixing up $R$, $R_{obs}$, and $R_{true}$** — examiners explicitly look for the correct definition of $C_b$, $C_p$, and $C_w$.
> 4. **Not distinguishing reversible vs. irreversible resistance** — always state whether $R_{cp}$ or $R_f$ is being discussed.
> 5. **Skipping the logarithmic derivation step** in film theory — a major 3-mark loss.
> 6. **Forgetting to comment on real-world implications** (fouling, scaling, osmotic effects) in numerical problems — KTU awards 1–2 marks for industrial context.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Membrane performance is quantified by Flux ($J$), Rejection ($R$), and Lifetime ($\tau$).**
- **Flux** has units of **L/m²·h (LMH)**; convert to m/s by dividing by $3.6 \times 10^6$.
- **Rejection** is dimensionless: $R = 1 - C_p / C_f$; observed rejection uses bulk feed, true rejection uses wall concentration.
- **Resistance-in-series model:** $R_{total} = R_m + R_f + R_{cp}$.
- **Darcy's law for membranes:** $J = \Delta P / (\mu \cdot R_{total})$.
- **Film theory equation (CP):** $C_w = C_p + (C_b - C_p) \cdot \exp(J/k)$.
- **Mass transfer coefficient** $k$ increases with **cross-flow velocity** and follows Dittus-Boelter-type correlations in turbulent flow.
- **Recovery** = $V_p / V_f = 1 - 1/\text{VCF}$.
- **Flux decline** is caused by **fouling (irreversible)** + **CP (reversible)**; quantified by MDVR.
- **MF** = ~0.1–10 µm; **UF** = 1–100 kDa; **NF** = 200–1000 Da; **RO** = <200 Da.
- In **food applications**, performance is judged by **flux + rejection + product quality** (color, flavor, nutrient retention).
- **Cleaning-in-Place (CIP)** with NaOH, HNO₃, and enzymes is standard protocol to recover flux.
- **Gel-polarization model** (UF limit): $J_{lim} = k \cdot \ln(C_g / C_b)$ where $C_g$ is the gel concentration.
- **Osmotic pressure model** (RO limit): $J = A \cdot (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$, where $A$ is the solvent permeability constant.
- **Higher cross-flow velocity** → higher $k$ → lower $C_w$ → higher sustainable flux.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrodialysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT527 - SEPARATION PROCESSES IN FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrodialysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrodialysis (ED)** is an **electro-membrane separation process** in which an electrical potential difference (direct current) is used as the driving force to transport ionised species (cations and anions) through **selectively permeable ion-exchange membranes** — a cation-exchange membrane (CEM) and an anion-exchange membrane (AEM) — to separate, concentrate, or demineralise ionic components from a feed solution.

The process exploits the differential ionic permeability of cation-exchange membranes (which pass only **cations**, blocking anions) and anion-exchange membranes (which pass only **anions**, blocking cations). Under the influence of a DC electric field, ions migrate to their oppositely charged electrode, leaving a **diluate stream** (low in salt) and producing a **concentrate stream** (high in salt).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Sorting Gate" Analogy for Electrodialysis**
>
> Imagine a busy crowd (mixed ions) trying to pass through a corridor that has **two special doors**:
> 1. **Door A** (Cation-Exchange Membrane, CEM) — has a bouncer that only allows **positively charged people** (cations like Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺) to pass and pushes all negative people back.
> 2. **Door B** (Anion-Exchange Membrane, AEM) — has a bouncer that only allows **negatively charged people** (anions like Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻) to pass and pushes all positive people back.
>
> Now, an electric "wind" (the DC current) blows in a fixed direction: **positive people** are pushed towards the **negative end (cathode)** and **negative people** are pushed towards the **positive end (anode)**. By cleverly stacking Door A and Door B in **alternating sequence** between one anode and one cathode, we create alternating **"trapping zones"** (concentrate, where both cation AND anion accumulate) and **"clearing zones"** (diluate, where both ion types have been removed).
>
> The result: salt is **moved** from one stream into another — without any phase change, no heating, and no chemical reagent. This is the essence of electrodialysis.

### 1.3 Key Process Metrics (Standard Industrial Figures)

| Metric | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Operating voltage per cell pair | **1 – 2 V DC** |
| Current density | **100 – 500 A/m²** |
| Energy consumption (desalination) | **0.5 – 5.0 kWh/m³** of diluate |
| Operating temperature | **25 – 45 °C** |
| Membrane thickness | **0.1 – 0.6 mm** |
| Membrane selectivity (transport number) | **> 0.90** |
| Salt removal per stage | **25 – 50 %** |

### 1.4 Historical & Industrial Context

- First commercial electrodialysis plant was installed in **Sahara, North Africa (Wadi Natrun)**, in 1956, for producing drinking water from brackish water.
- The breakthrough invention of **ion-exchange membranes** by Juda and McRae (1950) at Ionics Inc. enabled modern ED.
- In **food technology**, electrodialysis is used for **whey demineralisation**, **juice deacidification**, **wine tartaric stabilisation**, and **sugar refining** (colorant removal).
- Recognised as a **"green" separation technology** under the EU 2020 Best Available Techniques (BAT) framework because it operates at ambient temperature with no chemical addition.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Fundamental Membrane Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CEM** and **AEM** are the two pillars of electrodialysis. Memorise their fixed-charge and permselectivity logic.

| Property | Cation-Exchange Membrane (CEM) | Anion-Exchange Membrane (AEM) |
|---|---|---|
| Fixed charge on polymer matrix | **Negative** (e.g., –SO₃⁻, –COO⁻) | **Positive** (e.g., –NR₃⁺, –PR₃⁺) |
| Counter-ions repelled | Anions (co-ions) | Cations (co-ions) |
| Permits passage of | Cations | Anions |
| Blocks | Anions | Cations |
| Typical material | Sulfonated polystyrene, Nafion™ | Quaternary-amine cross-linked polystyrene |
| Direction of cation migration | Towards **cathode (−)** | Blocked |
| Direction of anion migration | Blocked | Towards **anode (+)** |

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Process Logic (How Electrodialysis Works)

The working of an electrodialysis cell can be understood as a sequence of logical steps:

1. **Feed stream entry:** A saline solution (feed) is pumped into the **diluate compartments** of the ED stack.
2. **Electrode rinse:** A separate electrolyte (e.g., Na₂SO₄ or NaCl) is circulated through the **electrode compartments** to carry gas bubbles (H₂, O₂, Cl₂) away from electrodes and prevent electrode damage.
3. **Application of DC field:** A DC voltage is applied across the **anode (+) and cathode (−)** — typically 1–2 V per membrane pair.
4. **Cation migration:** All cations (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺) are electrophoretically driven **towards the cathode**, but can only pass through a **CEM**; they are blocked by an **AEM**.
5. **Anion migration:** All anions (Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, lactate, citrate) are driven **towards the anode**, but can only pass through an **AEM**; they are blocked by a **CEM**.
6. **Diluate formation:** In every alternate compartment, both cations and anions are being **swept out** → concentration drops → this is the **diluate** stream.
7. **Concentrate formation:** In the compartments immediately adjacent, the cations coming from one side and anions coming from the other side **converge** → concentration rises → this is the **concentrate (brine)** stream.
8. **Recycling or single-pass:** Diluate and concentrate are either discharged (single-pass) or partially recycled to enhance the degree of desalination.
9. **Stack assembly:** Many membrane pairs (typically **200 – 600 cell pairs**) are sandwiched between two electrodes to form a single **electrodialysis stack** — this multiplies productivity.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why DC current, not AC?** Because alternating current would reverse the direction of ion migration every half-cycle, causing no net separation and rapid membrane fatigue.
- **Why alternate CEM and AEM?** The alternating arrangement is the **only way** to create paired concentrate/diluate streams from a single electrical field.
- **Why not exceed limiting current density?** Beyond the *limiting current density*, water splitting (H⁺/OH⁻ generation) occurs at the membrane surface, reducing **current efficiency** and causing pH shifts that may damage the product.
- **Why spacers between membranes?** Spacers (0.5 – 2 mm thick) provide turbulence to the feed, reduce concentration polarisation, and define compartment geometry.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations are the analytical backbone of electrodialysis. Memorise the symbols and units.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\bar{t}_+ + \bar{t}_- = 1$ | Transport number of cation/anion through membrane | Permselectivity check |
| 2 | $P = \bar{t}_+ - t_+ \;\; \text{(CEM)}$ | Permselectivity of membrane | Membrane quality; should be > 0.90 |
| 3 | $R = \dfrac{Q_{\text{dil}} (C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}})}{Q_{\text{in}} C_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$ | Salt removal efficiency (%) | Process performance |
| 4 | $\eta_{\text{curr}} = \dfrac{z F Q_{\text{dil}} (C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}})}{n_{\text{cell}} I}$ | Current efficiency (0–1) | Electrical efficiency |
| 5 | $J_i = -D_i \dfrac{dC_i}{dx} - \dfrac{z_i F D_i C_i}{RT} \dfrac{d\phi}{dx} + C_i v$ | Nernst–Planck equation (flux of ion $i$) | Fundamental transport equation |
| 6 | $i_{\text{lim}} = \dfrac{F D C}{\delta \, (t_+ - t_-)}$ | Limiting current density (A/m²) | Design ceiling for current |
| 7 | $E_{\text{spec}} = \dfrac{V I}{Q_{\text{dil}} \, n_{\text{cell}}} = \dfrac{U_{\text{cell}} I}{Q_{\text{dil}}}$ | Specific energy consumption (kWh/m³) | Energy cost estimation |
| 8 | $U_{\text{cell}} = I \,(R_{\text{a}} + R_{\text{m,CEM}} + R_{\text{m,AEM}} + R_{\text{bl}})$ | Cell voltage drop (Ohm's law) | Voltage budget |
| 9 | $C_{\text{out}} = C_{\text{in}} \left(\dfrac{Q_{\text{in}}}{Q_{\text{out}}}\right) = C_{\text{in}} \left(1 - R\right)$ | Outlet concentration | Mass balance shortcut |
| 10 | $A_{\text{mem}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{dil}} \cdot z F (C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}})}{i \cdot \eta_{\text{curr}} \cdot n_{\text{cell}}}$ | Required membrane area (m²) | Equipment sizing |

**Standard constant:**
$$F = 96{,}485 \;\; \text{C/mol} \;\; \text{(Faraday constant)}$$

**Where:**
- $z$ = charge number of the ion
- $n_{\text{cell}}$ = number of cell pairs
- $D_i$ = diffusion coefficient (m²/s)
- $C$ = salt concentration (mol/m³)
- $\delta$ = Nernst diffusion layer thickness (m)
- $t_+$ = transport number in solution
- $\bar{t}_+$ = transport number in membrane
- $Q$ = volumetric flow (m³/s)
- $I$ = current (A)
- $R_{\text{bl}}$ = boundary layer resistance

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Whey demineralisation:** A 50 % (w/w) whey solution contains ~5.5 % ash. ED can reduce ash to < 0.1 % for **infant formula** (lactose-dominant whey with high purity).
- **Wine tartaric stabilisation:** ED removes excess K⁺ and bitartrate ions to prevent KHT (potassium hydrogen tartrate) crystallisation in bottled wine.
- **Sugar refining:** ED removes coloured ionic impurities (melanoidins, polyphenols) from beet sugar juice without thermal degradation.
- **Seawater and brackish water desalination:** A major commercial application — uses up to **40 %** less energy than reverse osmosis for low-salinity feeds.
- **Soy sauce and fermentation broth desalting:** Removes NaCl while preserving amino acids, peptides, and flavour volatiles — a clear advantage over ion-exchange resins that non-selectively bind organic acids.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From the Nernst–Planck Equation to the Limiting Current Density

The **Nernst–Planck equation** is the master transport equation for ion flux through a solution. For a binary monovalent electrolyte (e.g., NaCl: $z_+ = +1$, $z_- = -1$) at steady state in a stagnant boundary layer of thickness $\delta$:

$$J_i = -D_i \, \dfrac{dC_i}{dx} - \dfrac{z_i F D_i C_i}{RT} \, \dfrac{d\phi}{dx} + C_i v$$

**Assumption 1 — No convection in the boundary layer:** The spacer-induced flow does not penetrate the stagnant Nernst diffusion layer. Therefore $v = 0$.

$$J_i = -D_i \, \dfrac{dC_i}{dx} - \dfrac{z_i F D_i C_i}{RT} \, \dfrac{d\phi}{dx}$$

**Assumption 2 — Electroneutrality in solution:** $C_+ = C_- = C$ in the bulk of the boundary layer.

**Assumption 3 — At the limiting current:** The cation concentration at the membrane surface on the diluate side becomes zero ($C_{+,s} = 0$). This is the *limiting condition* that defines the maximum transportable current.

Setting $J_+$ (cation flux) and $J_-$ (anion flux) at the membrane surface equal to their respective transport numbers times the total ionic flux, and solving with the boundary conditions $C_{+,s} = 0$ at $x = 0$ and $C_{+,b}$ at $x = \delta$:

$$\begin{aligned}
i_{\text{lim}} &= F \cdot \bar{t}_+ \cdot J_+ \\
&= \dfrac{F D_+ C_b}{\delta} \left( \dfrac{\bar{t}_+ - t_+}{1 - t_+} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

When the transport number in solution $t_+ \approx 0.5$ (symmetric salt such as NaCl) and $\bar{t}_+ \to 1$ (highly permselective membrane), the bracketed term simplifies:

$$\left( \dfrac{\bar{t}_+ - t_+}{1 - t_+} \right) = \dfrac{1 - 0.5}{1 - 0.5} = 1$$

Therefore the **limiting current density** reduces to the classic form used in KTU textbooks:

$$\boxed{\,i_{\text{lim}} = \dfrac{F D \, C_b}{\delta}\,}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $i_{\text{lim}}$ increases linearly with bulk concentration $C_b$.
- $i_{\text{lim}}$ decreases as the Nernst boundary layer $\delta$ becomes thicker (high $\delta$ = poor hydrodynamics).
- Operating **above** $i_{\text{lim}}$ causes **water splitting** (2H₂O → H⁺ + OH⁻), reducing current efficiency and altering pH.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Current Efficiency from Faraday's Law

**Faraday's law of electrolysis** states that the charge passed is proportional to the moles of ions transferred.

$$n_{\text{ions}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{charge}}}{z F} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{z F}$$

**Step 1:** Moles of salt actually removed from the diluate per unit time:

$$n_{\text{removed}} = Q_{\text{dil}} (C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}})$$

**Step 2:** Equivalent charge that *should* have been passed to remove this many moles (from Faraday's law):

$$Q_{\text{theoretical}} = z F \, n_{\text{removed}} \cdot n_{\text{cell}}$$

The factor $n_{\text{cell}}$ accounts for the fact that the same current removes salt from $n_{\text{cell}}$ cell pairs in parallel.

**Step 3:** Actual charge passed: $Q_{\text{actual}} = I \, t$.

**Step 4:** Define **current efficiency** as the ratio:

$$\eta_{\text{curr}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{theoretical}}}{Q_{\text{actual}}} = \dfrac{z F \, Q_{\text{dil}} \, n_{\text{cell}} \, (C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}})}{I}$$

**Step 5:** For a monovalent salt ($z = 1$):

$$\boxed{\,\eta_{\text{curr}} = \dfrac{F \, Q_{\text{dil}} \, n_{\text{cell}} \, (C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}})}{I}\,}$$

**Typical value in industry:** $\eta_{\text{curr}} = 0.85 - 0.95$ for a well-designed stack.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Specific Energy Consumption

The electrical power drawn by the ED stack is:

$$P = V \cdot I$$

For $n_{\text{cell}}$ cell pairs in series, total stack voltage $V = n_{\text{cell}} \cdot U_{\text{cell}}$, where $U_{\text{cell}}$ is the voltage drop per cell pair.

**Total energy consumed in time $t$:**

$$E = P \cdot t = n_{\text{cell}} \cdot U_{\text{cell}} \cdot I \cdot t$$

**Volume of diluate produced in time $t$:**

$$V_{\text{dil}} = Q_{\text{dil}} \cdot t$$

**Specific energy consumption (per m³ of diluate):**

$$\boxed{\,E_{\text{spec}} = \dfrac{E}{V_{\text{dil}}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{cell}} \cdot U_{\text{cell}} \cdot I}{Q_{\text{dil}}} = U_{\text{cell}} \cdot \dfrac{I}{Q_{\text{dil}}}\,}$$

**Units:** W·s/m³ = J/m³. To convert to kWh/m³, divide by $3.6 \times 10^6$.

**Numerical example:** A stack with 200 cell pairs, $U_{\text{cell}} = 0.6$ V, $I = 100$ A, $Q_{\text{dil}} = 5$ m³/h:

$$E_{\text{spec}} = \dfrac{200 \times 0.6 \times 100}{5} = 2400 \;\; \text{Wh/m}^3 = 2.4 \;\; \text{kWh/m}^3$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Membrane Area Required

From the current efficiency equation, solving for $I$:

$$I = \dfrac{z F \, Q_{\text{dil}} \, n_{\text{cell}} \, (C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}})}{\eta_{\text{curr}}}$$

Current density $i = I / A_{\text{mem}}$, so $I = i \cdot A_{\text{mem}}$:

$$i \cdot A_{\text{mem}} = \dfrac{z F \, Q_{\text{dil}} \, n_{\text{cell}} \, (C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}})}{\eta_{\text{curr}}}$$

$$\boxed{\,A_{\text{mem}} = \dfrac{z F \, Q_{\text{dil}} \, n_{\text{cell}} \, (C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}})}{i \cdot \eta_{\text{curr}}}\,}$$

This is the **design equation** used to size industrial ED plants. Always operate at $i = 0.7 - 0.8 \, i_{\text{lim}}$ to leave a safety margin.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The ED Membrane Stack

The following diagram maps the functional architecture of an electrodialysis stack — from the feed inlet through membrane interaction zones to the diluate and concentrate outlets, including the electrode rinse circuit.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Feed Inlet: Saline Solution] --> B[High-Pressure Pump]
    B --> C[Feed Manifold]
    C --> D[Diluate Distribution Compartment]
    C --> E[Concentrate Distribution Compartment]

    D -->|Cations M+| F[CEM: Cation Exchange Membrane]
    F -->|Anions Repelled| D
    F -->|Cations Pass to Concentrate| E

    E -->|Anions Pass| G[AEM: Anion Exchange Membrane]
    G -->|Cations Repelled| E
    G -->|Anions Pass to Diluate| D

    G --> H[Stack End - Cathode]
    F --> I[Stack End - Anode]

    D --> J[Diluate Outlet: Desalted Product]
    E --> K[Concentrate Outlet: Brine]

    I --> L[DC Power Supply: 1-2 V per cell pair]
    H --> L
    L --> M[Electrode Rinse Recirculation Tank]
    M --> N[Na2SO4 Electrolyte Loop]
    N --> I
    N --> H
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Alternating Membrane Sequence in an ED Cell Pair

The diagram below illustrates how ion transport creates the **diluate** and **concentrate** compartments when CEM, AEM, and spacers are arranged alternately between a single pair of electrodes.

```mermaid
graph LR
    AN[Anode (+)] -->|Drives anions| AEM1
    AEM1[AEM: Permits Anions, Blocks Cations] --> DIL1
    DIL1[Diluate Compartment: Loses Both Ions] --> CEM1
    CEM1[CEM: Permits Cations, Blocks Anions] --> CON1
    CON1[Concentrate Compartment: Gains Both Ions] --> AEM2
    AEM2[AEM: Permits Anions, Blocks Cations] --> DIL2
    DIL2[Diluate Compartment: Loses Both Ions] --> CEM2
    CEM2[CEM: Permits Cations, Blocks Anions] --> CON2
    CON2[Concentrate Compartment: Gains Both Ions] --> CAT
    CAT[Cathode (-)] -->|Drives cations| CON2
```
