# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Donnan Potential from First Principles of Electrochemistry

### Starting Point
Consider a cation-exchange membrane (CEM) in contact with a NaCl solution of concentration $c$ on side **I** and $c'$ on side **II**. The membrane has fixed anionic sites of concentration $\bar{X}^{-}$.

At thermodynamic equilibrium, the **electrochemical potential** of the Na⁺ ion must be equal on both sides:

$$\tilde{\mu}_{Na^{+}}^{I} = \tilde{\mu}_{Na^{+}}^{II}$$

### Step 1: Expand the electrochemical potential
By definition:

$$\tilde{\mu}_{i} = \mu_{i}^{0} + RT \ln a_{i} + z_{i} F \phi$$

where $a_{i}$ is activity, $z_{i}$ is valence, $\phi$ is electrical potential, and $F$ is Faraday's constant.

### Step 2: Equate the two sides
Substitute into the equilibrium condition:

$$\mu^{0} + RT \ln a_{Na^{+}}^{I} + F\phi^{I} = \mu^{0} + RT \ln a_{Na^{+}}^{II} + F\phi^{II}$$

(The standard chemical potential $\mu^{0}$ cancels because the species is the same on both sides.)

### Step 3: Rearrange to isolate the potential difference
Move the $\phi$ terms to one side and the activity terms to the other:

$$F(\phi^{II} - \phi^{I}) = RT \ln a_{Na^{+}}^{I} - RT \ln a_{Na^{+}}^{II}$$

### Step 4: Recognise the membrane-solution potential difference
Define $E_{Don} = \phi^{II} - \phi^{I}$. The above becomes:

$$E_{Don} = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\!\left(\frac{a_{Na^{+}}^{I}}{a_{Na^{+}}^{II}}\right)$$

### Step 5: Apply electroneutrality and the Donnan product rule
Because NaCl is fully dissociated and the bulk solutions are electrically neutral: $a_{Na^{+}} \approx a_{Cl^{-}} \approx c$ on each side (in dilute limit). Substituting $a_{Na^{+}} = c$ and $a_{Na^{+}}' = c'$:

$$\boxed{\,E_{Don} = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\!\left(\frac{c^{I}}{c^{II}}\right)\,}$$

This is the **Donnan potential** across the interface. The same derivation can be repeated for Cl⁻, which yields an identical $E_{Don}$ (with opposite sign convention), confirming thermodynamic consistency.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hagen–Poiseuille Flux Through a Single Membrane Pore

### Starting Point
A straight cylindrical pore of diameter $d_{p}$ and length $L$ carries a Newtonian fluid of viscosity $\mu$ under a pressure drop $\Delta P$.

### Step 1: Volumetric flow through one pore
The Hagen–Poiseuille equation gives the volumetric flow rate $q$ through a single pore:

$$q = \frac{\pi \, d_{p}^{4} \, \Delta P}{128 \, \mu \, L}$$

### Step 2: Number of pores per unit membrane area
If the surface porosity is $\varepsilon$ (fraction of surface area that is open), then the number of pores per square metre of membrane area $n$ is:

$$n = \frac{4 \varepsilon}{\pi d_{p}^{2}}$$

(This is obtained by setting $\varepsilon = n \cdot (\pi d_{p}^{2}/4)$.)

### Step 3: Total volumetric flow per unit membrane area
Multiply the single-pore flow by the number density of pores:

$$\frac{Q}{A} = n \cdot q = \frac{4 \varepsilon}{\pi d_{p}^{2}} \cdot \frac{\pi d_{p}^{4} \Delta P}{128 \mu L}$$

### Step 4: Simplify
The $\pi$ terms cancel, one $d_{p}^{2}$ cancels in the denominator:

$$\frac{Q}{A} = \frac{4 \varepsilon \, d_{p}^{2} \, \Delta P}{128 \, \mu \, L} = \frac{\varepsilon \, d_{p}^{2} \, \Delta P}{32 \, \mu \, L}$$

### Step 5: Recognise flux
Since flux $J = Q/A$:

$$\boxed{\,J = \frac{\varepsilon \, d_{p}^{2} \, \Delta P}{32 \, \mu \, L}\,}$$

This is the *clean-water* MF flux. The **rejection** $R$ for a solute of Stokes diameter $> d_p$ is essentially 1 (complete rejection), and for much smaller solutes it is ~0.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style 14-Mark Style Sub-Question

> **Problem (Modelled on KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> A dairy plant uses an MF membrane to clarify skimmed milk. Operating data:
> - Membrane area: $A = 5 \text{ m}^2$
> - Average pore diameter: $d_{p} = 0.45 \, \mu m = 0.45 \times 10^{-6}$ m
> - Surface porosity: $\varepsilon = 0.70$
> - Effective pore length: $L = 50 \, \mu m = 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m
> - Transmembrane pressure: $\Delta P = 0.5 \text{ bar} = 5 \times 10^{4}$ Pa
> - Viscosity of milk serum: $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
>
> **Calculate the clean-water permeate flux (LMH) and the daily permeate volume.**

### Step 1: Apply the Hagen–Poiseuille equation
$$J = \frac{\varepsilon \, d_{p}^{2} \, \Delta P}{32 \, \mu \, L}$$

### Step 2: Substitute the numerical values
$$J = \frac{0.70 \times (0.45 \times 10^{-6})^{2} \times 5 \times 10^{4}}{32 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the numerator
- $(0.45 \times 10^{-6})^{2} = 0.2025 \times 10^{-12} = 2.025 \times 10^{-13}$ m²
- Numerator = $0.70 \times 2.025 \times 10^{-13} \times 5 \times 10^{4}$
- = $0.70 \times 1.0125 \times 10^{-8}$
- = $7.0875 \times 10^{-9}$ m/s·m² (numerator)

### Step 4: Evaluate the denominator
- $32 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 50 \times 10^{-6}$
- = $32 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-8}$
- = $1.6 \times 10^{-6}$

### Step 5: Compute the flux
$$J = \frac{7.0875 \times 10^{-9}}{1.6 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.43 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 6: Convert to LMH (litres per m² per hour)
$$J = 4.43 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s} \times 1000 \text{ L/m}^3 \times 3600 \text{ s/h} = 15{,}948 \text{ L/m}^2\text{/h} = 1.59 \times 10^{4} \text{ LMH}$$

> [!WARNING]
> This is **clean-water flux**. In real milk filtration, observed flux will be **5–20 % of this value** due to fat/protein fouling and cake formation. Always state the assumption when writing KTU answers.

### Step 7: Daily permeate volume
$$Q_{p} = J \times A = 4.43 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s} \times 5 \text{ m}^2 = 2.215 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$
$$Q_{p,\text{daily}} = 2.215 \times 10^{-2} \times 86400 = 1913.8 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \approx 1.91 \times 10^{6} \text{ L/day}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Donnan Dialysis Concentration Build-up

> **Problem:**
> A Donnan dialysis cell is used to remove K⁺ from a sugar syrup using a CEM. The receiving compartment contains $0.5 \text{ mol/L}$ HCl. The membrane is $200 \, \mu m$ thick, and the K⁺ permeability is $P_{K^{+}} = 1.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$.
> **Find the initial flux of K⁺ when feed K⁺ concentration is $0.05$ mol/L and receiving concentration is $0.001$ mol/L.**

### Step 1: Apply the flux equation
$$J_{K^{+}} = \frac{P_{K^{+}}}{\delta} \bigl(c_{K^{+}}^{F} - c_{K^{+}}^{R}\bigr)$$

### Step 2: Convert units carefully
- $\delta = 200 \times 10^{-6} = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- $P/\delta = (1.5 \times 10^{-7}) / (2 \times 10^{-4}) = 7.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s
- $\Delta c = 0.05 - 0.001 = 0.049$ mol/L = $0.049 \times 10^{3}$ mol/m³ = $49$ mol/m³

### Step 3: Calculate
$$J_{K^{+}} = 7.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 49 = 0.0368 \text{ mol/(m}^2\text{·s)}$$

### Step 4: Convert to more familiar units
$$J_{K^{+}} = 0.0368 \times 3600 \times 24 = 3177 \text{ mol/(m}^2\text{·day)} \approx 3.18 \text{ kmol/(m}^2\text{·day)}$$

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cross-Flow Microfiltration System (Block-Level Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block renders a **functional process flow** of a typical cross-flow MF system used in a dairy / beverage plant, mapping every unit operation and the direction of the two outlet streams (permeate and retentate).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FEED TANK] --> B[FEED PUMP]
    B --> C[PRESSURE GAUGE INLET]
    C --> D[MICROFILTRATION MODULE]
    D --> E[PRESSURE GAUGE OUTLET]
    E --> F[RETENTATE RECIRCULATION]
    F --> A
    D --> G[PERMEATE COLLECTION TANK]
    H[CLEANING IN PLACE UNIT] --> D
    I[CONTROL VALVE BYPASS] --> F
    J[TEMPERATURE SENSOR] --> A
    K[PRESSURE TRANSMITTER] --> B
```

### Functional Walk-Through
- **FEED TANK** stores the liquid feed (e.g., skimmed milk, fruit juice).
- **FEED PUMP** delivers it at controlled TMP (0.1 – 2 bar).
- **MICROFILTRATION MODULE** houses the membrane (hollow fibre, spiral wound, or ceramic monolith).
- **RETENTATE RECIRCULATION** returns the concentrated retentate back to the feed tank (cross-flow mode).
- **PERMEATE COLLECTION TANK** stores the clarified product.
- **CLEANING IN PLACE UNIT** performs periodic chemical cleaning to recover flux.
- **CONTROL VALVE BYPASS** regulates TMP and cross-flow velocity.

---

## 4.2 Donnan Dialysis Cell — Functional Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagram below shows the two-compartment Donnan dialysis cell, the location of the ion-exchange membrane, the counter-ion flux direction, and the role of fixed charges in the membrane.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph COMPARTMENT_F[FEED COMPARTMENT]
        F1[FEED SOLUTION High counter-ion conc.]
        F2[Co-ions present but rejected]
    end
    subgraph MEMBRANE_ZONE[ION EXCHANGE MEMBRANE]
        M1[FIXED CHARGES attached to polymer matrix]
        M2[Counter-ions permeate]
        M3[Co-ions EXCLUDED by Donnan effect]
    end
    subgraph COMPARTMENT_R[RECEIVING COMPARTMENT]
        R1[RECEIVING SOLUTION Low counter-ion conc.]
        R2[Drives diffusion gradient]
    end
    F1 -- counter-ion flux direction --> M1
    M1 -- counter-ion flux direction --> R1
    M2 --> R2
    F2 -. blocked .-> M3
    R2 --> COMPARTMENT_F
```

### Functional Walk-Through
- The **FEED COMPARTMENT** contains the target counter-ion (e.g., K⁺, Na⁺) to be removed.
- The **ION EXCHANGE MEMBRANE** is sandwiched between compartments; its **FIXED CHARGES** repel co-ions (Donnan exclusion) and let counter-ions pass.
- The **RECEIVING COMPARTMENT** is typically a strip solution (e.g., HCl, H₂SO₄) at low target-ion concentration to maintain the concentration gradient.
- The **counter-ion flux direction** is from feed → membrane → receiving, driven by the concentration gradient (no electric field).
