## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Microfiltration (MF) and Ultrafiltration (UF) membrane processes with respect to (i) nominal pore size, (ii) operating transmembrane pressure, and (iii) the type of species they retain.**

#### Model Answer
(i) **Pore size:** MF membranes have nominal pore diameters in the range **0.1 – 10 µm**, whereas UF membranes are much tighter with pore sizes of **1 – 100 nm** (0.001 – 0.1 µm).
(ii) **TMP:** MF operates at low TMP of **0.1 – 2 bar**; UF requires higher TMP of **1 – 10 bar** because of smaller pores and higher hydraulic resistance.
(iii) **Species retained:** MF retains **suspended solids, bacteria (≈ 0.5 – 5 µm), fat globules, and large colloids**; UF retains **macromolecules** such as proteins, starch, and enzymes, while passing salts and sugars.

> [!TIP]
> Award full 3 marks only if all three sub-parts are explicitly compared in a tabular or contrastive format.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the Donnan exclusion principle. Explain why a cation-exchange membrane (CEM) rejects anions even when the bulk solution has a high anion concentration.**

#### Model Answer
The **Donnan exclusion principle** states that when an ion-exchange membrane carrying **fixed charged groups** is in equilibrium with an external electrolyte solution, the membrane phase will preferentially absorb **counter-ions** (ions of opposite sign to the fixed charge) and **exclude co-ions** (ions of the same sign) to maintain macroscopic electroneutrality.

In a **cation-exchange membrane**, the matrix carries fixed anionic sites (e.g., –SO₃⁻). For a NaCl solution, Na⁺ is the counter-ion and Cl⁻ is the co-ion. To preserve electroneutrality inside the membrane, the membrane must take up an equivalent amount of Na⁺ to balance the fixed –SO₃⁻ groups; the simultaneous uptake of Cl⁻ would create an additional unbalanced negative charge and is therefore thermodynamically rejected. Hence even at high external Cl⁻ concentration, the Cl⁻ inside the membrane is **near zero**, satisfying the Donnan product rule $c_{+}^{m} c_{-}^{m} = c_{+}^{b} c_{-}^{b}$ with $c_{-}^{m} \to 0$.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A [14 Marks]**

**[(a) 7 Marks] Discuss the construction, working principle, and modes of operation of Microfiltration in food processing. What are the common membrane materials used?**

#### Model Solution

**1. Definition and principle (2 marks):** MF is a pressure-driven membrane process that physically separates suspended particles, bacteria, and emulsified droplets from a liquid feed through a porous membrane of nominal pore size **0.1 – 10 µm**. The driving force is the **transmembrane pressure** (0.1 – 2 bar), and the principle is purely **size exclusion (sieving)**.

**2. Membrane construction (2 marks):** MF membranes are typically **asymmetric**, consisting of:
- A thin **top skin** (10 – 50 µm) that performs the actual separation.
- A thicker, highly porous **sub-layer** (100 – 500 µm) that provides mechanical support.
Configurations: **flat sheet, hollow fibre, tubular, spiral-wound, and ceramic monoliths**.

**3. Modes of operation (2 marks):**
- **Dead-end (direct) filtration** — feed is forced perpendicularly through the membrane. Cake builds up rapidly; flux declines with time. Suitable for low-solid feeds (sterile filtration of beverages).
- **Cross-flow (tangential) filtration** — feed flows parallel to the membrane. Tangential shear continually removes the deposited layer, enabling **steady-state flux**. Preferred for high-solid dairy and biotech feeds.

**4. Common membrane materials (1 mark):**
- **Polymeric:** PVDF, PES, PP, PTFE, cellulose acetate.
- **Inorganic / ceramic:** α-Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, TiO₂ (used in dairy for steam-sterilizable systems).

---

**[(b) 7 Marks] Derive the Hagen–Poiseuille equation for clean-water permeate flux through a single cylindrical pore. Using this, estimate the flux (in LMH) for a membrane with pore diameter 0.4 µm, porosity 0.65, pore length 50 µm, TMP 0.4 bar, and water viscosity 10⁻³ Pa·s.**

#### Model Solution

**Step 1:** Volumetric flow rate through one cylindrical pore (Hagen–Poiseuille):

$$q = \frac{\pi \, d_{p}^{4} \, \Delta P}{128 \, \mu \, L}$$

**Step 2:** Number of pores per unit membrane area for a membrane of surface porosity $\varepsilon$:

$$n = \frac{4 \varepsilon}{\pi d_{p}^{2}}$$

**Step 3:** Volumetric flux per unit membrane area:

$$J = n \cdot q = \frac{4 \varepsilon}{\pi d_{p}^{2}} \cdot \frac{\pi d_{p}^{4} \Delta P}{128 \mu L} = \frac{\varepsilon \, d_{p}^{2} \, \Delta P}{32 \, \mu \, L}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the numerical values:
- $d_{p} = 0.4 \times 10^{-6}$ m → $d_{p}^{2} = 1.6 \times 10^{-13}$ m²
- $\varepsilon = 0.65$
- $\Delta P = 0.4 \text{ bar} = 4 \times 10^{4}$ Pa
- $\mu = 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- $L = 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m

**Step 5:** Numerator = $0.65 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-13} \times 4 \times 10^{4} = 4.16 \times 10^{-9}$
**Step 6:** Denominator = $32 \times 10^{-3} \times 50 \times 10^{-6} = 1.6 \times 10^{-6}$
**Step 7:** $J = 4.16 \times 10^{-9} / 1.6 \times 10^{-6} = 2.6 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s

**Step 8:** Convert to LMH:
$$J = 2.6 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 \times 3600 = 9360 \text{ LMH}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by (i) forgetting to convert bar to Pa, (ii) writing pore diameter in µm without squaring correctly, or (iii) omitting the LMH conversion step. Always include unit conversions explicitly.

---

### **Question B [14 Marks] (Alternative Choice)**

**[(a) 7 Marks] Explain Donnan dialysis with a neat sketch. Derive the Donnan potential across a cation-exchange membrane separating two NaCl solutions of concentrations $c_{1}$ and $c_{2}$.**

#### Model Solution

**1. Donnan dialysis — definition (2 marks):** Donnan dialysis is an ion-exchange membrane separation process in which ions are transported across a charged membrane **driven purely by a concentration gradient**, with **no applied electric field**. A cation-exchange membrane allows Na⁺ (counter-ion) to diffuse from the high-concentration side to the low-concentration side, while rejecting Cl⁻ (co-ion). A typical cell consists of two compartments separated by the membrane, with a strip solution on the receiving side.

**2. Derivation of Donnan potential (5 marks):**
- At equilibrium, the electrochemical potential of Na⁺ on either side of the membrane–solution interface must be equal:
  $$\mu^{0} + RT \ln a_{Na^{+}}^{I} + F \phi^{I} = \mu^{0} + RT \ln a_{Na^{+}}^{II} + F \phi^{II}$$
- Rearranging:
  $$F(\phi^{II} - \phi^{I}) = RT \ln \frac{a_{Na^{+}}^{I}}{a_{Na^{+}}^{II}}$$
- In dilute solutions, $a \approx c$:
  $$E_{Don} = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{c_{1}}{c_{2}}$$
- The Donnan product rule (electroneutrality on each side) gives the same result via the Cl⁻ ion, confirming consistency.

---

**[(b) 7 Marks] A Donnan dialysis cell uses a CEM of thickness 250 µm to extract K⁺ from a dairy whey. The permeability of K⁺ is $2.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. Initially the feed K⁺ concentration is 0.04 mol/L and the receiving side is 0.001 mol/L. Estimate (i) the initial flux of K⁺ in mol/m²·h, and (ii) the daily K⁺ transfer for a 2 m² membrane.**

#### Model Solution

**Step 1:** Apply the diffusive flux equation:
$$J_{K^{+}} = \frac{P}{\delta}\,(c_{F} - c_{R})$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $P/\delta$:
- $\delta = 250 \times 10^{-6} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- $P/\delta = (2.0 \times 10^{-7}) / (2.5 \times 10^{-4}) = 8.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s

**Step 3:** Calculate the concentration difference in mol/m³:
- $c_{F} - c_{R} = (0.04 - 0.001) \text{ mol/L} = 0.039 \times 10^{3} = 39 \text{ mol/m}^3$

**Step 4:** Initial flux:
$$J_{K^{+}} = 8.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 39 = 0.0312 \text{ mol/(m}^2\text{·s)}$$

Convert to mol/m²·h:
$$J_{K^{+}} = 0.0312 \times 3600 = 112.3 \text{ mol/(m}^2\text{·h)}$$

**Step 5:** Daily K⁺ transfer for $A = 2$ m²:
$$\text{Daily transfer} = 112.3 \times 2 \times 24 = 5390 \text{ mol/day} \approx 5.39 \text{ kmol/day}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common errors students make in Donnan dialysis numericals:
> 1. Forgetting to convert **mol/L → mol/m³** (multiply by 10³).
> 2. Using membrane thickness in mm without converting to **m**.
> 3. Confusing **Donnan dialysis** (no electric field, concentration-driven) with **electrodialysis** (electric field applied). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this conceptual mix-up.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for the last hour before the KTU exam:**

- [x] **MF pore size range:** 0.1 – 10 µm; **TMP:** 0.1 – 2 bar.
- [x] MF is a **size-exclusion** process, **not** a solution-diffusion process.
- [x] Cross-flow MF is preferred over dead-end for **high-solid feeds** (milk, juice, broths).
- [x] **Darcy's law** ($J = \Delta P / \mu R_{tot}$) is the fundamental flux equation in MF.
- [x] **Hermia's four fouling models** describe flux decline mechanisms: complete blocking, intermediate blocking, standard blocking, cake filtration.
- [x] Clean-water flux through a single pore → **Hagen–Poiseuille:** $J = \varepsilon d_{p}^{2} \Delta P / (32 \mu L)$.
- [x] **Donnan dialysis is concentration-driven** — distinguish it from electrodialysis.
- [x] **CEM** carries fixed **negative** sites (–SO₃⁻, –COO⁻) → passes cations.
- [x] **AEM** carries fixed **positive** sites (–NR₃⁺) → passes anions.
- [x] **Donnan product rule:** $c_{+}^{I} c_{-}^{I} = c_{+}^{II} c_{-}^{II}$ (binary electrolyte).
- [x] **Donnan potential:** $E_{Don} = (RT / z_i F) \ln(c_{i}^{II} / c_{i}^{I})$.
- [x] Flux in Donnan dialysis: $J_i = (P_i / \delta)(c_i^{F} - c_i^{R})$.
- [x] **Food-industry applications of MF:** cold pasteurization of milk, juice clarification, beer/wine filtration, cell harvesting.
- [x] **Food-industry applications of Donnan dialysis:** de-acidification of juices, desalination of whey, ion recovery from dilute streams.
- [x] Remember to convert **bar → Pa** (× 10⁵) and **L → m³** (× 10⁻³) in all MF numericals.
- [x] Remember to convert **mol/L → mol/m³** (× 10³) in all Donnan dialysis flux numericals.
- [x] Common polymeric MF materials: **PVDF, PES, PP, PTFE, cellulose acetate**.
- [x] Common ceramic MF materials: **α-Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, TiO₂**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: External field induced membrane separation processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT527 - SEPARATION PROCESSES IN FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** External field induced membrane separation processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **External Field Induced Membrane Separation Processes** are a class of membrane-based separation techniques in which an **external physical field** (electric, magnetic, centrifugal, or thermal) acts as the **primary driving force** for the selective transport of species across a semi-permeable membrane, supplementing or replacing the conventional **pressure gradient** ($\Delta P$), **concentration gradient** ($\Delta C$), or **chemical potential gradient** ($\Delta \mu$) typically used in classical membrane operations.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEFTT527)** syllabus, these processes belong to the advanced sub-domain of **membrane technology** where the separation mechanism is **non-pressure-driven**. The external field couples directly with the target species (charged ions, magnetically susceptible particles, or temperature-responsive molecules), imparting directed motion across the membrane matrix.

The four major sub-classes recognized under this category are:

| Sub-Process | External Field Applied | Target Species |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrodialysis (ED)** | Electric field ($\mathbf{E}$) | Ions (cations/anions) |
| **Electrophoretic Membrane Separation** | Electric field ($\mathbf{E}$) | Colloids, proteins, macromolecules |
| **Magnetically Assisted Membrane Separation** | Magnetic field ($\mathbf{B}$) | Paramagnetic / ferromagnetic particles |
| **Piezodialysis / Thermodialysis** | Pressure / Temperature field | Mixed electrolytes, solutes |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine a crowded market (a mixture) where you want to let only specific people (target ions) pass through a turnstile gate (membrane) into a VIP zone. In **ordinary membrane filtration**, you simply push the entire crowd with a hydraulic press (pressure). In **external field induced separation**, you instead **flash a spotlight** (electric field / magnetic field) that *illuminates* only people wearing red shirts (charged ions or magnetic particles). The field then *physically pulls or pushes* them through the gate one at a time, leaving the rest behind. This is far more **selective** than brute-force pushing.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

> [!TIP]
> **Key Reference Values (used throughout this module):**
> - **Faraday's Constant** ($F$): **$F = 96{,}485\ \text{C mol}^{-1}$** — links electric charge to moles of ions.
> - **Universal Gas Constant** ($R$): **$R = 8.314\ \text{J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$**.
> - **Permittivity of Free Space** ($\varepsilon_0$): **$\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F m}^{-1}$**.
> - **Standard electric field strength** in industrial ED cells: **$1$–$10\ \text{V cm}^{-1}$**.
> - **Critical current density** ($i_{\text{lim}}$): typically **$10$–$500\ \text{A m}^{-2}$** in food-grade ED stacks.

These constants will appear repeatedly in the formulae and derivations that follow.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step by Step

The operational logic of any **external field induced membrane process** follows a structured sequence. We illustrate the principle using **Electrodialysis (ED)**, the most industrially relevant member of this family for food technology.

### Step 1: Stack Assembly
- A **repeating unit cell** consists of:
  - One **cation-exchange membrane (CEM)** — fixed negative charges ($\text{SO}_3^-$) — permits only **cations** ($\text{Na}^+$, $\text{Ca}^{2+}$, $\text{K}^+$).
  - One **anion-exchange membrane (AEM)** — fixed positive charges ($\text{NR}_4^+$) — permits only **anions** ($\text{Cl}^-$, $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$).
  - Two **spacers / gaskets** forming **diluate** and **concentrate** compartments.

### Step 2: Application of the External Field
- A **direct current (DC) electric field** is applied across the electrodes (anode + / cathode −) at the two ends of the stack.
- Cations migrate toward the **cathode**; anions migrate toward the **anode**.

### Step 3: Selective Permeation
- The **fixed-charge membranes** allow only one ion species to pass at a time.
- Result: in the **diluate compartment** → ion concentration **decreases** (demineralization). In the **concentrate compartment** → ion concentration **increases** (brine formation).

### Step 4: The 'Why' Behind Selectivity
- The **Donnan exclusion principle** governs ion-membrane interaction: co-ions (ions of the same charge as the membrane's fixed group) are electrostatically **repelled** and cannot enter the membrane matrix, ensuring strict charge-selective transport.

## 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility in Food Industry

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in food technology:**
> - **Demineralization of whey** for infant formula production.
> - **Desalination of milk permeate** to recover lactose.
> - **Tartrate stabilization** in wine (removal of $\text{K}^+$ and bitartrate ions).
> - **Reduction of acidity** in fruit juices.
> - **Selective recovery of organic acids** (citric, lactic) from fermentation broths.
> - **Magnetic recovery** of immobilized enzymes or metal-contaminant removal in process water.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the equations below. Every Part B question in this module will lean on at least one of these.**

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Variable Definitions | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nernst–Planck Flux (general) | $J_i = -D_i \dfrac{dC_i}{dx} - \dfrac{z_i F D_i C_i}{RT} \dfrac{d\phi}{dx} + v C_i$ | $J_i$ = flux of species $i$, $D_i$ = diffusivity, $z_i$ = charge, $C_i$ = concentration, $\phi$ = electric potential, $v$ = bulk velocity | $\text{mol m}^{-2}\text{s}^{-1}$, $\text{m}^2\text{s}^{-1}$, V |
| 2 | Faraday's Law of Electrolysis | $n = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{z \cdot F}$ | $n$ = moles transferred, $I$ = current, $t$ = time, $z$ = charge number | mol, A, s |
| 3 | Current Efficiency (CE) / Current Utilization | $\eta = \dfrac{z F N}{n_c I t} \times 100\%$ | $N$ = moles of salt actually transferred, $n_c$ = number of cells, $I$ = current, $t$ = time | % |
| 4 | Limiting Current Density | $i_{\text{lim}} = \dfrac{z F D C}{\delta \left( t^m - t^s \right)}$ | $D$ = diffusivity, $C$ = bulk concentration, $\delta$ = boundary-layer thickness, $t^m$ = ion transport number in membrane, $t^s$ = ion transport number in solution | $\text{A m}^{-2}$ |
| 5 | Ion Transport Number | $t_i = \dfrac{J_i}{\sum_j J_j}$ | $J_i$ = flux of ion $i$ carried by current | dimensionless |
| 6 | Electric Field Strength | $E = \dfrac{\Delta \phi}{L}$ | $\Delta \phi$ = potential difference, $L$ = distance between electrodes | $\text{V m}^{-1}$ |
| 7 | Energy Consumption | $E_{\text{sp}} = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot t}{m_{\text{salt}}}$ | $V$ = voltage, $I$ = current, $t$ = time, $m_{\text{salt}}$ = mass of salt removed | $\text{kWh kg}^{-1}$ |
| 8 | Magnetophoretic Velocity | $v_m = \dfrac{\chi B^2}{2 \mu_0 \eta} \cdot a^2 \cdot f(H)$ | $\chi$ = magnetic susceptibility, $B$ = flux density, $\mu_0$ = permeability of free space, $\eta$ = viscosity, $a$ = particle radius | $\text{m s}^{-1}$, T |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiners' Pet Trick:** Equation 4 (Limiting Current Density) is the most frequently tested formula in KTU exams. Memorize the **boundary-layer interpretation**: $i_{\text{lim}}$ is reached when the ion concentration at the membrane-solution interface drops to **zero** — a condition called *concentration polarization*.

## 2.4 Common Boundary Conditions Used in KTU Problems

- At the **membrane surface (boundary layer interface)**: $C_{i}(x=0) = C_{i,\text{bulk}}$ (in the bulk) and $C_{i}(x=\delta) \to 0$ (limiting condition).
- **Electroneutrality** in the bulk: $\sum_i z_i C_i = 0$.
- **No flux** of co-ions through an ideal ion-exchange membrane: $J_{\text{co-ion}} = 0$ (Donnan condition).
- **Iso-thermal** assumption in thermodialysis: $T = \text{constant}$ in $x$, but $\dfrac{dT}{dy} \neq 0$ across the membrane.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A: The Nernst–Planck Equation (Starting from First Principles)

> The Nernst–Planck equation is the **cornerstone** of all external field induced membrane transport. We derive it from the general transport equation for species $i$ in an electric field.

### Step 1: Start with the Molar Flux Expression for a Single Species
The total molar flux $J_i$ of ionic species $i$ through a membrane is the sum of three contributions:

$$
J_i = J_{\text{diff}} + J_{\text{migr}} + J_{\text{conv}}
$$

**Reasoning:** Diffusion arises from the concentration gradient; migration arises from the electric potential gradient acting on a charged species; convection arises from bulk solvent flow.

### Step 2: Write Fick's First Law for the Diffusive Term
For the diffusive component (Fick's law):

$$
J_{\text{diff}} = -D_i \, \dfrac{dC_i}{dx}
$$

**Reasoning:** Flux is proportional to the negative concentration gradient, with $D_i$ as the proportionality constant (the Fickian diffusivity).

### Step 3: Write the Migrative Term Using the Electrochemical Potential Gradient
The driving force for an ion in an electric field is the gradient of its **electrochemical potential** $\tilde{\mu}_i$:

$$
\tilde{\mu}_i = \mu_i^0 + RT \ln(C_i) + z_i F \phi
$$

The flux due to migration is:

$$
J_{\text{migr}} = -u_i \, C_i \, \dfrac{d\tilde{\mu}_i}{dx}
$$

where $u_i$ is the ionic mobility.

### Step 4: Substitute the Electrochemical Potential Expression
Expanding the gradient:

$$
\dfrac{d\tilde{\mu}_i}{dx} = \dfrac{RT}{C_i} \dfrac{dC_i}{dx} + z_i F \, \dfrac{d\phi}{dx}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
J_{\text{migr}} = -u_i \, C_i \left( \dfrac{RT}{C_i} \dfrac{dC_i}{dx} + z_i F \, \dfrac{d\phi}{dx} \right)
$$

$$
J_{\text{migr}} = -u_i \, RT \, \dfrac{dC_i}{dx} - u_i \, z_i \, F \, C_i \, \dfrac{d\phi}{dx}
$$

**Reasoning:** Two terms emerge — a diffusion-like term (concentration gradient) and a true migration term (electric field driven).

### Step 5: Apply the Nernst–Einstein Relation to Eliminate Mobility
The Nernst–Einstein relation links diffusivity $D_i$ to ionic mobility $u_i$:

$$
D_i = u_i \, RT
$$

Substituting $u_i = D_i / RT$ into the migration term:

$$
J_{\text{migr}} = -D_i \, \dfrac{dC_i}{dx} - \dfrac{z_i F D_i C_i}{RT} \, \dfrac{d\phi}{dx}
$$

### Step 6: Add the Convective Term
If there is bulk solvent flow with velocity $v$:

$$
J_{\text{conv}} = v \, C_i
$$

### Step 7: Combine All Three Contributions
Summing up the diffusive, migrative, and convective fluxes:

$$
J_i = -D_i \, \dfrac{dC_i}{dx} - \dfrac{z_i F D_i C_i}{RT} \, \dfrac{d\phi}{dx} + v \, C_i
$$

This is the **Nernst–Planck equation**, the master transport law for **electrodialysis, electrophoresis, and any electric-field-driven membrane process**. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation is examinable:** It explicitly links **Fick's law + Einstein relation + electrochemical potential** — three cornerstone concepts. Examiners love testing whether students can assemble these independently.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B: Limiting Current Density $i_{\text{lim}}$

### Step 1: Mass Balance Across the Boundary Layer
At steady state, the flux of ions through the membrane equals the flux arriving at the membrane surface from the bulk solution. The flux arriving is limited by diffusion across the stagnant boundary layer of thickness $\delta$:

$$
J_i = -D_i \, \dfrac{dC_i}{dx} \bigg|_{\text{boundary layer}}
$$

### Step 2: Solve the Linear Concentration Profile
Assuming a linear concentration profile across the boundary layer (standard assumption for a thin stagnant film):

$$
J_i = \dfrac{D_i}{\delta} \left( C_i^b - C_i^m \right)
$$

where $C_i^b$ = bulk concentration and $C_i^m$ = concentration at the membrane surface.

### Step 3: Apply the Limiting Condition
At the **limiting current density**, the ion concentration at the membrane surface drops to **zero** ($C_i^m \to 0$):

$$
J_{i,\text{lim}} = \dfrac{D_i \, C_i^b}{\delta}
$$

### Step 4: Convert Flux to Current Density Using Faraday's Law
The current density $i$ carried by species $i$ is related to its flux via Faraday's law:

$$
i = z_i F J_i
$$

Therefore, at the limiting condition:

$$
i_{\text{lim}} = \dfrac{z_i F D_i C_i^b}{\delta}
$$

### Step 5: Extend to Real Membranes Using Transport Numbers
In practice, the membrane itself contributes to ion transport. The true limiting current density in an ED cell, accounting for transport numbers in membrane ($t^m$) and solution ($t^s$), becomes:

$$
i_{\text{lim}} = \dfrac{z_i F D_i C_i}{\delta \left( t^m - t^s \right)}
$$

This is the **operational limiting current density** used in KTU problems. $\blacksquare$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** $(t^m - t^s)$ is the **selectivity enhancement** due to the membrane. If the membrane perfectly excludes co-ions, $t^m \to 1$ and the denominator shrinks, allowing high current density with low energy penalty.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C: Current Efficiency in Electrodialysis

### Step 1: Theoretical Salt Transfer (Faraday's Law)
For a single cell pair under current $I$ for time $t$:

$$
m_{\text{theoretical}} = \dfrac{I \, t}{F} \quad \text{(moles of monovalent salt)}
$$

### Step 2: Actual Salt Transfer from Mass Balance
Measured from concentration change in concentrate stream:

$$
m_{\text{actual}} = V \left( C_{\text{conc,out}} - C_{\text{conc,in}} \right)
$$

where $V$ is the volume of concentrate flow and $C$ are concentrations.

### Step 3: Define Current Efficiency
The ratio of actual to theoretical transfer:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} = \dfrac{V \left( C_{\text{out}} - C_{\text{in}} \right) F}{I \, t}
$$

For an ED stack with $n_c$ cell pairs, the equation generalizes to:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{z \, F \, N}{n_c \, I \, t}
$$

where $N$ = total moles of salt transferred. $\blacksquare$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `n1`, `n2`). No reserved keywords are used as standalone node names. No markdown formatting is embedded inside node labels. All labels use clean uppercase alphanumeric text.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Electrodialysis Stack Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    n1[DILUTE FEED TANK - RAW SALT SOLUTION]:::blue
    n2[ANODE POSITIVE ELECTRODE]:::red
    n3[AEM ANION EXCHANGE MEMBRANE]:::yellow
    n4[SPACER DILUTE COMPARTMENT]:::green
    n5[CEM CATION EXCHANGE MEMBRANE]:::yellow
    n6[SPACER CONCENTRATE COMPARTMENT]:::orange
    n7[CATHODE NEGATIVE ELECTRODE]:::red
    n8[CONCENTRATE TANK - BRINE RECOVERY]:::orange
    n9[DC POWER SUPPLY EXTERNAL FIELD]:::purple
    n10[CONTROL AND MONITORING UNIT]:::blue

    n9 -->|APPLIES DC VOLTAGE| n2
    n9 -->|CLOSES CIRCUIT| n7
    n2 --- n3
    n3 --- n4
    n4 --- n5
    n5 --- n6
    n6 --- n7
    n1 --> n4
    n4 --> n8
    n10 -.->|MONITORS VOLTAGE CURRENT| n9
    n10 -.->|MEASURES pH CONDUCTIVITY| n4
    n10 -.->|MEASURES pH CONDUCTIVITY| n6

    classDef blue fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef red fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000,color:#000
    classDef yellow fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#666600,color:#000
    classDef green fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#003300,color:#000
    classDef orange fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#663300,color:#000
    classDef purple fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#330066,color:#000
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The **external field (DC power supply)** drives the entire process — without it, no migration occurs.
- **AEM** lets anions pass; **CEM** lets cations pass; together they create alternating **dilute** and **concentrate** compartments.
- The **monitoring unit** enforces operation **below the limiting current density** to prevent water dissociation.

## 4.2 Process Flow Architecture — Generic External Field Membrane System

```mermaid
graph TB
    sA[FEED STREAM MIXTURE OF TARGET AND NON-TARGET SPECIES]:::inlet
    sB[PRE-FILTRATION UNIT REMOVES SUSPENDED SOLIDS]:::pretreat
    sC[PUMP AND FLOW DISTRIBUTION NETWORK]:::pretreat
    sD[EXTERNAL FIELD GENERATOR DC OR MAGNETIC OR THERMAL]:::field
    sE[MEMBRANE MODULE CONTAINS SELECTIVE ION EXCHANGE OR MAGNETIC MEMBRANE]:::core
    sF[PERMEATE STREAM ENRICHED IN TARGET SPECIES]:::prod
    sG[RETENTATE STREAM DEPLETED IN TARGET SPECIES]:::waste
    sH[POST-TREATMENT pH ADJUSTMENT AND POLISHING]:::post
    sI[QUALITY CONTROL ANALYTICAL LABORATORY]:::qc
    sJ[FINAL PRODUCT STORAGE]:::storage
    sK[WASTE BRINE MANAGEMENT AND RECOVERY]:::waste

    sA --> sB
    sB --> sC
    sC --> sE
    sD -->|INDUCES DRIVING FORCE| sE
    sE --> sF
    sE --> sG
    sF --> sH
    sH --> sI
    sI --> sJ
    sG --> sK
    sI -.->|FEEDBACK CONTROL SIGNAL| sD

    classDef inlet fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000
    classDef pretreat fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    classDef field fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,color:#000
    classDef prod fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    classDef waste fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000
    classDef post fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000
    classDef qc fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d79b00,color:#000
    classDef storage fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#666666,color:#000
```

**Key takeaways from the architecture:**
- The **feedback loop** from QC to the field generator enables **adaptive control** of field strength — critical for staying below $i_{\text{lim}}$.
- **Pre-filtration** is mandatory: particulates foul the membrane surface and disrupt the uniform field distribution.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 1, Dec 2023, Module 2]**
> **Q1.** *Define external field induced membrane separation. List the four major types of external fields used in such processes.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** External field induced membrane separation processes are membrane-based techniques in which an external physical field — electric, magnetic, centrifugal, or thermal — is the **primary driving force** for selective transport of species across a semi-permeable membrane, replacing or supplementing conventional pressure-driven mechanisms.
- **List of four external fields (1 mark):** (i) **Electric field** (electrodialysis, electrophoresis), (ii) **Magnetic field** (magnetically assisted separation), (iii) **Thermal field** (thermodialysis, membrane distillation variants), (iv) **Centrifugal field** (centrifugal membrane separation).

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2, May 2024, Module 2]**
> **Q2.** *State the Nernst-Planck equation and explain the physical significance of each term.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Equation (1.5 marks):**

$$
J_i = -D_i \dfrac{dC_i}{dx} - \dfrac{z_i F D_i C_i}{RT} \dfrac{d\phi}{dx} + v C_i
$$

- **Physical significance of each term (1.5 marks):**
  - **First term** $\left(-D_i \dfrac{dC_i}{dx}\right)$: **Fickian diffusion** due to concentration gradient.
  - **Second term** $\left(-\dfrac{z_i F D_i C_i}{RT} \dfrac{d\phi}{dx}\right)$: **Electromigration** — the electric field pushes the charged species across the membrane.
  - **Third term** $\left(v C_i\right)$: **Convective transport** due to bulk solvent flow.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, ESE Module 2, Q9, 14 Marks]**
> **Q3 (A).** *(a) Describe the principle, construction, and working of an electrodialysis plant used in the food industry. Discuss the role of cation- and anion-exchange membranes with the help of a neat sketch.* **(7 marks)**
> *(b) Derive the Nernst-Planck equation for ionic transport across a membrane and explain its application in electrodialysis.* **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question 3(A) (a)

**Principle (2 marks):**
- Electrodialysis separates ionic species from a solution using **ion-exchange membranes** under the influence of a **DC electric field**.
- Cations migrate toward the cathode; anions migrate toward the anode.
- Selective membranes allow only one ion type to pass, creating alternating **diluate** and **concentrate** streams.

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Electrode compartments:** anode (Pt-coated Ti or graphite) and cathode (stainless steel).
- **Membrane stack:** hundreds of alternating CEM and AEM sheets separated by **spacers** (typically 0.5–2 mm thick).
- **Spacers:** create turbulence, support the membrane, and define flow channels.
- **Inlet/Outlet manifolds:** distribute feed, diluate, and concentrate flows uniformly.
- **DC power supply:** typically 1–10 V per cell pair, current 10–500 A.

**Working (2 marks):**
- Feed solution enters the **diluate compartments**.
- Under the electric field, cations pass through CEM into adjacent concentrate streams; anions pass through AEM.
- The diluate stream exits **depleted of ions** (e.g., demineralized whey); the concentrate stream exits **enriched in salts** (brine).

**Role of Membranes:**
- **CEM (Cation Exchange Membrane):** Contains fixed negative groups ($\text{SO}_3^-$). Repels anions; transports cations.
- **AEM (Anion Exchange Membrane):** Contains fixed positive groups ($\text{NR}_4^+$). Repels cations; transports anions.
- This charge selectivity arises from the **Donnan exclusion principle**.

### Model Solution — Question 3(A) (b)

The full derivation is given in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The student must reproduce all 7 steps, ending with the final Nernst-Planck equation. The examiner expects the student to:

1. Start with the **three flux contributions**.
2. Write **Fick's law** for diffusion.
3. Write the **electrochemical potential gradient** for migration.
4. Use the **Nernst–Einstein relation** ($D_i = u_i RT$) to link mobility and diffusivity.
5. Add the **convective term**.
6. Combine to obtain the **final Nernst-Planck equation**.

**Application in electrodialysis (1 mark):**
- The Nernst-Planck equation predicts the **ionic flux** through each membrane under a given electric field, allowing engineers to size stacks, estimate desalting rates, and determine **current efficiency** and **energy consumption** per kg of salt removed.

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, ESE Module 2, Q10, 14 Marks]**
> **Q3 (B).** *(a) Explain the concept of limiting current density in electrodialysis. Derive an expression for $i_{\text{lim}}$ and discuss its significance in plant design.* **(7 marks)**
> *(b) With a neat block diagram, describe the various external field induced membrane separation processes used in food technology. State at least two industrial applications of each.* **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question 3(B) (a)

**Concept (2 marks):**
- The **limiting current density** ($i_{\text{lim}}$) is the maximum sustainable current per unit membrane area, beyond which **water dissociation** occurs at the membrane-solution interface, causing pH shifts, scale formation, and membrane damage.
- Physically, $i_{\text{lim}}$ corresponds to the condition where ion concentration at the membrane surface drops to **zero** (concentration polarization).

**Derivation (3 marks):**
The full derivation is in **Section 3.2** of these notes. The student should reproduce Steps 1–5, ending with:

$$
i_{\text{lim}} = \dfrac{z_i F D_i C_i}{\delta \left( t^m - t^s \right)}
$$

**Significance in plant design (2 marks):**
- **Stack sizing:** Operating current must remain **below $i_{\text{lim}}$** to prevent water splitting; this sets the maximum membrane area.
- **Flow velocity:** Higher flow thins the boundary layer ($\delta \downarrow$), raising $i_{\text{lim}}$.
- **Concentration:** $i_{\text{lim}}$ scales with bulk concentration — as the diluate becomes more dilute, the allowable current drops.
- **Energy optimization:** Operating near 70–80% of $i_{\text{lim}}$ gives optimal trade-off between throughput and energy efficiency.

### Model Solution — Question 3(B) (b)

**Block diagram (3 marks):**
The student should reproduce the **generic external field membrane system** flowchart from **Section 4.2** of these notes, showing: feed → pre-treatment → membrane module (with external field generator) → permeate + retentate → post-treatment → QC → product/waste.

**Processes & applications (4 marks):**

| Process | External Field | Industrial Applications in Food Tech |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrodialysis (ED)** | Electric field | (1) Whey demineralization for infant formula; (2) Tartrate stabilization of wine |
| **Electrophoretic Membrane Separation** | Electric field | (1) Protein fractionation from milk; (2) Recovery of enzymes from fermentation broth |
| **Magnetically Assisted Membrane Separation** | Magnetic field | (1) Removal of iron contaminants from process water; (2) Recovery of immobilized magnetic enzyme carriers |
| **Thermodialysis** | Thermal gradient | (1) Concentration of fruit juices; (2) Separation of volatile flavor compounds |
| **Piezodialysis** | Pressure + electric field | (1) Salt-acid separation in pickling brines; (2) Recovery of valuable organic acids |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up $i_{\text{lim}}$ and $i_{\text{operational}}$:** Many students forget the transport-number denominator $(t^m - t^s)$ in the formula. Marks are deducted if this term is missing.
> 2. **Confusing flux direction in Nernst-Planck:** The migrative term is sometimes written with the **wrong sign** — the negative sign is critical because cations move toward the *cathode* (negative potential), so the sign of $z_i F$ and $\dfrac{d\phi}{dx}$ must combine correctly.
> 3. **Forgetting Donnan exclusion:** When asked about ion selectivity, students often state "the membrane allows ions through" without invoking **Donnan exclusion**, which is the *fundamental* reason for selectivity.
> 4. **Units mismatch:** Forgetting to convert $\delta$ from $\mu\text{m}$ to $\text{m}$ when computing $i_{\text{lim}}$ is a very common error. Always show unit conversions in the calculation.
> 5. **No sketch in 7-mark sub-parts:** Even when not explicitly demanded, examiners *expect* a labelled diagram for electrodialysis questions. Skipping it can cost 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ☐ **Definition:** External field induced membrane separation uses an **external field (E, B, T)** as the *primary* driving force, *not* pressure.
- ☐ **Four sub-classes:** **E**lectrodialysis, **E**lectrophoresis, **M**agnetic separation, **T**hermodialysis (mnemonic: **EEMT**).
- ☐ **Master equation:** Nernst-Planck $J_i = -D_i \dfrac{dC_i}{dx} - \dfrac{z_i F D_i C_i}{RT} \dfrac{d\phi}{dx} + v C_i$ — three terms: **diffusion + migration + convection**.
- ☐ **Limiting current density formula:** $i_{\text{lim}} = \dfrac{z_i F D_i C_i}{\delta (t^m - t^s)}$ — derived from boundary-layer theory.
- ☐ **Faraday's constant** $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C mol}^{-1}$ — use it for all current-mole conversions.
- ☐ **Current efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{z F N}{n_c I t}$ — never forget $n_c$ for multi-cell stacks.
- ☐ **Donnan exclusion** is the reason ion-exchange membranes are **charge-selective**.
- ☐ **CEM** has fixed **negative** groups ($\text{SO}_3^-$); **AEM** has fixed **positive** groups ($\text{NR}_4^+$).
- ☐ **Limiting condition:** $C^m \to 0$ at membrane surface → water dissociation → pH shift → membrane damage.
- ☐ **Industrial food applications:** whey demineralization, wine tartrate stabilization, juice deacidification, lactose desalting, organic acid recovery.
- ☐ **Magnetic field application:** Used for **paramagnetic/ferromagnetic** particles, NOT directly for dissolved ions.
- ☐ **Donnan potential** at the membrane-solution interface: $\phi_D = \dfrac{RT}{z_i F} \ln \dfrac{C_i^m}{C_i^b}$.
- ☐ **Energy consumption** $E_{\text{sp}}$ in $\text{kWh/kg salt}$ is the **key performance metric** for plant economics.
- ☐ **Pre-filtration is mandatory** — particulates distort the field and foul the membrane.
- ☐ **Operating rule of thumb:** Operate at **70–80% of $i_{\text{lim}}$** for optimal energy-throughput trade-off.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications in food processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT527 - SEPARATION PROCESSES IN FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications in food processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Separation Process Applications in Food Processing

In the context of **PEFTT527 – Separation Processes in Food Technology**, the term **"Applications in food processing"** refers to the systematic deployment of unit operations — physical, physico-chemical, and membrane-based — for the purpose of isolating, purifying, concentrating, fractionating, or selectively removing specific components from complex food matrices. These matrices are typically multi-phase, thermally sensitive, and biologically active systems (e.g., milk, fruit juice, vegetable oil, sugar cane juice, fermentation broths).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 2 Focus):**
> *Separation processes in food technology encompass a range of operations — including membrane separation (UF, NF, RO), supercritical fluid extraction (SCFE), adsorption, ion-exchange, chromatography, and crystallization — applied to upgrade raw agricultural produce into stable, safe, and value-added food products while preserving nutritional and sensory quality.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Sieve" Metaphor

Imagine a **professional chef** in a busy kitchen who has a large pot of mixed vegetable soup. She needs to:
- Remove the large chunks of vegetables (**sieving / screening**).
- Separate the dissolved salt from water (**reverse osmosis / evaporation**).
- Pull out the cream floating on milk (**centrifugation**).
- Capture the aromatic oils from spices without boiling them (**supercritical CO₂ extraction**).

Each tool in her kitchen corresponds to an **industrial separation process**. The "soup" is the **feed**, the "tool" is the **separator**, and the "cleaned soup" is the **permeate/product stream**. The food engineer simply scales up these everyday operations to **industrial capacities** while preserving the delicate bioactive compounds (vitamins, polyphenols, essential oils) that are destroyed by harsh chemical or thermal treatments.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Modern food separation processes are chosen based on three pillars:
> 1. **Selectivity** — does it pick the *right* molecule?
> 2. **Throughput** — can it handle industrial volumes (kg/h or tonnes/day)?
> 3. **Bio-compatibility** — does it preserve heat-labile nutrients?

---

## 1.3 Why Food Industry Demands Specialized Separation

Food materials differ from petrochemical or pharmaceutical feedstocks in three critical ways:

| Property | Food Matrix | Consequence for Separation |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermal sensitivity** | Most vitamins, enzymes, and pigments degrade above 60–80 °C | Favors non-thermal methods (membranes, SCFE) |
| **Biological activity** | Live cultures, active proteins | Requires gentle pH and shear conditions |
| **Regulatory compliance** | Must meet FSSAI / Codex / FDA purity standards | Selectivity and hygiene are non-negotiable |

These constraints force food technologists to prioritize **membrane processes, supercritical extraction, simulated moving bed (SMB) chromatography, and freeze concentration** over traditional high-temperature distillation wherever possible.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants Used in Food Separation

The following standard metrics are universally applied in the KTU 2024 scheme for evaluating any food separation process:

- **Selectivity (α)** — dimensionless; ratio of transmission rates of two species.
- **Permeate flux (J)** — typically **L/m²·h (LMH)** for membrane processes; **kg/m²·s** for filtration.
- **Retention/Rejection (R)** — dimensionless, expressed as a percentage; **0 %** means full passage, **100 %** means full rejection.
- **Concentration factor (CF)** — dimensionless ratio of feed solute concentration to retentate concentration.
- **Yield (η)** — expressed as a percentage of theoretical maximum recovery.
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** **1.013 × 10⁵ Pa** (used as reference in pressure-driven membrane calculations).
- **Standard temperatures:** **298 K (25 °C)** for ambient operations; **277 K (4 °C)** for cold-chain dairy processing.

> [!NOTE]
> These metrics are **CO-mapped** to the KTU 2024 outcome *CO2: Apply the principles of separation processes to solve food engineering problems*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Separation Processes in Food Industry

Food separation processes can be classified along multiple axes. The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 2) emphasizes the following five families:

1. **Mechanical / Physical separations** — centrifugation, sedimentation, filtration, sieving.
2. **Membrane-based separations** — microfiltration (MF), ultrafiltration (UF), nanofiltration (NF), reverse osmosis (RO), electrodialysis (ED).
3. **Extractive separations** — liquid–liquid extraction, supercritical fluid extraction (SCFE).
4. **Diffusion / Sorption-based** — adsorption, ion-exchange, chromatography (SMB, HPLC prep).
5. **Phase-change separations** — evaporation, crystallization, freeze concentration, distillation.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic of Major Food Separation Techniques

### 2.2.1 Membrane Separation (UF / NF / RO)
- **Driving force:** Trans-membrane pressure (ΔP), typically **0.1 – 6.0 MPa**.
- **Mechanism:** Size exclusion (UF/NF) and solution–diffusion (RO).
- **Why it matters:** Operates at ambient temperature → preserves flavor, color, nutrients.
- **KTU-relevant applications:** Whey protein concentration, lactose removal, fruit juice clarification, sugar juice concentration, potable water from whey.

### 2.2.2 Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SCFE)
- **Working fluid:** Almost always **CO₂** (critical point: **T_c = 304.25 K, P_c = 7.38 MPa**).
- **Why CO₂:** Non-toxic, GRAS (Generally Recognized As Safe), easily recovered by depressurization, leaves **zero solvent residue**.
- **KTU-relevant applications:** Decaffeination of coffee/tea, hop extract for beer, essential oils (oleoresin), spice oleoresins, removal of cholesterol from butterfat.

### 2.2.3 Centrifugation
- **Driving force:** Centrifugal acceleration ω²r (g-force).
- **Why it matters:** Continuous separation of immiscible liquids (cream from milk) and solids (yeast from beer, crystals from mother liquor).
- **Key equation:** Stokes' law under centrifugal field:  

$$v_{s} = \frac{d^{2}\,(\rho_{p}-\rho_{f})\,\omega^{2}\,r}{18\,\mu}$$

where **d** = particle diameter, **ρₚ** = particle density, **ρ_f** = fluid density, **ω** = angular velocity, **r** = radial distance, **μ** = viscosity.

### 2.2.4 Evaporation & Multiple-Effect Evaporators
- **Driving force:** Latent heat of vaporization (water: **2,260 kJ/kg at 100 °C**).
- **Why it matters:** Concentration of liquid foods (tomato paste, milk, fruit purees).
- **Energy optimization:** Multiple-effect evaporator (n-effect) reduces steam consumption by factor n.

### 2.2.5 Crystallization
- **Driving force:** Supersaturation (ΔC = C − C*).
- **Why it matters:** Production of sugar crystals, salt, ice in freeze-concentration.
- **Industrial form:** Vacuum pan crystallizers, continuous cooling crystallizers.

### 2.2.6 Adsorption / Ion-Exchange
- **Driving force:** Affinity of solute to a solid surface or resin.
- **Why it matters:** Decolorization of sugar syrup, demineralization of whey, removal of bitter compounds (naringin from grapefruit juice).

### 2.2.7 Chromatography (SMB — Simulated Moving Bed)
- **Driving force:** Differential partitioning between stationary and mobile phases.
- **Why it matters:** Separation of sugars (glucose/fructose), isomer separation, fractionation of tocopherols.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2**

| # | Process | Governing Equation | Variables / Units | Boundary / Limiting Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Membrane flux (Darcy's law) | $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \, R_{m}}$ | J in m³/m²·s; ΔP in Pa; μ in Pa·s; Rₘ in m⁻¹ | Valid for laminar flow through membrane |
| 2 | Rejection coefficient | $R = 1 - \dfrac{C_{p}}{C_{f}}$ | Cₚ permeate, C_f feed concentration (kg/m³) | R → 0 for free solute, R → 1 for fully rejected |
| 3 | Concentration factor | $CF = \dfrac{C_{r}}{C_{f}}$ | C_r retentate, C_f feed | CF > 1 for concentration process |
| 4 | Yield | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{recovered}}{m_{initial}} \times 100\%$ | Mass in kg | 0 < η ≤ 100 % |
| 5 | Selectivity (binary) | $\alpha_{A/B} = \dfrac{y_{A}/y_{B}}{x_{A}/x_{B}}$ | y, x = mole fractions | α = 1 → no separation |
| 6 | Stokes settling (gravity) | $v_{s} = \dfrac{d^{2}\,g\,(\rho_{p}-\rho_{f})}{18\,\mu}$ | d (m), g = **9.81 m/s²**, ρ (kg/m³), μ (Pa·s) | Re_p < 1 (laminar) |
| 7 | Stokes settling (centrifuge) | $v_{s} = \dfrac{d^{2}\,\omega^{2}\,r\,(\rho_{p}-\rho_{f})}{18\,\mu}$ | ω in rad/s, r in m | Re_p < 1 |
| 8 | Mass balance (single-stage) | $F = P + R$ | Mass flow rates kg/s | Steady state |
| 9 | Component balance | $F \, x_{F} = P \, x_{P} + R \, x_{R}$ | Mass fractions | Steady state |
| 10 | Mass ratio / cut fraction | $\theta = \dfrac{P}{F}$ | Dimensionless | 0 ≤ θ ≤ 1 |
| 11 | Multiple-effect economy | $E = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{steam}} \approx n$ | Dimensionless | n = number of effects |
| 12 | Crystallization yield | $Y = \dfrac{C_{0} - C_{f}}{C_{0} - C^{*}} \times 100\%$ | Concentration kg solute/kg solvent | C_f > C* (supersaturated) |
| 13 | SCFE solubility (empirical Chrastil) | $c = \rho^{\,k} \, \exp\!\left(\dfrac{a}{T} + b\right)$ | c in kg/m³, ρ in kg/m³, T in K, a, b, k empirical | 0.7 < k < 1.4 typical for CO₂ |
| 14 | Distillate (Rayleigh) | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{F}{W}\right) = \int_{x_{W}}^{x_{F}} \dfrac{dx}{y-x}$ | x, y mole fractions | Differential distillation |
| 15 | Membrane log-mean ΔC | $\Delta C_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta C_{1} - \Delta C_{2}}{\ln(\Delta C_{1}/\Delta C_{2})}$ | Concentration differences | ΔC₁ ≠ ΔC₂ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Units Pitfall:** Always express concentrations in **kg/m³** or **mol/m³** consistently across a calculation. Mixing units (e.g., g/L with kg/m³) is a guaranteed mark deduction in KTU valuation.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

| Application | Preferred Separation | Industrial Benefit |
|---|---|---|
| Whey protein isolate (WPI) | UF + RO | Yields **>90 %** protein purity from cheese whey |
| Fruit juice clarification | UF + NF | Removes haze-forming pectins, **cold-process** |
| Lactose-free milk | UF + enzymatic + chromatographic | Lactose < 0.01 % (regulatory limit) |
| Olive oil refining | Centrifugation + SCFE | Vitamin E retention, **solvent-free** |
| Decaffeinated coffee | SCFE with CO₂ | **>99.9 %** caffeine removal, no toxic residue |
| Beet sugar refining | Carbonatation + ion-exchange + evaporation + crystallization | White sugar of ICUMSA < 45 |
| Grape juice (dealcoholized wine) | RO + osmotic distillation | Alcohol < 0.5 % ABV |
| Edible oil degumming | Membrane (NF) + centrifugation | Lower chemical usage |
| Pectin extraction | Acid extraction + UF concentration | High-methoxyl pectin for jams |
| Tea polyphenols (catechins) | SCFE + SMB chromatography | **>90 %** EGCG purity |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Membrane Permeate Flux from Darcy's Law

**Objective:** Derive the steady-state volumetric flux of a solvent (typically water) through a porous membrane under a pressure difference, starting from the **Hagen–Poiseuille flow** in a single cylindrical pore.

### Step 1 — Single-pore velocity (Hagen–Poiseuille)

For a cylindrical pore of radius **r_p** and length **L**, the average velocity of the fluid inside the pore is given by the well-known result:

$$v_{avg} = \frac{r_{p}^{\,2}\,\Delta P}{8\,\mu\,L}$$

**Logic:** This equation arises from integrating the parabolic velocity profile across the pore cross-section, with no-slip at the wall and a constant pressure gradient along the pore length.

### Step 2 — Total volumetric flow per pore

Multiply the velocity by the cross-sectional area of the pore (π r_p²):

$$q_{pore} = v_{avg}\cdot \pi r_{p}^{\,2} = \frac{\pi \, r_{p}^{\,4}\,\Delta P}{8\,\mu\,L}$$

**Logic:** The flow rate is proportional to the **fourth power of the pore radius**, which explains why even a small increase in pore size dramatically increases flux.

### Step 3 — Number of pores per unit membrane area

Let **N_p** be the number of pores per unit membrane area A. Then total volumetric flow per unit area is:

$$J = \frac{Q}{A} = N_{p} \cdot q_{pore} = \frac{N_{p}\,\pi\,r_{p}^{\,4}\,\Delta P}{8\,\mu\,L}$$

**Logic:** Dividing by total area gives the superficial flux, which is what is measured experimentally on a flat-sheet or hollow-fiber module.

### Step 4 — Group into membrane resistance

Define the **membrane hydraulic resistance** as:

$$R_{m} = \frac{8\,L}{N_{p}\,\pi\,r_{p}^{\,4}}$$

**Logic:** This is purely a geometric property of the membrane (pore density, radius, length). It encapsulates every structural detail in a single parameter.

### Step 5 — Final Darcy form

Substituting the definition of R_m, the flux becomes:

$$J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu\,R_{m}}$$

**Logic:** This is the classical **Darcy-style equation** for membrane flux. It is the foundation for the **resistance-in-series** model:

$$J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu\,(R_{m} + R_{f})}$$

where R_f is the fouling resistance, which grows over time and must be controlled by periodic backwashing in food plant operations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Material Balance for a Single-Stage Membrane Separator

**Given:** A feed stream **F** (kg/h) with solute mass fraction **x_F** enters a membrane module producing a permeate **P** (kg/h, fraction **x_P**) and a retentate **R** (kg/h, fraction **x_R**).

### Step 1 — Total mass balance (conservation of mass)

$$F = P + R$$

**Logic:** Whatever enters the system must leave as either permeate or retentate, under steady state.

### Step 2 — Solute (component) mass balance

$$F \, x_{F} = P \, x_{P} + R \, x_{R}$$

**Logic:** The amount of solute entering the system is distributed between the two exit streams. No solute is created or destroyed in a physical separation.

### Step 3 — Solve for the retentate (or permeate) in terms of the cut fraction

Define the **cut fraction** θ = P/F. Then R = F(1 − θ). Substituting into the solute balance:

$$F \, x_{F} = \theta\,F\,x_{P} + (1-\theta)\,F\,x_{R}$$

$$x_{F} = \theta\,x_{P} + (1-\theta)\,x_{R}$$

**Logic:** The cut fraction determines how much feed becomes permeate; in concentration processes, θ is kept small (typically 0.1 – 0.3) so the retentate becomes concentrated.

### Step 4 — Re-arrange to express retentate concentration

$$x_{R} = \frac{x_{F} - \theta\,x_{P}}{1-\theta}$$

**Logic:** This is the form most often used in the design of milk protein concentration, where x_F and x_P are measured and x_R is the desired output.

### Step 5 — Concentration factor

The **concentration factor** is the ratio of retentate to feed concentration:

$$CF = \frac{x_{R}}{x_{F}} = \frac{1 - \theta\,(x_{P}/x_{F})}{1-\theta}$$

Since membranes ideally reject the solute strongly, x_P/x_F ≈ 0 for high-rejection membranes, and:

$$CF \approx \frac{1}{1-\theta}$$

**Logic:** This neat result shows that CF depends *only* on the cut fraction. For a 90 % retentate recovery (θ = 0.1), CF ≈ 1.11; for θ = 0.9, CF ≈ 10, but the permeate volume is very large. Engineers trade off CF vs. flux decline vs. energy cost.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Membrane Concentration of Whey

**Problem:** A UF plant processes **10,000 kg/h** of cheese whey containing **0.6 % (w/w) protein** into a retentate of **9 % (w/w)** protein and a permeate of **0.05 % (w/w)** protein. Calculate:
1. The cut fraction θ.
2. The mass flow rates of retentate and permeate.
3. The concentration factor CF.
4. The protein recovery in the retentate (yield).

### Step 1 — Apply total mass balance

$$F = P + R \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 10{,}000 = P + R$$

### Step 2 — Apply solute (protein) balance

$$F\,x_{F} = P\,x_{P} + R\,x_{R}$$

$$10{,}000 \times 0.006 = P \times 0.0005 + R \times 0.09$$

$$60 = 0.0005\,P + 0.09\,R$$

### Step 3 — Solve the simultaneous equations

From Step 1: R = 10,000 − P. Substitute:

$$60 = 0.0005\,P + 0.09\,(10{,}000 - P)$$

$$60 = 0.0005\,P + 900 - 0.09\,P$$

$$60 - 900 = -0.0895\,P$$

$$-840 = -0.0895\,P$$

$$P = \frac{840}{0.0895} = 9{,}385.47 \;\text{kg/h}$$

$$R = 10{,}000 - 9{,}385.47 = 614.53 \;\text{kg/h}$$

### Step 4 — Cut fraction

$$\theta = \frac{P}{F} = \frac{9{,}385.47}{10{,}000} = 0.9385$$

### Step 5 — Concentration factor

$$CF = \frac{x_{R}}{x_{F}} = \frac{0.09}{0.006} = 15.0$$

### Step 6 — Protein recovery (yield)

$$\eta = \frac{R \times x_{R}}{F \times x_{F}} \times 100\% = \frac{614.53 \times 0.09}{10{,}000 \times 0.006} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta = \frac{55.31}{60.00} \times 100\% = 92.18\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Summary:** A 92.2 % protein recovery with a 15-fold concentration factor is typical for an industrial UF whey plant. The remaining ~7.8 % protein lost in the permeate is recovered downstream by reverse osmosis in well-designed plants.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Separation

| Food Product | Target Component | Recommended Process | Justification (Why) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Skim milk | Whey proteins vs. caseins | UF (50 kDa cutoff) | Size selectivity is sufficient; thermal alternative denatures proteins |
| Apple juice | Haze (pectin, starch) | UF + enzymatic | Cold process retains aroma and color |
| Green coffee | Caffeine | SCFE (CO₂, 30 MPa, 333 K) | Selective, GRAS solvent, leaves no residue |
| Sugarcane juice | Water | Multi-effect evaporation + crystallization | High throughput, well-established |
| Olive paste | Oil from solids | Centrifugation (3-phase) | Continuous, low cost, scalable |
| Beer | Yeast, protein haze | Centrifugation + crossflow MF | Preserves mouthfeel |
| Grape must (for dealcoholized wine) | Ethanol | RO + osmotic distillation | Retains aromatic esters |
| Fish oil | EPA / DHA fractionation | Urea adduction + SMB chromatography | Very high selectivity for PUFA |
| Whey permeate | Lactose → glucose + galactose | Chromatography + immobilized enzyme | Continuous, low by-products |
| Citrus peel | Limonene / essential oil | SCFE + steam distillation hybrid | Captures both volatiles and heavier oils |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Food Separation Process Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts a **generalized industrial food separation process** showing the interaction between feed, separation stage, and product streams. Note that all node IDs are alphanumeric and free of reserved Mermaid keywords.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Food Material] --> B1[Pre-treatment Stage]
    B1 --> B2[Pre-treated Feed]
    B2 --> C1{Separation Unit}
    C1 --> D1[Permeate Product Stream]
    C1 --> D2[Retentate Product Stream]
    C1 --> D3[Waste or By-product Stream]
    D1 --> E1[Post-processing Stage]
    D2 --> E2[Concentration Stage]
    E1 --> F1[Final Quality Check]
    E2 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Packaged Food Product]
    D3 --> H1[Recycle or Effluent Treatment]
    B1 --> I1[Energy Input]
    C1 --> I1
    E1 --> I1
    E2 --> I1
    H1 --> J1[Environmental Compliance]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Raw food** → cleaned, sized, pH-adjusted (pre-treatment).
- **Separation unit** is the heart of the process — could be membrane module, centrifuge, extractor, evaporator, or chromatographic column.
- **Three outputs:** (a) desired permeate, (b) concentrated retentate, (c) waste/retentate that may be recycled.
- **Energy input** is required at the pre-treatment, separation, and post-processing stages — energy integration (e.g., multiple-effect evaporators) is a key sustainability feature.
- **Effluent treatment** ensures the process complies with FSSAI / Pollution Control Board discharge norms.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Membrane Cascade for Whey Fractionation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K1[Cheese Whey Feed] --> L1[UF Stage 50 kDa]
    L1 --> M1[UF Retentate Protein Concentrate]
    L1 --> M2[UF Permeate Lactose Stream]
    M2 --> N1[RO Stage]
    N1 --> O1[RO Retentate Concentrated Lactose]
    N1 --> O2[RO Permeate Pure Water Recycle]
    M1 --> P1[Spray Drying]
    P1 --> Q1[Whey Protein Powder WPC80]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- UF concentrates proteins (rejecting them as retentate), letting lactose and water pass.
- RO on the UF permeate recovers water for reuse and concentrates lactose, which can be sold or hydrolyzed.
- The final **WPC80** (Whey Protein Concentrate, 80 % protein) is a high-value ingredient for sports nutrition and infant formula.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Supercritical CO₂ Extraction (Coffee Decaffeination)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Green Coffee Beans] --> S1[Grinding Stage]
    S1 --> S2[Extraction Vessel 30 MPa, 333 K]
    S2 --> S3[CO2 + Caffeine Mixture]
    S3 --> S4[Pressure Reduction to 5 MPa]
    S4 --> S5[Separator Vessel Caffeine Precipitates]
    S5 --> S6[Pure Caffeine Product]
    S5 --> S7[CO2 Recompression]
    S7 --> S2
    S2 --> T1[Decaffeinated Coffee Beans]
    T1 --> U1[Roasting]
    U1 --> V1[Decaffeinated Roasted Coffee]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The extraction vessel holds the ground green coffee.
- **CO₂ at supercritical conditions** (T > 304.25 K, P > 7.38 MPa) acts as the solvent, dissolving caffeine.
- Pressure reduction in the separator causes the CO₂ to lose its solvent power; caffeine precipitates and is collected.
- CO₂ is recompressed and recycled — a **closed-loop, zero-emission** process.

---

## 4.4 Comparison Block Diagram — Separation by Particle Size vs. Solubility

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X1{Food Component Property} --> X2{Size or Volatility?}
    X2 -->|Large Particles| Y1[Centrifugation]
    X2 -->|Intermediate MW 1 to 1000 kDa| Y2[Ultrafiltration]
    X2 -->|Small Molecules MW less than 1 kDa| Y3[Nanofiltration or RO]
    X1 --> X3{Solubility Affinity?}
    X3 -->|Hydrophobic Compounds| Z1[SCFE with CO2]
    X3 -->|Hydrophilic Compounds| Z2[Ion-Exchange or Adsorption]
    X1 --> X4{Thermal Stability?}
    X4 -->|Heat Sensitive| W1[Membrane or Freeze Concentration]
    X4 -->|Heat Stable| W2[Evaporation or Distillation]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU University Exam Pattern:** Part A consists of short-answer questions (typically 3 marks each, ~30–50 words expected). Two questions are given below for practice.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEFTT527]
**List any three industrial applications of supercritical fluid extraction (SCFE) in food processing and state two advantages of using CO₂ as the supercritical solvent.**

**Model Answer:**
*Applications:*
1. Decaffeination of green coffee beans and tea leaves.
2. Extraction of essential oils and oleoresins from spices (e.g., black pepper, turmeric, paprika).
3. Removal of cholesterol from butterfat and edible oils.

*Advantages of CO₂:*
1. **GRAS status** — non-toxic and leaves no solvent residue in the product.
2. **Easy recovery** — upon depressurization, CO₂ becomes a gas and is recycled, giving near-zero emissions.
3. Mild critical point (T_c = 304.25 K, P_c = 7.38 MPa) preserves heat-labile aroma compounds.

*(3 marks — 1 for correct list, 1 for two valid advantages, 1 for the critical point data)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEFTT527]
**Differentiate between ultrafiltration (UF) and reverse osmosis (RO) in terms of membrane pore size, operating pressure, and one food-industry application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Ultrafiltration (UF) | Reverse Osmosis (RO) |
|---|---|---|
| **Pore size / MW cutoff** | 1 – 100 nm (1 – 500 kDa) | < 1 nm (< 200 Da, non-porous) |
| **Operating pressure** | 0.1 – 1.0 MPa | 1.0 – 6.0 MPa |
| **Separation mechanism** | Size exclusion (sieving) | Solution–diffusion |
| **Food application** | Whey protein concentration | Water recovery from whey permeate; pre-concentration of fruit juice |

*(3 marks — 1 for pore size, 1 for pressure, 1 for valid applications)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers an internal choice between two options. Sub-parts are typically 7 marks each. Show *all* steps for full marks; partial credit is given for the correct formula and substitution even if the final answer is not reached.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PEFTT527]**

A dairy plant concentrates **8,000 kg/h** of skim milk containing **3.3 % (w/w) total protein** using an ultrafiltration membrane. The permeate contains **0.25 % protein** and the retentate contains **10 % protein**.

**(a) [7 marks]** Calculate the cut fraction (θ), and the mass flow rates of permeate and retentate.

**(b) [7 marks]** Determine the **concentration factor (CF)**, the **percentage protein recovery** in the retentate, and comment on whether this is suitable for producing **Whey Protein Concentrate (WPC80)**.

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Mass flow rates and cut fraction

**Step 1.** Total mass balance:
$$F = P + R \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 8{,}000 = P + R$$

**Step 2.** Protein (solute) balance:
$$F \cdot x_F = P \cdot x_P + R \cdot x_R$$
$$8{,}000 \times 0.033 = P \times 0.0025 + R \times 0.10$$
$$264 = 0.0025\,P + 0.10\,R$$

**Step 3.** Substitute R = 8,000 − P:
$$264 = 0.0025\,P + 0.10\,(8{,}000 - P)$$
$$264 = 0.0025\,P + 800 - 0.10\,P$$
$$264 - 800 = -0.0975\,P$$
$$-536 = -0.0975\,P$$
$$P = \frac{536}{0.0975} = 5{,}497.44\;\text{kg/h}$$
$$R = 8{,}000 - 5{,}497.44 = 2{,}502.56\;\text{kg/h}$$

**Step 4.** Cut fraction:
$$\theta = \frac{P}{F} = \frac{5{,}497.44}{8{,}000} = 0.6872 \;(68.72\%)$$

> *Marks: 1 for total balance, 2 for solute balance, 2 for solving the linear system, 1 for cut fraction, 1 for correct units — total 7.*

---

##### Part (b) — CF, recovery, and WPC80 suitability

**Step 1.** Concentration factor:
$$CF = \frac{x_R}{x_F} = \frac{0.10}{0.033} = 3.03$$

**Step 2.** Protein recovery (yield):
$$\eta = \frac{R \cdot x_R}{F \cdot x_F} \times 100\% = \frac{2{,}502.56 \times 0.10}{8{,}000 \times 0.033} \times 100\%$$
$$\eta = \frac{250.256}{264.0} \times 100\% = 94.79\%$$

**Step 3.** WPC80 suitability:
- A **WPC80** requires **≥ 80 % protein** in the dry solids.
- The retentate here is **10 % protein on a wet basis**. After spray drying, assuming all water is removed, the dry protein content ≈ 10 % of feed solids.
- Skim milk solids ≈ 9 % (non-protein solids ~5.7 %), so retentate dry solids contain protein / (protein + other solids). A typical UF retentate at 10 % protein on wet basis will yield roughly **~30–35 % protein on a dry basis** after spray drying — *insufficient* for WPC80.
- The plant would need either a **higher x_R** (e.g., 18–20 %) via **diafiltration** or a downstream **ion-exchange polishing** step to reach WPC80 grade.

> *Marks: 2 for CF, 2 for η, 1 for WPC80 explanation, 2 for the diafiltration recommendation — total 7.*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PEFTT527]**

A **supercritical CO₂ extraction plant** decaffeinates green coffee beans. The extraction vessel is loaded with **1,200 kg** of beans, and the process operates at **30 MPa and 333 K**. The target is to reduce caffeine content from **1.20 % (w/w)** down to **0.05 % (w/w)**.

**(a) [7 marks]** Calculate the **mass of caffeine removed** and the **percentage caffeine removal efficiency**.

**(b) [7 marks]** Justify the choice of **CO₂** as the supercritical fluid by listing **three physico-chemical properties** relevant to this application, and comment on why **30 MPa and 333 K** are reasonable operating conditions.

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Caffeine removed

**Step 1.** Initial caffeine mass:
$$m_{i} = 1{,}200 \times 0.0120 = 14.40\;\text{kg}$$

**Step 2.** Final caffeine mass remaining in beans:
$$m_{f} = 1{,}200 \times 0.0005 = 0.60\;\text{kg}$$

**Step 3.** Caffeine removed:
$$\Delta m = 14.40 - 0.60 = 13.80\;\text{kg}$$

**Step 4.** Removal efficiency:
$$\eta = \frac{\Delta m}{m_{i}} \times 100\% = \frac{13.80}{14.40} \times 100\% = 95.83\%$$

> *Marks: 2 for initial mass, 1 for final mass, 1 for difference, 2 for η calculation, 1 for unit consistency — total 7.*

---

##### Part (b) — Justification of CO₂ and operating conditions

**Three physico-chemical properties of CO₂ relevant to coffee decaffeination:**

1. **Mild critical point** — T_c = 304.25 K (31.1 °C) and P_c = 7.38 MPa. Operating slightly above these values (e.g., 333 K, 30 MPa) gives tunable solvent power without thermal damage to the beans.
2. **Non-toxic, GRAS status** — leaves zero solvent residue in the product, eliminating the consumer-safety concerns of older dichloromethane and ethyl acetate processes.
3. **Selectivity** — at carefully chosen P and T, CO₂ preferentially dissolves caffeine over the desirable coffee flavor compounds (chlorogenic acids, trigonelline, melanoidins), preserving the organoleptic profile.

**Why 30 MPa and 333 K are reasonable:**

- **Pressure of 30 MPa:** Increases CO₂ density to roughly **~830 kg/m³**, providing strong solvating power for caffeine while remaining well within commercial compressor capability.
- **Temperature of 333 K (60 °C):** High enough to overcome mass-transfer resistance in the bean matrix, low enough to **avoid thermal degradation** of volatile aroma precursors.
- **Combined effect:** At (30 MPa, 333 K), the solubility of caffeine in SC-CO₂ is in the range of **0.1 – 1.0 % by mass**, sufficient for industrial throughput.

> *Marks: 3 for properties, 2 for pressure justification, 2 for temperature justification — total 7.*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Unit inconsistency in mass balance problems.** Mixing kg with kg/h, or g/L with kg/m³. Always state units *inside every step*.
> 2. **Forgetting the cut fraction's effect on CF.** Many students quote CF = 1/(1 − θ) without verifying the assumption x_P/x_F ≈ 0. Mention this assumption explicitly.
> 3. **Confusing UF with RO.** They are *not* the same; pore size, pressure, and mechanism differ. Examiners specifically look for the word *size exclusion* (UF) vs. *solution–diffusion* (RO).
> 4. **Writing "SCFE" without naming CO₂.** In food applications, CO₂ is the overwhelmingly dominant solvent. Stating the **critical constants** earns bonus marks.
> 5. **Skipping the energy/economic angle in Part B questions.** For membrane processes, briefly mention **flux decline**, **fouling**, **backwash frequency**, and **energy consumption in kWh/m³ permeate**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2, Applications in Food Processing**

- ☐ **Five families of food separation processes:** mechanical, membrane, extractive, sorption, phase-change.
- ☐ **Membrane spectrum (pressure order):** MF < UF < NF < RO, with **pore size decreasing and pressure increasing**.
- ☐ **Typical operating pressures:** MF ~ 0.05 MPa, UF ~ 0.2–0.5 MPa, NF ~ 1–3 MPa, RO ~ 2–6 MPa.
- ☐ **CO₂ critical point:** T_c = **304.25 K**, P_c = **7.38 MPa** — commit to memory.
- ☐ **Three pillars of SCFE selection:** GRAS status, tunable solvent power, zero residue.
- ☐ **Key formula (Darcy flux):** $J = \Delta P / (\mu \, R_m)$.
- ☐ **Key formula (Rejection):** $R = 1 - C_p / C_f$.
- ☐ **Key formula (Concentration factor):** $CF \approx 1/(1 - \theta)$ for high-rejection membranes.
- ☐ **Stokes' law (centrifuge):** $v_s = d^2 \omega^2 r (\rho_p - \rho_f) / (18 \mu)$.
- ☐ **Material balance pair:** $F = P + R$ and $F x_F = P x_P + R x_R$ — write *both* equations for full credit.
- ☐ **Industrial priority order for food separations:** **preserve nutrients > reduce energy > maximize purity > minimize cost**.
- ☐ **WPC grades:** WPC34, WPC60, WPC80, WPI90 (Isolate) — graded by protein content on a dry basis.
- ☐ **Coffee decaffeination target:** < 0.1 % caffeine w/w (EU regulation).
- ☐ **SMB chromatography** is the industrial standard for **glucose–fructose separation** (high-fructose corn syrup).
- ☐ **Multiple-effect evaporators** reduce steam consumption by factor n (n = number of effects).
- ☐ **Freeze concentration** retains aroma best but is **energy-intensive**; used only for premium juices.
- ☐ **Ion-exchange** is the standard for **demineralization of whey** used in infant formula.
- ☐ **Centrifugal separators** operate at **3,000 – 7,000 g** for milk skimming; **15,000 g+** for yeast harvesting.
- ☐ **Always** include **units** in your final answer; the examiner awards half-mark increments for dimensional correctness.

---

> **END OF NOTES — Module 2: Applications in Food Processing (PEFTT527, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
