topics.title: Theory of chromatographic separation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT527 - SEPARATION PROCESSES IN FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Theory of chromatographic separation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chromatography** is a versatile physical method of **separation** in which the components of a mixture are separated and identified by differential distribution between two immiscible phases — a **stationary phase** (held fixed in a column or on a planar surface) and a **mobile phase** (which percolates through or over the stationary phase, carrying the sample components with it).

The Russian–Italian botanist **Mikhail Tswett (1906)** coined the term *chromatography* (Greek: *chroma* = color, *graphein* = to write) when he successfully separated plant pigments (chlorophyll, xanthophylls, carotenes) into distinct colored bands on a calcium carbonate column using petroleum ether as the eluent.

In modern analytical chemistry, chromatography is described by the **distribution equilibrium** of each solute between the two phases. For a solute $i$:

$$K_i = \frac{C_{S,i}}{C_{M,i}}$$

where $K_i$ is the **partition (distribution) coefficient**, $C_{S,i}$ is the equilibrium concentration of component $i$ in the stationary phase, and $C_{M,i}$ is the equilibrium concentration in the mobile phase.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tortoise and Hare" Race

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a group of runners (**solutes**) racing on a track that is partly **clear tarmac (mobile phase)** and partly **deep sand pits (stationary phase)**. Every runner must repeatedly enter the sand, slow down, and exit back onto the tarmac. Runners who *prefer* the tarmac (low $K$) — the "hares" — sprint through quickly. Runners who *prefer* the sand (high $K$) — the "tortoises" — lag behind because they spend more time in the slow medium. By the finish line, the runners are no longer a cluster but are well separated in space and time. **The chromatographic column is the track, the eluent is the timing clock, and the detector is the finish line.**

## 1.3 Why Chromatography Matters in Food Technology

| Food Industry Application | Chromatographic Technique Used |
| :--- | :--- |
| Quantification of sugars, organic acids, vitamins in juices | **HPLC** (High-Performance Liquid Chromatography) |
| Detection of pesticide residues, mycotoxins, contaminants | **GC–MS / HPLC–MS** |
| Profiling of fatty acids and triglycerides in edible oils | **GC** with FID detector |
| Separation and identification of food colorants | **TLC** / **HPLC–DAD** |
| Analysis of amino acids, peptides, proteins in dairy | **Ion-Exchange LC** / **Size-Exclusion LC** |
| Authentication of fruit juices, wines, olive oil | **Chiral / GC Fingerprinting** |

## 1.4 Classification of Chromatographic Methods (Quick View)

- **By geometry of the bed:** *Planar* (Paper, TLC) and *Column* (Packed, Capillary)
- **By physical state of the mobile phase:** *Gas Chromatography (GC)* and *Liquid Chromatography (LC)*
- **By separation mechanism:** *Adsorption, Partition, Ion-Exchange, Size-Exclusion (Gel Permeation), Affinity, Chiral*
- **By purpose:** *Analytical* (qualitative/quantitative) and *Preparative* (isolation of pure compounds)

> [!TIP]
> The 2024 KTU syllabus emphasizes the **theoretical foundation** of separation, not the hardware. Master the *mathematics* of band broadening, plate height, and resolution — these appear in almost every KTU university exam.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Retention Phenomenon

When a solute is injected into a flowing mobile phase, it experiences a series of sorption–desorption steps with the stationary phase. Solutes that interact more strongly with the stationary phase move more slowly.

### Key Retention Parameters

- **Total (Absolute) Retention Time, $t_R$**: Time elapsed from injection to the appearance of the peak maximum.
- **Dead Time (Hold-up Time), $t_M$**: Time required for an *unretained* solute to traverse the column (i.e., the time the mobile phase itself takes).
- **Adjusted Retention Time, $t'_R$**: Time the solute actually spends interacting with the stationary phase.
- **Retention (Capacity) Factor, $k'$**: Dimensionless measure of retention strength.
- **Selectivity Factor, $\alpha$**: Ratio of capacity factors of two adjacent peaks — measures the *thermodynamic separability*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Capacity Factor $k'$ is the central thermodynamic parameter in chromatography. A value of $k'$ in the range $1 \le k' \le 10$ is considered ideal for analytical separations.** Below 1, peaks elute too close to the dead volume; above 20, analysis time becomes excessive.

## 2.2 Plate Theory (Martin and Synge, 1941)

The plate model treats the chromatographic column as if it were a stack of $N$ discrete, equilibrium "plates," analogous to distillation or extraction stages.

- Each plate is a single equilibrium stage where the solute partitions instantaneously between mobile and stationary phases.
- The number of theoretical plates $N$ characterizes **column efficiency**.
- The **Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate (HETP)**, $H$, links the column length $L$ to the number of plates:

$$N = \frac{L}{H}$$

The efficiency can be measured experimentally from any chromatographic peak:

$$N = 16 \left(\frac{t_R}{w}\right)^{2} \quad \text{or} \quad N = 5.54 \left(\frac{t_R}{w_{1/2}}\right)^{2}$$

where $w$ is the baseline peak width and $w_{1/2}$ is the peak width at half-height.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning of $H$:** $H$ is the length of column required to achieve one equilibrium partition. A *small* $H$ (and therefore a *large* $N$) means a more efficient column with sharper peaks.

## 2.3 Rate Theory — The Van Deemter Equation

The plate theory is purely thermodynamic and ignores kinetics. The **rate theory** of Van Deemter, Zuiderweg, and Klinkenberg (1956) describes the dependence of $H$ on the linear mobile-phase velocity $u$:

$$H = A + \frac{B}{u} + C \cdot u$$

| Term | Name | Physical Origin | Behavior with $u$ |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $A$ | **Eddy diffusion** | Multiple flow paths around stationary-phase particles | Independent of $u$ |
| $B/u$ | **Longitudinal (molecular) diffusion** | Solute diffuses along the column axis while in the mobile phase | Decreases as $u$ increases |
| $C \cdot u$ | **Mass-transfer resistance** | Finite time to establish sorption/desorption equilibrium | Increases as $u$ increases |

The constants are written explicitly as:

$$A = 2\lambda \, d_p \qquad B = 2\gamma \, D_M \qquad C = C_S + C_M = \frac{2 k'}{(1+k')^{2}} \cdot \frac{d_f^{2}}{D_S} + \frac{2(1+6k'+11k'^{2})}{24(1+k')^{2}} \cdot \frac{d_p^{2}}{D_M}$$

where $d_p$ = particle diameter, $d_f$ = stationary-phase film thickness, $D_M, D_S$ = diffusion coefficients in mobile/stationary phases, $\lambda$ = packing irregularity factor, $\gamma$ = obstruction factor.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Favorite:** Plotting $H$ vs. $u$ yields a **hyperbolic curve** with a minimum at the **optimum velocity** $u_{opt} = \sqrt{B/C}$. This minimum $H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{BC}$ corresponds to the most efficient column operation.

## 2.4 Resolution — The Master Equation

**Resolution ($R_s$)** is the single most important practical measure of how well two adjacent peaks are separated. For two neighboring peaks 1 and 2:

$$R_s = \frac{t_{R,2} - t_{R,1}}{(w_1 + w_2)/2} = \frac{\Delta t_R}{\bar{w}}$$

A value of $R_s = 1.0$ indicates ~2% peak overlap; **$R_s \ge 1.5$ is considered "baseline separation."**

The **Purnell Resolution Equation** (the *master* KTU formula) connects $R_s$ to the three fundamental control variables:

$$R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k'_{2}}{1 + k'_{2}}$$

| Factor | Controls | How to Improve |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\sqrt{N}/4$ | **Efficiency** (kinetic) | Use smaller particles, longer column, lower $u$ |
| $(\alpha-1)/\alpha$ | **Selectivity** (thermodynamic) | Change stationary phase, change temperature, change mobile-phase composition |
| $k'/(1+k')$ | **Retention** (thermodynamic) | Adjust mobile-phase strength so $k' \approx 2-10$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three bracketed factors are *independent*. A KTU examiner loves to ask: *"How would you double $R_s$?"* — Answer: Doubling $N$ (4× the column length) doubles $R_s$; doubling $N$ is the *only* way to improve resolution without changing chemistry. The other two factors are non-linear — at $\alpha = 1.10$, $R_s$ is already very sensitive to small selectivity changes.

## 2.5 Peak Broadening — Sources and Effects

Band broadening arises whenever the variance of the solute distribution grows along the column. Total variance is the sum of independent contributions:

$$\sigma_{tot}^{2} = \sigma_{inj}^{2} + \sigma_{col}^{2} + \sigma_{det}^{2} + \sigma_{extra}^{2}$$

Extra-column broadening (injection, detection, connecting tubing) must be minimized — it contributes nothing to separation but adds to peak width and lowers resolution.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Retention Factor $k'$

**Starting premise:** The total time a solute spends in the column $t_R$ equals the sum of (i) the time it travels with the mobile phase ($t_M$) and (ii) the time it is *retained* in the stationary phase ($t'_R$).

$$t_R = t_M + t'_R$$

The amount of solute in the stationary phase $q_S$ versus the mobile phase $q_M$ is governed by the partition coefficient $K$ and the phase ratio $\beta = V_S / V_M$ (volume of stationary phase ÷ volume of mobile phase in the column):

$$q_S = K \, q_M \quad \text{and} \quad \beta = \frac{V_S}{V_M}$$

The fraction of time the solute spends in the stationary phase is:

$$\text{Fraction in S} = \frac{K V_S}{K V_S + V_M} = \frac{K \beta}{1 + K \beta}$$

The retention time can therefore be expressed as:

$$t_R = t_M \left(1 + K \beta\right) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad t_R = t_M \left(1 + k'\right)$$

where we define the **capacity factor** $k' = K \beta$. Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\,k' = \frac{t_R - t_M}{t_M} = \frac{t'_R}{t_M}\,}$$

**Physical meaning:** $k'$ is the ratio of the time spent in the stationary phase to the time spent in the mobile phase. It is independent of flow rate and depends only on thermodynamics ($K$) and column geometry ($\beta$).

## 3.2 Derivation of the Number of Theoretical Plates from a Gaussian Peak

Chromatographic peaks are nearly ideal Gaussians of the form:

$$C(t) = C_{max} \exp\!\left[ -\frac{(t - t_R)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}} \right]$$

A Gaussian has the property that the inflection points occur at $t_R \pm \sigma$, and the *tangent line at the inflection point* intersects the baseline at $t_R \pm 2\sigma$. Hence the **baseline peak width** $w = 4\sigma$.

For the half-height width $w_{1/2}$: setting $C = C_{max}/2$ and solving for $t$:

$$\frac{1}{2} = \exp\!\left[-\frac{(w_{1/2}/2)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right] \;\Longrightarrow\; w_{1/2}^{2} = 8\sigma^{2} \ln 2$$

Since $w = 4\sigma$:

$$N = \frac{L}{H} = \left(\frac{L}{\sigma}\right)^{2}\left(\frac{\sigma^{2}}{L \cdot H}\right)$$

A more direct approach uses the fact that $N$ equals the square of the ratio of distance to dispersion:

$$N = \left(\frac{t_R}{\sigma}\right)^{2}$$

Substituting $w = 4\sigma$ (baseline) and $w_{1/2} = 2\sigma\sqrt{2\ln 2}$:

$$\boxed{\,N = 16 \left(\frac{t_R}{w}\right)^{2} = 5.54 \left(\frac{t_R}{w_{1/2}}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{t_R}{\sigma}\right)^{2}\,}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Van Deemter Equation

The variance per unit length of column is the sum of three independent broadening mechanisms:

$$\frac{d\sigma^{2}}{dx} = \frac{A}{L} + \frac{B}{u} + C$$

With $H = d\sigma^{2}/dx$ and $u = L/t_M$:

**Step 1 — Eddy diffusion (A-term):** A solute molecule takes different paths around the stationary-phase particles. The variance of arrival times scales with particle diameter $d_p$ and packing irregularity $\lambda$:

$$\frac{d\sigma^{2}_A}{dx} = 2 \lambda d_p \quad\Rightarrow\quad H_A = 2 \lambda d_p = A$$

**Step 2 — Longitudinal molecular diffusion (B-term):** Einstein's one-dimensional diffusion gives a variance growing linearly with time. Since time in the mobile phase scales as $x/u$:

$$\frac{d\sigma^{2}_B}{dx} = \frac{2 \gamma D_M}{u} \quad\Rightarrow\quad H_B = \frac{2 \gamma D_M}{u} = \frac{B}{u}$$

**Step 3 — Mass-transfer resistance (C-term):** A finite time is required to establish local sorption/desorption equilibrium. The local linear velocity is $u$, so the effective plate-height contribution is proportional to $u$:

$$\frac{d\sigma^{2}_C}{dx} = (C_S + C_M) \, u \quad\Rightarrow\quad H_C = (C_S + C_M)\,u = C \cdot u$$

**Step 4 — Summation (assuming statistical independence of variances):**

$$\boxed{\,H = A + \frac{B}{u} + C \cdot u\,}$$

**Optimum velocity:** Differentiate and set $dH/du = 0$:

$$\frac{dH}{du} = -\frac{B}{u^{2}} + C = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad u_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{B}{C}}$$

Substituting back:

$$H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{BC}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Purnell Resolution Equation

Consider two solutes 1 and 2 with adjusted retention times $t'_{R,1}$ and $t'_{R,2}$. With the assumption $w_1 \approx w_2 = w$ (peaks of similar width) and $\sigma = w/4$:

$$R_s = \frac{t_{R,2} - t_{R,1}}{(w_1 + w_2)/2} = \frac{t_{R,2} - t_{R,1}}{w} = \frac{\Delta t_R}{4\sigma}$$

Express the time difference via the capacity factors. From $t_R = t_M (1 + k')$:

$$\Delta t_R = t_M (k'_2 - k'_1)$$

Define the **selectivity factor** $\alpha = k'_2 / k'_1 \ge 1$ and rearrange:

$$\Delta t_R = t_M k'_2 \left(1 - \frac{1}{\alpha}\right) = t_M k'_2 \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha}$$

Substituting $\sigma = t_M (1 + k'_2)/\sqrt{N}$ from the plate model (with $N = (t_R/\sigma)^2$):

$$R_s = \frac{t_M k'_2 \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha}}{4 \cdot \frac{t_M (1 + k'_2)}{\sqrt{N}}} = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k'_2}{1 + k'_2}$$

$$\boxed{\,R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k'_2}{1 + k'_2}\,}$$

## 3.5 The Famous "Doubling $R_s$" Calculations (Board-Favorite)

| Strategy | Effect on $R_s$ | Required Change |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Increase $N$ | $R_s \propto \sqrt{N}$ | 4× longer column (or 4× smaller $d_p$) |
| Increase $\alpha$ | Non-linear | Change stationary/mobile phase chemistry |
| Optimize $k'$ | $f(k') = k'/(1+k')$ | Adjust mobile-phase strength so $k' \approx 2-5$ |

> [!NOTE]
> For example, if $k'_2 = 9$, then $f(k') = 9/10 = 0.90$. If $k'_2$ drops to $k'_2 = 1$, $f(k') = 0.5$. Hence at high retention, the third factor saturates — diminishing returns. **Always target $k'_2 \approx 2-5$ for fastest analysis at given $R_s$.**

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A column of length 25 cm produces a peak with $t_R = 9.0$ min and baseline width $w = 1.0$ min. An unretained marker gives $t_M = 1.0$ min. Calculate $N$, $H$, $k'$, and the resolution between this peak and a second peak with $t_{R,2} = 10.0$ min and $w_2 = 1.0$ min. If you need $R_s = 1.5$, how long should the column be?

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Number of plates:
$$N_1 = 16 \left(\frac{9.0}{1.0}\right)^{2} = 16 \times 81 = 1296$$

Step 2 — HETP:
$$H = \frac{L}{N} = \frac{25\,\text{cm}}{1296} = 0.0193\,\text{cm} = 0.193\,\text{mm}$$

Step 3 — Capacity factor:
$$k' = \frac{9.0 - 1.0}{1.0} = 8.0$$

Step 4 — Current resolution:
$$R_s = \frac{10.0 - 9.0}{(1.0 + 1.0)/2} = \frac{1.0}{1.0} = 1.0$$

Step 5 — Required $N$ for $R_s = 1.5$. Since $R_s \propto \sqrt{N}$:
$$N_2 = N_1 \left(\frac{R_{s,2}}{R_{s,1}}\right)^{2} = 1296 \times (1.5)^{2} = 1296 \times 2.25 = 2916$$

Step 6 — Required length (at constant $H$):
$$L_2 = N_2 \cdot H = 2916 \times 0.0193\,\text{cm} = 56.3\,\text{cm}$$

**Result:** The column must be extended from 25 cm to ~56 cm to achieve baseline separation. This is a *2.25×* length increase for a *1.5×* resolution increase — a vivid illustration of the $\sqrt{N}$ dependence.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
