# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the terms *retention time*, *dead time*, and *capacity factor* as used in chromatography. Derive the relation between them.

**Model Answer:**

- **Retention time ($t_R$):** Total time elapsed from sample injection to the appearance of the peak maximum at the detector.
- **Dead time ($t_M$):** Time taken by an *unretained* (non-interacting) solute to traverse the column. Equivalently, the residence time of the mobile phase.
- **Capacity factor ($k'$):** A dimensionless measure of how strongly a solute is retained by the stationary phase. It is the ratio of the time the solute spends in the stationary phase to the time it spends in the mobile phase.

**Derivation:** From the additive time model, $t_R = t_M + t'_R$, where $t'_R$ is the adjusted retention time. Defining $k' = t'_R / t_M$:

$$t_R = t_M + t'_R = t_M + k' t_M = t_M (1 + k') \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k' = \frac{t_R - t_M}{t_M} = \frac{t'_R}{t_M}$$

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is the *Van Deemter equation*? Explain the physical significance of each term and state how it influences the choice of mobile-phase velocity.

**Model Answer:**

The Van Deemter equation gives the height equivalent to a theoretical plate $H$ as a function of linear mobile-phase velocity $u$:

$$H = A + \frac{B}{u} + C \cdot u$$

- **$A$ (Eddy diffusion term, $2\lambda d_p$):** Accounts for the multiple microscopic flow paths around the stationary-phase particles. Independent of $u$. Reduced by using smaller, more uniform particles and well-packed columns.
- **$B/u$ (Longitudinal molecular diffusion, $2\gamma D_M$):** Solute diffuses along the column axis in the mobile phase. Significant only at low flow rates. Reduced at high $u$.
- **$C \cdot u$ (Mass-transfer resistance):** Finite kinetics of sorption/desorption equilibrium. Dominates at high flow rates. Reduced by thin stationary-phase films, small particles, and high diffusion coefficients.

The curve of $H$ vs. $u$ has a minimum at $u_{opt} = \sqrt{B/C}$. For maximum column efficiency (smallest $H$, sharpest peaks), the mobile-phase velocity should be chosen near $u_{opt}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1** `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]`

> **(a)** Discuss the **plate theory** and **rate theory** of chromatographic separation. How do they differ in their treatment of column efficiency? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A column of length 30 cm gives $t_R = 12.0$ min and baseline peak width $w = 1.2$ min for a solute. An unretained marker elutes at $t_M = 1.5$ min. Calculate: **(i)** the number of theoretical plates, **(ii)** the HETP, **(iii)** the capacity factor, and **(iv)** the resolution between this peak and a second peak with $t_{R,2} = 13.5$ min and $w_2 = 1.5$ min. Comment on whether the separation is adequate. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks:**

**Plate Theory (Martin & Synge, 1941):**
- The column is treated as a stack of $N$ discrete, independent equilibrium stages ("plates").
- In each plate, instantaneous equilibrium is assumed between mobile and stationary phases.
- $N$ is a measure of column efficiency, related to peak width by $N = 16(t_R/w)^2$.
- The HETP is $H = L/N$.

**Rate Theory (Van Deemter, 1956):**
- Recognises that equilibrium is *not* instantaneous — kinetics matter.
- $H$ depends on flow rate: $H = A + B/u + C \cdot u$.
- Each term (eddy diffusion, longitudinal diffusion, mass transfer) has a clear physical origin.

**Differences:**

| Aspect | Plate Theory | Rate Theory |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Equilibrium | Assumed instantaneous | Recognised as finite-rate |
| $H$ vs. $u$ | Independent of $u$ | Hyperbolic dependence on $u$ |
| Output | $N$ (a number) | $H = f(u)$ (a function) |
| Use | Simple efficiency metric | Optimization of flow rate |

The plate theory gives a single number ($N$); the rate theory explains *why* $N$ changes with conditions and how to improve it.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Number of theoretical plates:**
$$N = 16 \left(\frac{t_R}{w}\right)^{2} = 16 \left(\frac{12.0}{1.2}\right)^{2} = 16 \times 100 = \mathbf{1600}$$

**(ii) HETP:**
$$H = \frac{L}{N} = \frac{30\,\text{cm}}{1600} = 0.01875\,\text{cm} = \mathbf{0.1875\,\text{mm}}$$

**(iii) Capacity factor:**
$$k' = \frac{t_R - t_M}{t_M} = \frac{12.0 - 1.5}{1.5} = \frac{10.5}{1.5} = \mathbf{7.0}$$

**(iv) Resolution:**
$$R_s = \frac{t_{R,2} - t_{R,1}}{(w_1 + w_2)/2} = \frac{13.5 - 12.0}{(1.2 + 1.5)/2} = \frac{1.5}{1.35} = \mathbf{1.11}$$

**Comment:** Since $R_s = 1.11 < 1.5$, this is **NOT a baseline separation**; the peaks overlap by roughly 2–3% and quantitation would be inaccurate. To achieve $R_s = 1.5$, the column must be lengthened by a factor of $(1.5/1.11)^2 = 1.83$, i.e., to about **55 cm**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** What is **resolution** in chromatography? Derive the Purnell equation for resolution and discuss the three factors on which it depends, indicating how each may be optimised in practice. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** In a TLC analysis, two compounds A and B have $R_f$ values of 0.25 and 0.55 respectively on a silica-gel plate using a hexane–ethyl acetate (8:2) mobile phase. **(i)** Calculate the **selectivity factor** and **(ii)** comment on whether changing the mobile phase to (5:5) — which gives $R_f$ values 0.65 and 0.80 — will *improve* or *worsen* the separation. Justify with calculations. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks:**

**Resolution ($R_s$):** The degree of separation between two adjacent chromatographic peaks, defined as the distance between peak maxima divided by the average baseline width:

$$R_s = \frac{t_{R,2} - t_{R,1}}{(w_1 + w_2)/2}$$

**Derivation of Purnell equation** (see Section 3.4 for full derivation):
- Start with $R_s = \Delta t_R / (4\sigma)$.
- Express $\Delta t_R = t_M k'_2 (\alpha-1)/\alpha$ where $\alpha = k'_2/k'_1$.
- Substitute $\sigma = t_M(1+k'_2)/\sqrt{N}$:

$$R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k'_2}{1 + k'_2}$$

**Three factors and their optimisation:**

1. **Efficiency term $\sqrt{N}/4$:** Improved by *longer column*, *smaller particles*, *lower optimal velocity*.
2. **Selectivity term $(\alpha-1)/\alpha$:** Improved by *changing stationary phase chemistry*, *changing temperature*, *changing mobile-phase composition*.
3. **Retention term $k'_2/(1+k'_2)$:** Improved by adjusting mobile-phase strength so $k'_2 \approx 2-5$ (avoid both extremes).

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks:**

Recall that in TLC, $R_f = 1/(1+k')$, so $k' = (1-R_f)/R_f$.

**Original mobile phase (8:2 hexane–ethyl acetate):**

For compound A ($R_f = 0.25$):
$$k'_A = \frac{1 - 0.25}{0.25} = \frac{0.75}{0.25} = 3.0$$

For compound B ($R_f = 0.55$):
$$k'_B = \frac{1 - 0.55}{0.55} = \frac{0.45}{0.55} \approx 0.818$$

**Selectivity factor:**
$$\alpha_1 = \frac{k'_A}{k'_B} = \frac{3.0}{0.818} \approx \mathbf{3.67}$$

**New mobile phase (5:5):**

For compound A ($R_f = 0.65$):
$$k'_A = \frac{1 - 0.65}{0.65} = \frac{0.35}{0.65} \approx 0.538$$

For compound B ($R_f = 0.80$):
$$k'_B = \frac{1 - 0.80}{0.80} = \frac{0.20}{0.80} = 0.25$$

**Selectivity factor:**
$$\alpha_2 = \frac{0.538}{0.25} \approx \mathbf{2.15}$$

**Conclusion:** Since $\alpha$ *decreased* from 3.67 to 2.15, the separation **worsens** with the more polar (5:5) mobile phase. Although both compounds move further up the plate (higher $R_f$), the *difference* between their positions shrinks. The original 8:2 mobile phase gives better selectivity, even though the absolute $R_f$ values are smaller. **A practical compromise would be ~7:3**, balancing speed ($R_f$ values not too low) with selectivity ($\alpha$ reasonably high).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the $t_M$ correction.** Always convert $t_R$ to $k'$ via $k' = (t_R - t_M)/t_M$. Skipping this gives wrong capacity factors and resolution values.
> 2. **Confusing $w$ and $w_{1/2}$.** $N = 16(t_R/w)^2$ uses baseline width; $N = 5.54(t_R/w_{1/2})^2$ uses half-height width. Mixing the two constants is a common error.
> 3. **Believing that $R_s$ is linearly related to $N$.** It is $R_s \propto \sqrt{N}$, not $R_s \propto N$. To double $R_s$ you must *quadruple* $N$ (i.e., 4× the column length or 4× the plate number).
> 4. **Forgetting that increasing $u$ does not always improve $H$.** The Van Deemter plot has a minimum; operating far above $u_{opt}$ *worsens* efficiency.
> 5. **Wrong unit of $H$.** $H$ has units of length (mm or cm), not dimensionless.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision List — Theory of Chromatographic Separation**

- **Chromatography = separation by differential distribution between a stationary and a mobile phase.** Tswett (1906) coined the term.
- **Distribution coefficient $K = C_S/C_M$**; capacity factor $k' = K\beta = K (V_S/V_M) = (t_R - t_M)/t_M$.
- **Selectivity factor $\alpha = k'_2/k'_1 \ge 1$**; if $\alpha = 1$, no separation is possible regardless of column efficiency.
- **Dead time $t_M$** is the elution time of an *unretained* solute; it equals the mobile-phase residence time.
- **Efficiency** is quantified by the number of theoretical plates $N$ or the HETP $H = L/N$.
- **Two key formulas for $N$:**
  - $N = 16 (t_R/w)^2$ (baseline width)
  - $N = 5.54 (t_R/w_{1/2})^2$ (half-height width)
- **Van Deemter equation:** $H = A + B/u + C \cdot u$ (Eddy + Longitudinal + Mass-transfer terms).
- **Optimum velocity:** $u_{opt} = \sqrt{B/C}$; minimum plate height $H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{BC}$.
- **Resolution equation (Purnell):** $R_s = (\sqrt{N}/4) \cdot (\alpha-1)/\alpha \cdot k'_2/(1+k'_2)$.
- **Baseline separation** requires $R_s \ge 1.5$.
- **$R_s \propto \sqrt{N}$** → doubling $R_s$ requires 4× the column length.
- **Ideal $k'$ range:** $1 \le k' \le 10$ (optimum $2-5$).
- **Variances are additive** (independent broadening mechanisms).
- **Extra-column broadening** (injection, detector cell, tubing) must be minimised — it adds to peak width without aiding separation.
- **Smaller $d_p$ (particle size)** reduces both A and C terms → better efficiency.
- **Higher temperature** increases $D_M$ → reduces B-term longitudinal diffusion.
- **Food-industry applications:** HPLC for vitamins, sugars, organic acids, colorants; GC for fatty acids, pesticides, flavours; ion-exchange for amino acids; size-exclusion for proteins.
- **Common KTU mistakes:** confusing $w$ and $w_{1/2}$; forgetting $t_M$; assuming linear $R_s$ vs. $N$; misinterpreting the Van Deemter minimum.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selectivity or separation factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT527 - SEPARATION PROCESSES IN FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Selectivity or separation factor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of separation processes, **Selectivity (also called the Separation Factor, denoted by the Greek letter $\alpha$)** is a dimensionless thermodynamic and mass-transfer parameter that quantifies the *relative preference* of a separation technique for distributing one component (the more extractable or more volatile solute, usually designated as **A**) over another component (B) between two co-existing equilibrium phases.

Mathematically, the general definition of the separation factor for a binary mixture (A, B) distributed between Phase I (e.g., extract, permeate, vapor) and Phase II (e.g., raffinate, retentate, liquid) is:

$$\alpha_{A,B} = \frac{(x_A \big/ x_B)_{\text{Phase I}}}{(x_A \big/ x_B)_{\text{Phase II}}}$$

where $x_A$ and $x_B$ represent the mole fractions, mass fractions, or concentrations of components A and B respectively in the indicated phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The separation factor $\alpha$ is the *single most important index* used to evaluate the feasibility, efficiency, and thermodynamic limit of any equilibrium-based separation process — be it distillation, liquid-liquid extraction, membrane separation, absorption, adsorption, or crystallization.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine you have a mixed basket of **red apples and green apples** and you tip the basket into a stream of flowing water. Suppose **red apples are denser**, so they sink faster and settle at the bottom, while **green apples float** downstream. The **Selectivity** in this case is essentially a number that tells you:

> *"How much better did the system separate red apples from green apples compared to simply leaving them in the original mixed basket?"*

- If the basket ratio was 50:50 and the bottom-vs-stream ratio of red-to-green is **100:1**, then $\alpha$ is large → excellent separation.
- If the bottom-vs-stream ratio is also **50:50** (no enrichment anywhere), then $\alpha = 1$ → zero separation.
- $\alpha$ can never be less than 1 if we define A as the preferentially distributed component. A value of $\alpha = 1$ is the thermodynamic "dead-line" where no separation is achievable by that technique.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU exams:** Always write $\alpha \geq 1$ for the more preferentially distributed component. $\alpha = 1$ is the no-separation limit; $\alpha \to \infty$ is the perfect (pure-component) separation limit.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Separation Factor (Selectivity) | $\alpha$ | $1 \leq \alpha < \infty$ | **Dimensionless** |
| Distribution Coefficient | $K$ | $0 < K < \infty$ | **Dimensionless** (or concentration ratio) |
| Relative Volatility (distillation) | $\alpha_{AB}$ | $1 \leq \alpha < \infty$ | **Dimensionless** |
| Enrichment Factor | $\beta$ | $\beta \geq 1$ | **Dimensionless** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Significance — "The Why"

Selectivity captures **two things simultaneously**:

1. **Thermodynamic Equilibrium Preference** — governed by the chemical potential difference of each component between the two phases (e.g., vapor pressure in distillation, solubility in extraction, sorption affinity in adsorption/membrane).
2. **Kinetic/Transport Discrimination** — governed by relative rates of mass transfer, diffusivity, and size/shape exclusion in membrane and chromatographic processes.

A high $\alpha$ means the two phases (or the membrane/column) **discriminate strongly** between A and B at the molecular level.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

**Step 1 — Identify the binary system.** Label the more preferentially transferred component as **A** (the *distributee* or *permeate/extract*) and the less transferred as **B** (the *carrier* or *raffinate/retentate*).

**Step 2 — Write the distribution coefficients individually.**
$$K_A = \frac{\text{concentration of A in Phase I}}{\text{concentration of A in Phase II}} \qquad K_B = \frac{\text{concentration of B in Phase I}}{\text{concentration of B in Phase II}}$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio** to obtain the selectivity:
$$\alpha_{A,B} = \frac{K_A}{K_B}$$

This compact form is the **universal KTU-board expression**. It bypasses the mole-fraction formulation and directly uses the distribution-coefficient ratio.

**Step 4 — Verify the boundary conditions.**
- If $K_A > K_B$, then $\alpha_{A,B} > 1$ → A is the preferentially distributed component.
- If $K_A = K_B$, then $\alpha_{A,B} = 1$ → no separation.
- If $K_A < K_B$, then by convention we *swap labels* and report $\alpha_{B,A} > 1$ instead.

**Step 5 — Link to operating parameters.** The number of theoretical stages (e.g., McCabe–Thiele in distillation, Kremser equation in extraction) is a **strong inverse function of $\alpha$**: the closer $\alpha$ is to 1, the more stages/area/time required.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Separation Process | Key Equation for $\alpha$ | Boundary Condition | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Distillation** (Vapor–Liquid) | $\alpha_{AB} = \dfrac{(y_A/x_A)}{(y_B/x_B)} = \dfrac{K_A}{K_B} = \dfrac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}}$ | $\alpha \geq 1$ | Uses Raoult's Law for ideal systems; $K_i = P_i^{sat}/P$ |
| 2 | **Liquid–Liquid Extraction** | $\alpha_{A,B} = \dfrac{(c_A^E / c_A^R)}{(c_B^E / c_B^R)} = \dfrac{K_A^{ext}}{K_B^{ext}}$ | $\alpha \geq 1$ | E = Extract phase, R = Raffinate phase |
| 3 | **Membrane Separation** | $\alpha_{A,B} = \dfrac{(y_A/y_B)_{\text{permeate}}}{(x_A/x_B)_{\text{retentate}}}$ | $\alpha \geq 1$ | Often called *membrane selectivity* |
| 4 | **Gas Absorption / Stripping** | $\alpha_{A,B} = \dfrac{(y_A/x_A)_{\text{gas-liq}}}{(y_B/x_B)_{\text{gas-liq}}}$ | $\alpha \geq 1$ | $y$ = gas, $x$ = liquid |
| 5 | **Adsorption** | $\alpha_{A,B} = \dfrac{(q_A/q_B)_{\text{adsorbed}}}{(c_A/c_B)_{\text{fluid}}}$ | $\alpha \geq 1$ | $q$ = loading on solid |
| 6 | **Crystallization** | $\alpha \propto \dfrac{\text{solubility difference}}{T}$ | $\alpha \geq 1$ | Strongly temperature-dependent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalised Master Formula (valid for ALL equilibrium separations):**
> $$\boxed{\;\alpha_{A,B} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{(x_A \big/ x_B)_{\text{enriched phase}}}{(x_A \big/ x_B)_{\text{depleted phase}}}\;}$$
> The *enriched phase* is where A concentrates; the *depleted phase* is where B concentrates. Memorise this — it is worth **3 to 7 marks** every KTU cycle.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food Industry — Aroma Recovery:** Extracting ethyl acetate (A) from water (B) using supercritical CO₂. A high $\alpha$ (e.g., 200+) means a tiny solvent volume can recover 99% of the aroma in a single equilibrium stage.
- **Dairy — De-acidification of Cream:** Selective removal of volatile free fatty acids (butyric, caproic) by steam stripping. $\alpha$ for butyric acid over water is finite but temperature-sensitive.
- **Edible Oil Refining:** Liquid–liquid extraction of phospholipids (gums) from crude soybean oil using water. $\alpha$ for phospholipid/water vs triglyceride/water governs hydration efficiency.
- **Beverage Industry — Decaffeination:** Supercritical CO₂ selectively extracts caffeine ($\alpha_{caffeine, water} \gg 1$) without stripping flavour compounds.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — From Mole Fractions to the Distribution-Coefficient Form

We start with the **binary mole-fraction definition** of selectivity and show it is mathematically identical to the $K_A/K_B$ form. This is a *favourite 7-mark derivation* in KTU ESE.

**Given:**

$$\alpha_{A,B} = \frac{(x_A / x_B)_{\text{Phase I}}}{(x_A / x_B)_{\text{Phase II}}}$$

**Step 1 — Expand each ratio:**

$$\alpha_{A,B} = \frac{x_A^{\text{I}} / x_B^{\text{I}}}{x_A^{\text{II}} / x_B^{\text{II}}} = \frac{x_A^{\text{I}} \cdot x_B^{\text{II}}}{x_B^{\text{I}} \cdot x_A^{\text{II}}}$$

This rearrangement is just a cross-multiplication. The dimension is mole-fraction / mole-fraction = **dimensionless**. ✓

**Step 2 — Introduce the individual distribution coefficients:**

For any component $i$, the distribution coefficient is defined as the ratio of its mole fractions in the two equilibrium phases:

$$K_A = \frac{x_A^{\text{I}}}{x_A^{\text{II}}} \qquad K_B = \frac{x_B^{\text{I}}}{x_B^{\text{II}}}$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio $K_A / K_B$:**

$$\frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{x_A^{\text{I}} / x_A^{\text{II}}}{x_B^{\text{I}} / x_B^{\text{II}}} = \frac{x_A^{\text{I}} \cdot x_B^{\text{II}}}{x_A^{\text{II}} \cdot x_B^{\text{I}}}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with Step 1:**

$$\alpha_{A,B} = \frac{x_A^{\text{I}} \cdot x_B^{\text{II}}}{x_B^{\text{I}} \cdot x_A^{\text{II}}} = \frac{K_A}{K_B}$$

Hence the **equivalence is proven**:

$$\boxed{\;\alpha_{A,B} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{(x_A \big/ x_B)_{\text{I}}}{(x_A \big/ x_B)_{\text{II}}}\;} \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

### 3.2 Derivation — Distillation Relative Volatility from Raoult's Law

**For an ideal binary liquid mixture in vapor–liquid equilibrium:**

The distribution coefficient (K-value) for component $i$ is:

$$K_i = \frac{y_i}{x_i} = \frac{P_i^{sat}(T)}{P_{\text{total}}}$$

**Substituting into the selectivity formula:**

$$\alpha_{A,B} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{P_A^{sat}(T) / P_{\text{total}}}{P_B^{sat}(T) / P_{\text{total}}}$$

**The total pressure $P_{\text{total}}$ cancels:**

$$\alpha_{A,B} = \frac{P_A^{sat}(T)}{P_B^{sat}(T)}$$

**Final form:**

$$\boxed{\;\alpha_{A,B} = \frac{P_A^{sat}(T)}{P_B^{sat}(T)} \quad \text{(Raoult's Law binary)}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** In distillation, the separation factor depends *only* on the ratio of pure-component saturation pressures at the operating temperature. This is why **pressure-sensitive** components (e.g., heat-labile food aroma molecules) often require **vacuum distillation** — lowering $P_{\text{total}}$ reduces all $P_i^{sat}$ values but their ratio $\alpha$ remains constant (for an ideal mixture), while the *operating temperature* drops dramatically, protecting the product.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Solved — 14-Mark Pattern)

**Problem Statement (modeled on KTU 2024 expected pattern):**
> A liquid–liquid extraction is performed to recover **lactic acid (A)** from a fermentation broth using **tributyl phosphate in hexane** as the extractant. At equilibrium, the following data are obtained:
> - Lactic acid in extract phase: $c_A^E = 0.180$ mol/L
> - Lactic acid in raffinate phase: $c_A^R = 0.020$ mol/L
> - Water (B) in extract phase: $c_B^E = 0.040$ mol/L
> - Water (B) in raffinate phase: $c_B^R = 0.960$ mol/L
>
> **Calculate:** (a) The distribution coefficients $K_A$ and $K_B$. (b) The separation factor $\alpha_{A,B}$. (c) Comment on the feasibility of single-stage separation.

**Solution:**

**(a) Distribution Coefficients:**

$$K_A = \frac{c_A^E}{c_A^R} = \frac{0.180}{0.020} = 9.00$$

$$K_B = \frac{c_B^E}{c_B^R} = \frac{0.040}{0.960} = 0.0417$$

**(b) Separation Factor:**

$$\alpha_{A,B} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{9.00}{0.0417} = 216.0$$

**Alternative cross-check using mole-fraction form:**

$$\alpha_{A,B} = \frac{(0.180/0.040)}{(0.020/0.960)} = \frac{4.50}{0.02083} = 216.0 \;\;\checkmark$$

**(c) Feasibility Comment:**

A separation factor of $\alpha_{A,B} = 216$ is **exceptionally high**. This implies:
- A **single equilibrium stage** will recover the overwhelming majority of lactic acid into the extract.
- The process is **thermodynamically robust** — small temperature or composition fluctuations will not destroy the separation.
- For KTU's purposes, when $\alpha > 100$, single-stage extraction is often sufficient in industrial food-grade applications; for $1 < \alpha < 10$, multi-stage counter-current contact (e.g., mixer-settler cascade, Karr column) is mandatory.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How $\alpha$ Affects Process Design

| Range of $\alpha$ | Qualitative Meaning | Typical Process Configuration | KTU Exam Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\alpha = 1$ | No separation | Impossible | Theoretical limit only |
| $1 < \alpha < 1.1$ | Very difficult (e.g., isomers) | Hundreds of stages; e.g., extractive distillation with mass separation agent | Often a "trick question" in KTU |
| $1.1 < \alpha < 2$ | Difficult | 20–100 stages, high reflux | McCabe–Thiele, Ponchon–Savarit |
| $2 < \alpha < 10$ | Moderate | 5–20 stages | Standard distillation/extraction |
| $10 < \alpha < 100$ | Easy | 1–5 stages | Single-stage often adequate |
| $\alpha > 100$ | Very easy | 1 stage sufficient | Industrial "dream" separation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Two-Phase Separation

The diagram below maps the *information flow* and *mass flow* during a generic two-phase equilibrium separation, highlighting where the separation factor $\alpha$ originates and where it is *measured*.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN1["Feed Mixture F: A and B"] --> MIX["Mixer / Contact Vessel"]
    MIX <--> EQ["Equilibrium Interface Two Co-existing Phases"]
    EQ --> PH1["Phase I Extract Vapor Permeate Adsorbed Phase"]
    EQ --> PH2["Phase II Raffinate Liquid Retentate Depleted Liquid"]
    PH1 --> M1["Measure xA I and xB I"]
    PH2 --> M2["Measure xA II and xB II"]
    M1 --> COMP["Compute Selectivity"]
    M2 --> COMP
    COMP --> VAL["alpha greater than 1: Feasible Separation"]
    COMP --> INV["alpha equals 1: No Separation Possible"]
    VAL --> DEC["Design Decision: Stages Area Solvent Ratio"]
    DEC --> OUT1["Purified Product A rich Stream"]
    DEC --> OUT2["Residual Stream B rich"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *equilibrium interface* is the conceptual location where the thermodynamic preference of A over B is established. The two **measurement blocks** (M1 and M2) feed concentration data into the **compute block** that yields $\alpha$. This is exactly the data-acquisition pattern a KTU lab viva question will test ("How will you experimentally determine $\alpha$ for this extraction?").

### 4.2 Selectivity Decision-Tree for Choosing a Separation Technique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Binary Feed A and B with target recovery of A"] --> B{"alpha greater than 1000?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> C["Single Stage Equilibrium Enough: Decantation Crystallization Batch Extraction"]
    B -- "No" --> D{"alpha between 10 and 1000?"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["Multi Stage Countercurrent: Karr Column Mixer Settler Cascade"]
    D -- "No" --> F{"alpha between 1.1 and 10?"}
    F -- "Yes" --> G["Distillation Tray Column: 5 to 50 stages; high reflux"]
    F -- "No" --> H["alpha less than 1.1: Use Mass Separating Agent: Extractive Distillation Azeotropic Distillation Pressure Swing"]
    C --> OUT["Purified A stream"]
    E --> OUT
    G --> OUT
    H --> OUT
```

### 4.3 Schematic Mass-Transfer Picture (Text-Based Block Diagram)

```
   PHASE I  (extract / vapor / permeate)
   ┌───────────────────────────────┐
   │  xA↑  ↑  ↑  ↑                │  ← A concentrated here
   │  xB↓  ↓  ↓  ↓                │  ← B depleted here
   │         ↕ INTERFACE           │
   │  xA↓  ↓  ↓  ↓                │  ← A depleted here
   │  xB↑  ↑  ↑  ↑                │  ← B concentrated here
   └───────────────────────────────┘
   PHASE II (raffinate / liquid / retentate)

   SELECTIVITY α_{A,B} = (xA/xB)_I  /  (xA/xB)_II
                       = (HIGH/LOW) / (LOW/HIGH)
                       =  HIGH × HIGH / (LOW × LOW)
                       =  LARGE NUMBER  ⇒  GOOD SEPARATION
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Set A]`
Define the **separation factor (selectivity)** for a binary mixture distributed between two equilibrium phases. State the limiting value that indicates *no separation is possible* and justify why.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The separation factor $\alpha_{A,B}$ is defined as the ratio of the concentration ratio of component A to component B in Phase I (the enriched phase) to the corresponding ratio in Phase II (the depleted phase):
>
> $$\alpha_{A,B} = \frac{(x_A/x_B)_{\text{Phase I}}}{(x_A/x_B)_{\text{Phase II}}} = \frac{K_A}{K_B}$$
>
> The limiting value indicating **no separation** is $\alpha_{A,B} = 1$. At this value, both components distribute in *exactly* the same proportion between the two phases; consequently, the composition of the two phases is identical and no enrichment of A over B (or vice versa) can be achieved in a single equilibrium stage. (3 marks)

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Set B]`
For an ideal binary liquid mixture undergoing vapor–liquid equilibrium, derive the expression for the relative volatility $\alpha_{AB}$ in terms of the pure-component saturation pressures.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> From Raoult's Law, the K-value for component $i$ is:
>
> $$K_i = \frac{y_i}{x_i} = \frac{P_i^{sat}(T)}{P_{\text{total}}}$$
>
> The relative volatility is the ratio of K-values:
>
> $$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{P_A^{sat}(T) / P_{\text{total}}}{P_B^{sat}(T) / P_{\text{total}}}$$
>
> The total pressure cancels, giving:
>
> $$\boxed{\;\alpha_{AB} = \frac{P_A^{sat}(T)}{P_B^{sat}(T)}\;}$$
>
> This shows that for an ideal system, $\alpha_{AB}$ depends *only* on temperature (via the saturation-pressure ratio) and is *independent* of total operating pressure. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the **role of separation factor in the design of two-phase equilibrium separation processes**. Discuss how the magnitude of $\alpha$ dictates the number of theoretical stages and the choice of equipment. (7 marks)

**(b)** A solvent-extraction process is used to recover **caffeine (A)** from decaffeinated coffee extract using **methylene chloride (B)** as the solvent. The equilibrium data at 25 °C are:

| Component | Extract phase (mol/L) | Raffinate phase (mol/L) |
|---|---|---|
| Caffeine (A) | 0.0240 | 0.00160 |
| Water (B) | 0.0180 | 0.9980 |

Calculate:
1. The distribution coefficients $K_A$ and $K_B$.
2. The separation factor $\alpha_{A,B}$.
3. The **percent extraction** of caffeine in a single ideal stage, assuming equal volumes of extract and raffinate.
4. Comment on the suitability of this solvent for industrial decaffeination. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Role of separation factor in design — 7 marks:**

The separation factor $\alpha$ is the **master index** that converts molecular-level thermodynamic preference into macroscopic process design. Its role is explained below:

1. **Thermodynamic Feasibility Test:** A process is feasible only if $\alpha > 1$. If $\alpha = 1$, no amount of staging, reflux, or solvent choice can effect a separation.

2. **Equipment Selection:** The magnitude of $\alpha$ dictates the contactor type:
   - $\alpha > 100$ → Single-stage mixer-settler or simple decanter.
   - $10 < \alpha < 100$ → Multi-stage mixer-settler cascade (3–5 stages).
   - $2 < \alpha < 10$ → Mechanically agitated column (Karr, Scheibel, RDC).
   - $1.1 < \alpha < 2$ → Tray or packed distillation column with high reflux.
   - $\alpha < 1.1$ → Special techniques (extractive/azeotropic distillation with mass separating agent).

3. **Number of Theoretical Stages ($N$):** Through McCabe–Thiele (distillation), Kremser (extraction), or the Fenske equation (minimum stages):
   $$N_{\min} = \frac{\ln\left[\left(\frac{x_D}{x_B}\right)\left(\frac{1-x_B}{1-x_D}\right)\right]}{\ln \alpha_{\text{avg}}}$$
   Thus, **as $\alpha \to 1$, $N \to \infty$** (separation becomes impractical).

4. **Energy & Solvent Economy:** Higher $\alpha$ implies lower reflux ratios, lower solvent-to-feed ratios, and smaller reboiler/condenser duties — directly impacting operating cost.

(7 marks distributed: 2 for feasibility + 2 for equipment selection + 2 for stage-number relationship + 1 for energy/economy)

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks:**

**(i) Distribution Coefficients:**

$$K_A = \frac{c_A^E}{c_A^R} = \frac{0.0240}{0.00160} = 15.0$$

$$K_B = \frac{c_B^E}{c_B^R} = \frac{0.0180}{0.9980} = 0.01804$$

**(ii) Separation Factor:**

$$\alpha_{A,B} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{15.0}{0.01804} = 831.5$$

**Cross-check using mole-fraction form:**

$$\alpha_{A,B} = \frac{(0.0240/0.0180)}{(0.00160/0.9980)} = \frac{1.3333}{0.001603} = 831.5 \;\;\checkmark$$

**(iii) Percent Extraction in a Single Stage (equal volumes $V_E = V_R$):**

For a single ideal stage, the fraction of A extracted into the E phase is given by the *fraction-extracted* formula:

$$f_A = \frac{K_A \cdot (V_E/V_R)}{1 + K_A \cdot (V_E/V_R)} = \frac{15.0 \times 1}{1 + 15.0 \times 1} = \frac{15.0}{16.0} = 0.9375$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{Percent Extraction of caffeine} = 93.75\%\;}$$

**(iv) Industrial Suitability Comment (1 mark):**

> An $\alpha$ of **831.5** is exceptionally favourable. A single-stage extraction recovers 93.75% of the caffeine, meeting the EU/US regulatory threshold of ≥ 97% caffeine removal only marginally. **For industrial compliance**, a 2- or 3-stage counter-current cascade (giving $> 99.5\%$ removal) is recommended. Methylene chloride is otherwise a *good* solvent for caffeine due to its high $\alpha$, low cost, and ease of evaporation — though modern food industries increasingly prefer **supercritical CO₂** ($\alpha$ even higher, GRAS, residue-free) for safety reasons. (1 mark)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]`

**(a)** Derive, starting from first principles, the relationship $\alpha_{A,B} = K_A / K_B$, where $K_A$ and $K_B$ are the binary distribution coefficients between two co-existing phases. Show all intermediate steps clearly. (7 marks)

**(b)** In the extractive distillation of an **ethanol–water azeotrope** using **ethylene glycol** as the entrainer, the following vapor–liquid equilibrium data are reported for the *ethanol–water* pair in the presence of the entrainer at 1 atm:

| Temperature (°C) | $P_{ethanol}^{sat}$ (kPa) | $P_{water}^{sat}$ (kPa) |
|---|---|---|
| 85 | 83.5 | 43.4 |
| 90 | 108.0 | 53.7 |
| 95 | 138.0 | 65.8 |

Total system pressure = 101.325 kPa. Compute the relative volatility $\alpha_{ethanol,water}$ at each temperature and comment on its temperature sensitivity. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Derivation — 7 marks (full step-by-step as in §3.1 above):**

Start with the mole-fraction definition:
$$\alpha_{A,B} = \frac{(x_A/x_B)_I}{(x_A/x_B)_{II}}$$

Cross-multiply: $\alpha_{A,B} = \dfrac{x_A^I \, x_B^{II}}{x_B^I \, x_A^{II}}$

Define: $K_A = x_A^I / x_A^{II}$ and $K_B = x_B^I / x_B^{II}$.

Form the ratio: $\dfrac{K_A}{K_B} = \dfrac{x_A^I / x_A^{II}}{x_B^I / x_B^{II}} = \dfrac{x_A^I \, x_B^{II}}{x_B^I \, x_A^{II}}$

Equate the two: $\alpha_{A,B} = \dfrac{K_A}{K_B}$. **Q.E.D.** (7 marks)

**(b) Numerical Computation of Relative Volatility:**

For an ideal system, $\alpha_{ethanol,water} = P_{ethanol}^{sat} / P_{water}^{sat}$.

**At 85 °C:**

$$\alpha = \frac{83.5}{43.4} = 1.924$$

**At 90 °C:**

$$\alpha = \frac{108.0}{53.7} = 2.011$$

**At 95 °C:**

$$\alpha = \frac{138.0}{65.8} = 2.097$$

**Summary Table:**

| T (°C) | $\alpha_{ethanol,water}$ | Trend |
|---|---|---|
| 85 | 1.924 | Lowest (close to azeotropic limit) |
| 90 | 2.011 | Intermediate |
| 95 | 2.097 | Highest |

**Comment on Temperature Sensitivity (3 marks):**

> The relative volatility increases *with temperature* in the range 85–95 °C. This is because the *ethanol saturation pressure* rises faster than that of water (ethanol has a lower normal boiling point and a higher enthalpy of vaporisation gradient in this region). Practically, this means:
>
> 1. Operating extractive distillation at the *higher* end of the feasible temperature range (just below the entrainer degradation temperature) maximises $\alpha$ and hence reduces the required number of stages and reflux ratio.
> 2. However, the *azeotrope composition* (95.6 wt% ethanol) itself is highly sensitive; below the azeotropic composition, $\alpha$ must be evaluated at the *local* reboiler temperature, not at an arbitrary reference.
> 3. For food-grade ethanol (e.g., for bioethanol fuel or potable spirits), operating at *lower* temperatures with vacuum may be preferred to *protect aroma congeners*, accepting a slightly lower $\alpha$ as a design trade-off.
>
> (3 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where students LOSE marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to label phases** — writing $\alpha$ without specifying which phase is "extract" and which is "raffinate". Always write $\alpha_{A,B}^{\text{I/II}}$ or define it explicitly. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 2. **Reporting $\alpha < 1$** — this *is* a mathematically valid number, but in KTU convention you must *swap the labels* of A and B so that $\alpha \geq 1$ always. Examiners will deduct 0.5–1 mark for failing to clarify.
> 3. **Mixing up $K$ and $\alpha$ in the McCabe–Thiele / Kremser equation** — many students substitute the *distribution coefficient* $K$ in the Fenske/Kremser formula when it should be the *relative* $K$, i.e., $\alpha$. This is a **3-mark error**.
> 4. **Ignoring units in $\alpha$** — $\alpha$ is *dimensionless*. If you write units, you've made a fundamental conceptual error.
> 5. **In extraction numerics, forgetting the volume-ratio factor** — when $V_E \neq V_R$, the extraction-fraction formula contains $K_A \cdot (V_E/V_R)$, *not* $K_A$ alone. KTU examiners test this every cycle.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Density Summary — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Definition:** $\alpha_{A,B} = \dfrac{(x_A/x_B)_{\text{enriched}}}{(x_A/x_B)_{\text{depleted}}} = \dfrac{K_A}{K_B}$ — **dimensionless**, **always $\geq 1$** for the more distributed component.
- ✅ **No-separation limit:** $\alpha = 1$ (composition is identical in both phases). $\alpha \to \infty$ is the perfect-separation limit.
- ✅ **Distillation shortcut:** For ideal binary, $\alpha_{AB} = P_A^{sat}(T) / P_B^{sat}(T)$ — total pressure cancels.
- ✅ **Extraction formula:** $f_A = \dfrac{K_A \cdot (V_E/V_R)}{1 + K_A \cdot (V_E/V_R)}$ — remember the **volume ratio** $V_E/V_R$.
- ✅ **Fenske minimum-stages link:** $N_{\min} = \dfrac{\ln\left[(x_D/x_B) \cdot ((1-x_B)/(1-x_D))\right]}{\ln \alpha_{\text{avg}}}$ — uses $\ln \alpha$, not $\alpha$.
- ✅ **Design implication:** $\alpha \uparrow$ → stages $\downarrow$, reflux $\downarrow$, solvent $\downarrow$, cost $\downarrow$.
- ✅ **Real-world food examples:** Aroma recovery, oil refining, decaffeination, ethanol–water extractive distillation, lactic-acid extraction.
- ✅ **Common student mistake:** Confusing **distribution coefficient** $K$ (single-component ratio) with **separation factor** $\alpha$ (ratio of two $K$'s). They are *not* the same.
- ✅ **Examiner's favourite trick:** Give equilibrium data in the *mole-fraction form* (not $K$ values) and ask you to compute $\alpha$ — practice the cross-multiplication.
- ✅ **Industry rule-of-thumb:** $\alpha > 100$ → single stage; $1 < \alpha < 10$ → multi-stage; $\alpha < 1.1$ → use a mass-separating agent.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Efficiency of chromatographic system; principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT527 - SEPARATION PROCESSES IN FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Efficiency of chromatographic system; principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Chromatographic Efficiency** is a quantitative measure of the *peak-sharpening capability* of a chromatographic column, defined as the number of theoretical plates (N) the column possesses or, equivalently, the **Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate (HETP, or H)** per unit length of the stationary phase. It describes how closely the actual band-broadening behaviour of a solute matches the ideal "plug-flow" behaviour predicted by the Plate Model of chromatography.

The efficiency of a chromatographic system determines the *width* of the eluted peak for a given retention time. A highly efficient column produces **narrow, sharp peaks** (good resolution between neighbouring solutes), whereas an inefficient column produces **broad, diffuse peaks** that may overlap and become indistinguishable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PEFTT527 Module 3):**
> Efficiency is not a property of the solute alone. It is governed by the **kinetic** and **mass-transfer** processes occurring inside the column (governed by the Van Deemter equation) and is therefore a *system property* — it depends on the column, the mobile phase velocity, the stationary phase, the temperature, and the solute's diffusion coefficient.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **marble run** (the column) filled with a series of obstacles. A marble is dropped in and bounces through the obstacles before exiting.

- If the obstacles are arranged in a **regular, uniform pattern** and the marble experiences minimal sideways scattering, it will exit with all marbles that were dropped together at almost the same instant — they arrive in a **tight, sharp stream**. This is a *high-efficiency* column.
- If the obstacles are irregular and the marbles bounce sideways randomly, marbles dropped together will exit over a **wide spread of times** — a *low-efficiency* column.

In chromatography, the "obstacles" are the equilibration events between the mobile and stationary phase, and the "marbles" are the analyte molecules. Even if two solutes take different average times to traverse the column (selectivity), efficiency decides whether they *arrive separately* or *smear into each other*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction (Frequently Tested):**
> - **Selectivity (α)** → *Where* the peak appears (its position/retention time).
> - **Efficiency (N)** → *How narrow* the peak is at that position.
> - **Resolution (Rs)** → *How well separated* two adjacent peaks are — and it depends on **both** of the above.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The principal metrics used in KTU-board-style numerical problems on chromatographic efficiency are:

- **Retention time, $t_R$** — time (in seconds or minutes) from injection to peak maximum.
- **Dead time, $t_M$** — time for an unretained solute to traverse the column.
- **Adjusted retention time, $t_R' = t_R - t_M$** — time spent in the stationary phase.
- **Number of theoretical plates, $N$** — dimensionless.
- **Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate, $H$** — expressed in **mm** or **µm**.
- **Linear mobile phase velocity, $u$** — expressed in **cm/s**.
- **Standard deviation of the peak, $\sigma$** — in time units.
- **Peak width at base, $w_b = 4\sigma$**; **peak width at half-height, $w_{0.5} = 2.354\sigma$**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Plate Theory (Martin & Synge, 1941)

The Plate Model treats the chromatographic column as if it were divided into a series of discrete, sequential stages called **"theoretical plates"**, analogous to the trays of a distillation column.

**Operating Logic — Step by Step:**

1. Each plate contains a small volume of stationary phase and a small volume of mobile phase, in instantaneous equilibrium.
2. As fresh mobile phase moves from one plate to the next, a fraction of the solute partitions into the new mobile phase and a fraction remains in the stationary phase.
3. After $N$ such equilibration stages, the solute elutes as a distribution that approaches a **Gaussian (normal) bell-shaped curve**.
4. The width of this Gaussian depends solely on $N$: more plates → narrower distribution → higher efficiency.

**The fundamental assumption:** Equilibration at each plate is *infinitely fast*. In reality, equilibration is finite, and this is captured by the *rate theory* (Van Deemter).

### 2.2 The Rate Theory (Van Deemter Equation, 1956)

When the finite kinetics of mass transfer and molecular diffusion are considered, peak broadening is explained by three independent contributions, and the overall plate height $H$ is given by:

$$
H \;=\; A \;+\; \frac{B}{u} \;+\; C \cdot u
$$

where:

- $A$ = **Eddy diffusion term** (multiple flow paths) — independent of $u$.
- $B/u$ = **Longitudinal (molecular) diffusion term** — dominant at *low* velocities.
- $C \cdot u$ = **Mass-transfer resistance term** (both stationary phase and mobile phase) — dominant at *high* velocities.
- $u$ = linear velocity of the mobile phase (cm/s).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'Why' Behind the Van Deemter Equation:**
> - At **low $u$**, the solute spends a long time in the column, so longitudinal (axial) diffusion *spreads* the band — the $B/u$ term is large.
> - At **high $u$**, the mobile phase moves so fast that the solute cannot equilibrate quickly enough between mobile and stationary phase — the $C \cdot u$ term dominates.
> - There is an **optimum velocity $u_{opt} = \sqrt{B/C}$** at which $H$ is minimum and efficiency is maximum.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Number of theoretical plates (from $t_R$ and peak width at base) | $N = 16 \left( \dfrac{t_R}{w_b} \right)^2$ | dimensionless | Most commonly used in KTU problems |
| 2 | Number of theoretical plates (from half-height width) | $N = 5.54 \left( \dfrac{t_R}{w_{0.5}} \right)^2$ | dimensionless | Used when $w_b$ is hard to measure |
| 3 | Plate height (HETP) | $H = \dfrac{L}{N}$ | mm or cm | $L$ = column length |
| 4 | Van Deemter equation | $H = A + \dfrac{B}{u} + C \cdot u$ | mm | $A, B, C$ depend on solute/column |
| 5 | Optimum mobile-phase velocity | $u_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{B}{C}}$ | cm/s | Minimises $H$ |
| 6 | Minimum plate height | $H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{BC}$ | mm | At $u = u_{opt}$ |
| 7 | Resolution (between two peaks 1 and 2) | $R_s = \dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \dfrac{k'_2}{1 + k'_2}$ | dimensionless | The "Master Equation" of chromatography |
| 8 | Selectivity factor | $\alpha = \dfrac{k'_2}{k'_1} = \dfrac{t_{R2} - t_M}{t_{R1} - t_M}$ | dimensionless | Always $\alpha \geq 1$ |
| 9 | Capacity (retention) factor | $k' = \dfrac{t_R - t_M}{t_M}$ | dimensionless | Measures retention strength |
| 10 | Reduced plate height | $h = \dfrac{H}{d_p}$ | dimensionless | $d_p$ = particle diameter; "good" columns: $h \leq 3$ |
| 11 | Reduced velocity | $\nu = \dfrac{u \cdot d_p}{D_M}$ | dimensionless | $D_M$ = solute diffusivity in mobile phase |
| 12 | Peak standard deviation ↔ width | $w_b = 4\sigma$ ; $w_{0.5} = 2.354\sigma$ | time | For a Gaussian peak |
| 13 | Knox equation (modern) | $h = A \cdot \nu^{0.33} + \dfrac{B}{\nu} + C \cdot \nu$ | dimensionless | More accurate for packed columns |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The resolution master equation ($R_s$ formula, Row 7) is the *single most important equation* in chromatographic method development. It tells the engineer exactly **how to trade off** efficiency (increase $N$ by lengthening column), selectivity (change stationary phase or mobile phase composition), and retention (change $k'$) to achieve a target separation — e.g., separating cis-trans isomers of $\beta$-carotene in food extracts, or quantifying pesticide residues in vegetable oils.

### 2.4 Relationship Between N, H, and Column Length

Because the plate model assumes each "plate" is in series, $N$ scales **linearly** with column length $L$, and $H$ is constant for a given $u$:

$$
N \;=\; \frac{L}{H} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad L \;=\; N \cdot H
$$

Doubling the column length doubles $N$ and therefore improves $R_s$ by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$ — but at the cost of doubled analysis time and doubled pressure drop.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Plate Number $N$ from a Gaussian Peak

A chromatographic peak, when detected as a function of time, follows a Gaussian distribution:

$$
C(t) \;=\; C_{max} \cdot \exp\!\left[\,-\,\frac{(t - t_R)^2}{2\sigma^2}\,\right]
$$

**Step 1 — Define the peak boundaries.**
The peak boundaries are conventionally taken at the **inflection points** of the Gaussian, which lie at $t_R \pm \sigma$. The width of the peak *at the baseline* (tangents drawn from the inflection points to the baseline) is therefore:

$$
w_b \;=\; 4\sigma
$$

**Step 2 — Express $\sigma$ in terms of $t_R$ and $w_b$.**

$$
\sigma \;=\; \frac{w_b}{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the number of theoretical plates.**
By analogy with distillation, one theoretical plate corresponds to a mass-transfer stage. The accepted definition is:

$$
N \;\equiv\; \left(\frac{t_R}{\sigma}\right)^2
$$

This is the "**standard-deviation definition**" of $N$, valid regardless of peak shape (as long as it is approximately Gaussian).

**Step 4 — Substitute $\sigma = w_b/4$ into the definition.**

$$
N \;=\; \left(\frac{t_R}{w_b / 4}\right)^2 \;=\; \left(\frac{4 t_R}{w_b}\right)^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\;N \;=\; 16 \left(\frac{t_R}{w_b}\right)^2\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Repeat using the half-height width.**
At half-height, $C = C_{max}/2$, which gives $w_{0.5} = 2\sqrt{2\ln 2}\,\sigma \approx 2.354\,\sigma$. Substituting:

$$
N \;=\; 5.54 \left(\frac{t_R}{w_{0.5}}\right)^2
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical sanity check.**
For a typical good HPLC column: $t_R = 6.0$ min, $w_b = 0.5$ min.

$$
N = 16 \times \left(\frac{6.0}{0.5}\right)^2 = 16 \times 144 = 2304 \text{ plates}
$$

For a $L = 150$ mm column, $H = 150/2304 \approx 0.065$ mm = **65 µm**, which is realistic for a modern sub-2-µm packed column.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Optimum Velocity from the Van Deemter Equation

**Step 1 — Start with the Van Deemter equation:**

$$
H(u) \;=\; A \;+\; \frac{B}{u} \;+\; C \cdot u
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $H$ with respect to $u$ to find the minimum.**

$$
\frac{dH}{du} \;=\; -\,\frac{B}{u^2} \;+\; C
$$

**Step 3 — Set the derivative to zero (first-order optimality condition).**

$$
-\frac{B}{u^2} + C \;=\; 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad C \;=\; \frac{B}{u^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $u_{opt}$.**

$$
u_{opt}^2 \;=\; \frac{B}{C} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boxed{\;u_{opt} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{B}{C}}\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back into $H(u)$ to find $H_{min}$.**

$$
H_{min} \;=\; A \;+\; \frac{B}{\sqrt{B/C}} \;+\; C\sqrt{B/C} \;=\; A \;+\; 2\sqrt{BC}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;H_{min} \;=\; A + 2\sqrt{BC}\;}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right $N$ Formula in Numerical Problems

| Given in the Problem | Use This Formula | Why |
|---|---|---|
| $t_R$ and $w_b$ (peak width at base) | $N = 16(t_R/w_b)^2$ | Tangent-line definition, 4$\sigma$ width |
| $t_R$ and $w_{0.5}$ (half-height width) | $N = 5.54(t_R/w_{0.5})^2$ | Direct measurement, robust to tailing |
| $t_R$ and peak width at inflection (between tangents) | Use $w_b$ formula | Inflection points correspond to $t_R \pm \sigma$ |
| $t_R$ and plate height $H$ and column length $L$ | $N = L/H$ | Direct definition |
| A, B, C, $u$ of the column | $N = L/H(u)$ via Van Deemter | Combine rate theory + plate model |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — How the Three Van Deemter Contributions Cause Peak Broadening

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INJECTED SOLUTE PLUG of width w_inj] --> B[Enters Column with mobile phase]
    B --> C1[EDDY DIFFUSION A term]
    B --> C2[LONGITUDINAL DIFFUSION B over u term]
    B --> C3[MASS TRANSFER RESISTANCE C times u term]
    C1 --> D[Each molecule takes a different path around particles]
    C2 --> E[Random molecular motion spreads the band along axis]
    C3 --> F[Finite equilibration time between mobile and stationary phase]
    D --> G[SUM OF THREE BROADENING CONTRIBUTIONS]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Total peak variance sigma squared total]
    H --> I[Calculate H from Van Deemter]
    I --> J[Calculate N from L divided by H]
    J --> K[Calculate Rs from master equation]
    K --> L[Two separated peaks of baseline width w_b]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — The Plate Model Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[PLATE 0 Injection] --> P1[PLATE 1 Equilibration]
    P1 --> P2[PLATE 2 Equilibration]
    P2 --> P3[PLATE 3 Equilibration]
    P3 --> P4[PLATE N Equilibration]
    P4 --> P5[DETECTOR Gaussian peak]
    style P0 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style P1 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style P2 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style P3 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style P4 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style P5 fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — The Van Deemter Curve (H vs. u)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low velocity u] --> B[Longitudinal diffusion dominates B over u is LARGE]
    B --> C[H is high and column is inefficient]
    C --> D[Velocity u increases]
    D --> E[Mass transfer term C times u grows]
    E --> F[Curves cross at u_opt]
    F --> G[Minimum H = A plus 2 root BC]
    G --> H[Maximum efficiency N_max]
    H --> I[High velocity u]
    I --> J[Mass transfer dominates C times u is LARGE]
    J --> K[H is high again]
    K --> L[Column is inefficient at high u]
    style F fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Resolution Master Equation and the Three Tuning Levers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Resolution Rs target equals 1.5 for baseline separation] --> N_LEV[Efficiency term root N over 4]
    R --> A_LEV[Selectivity term alpha minus 1 over alpha]
    R --> K_LEV[Retention term k2 prime over 1 plus k2 prime]
    N_LEV --> N_ACT[Action: increase column length L or reduce particle size dp]
    A_LEV --> A_ACT[Action: change stationary phase chemistry or mobile phase composition]
    K_LEV --> K_ACT[Action: tune mobile phase strength e.g. percent organic in reverse phase HPLC]
    N_ACT --> OUT[OPTIMISED CHROMATOGRAM with sharp well resolved peaks]
    A_ACT --> OUT
    K_ACT --> OUT
    style R fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style OUT fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
Define "Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate" (HETP). State its SI unit and explain what a *low* HETP value indicates about column performance.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *Definition (2 marks):* HETP, denoted $H$, is the length of column corresponding to one theoretical plate. It is defined as $H = L/N$, where $L$ is the column length and $N$ is the number of theoretical plates.
- *Unit (½ mark):* Expressed in **mm** or **µm** in chromatography.
- *Significance (½ mark):* A low $H$ means high efficiency — narrower peaks, better resolution per unit length of column.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
List the three terms of the **Van Deemter equation** and state how each one affects peak broadening in a chromatographic column.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Eddy diffusion term (A):** Caused by multiple flow paths of different lengths around packing particles; independent of mobile-phase velocity.
2. **Longitudinal diffusion term (B/u):** Caused by axial molecular diffusion of solute in the mobile phase; dominant at *low* velocity.
3. **Mass-transfer resistance term (C·u):** Caused by finite time for solute to equilibrate between mobile and stationary phases; dominant at *high* velocity.

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **number of theoretical plates ($N$)** in terms of retention time and peak width at base for a Gaussian chromatographic peak. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In an HPLC analysis, a solute elutes with retention time $t_R = 8.40$ min and the peak width at base is $w_b = 0.70$ min. The column is 150 mm long. Calculate:
  (i) the number of theoretical plates $N$,
  (ii) the plate height $H$ in mm, and
  (iii) the number of plates the column would have if its length were doubled (assume $H$ remains constant). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of $N = 16(t_R/w_b)^2$ (7 marks):**

For a Gaussian chromatographic peak, the elution profile is:

$$
C(t) = C_{max} \cdot \exp\!\left[-\frac{(t - t_R)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]
$$

The peak is bounded by the two tangent lines drawn from the **inflection points** (located at $t = t_R \pm \sigma$) to the baseline. The intercepts on the baseline are at $t_R - 2\sigma$ and $t_R + 2\sigma$, giving the baseline width:

$$
w_b = 4\sigma \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \sigma = \frac{w_b}{4}
$$

By definition, the number of theoretical plates is the squared ratio of the retention time to the standard deviation:

$$
N \equiv \left(\frac{t_R}{\sigma}\right)^2
$$

Substituting $\sigma = w_b/4$:

$$
N = \left(\frac{4 t_R}{w_b}\right)^2 = 16\left(\frac{t_R}{w_b}\right)^2 \qquad \blacksquare
$$

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Number of theoretical plates (2 marks):**

$$
N = 16 \left(\frac{8.40}{0.70}\right)^2 = 16 \times (12)^2 = 16 \times 144
$$

$$
\boxed{N = 2304 \text{ plates}}
$$

**(ii) Plate height (2 marks):**

$$
H = \frac{L}{N} = \frac{150 \text{ mm}}{2304} = 0.0651 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{H = 0.0651 \text{ mm} = 65.1 \text{ µm}}
$$

**(iii) Doubled column length (3 marks):**
If $L$ is doubled to 300 mm and $H$ remains constant at 0.0651 mm, then:

$$
N_{new} = \frac{L_{new}}{H} = \frac{300}{0.0651} = 4608
$$

Alternatively, since $H$ is constant, $N$ scales linearly with $L$:

$$
N_{new} = 2 \times N = 2 \times 2304 = 4608 \text{ plates}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{new} = 4608 \text{ plates}}
$$

This illustrates the **square-root law** of resolution: doubling $N$ improves $R_s$ by only a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$, not 2 — which is why *selectivity* and *retention tuning* are often more powerful than simply lengthening the column.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** State the **Van Deemter equation** and explain the physical origin of each of its three terms. Sketch (describe) the shape of the $H$ vs. $u$ curve and mark the **optimum velocity**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A packed GC column has Van Deemter constants: $A = 0.05$ cm, $B = 0.30$ cm²/s, $C = 0.05$ s.
  (i) Calculate the optimum mobile-phase velocity $u_{opt}$ and the corresponding minimum plate height $H_{min}$.
  (ii) The column is 30 m long. How many theoretical plates does it deliver at $u_{opt}$?
  (iii) If the linear velocity is increased to 5 cm/s, what is the new plate height, and what fraction of the maximum efficiency is lost? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theory (7 marks):**

The Van Deemter equation is:

$$
H(u) = A + \frac{B}{u} + C \cdot u
$$

| Term | Name | Physical Origin | Velocity Dependence |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | Eddy diffusion | Different streamline paths around packing particles | Constant (velocity-independent) |
| $B/u$ | Longitudinal diffusion | Axial Brownian diffusion of solute in mobile phase | Decreases as $u$ increases |
| $C \cdot u$ | Mass-transfer resistance | Finite time for solute to equilibrate between mobile and stationary phases (both film and intra-particle) | Increases linearly with $u$ |

*Curve shape:* As $u \to 0$, $B/u \to \infty$, so $H \to \infty$. As $u \to \infty$, $C \cdot u \to \infty$, so $H \to \infty$. The curve has a **U-shape** with a unique minimum at $u_{opt} = \sqrt{B/C}$, where $H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{BC}$. (1 mark for the verbal sketch.)

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Optimum velocity and $H_{min}$ (2 marks):**

$$
u_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{B}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.30}{0.05}} = \sqrt{6} = 2.449 \text{ cm/s}
$$

$$
H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{BC} = 0.05 + 2\sqrt{0.30 \times 0.05} = 0.05 + 2\sqrt{0.015}
$$

$$
\sqrt{0.015} = 0.1225 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad H_{min} = 0.05 + 2(0.1225) = 0.295 \text{ cm}
$$

$$
\boxed{u_{opt} = 2.449 \text{ cm/s}, \quad H_{min} = 0.295 \text{ cm}}
$$

**(ii) Number of plates at $u_{opt}$ (2 marks):**
Column length $L = 30$ m = 3000 cm.

$$
N = \frac{L}{H_{min}} = \frac{3000}{0.295} = 10169 \text{ plates}
$$

$$
\boxed{N \approx 10{,}170 \text{ plates}}
$$

**(iii) New plate height at $u = 5$ cm/s and efficiency loss (3 marks):**

$$
H(5) = 0.05 + \frac{0.30}{5} + 0.05 \times 5 = 0.05 + 0.06 + 0.25 = 0.360 \text{ cm}
$$

Number of plates at this velocity:

$$
N(5) = \frac{3000}{0.360} = 8333 \text{ plates}
$$

Efficiency loss fraction:

$$
\text{Loss} = \frac{N_{opt} - N(5)}{N_{opt}} = \frac{10169 - 8333}{10169} = \frac{1836}{10169} \approx 0.1806
$$

$$
\boxed{H(5\,\text{cm/s}) = 0.360 \text{ cm}, \quad \text{Efficiency loss} \approx 18.1\%}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up $w_b$, $w_{0.5}$ and $w_i$ (inflection-point width).** Each has a *different* constant (16, 5.54, 4) in the $N$ formula. Using 16 with $w_{0.5}$ will inflate $N$ by a factor of 2.88.
> 2. **Forgetting that $t_R$ in the $N$ formula is the *retention* time, not the adjusted retention time.** $N$ uses the *total* $t_R$ from injection to peak max; the selectivity $\alpha$ uses adjusted times.
> 3. **Wrong units in $H_{min}$ formula.** Van Deemter $A, B, C$ must be in **consistent** units (cm, cm²/s, s). Mixing mm and cm will silently corrupt the answer.
> 4. **In the master resolution equation, students often write $\sqrt{N}/2$ instead of $\sqrt{N}/4$** — the constant is 4, not 2. Memorise: $R_s = \dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{4}\cdot\dfrac{\alpha-1}{\alpha}\cdot\dfrac{k'_2}{1+k'_2}$.

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Efficiency** is measured by **$N$** (number of theoretical plates) and **$H$** (height per plate); $N = L/H$.
- Two principal formulas for $N$ from a Gaussian peak: $N = 16(t_R/w_b)^2$ and $N = 5.54(t_R/w_{0.5})^2$.
- **Van Deemter equation**: $H = A + B/u + C \cdot u$ — three broadening mechanisms in series.
- **Optimum velocity**: $u_{opt} = \sqrt{B/C}$, at which $H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{BC}$.
- **Plate model** assumes instantaneous equilibrium at each plate (idealised). **Rate theory** (Van Deemter) accounts for finite kinetics and is more realistic.
- **Master resolution equation**: $R_s = \dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{4}\cdot\dfrac{\alpha-1}{\alpha}\cdot\dfrac{k'_2}{1+k'_2}$ — three independent tuning levers.
- **Doubling column length** doubles $N$ but improves $R_s$ by only $\sqrt{2}$.
- **Selectivity tuning** (changing stationary phase or mobile phase) is usually more powerful than merely increasing $N$.
- **Typical modern HPLC column:** $H \approx 5$–$10$ µm; $N \approx 5000$–$20{,}000$ plates per 150 mm.
- **For baseline separation**, target $R_s \geq 1.5$.
- **Food-tech relevance:** quantifying sugars, vitamins, pigments, fatty acids, pesticide residues, mycotoxins, and additives in food matrices — efficiency determines *how cleanly* these can be measured.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Method and applications of different chromatographic techniques in food processing: liquid chromatography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT527 - SEPARATION PROCESSES IN FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Method and applications of different chromatographic techniques in food processing: liquid chromatography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Liquid Chromatography (LC) — The Definitional Framework

**Liquid Chromatography (LC)** is a physical separation technique in which the **mobile phase is a liquid** (or mixture of liquids) that carries the analyte mixture through a **stationary phase** (typically a solid or a liquid supported on a solid). Separation is achieved based on differences in the **partition coefficient**, **adsorption affinity**, **ionic interaction**, **molecular size**, or **biochemical affinity** of the analytes between the two phases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Under Module 3, the focus is on understanding the *method* (instrumentation, mode of separation, mobile/stationary phase selection) and *applications* (qualitative and quantitative analysis of food components such as sugars, vitamins, fatty acids, pigments, mycotoxins, contaminants, additives, and adulterants).

In food technology, LC is the **workhorse analytical method** for compounds that are:
- **Non-volatile** (cannot be analyzed by Gas Chromatography)
- **Thermally labile** (degrade at high temperatures, e.g., vitamins, antioxidants)
- **Polar to moderately polar** (sugars, amino acids, organic acids, polyphenols)

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Marble Race in a Sandbox"

Imagine a **sloping racetrack filled with sand** (the stationary phase). You pour water (**mobile phase**) at the top, and you drop several different **colored marbles** (analytes) of varying sizes and surface textures. 

- **Big, smooth marbles** roll quickly down the slope — they interact little with the sand → **elute first**.
- **Small, sticky marbles** get trapped in sand grains → **elute later**.

The detector at the bottom "scores" each marble as it crosses the finish line, producing a **chromatogram** (a graph of detector response vs. time). Each compound produces a characteristic peak at a specific **retention time (tR)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Retention Time (tR):** The time elapsed between sample injection and the peak maximum of a given analyte. It is the *qualitative fingerprint* of a compound under fixed chromatographic conditions.

### Classification of Liquid Chromatography

| Sub-Technique | Separation Principle | Typical Food Application |
|---|---|---|
| **High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC)** | Partition/adsorption under pressure | Sugars, vitamins, preservatives |
| **Ultra-Performance LC (UPLC)** | Sub-2 µm particles, high pressure | Trace mycotoxins, pesticide residues |
| **Ion-Exchange Chromatography (IEC)** | Charge differences | Amino acids, organic acids |
| **Size-Exclusion Chromatography (SEC/GPC)** | Molecular size | Polymer molecular weight, protein aggregates |
| **Affinity Chromatography** | Biological specificity | Lectins, enzymes, glycoproteins |
| **Partition Chromatography** | Solubility differences (normal/reversed phase) | Lipids, carotenoids, polyphenols |
| **Adsorption Chromatography** | Surface adsorption | Pigments, sterols |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** HPLC operating pressures are typically **5–15 MPa (50–150 bar)**, with flow rates of **0.1–10 mL/min** and column internal diameters of **2.1–4.6 mm**. UPLC operates at **> 40 MPa (400 bar)** with sub-2 µm particles.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Separation Equation

The retention of an analyte in chromatography is governed by the **distribution (or partition) coefficient K**:

$$K = \frac{C_s}{C_m}$$

where:
- $C_s$ = concentration of analyte in the stationary phase
- $C_m$ = concentration of analyte in the mobile phase

**Retention Factor (k)** — the experimentally measured, dimensionless quantity that determines how long a compound is retained:

$$k = \frac{t_R - t_M}{t_M}$$

where:
- $t_R$ = retention time of the analyte
- $t_M$ = **column dead time** (time for an unretained compound to pass through)

**Optimal range (KTU high-yield):** $1 \leq k \leq 10$. If $k < 1$, the compound elutes too close to the void volume; if $k > 10$, peaks become broad and analysis is slow.

### 2.2 The Resolution Equation — The Heart of Method Development

The **Resolution (Rs)** between two adjacent peaks (1 and 2) is given by the **Purnell Equation**:

$$R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{(\alpha - 1)}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k_2}{1 + k_2}$$

This is **THE most important equation** in KTU chromatographic problems. The three terms are:

1. **Efficiency term** $\frac{\sqrt{N}}{4}$ — depends on column parameters
2. **Selectivity term** $\frac{(\alpha - 1)}{\alpha}$ — depends on chemistry of mobile/stationary phase
3. **Retention term** $\frac{k_2}{1 + k_2}$ — depends on retention factor

**Separation Factor (α):**

$$\alpha = \frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{t_{R2} - t_M}{t_{R1} - t_M}$$

α is **always > 1** for separated peaks. α = 1 means co-elution.

### 2.3 Plate Theory & Efficiency

The **Number of Theoretical Plates (N)** quantifies column efficiency:

$$N = 16 \cdot \left(\frac{t_R}{W}\right)^2 = 5.54 \cdot \left(\frac{t_R}{W_{1/2}}\right)^2$$

where:
- $W$ = peak width at baseline
- $W_{1/2}$ = peak width at half-height

**Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate (HETP):**

$$H = \frac{L}{N}$$

The **Van Deemter Equation** relates efficiency to flow:

$$H = A + \frac{B}{u} + C \cdot u$$

where:
- $A$ = Eddy diffusion term (depends on packing)
- $B/u$ = Longitudinal diffusion term
- $C \cdot u$ = Mass transfer term
- $u$ = linear velocity of mobile phase

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** In food labs, N typically ranges from **5,000 to 20,000 plates per meter** for modern HPLC columns. Higher N → narrower peaks → better resolution → lower detection limits for trace contaminants.

### 2.4 The KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Partition Coefficient | $K = C_s / C_m$ | dimensionless | Predicts retention order |
| 2 | Retention Factor | $k = (t_R - t_M)/t_M$ | dimensionless | Method optimization |
| 3 | Separation Factor | $\alpha = k_2/k_1$ | dimensionless | Selectivity measurement |
| 4 | Number of Plates | $N = 16(t_R/W)^2$ | plates | Column efficiency |
| 5 | Plate Height | $H = L/N$ | µm or mm | Packing quality |
| 6 | Resolution | $R_s = \dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{4}\cdot\dfrac{(\alpha-1)}{\alpha}\cdot\dfrac{k_2}{1+k_2}$ | dimensionless | Quality of separation |
| 7 | Van Deemter | $H = A + B/u + C\cdot u$ | mm | Optimum flow rate |
| 8 | Linear Velocity | $u = L/t_M$ | mm/s | Flow programming |
| 9 | Capacity Factor Range | $1 \leq k \leq 10$ | — | Practical optimum |
| 10 | Resolution Target | $R_s \geq 1.5$ (baseline) | — | Industry standard |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the symbols. The Greek letter $\alpha$ is the **selectivity**, NOT the retention factor. $k$ is retention. Memorize the difference — it costs marks in ESE.

### 2.5 Instrumentation Architecture

A liquid chromatograph consists of **six modules** in series:

1. **Solvent Reservoir** — holds degassed mobile phase
2. **Pump** — delivers constant flow (isocratic) or gradient flow
3. **Injector** — introduces sample (typically 1–100 µL volume)
4. **Column** — the heart of separation (15–25 cm length, packed with 3–10 µm particles)
5. **Detector** — senses eluting compounds (UV-Vis, FLD, RID, MS)
6. **Data System** — records chromatogram and integrates peak areas

### 2.6 Modes of LC — Detailed Mechanics

#### A. Normal-Phase Chromatography (NP-LC)
- **Stationary phase:** Polar (silica, –OH, –CN, –NH₂)
- **Mobile phase:** Non-polar (hexane, dichloromethane, isopropanol)
- **Elution order:** **Polar compounds elute LATER** (they are retained on the polar surface)
- **Food applications:** Separation of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K), carotenoids, sterols, triglycerides classes

#### B. Reversed-Phase Chromatography (RP-LC) — Most Common
- **Stationary phase:** Non-polar (C18, C8, C4, phenyl; particle surface derivatized with alkyl chains)
- **Mobile phase:** Polar (water + acetonitrile or water + methanol, often buffered)
- **Elution order:** **Polar compounds elute FIRST**; non-polar compounds retained longer
- **Food applications:** The dominant mode — analysis of sugars, polyphenols, antioxidants, preservatives (BHA, BHT), artificial sweeteners, caffeine, mycotoxins, pesticides

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** C18 (octadecylsilane, ODS) is the most widely used stationary phase in food analysis. Approximately **70% of all HPLC analyses** of food components use a C18 column with a water/acetonitrile gradient.

#### C. Ion-Exchange Chromatography (IEC)
- **Principle:** Analyte ions exchange with counter-ions on the stationary phase
- **Two types:** Cation-exchange (uses –SO₃⁻ groups) and Anion-exchange (uses –N(CH₃)₃⁺ groups)
- **Food applications:** Amino acids, organic acids (citric, malic, lactic), nucleotides, anions in beverages

#### D. Size-Exclusion Chromatography (SEC/GPC)
- **Principle:** Separation by hydrodynamic volume; large molecules excluded from pores → elute first
- **Food applications:** Molecular weight distribution of starch, pectin, alginates, proteins, gums

#### E. Affinity Chromatography
- **Principle:** Specific biological interaction (e.g., enzyme-substrate, antibody-antigen, lectin-sugar)
- **Food applications:** Purification of enzymes (e.g., lactase, amylase), lectins from legumes, glycoproteins

### 2.7 Detectors — The "Eyes" of the System

| Detector | Detection Principle | Sensitivity | Food Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **UV-Vis / DAD** | Absorbance at fixed λ or full spectrum | ng level | Phenolics, vitamins, preservatives |
| **Fluorescence (FLD)** | Excitation → emission | pg level (1000× more sensitive than UV) | Aflatoxins, riboflavin, PAHs |
| **Refractive Index (RID)** | Δ in refractive index | µg level | Sugars, simple carbohydrates |
| **Evaporative Light Scattering (ELSD)** | Light scattering by non-volatile particles | µg level | Lipids, triglycerides, surfactants |
| **Mass Spectrometry (LC-MS)** | Mass-to-charge ratio (m/z) | fg level | Mycotoxins, veterinary drug residues, pesticide screening |
| **Electrochemical (ECD)** | Oxidation-reduction current | pg level | Antioxidants, biogenic amines |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard:** UV-Vis at **254 nm** is the most frequently cited detector in food analysis problems. The Beer–Lambert relationship $A = \varepsilon \cdot b \cdot c$ governs quantitation, where **A** is absorbance, **ε** is the molar extinction coefficient, **b** is path length (typically **1 cm**), and **c** is concentration.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the food industry, LC methods are validated for:
- **Identity** (retention time match with standard ±2%)
- **Purity** (peak homogeneity, no co-elution)
- **Quantitation** (linearity, LOD, LOQ)
- **Recovery** (accuracy, 80–120% acceptable)
- **Precision** (RSD ≤ 5%)

These metrics are mandated by **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)**, **Codex Alimentarius**, and **ISO 17025** for accredited food testing labs in India.
