- **pH reduction:** pH 4.5–5.0 protonates the imidazole nitrogen.
- **Chelation:** EDTA strips the metal, releasing the protein.

**Application – Purification of His-tagged food enzymes:**

A recombinant food enzyme (e.g., **chitinase** for fungal control in fruits, or **α-amylase** for starch processing) is engineered with a 6×His tag at the N- or C-terminus and expressed in *E. coli* or *Pichia pastoris*. After cell lysis, the crude extract is loaded on a Ni-NTA column at pH 7.4. Non-tagged *E. coli* proteins elute in the wash. The chitinase is eluted with 250 mM imidazole, yielding a single-step purity >90% with high recovery (>85%).

**Advantages of IMAC in food industry:**
- Low cost of matrix.
- High capacity (10–40 mg·mL⁻¹).
- Excellent reusability (>500 cycles with proper recharging).
- Compatible with denaturing conditions (8 M urea, 6 M guanidine-HCl) for solubilizing inclusion bodies.
- FSSAI approval for food-enzyme processing aids is straightforward.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Module 3]**

(a) **Explain lectin affinity chromatography. Discuss its role in glycoprotein isolation from food sources.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
Lectin affinity chromatography uses immobilized lectins (carbohydrate-binding proteins of non-immune origin) as ligands. Lectins recognize specific sugar residues (e.g., mannose, glucose, galactose, N-acetylglucosamine) with high affinity and reversibility.

**Common lectins and their specificities:**

| Lectin | Source | Sugar Specificity |
|--------|--------|-------------------|
| ConA (Concanavalin A) | Jack bean | α-D-mannose, α-D-glucose |
| WGA (Wheat germ agglutinin) | Wheat germ | N-acetylglucosamine (GlcNAc) |
| PNA (Peanut agglutinin) | Peanut | Galactose-β-1,3-GalNAc |
| SBA (Soybean agglutinin) | Soybean | N-acetylgalactosamine |

**Process:**
1. ConA-Sepharose is equilibrated with 20 mM phosphate buffer, pH 7.4, containing 0.5 M NaCl and 1 mM CaCl₂, MnCl₂, MgCl₂ (metal ions essential for ConA activity).
2. Crude food extract (e.g., soy protein hydrolysate) is loaded.
3. Glycoproteins with mannose-rich N-glycans bind; non-glycosylated proteins elute in the wash.
4. Elution: 100–200 mM **α-methyl-D-mannoside** (competitive sugar) or low pH (4.0).

**Role in glycoprotein isolation from food sources:**
- **Soybean glycoproteins:** Glycinin and β-conglycinin are major glycoproteins; ConA binds the mannose-rich 7S fraction.
- **Egg white proteins:** Ovalbumin (a glycoprotein) is purified on ConA.
- **Whey glycoproteins:** β-lactoglobulin and lactoferrin are glycoforms amenable to ConA purification.
- **Yeast cell wall proteins:** Mannoproteins are highly retained on ConA columns.

**Significance:** Lectin affinity allows **discrimination between glyco-isoforms** of the same protein — impossible with IEX or HIC.

(b) **With a suitable example, explain immunoaffinity chromatography. State its application in mycotoxin detection in food.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:**
Immunoaffinity chromatography (IAC) employs **antibodies (usually monoclonal IgG)** as the immobilized ligand, exploiting the highly specific **antigen–antibody (Ag–Ab) interaction** with $K_d$ values in the range **10⁻⁸ to 10⁻¹⁰ M**.

**Steps:**
1. Antibodies are raised against the target (antigen) in animals (rabbit, mouse) or by hybridoma technology (monoclonal).
2. IgG is purified on Protein A/G-Sepharose and coupled to CNBr-activated Sepharose 4B.
3. Sample is loaded; antigen binds selectively.
4. Elution: typically pH 2.5 (glycine-HCl) or 3 M KSCN — both disrupt non-covalent Ag–Ab interactions.

**Example – Purification of Ovalbumin from Egg White:**
Anti-ovalbumin IgG is immobilized on Sepharose. Crude egg white is loaded; ovalbumin binds; albumin (non-target) and lysozyme pass through. Elution at pH 2.5 yields pure ovalbumin.

**Application in Mycotoxin Detection:**

Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) is a potent hepatocarcinogen contaminating peanuts, maize, and spices. **AFB1 immunoaffinity columns** (e.g., Aflaprep M, AflaTest) are the **gold standard** for sample cleanup before HPLC-FLD analysis:

1. Ground food sample is extracted with 80% methanol.
2. Extract is diluted and passed through the IAC column.
3. AFB1 binds to anti-AFB1 antibody; interferences pass through.
4. Column is washed with water.
5. AFB1 is eluted with methanol (2 mL).
6. Eluate is injected into HPLC-FLD for quantification (LOQ: **0.1 μg·kg⁻¹**).

**Advantages of IAC in food safety:**
- Extremely high selectivity (one-shot cleanup).
- Complies with **AOAC Official Method 2000.16**, **EC 401/2006**, and **FSSAI regulations**.
- Replaceable single-use columns avoid cross-contamination.
- Detection limits comply with maximum permitted levels: AFB1 = 5 μg·kg⁻¹ in spices (FSSAI); 2 μg·kg⁻¹ in cereals (EU).

**Limitations:**
- Antibody cost is high.
- Limited column reusability (10–30 cycles) due to denaturation at low pH.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Confusing "affinity" with "adsorption":** Affinity specifically implies *biological recognition*. Do not write about "general adsorption" — you will lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the spacer arm:** If a question asks for components, ALWAYS mention **spacer arm** as a separate entity. Many students omit it and lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Writing "Con A" instead of "Concanavalin A" or "ConA":** Use the full name at first mention.
> 4. **Numerical mistakes in $P_f$ and $Y$:** Always carry units through; do not mix U and mg. The KTU board examiner deducts 1 mark per unit inconsistency.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram label list:** In 7-mark questions with diagrams, you must label **at least 6 parts** of the setup — empty diagrams fetch 0 marks for the diagram portion.
> 6. **Writing "antibody" without specifying monoclonal/polyclonal:** Be precise — specify "monoclonal IgG" for reproducibility and regulatory compliance.
> 7. **Forgetting to state regeneration step:** The KTU 2024 rubric awards 1 mark for the "regeneration and re-equilibration" step in process descriptions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Affinity chromatography** is the **highest-selectivity** LC technique, exploiting **biological recognition** between an immobilized ligand and target biomolecule.
- **Bind–Wash–Elute–Regenerate** is the universal four-step protocol; ALWAYS describe all four.
- The three essential components of an affinity system are **Matrix + Spacer Arm + Ligand**. Spacer arm length (6–12 atoms) is critical to overcome steric hindrance.
- **Pseudo-affinity** (Cibacron Blue, IMAC, Heparin) is **cheaper and more stable** than biospecific ligands, and is widely used in industrial food processing.
- **IMAC (Ni²⁺-NTA)** is the workhorse for recombinant His-tagged food enzymes; elute with **250 mM imidazole**.
- **Lectin affinity (ConA)** is the method of choice for **glycoprotein** purification (e.g., β-lactoglobulin, ovalbumin, lactoferrin).
- **Immunoaffinity (IAC)** is the **gold standard** for **mycotoxin cleanup** in food safety analysis (Aflatoxin, Ochratoxin, Zearalenone).
- **Heparin affinity** is used for **lipases, lipoproteins, and coagulation factors**.
- Typical **purification fold** in a single step = **100× to 10000×** (vs. 2–5× for IEX or GFC).
- **Elution strategies:** pH shift, ionic strength change, competitive analog, denaturant, or specific cleavage of linker.
- **Industrial form:** **Expanded Bed Adsorption (EBA)** allows direct processing of unclarified broths — eliminates centrifugation/filtration.
- For **KTU numerical problems**: master the **Langmuir isotherm**, **purification fold** ($P_f$), and **recovery yield** ($Y$) formulas.
- **Association constant** $K_a$ ranges from **10³ to 10¹² M⁻¹**; stronger binding = lower $K_d$ = harder elution.
- **Standard binding buffer:** 20 mM phosphate, pH 7.4, 0.15 M NaCl; **standard elution buffer:** 0.1 M glycine-HCl, pH 2.5.
- **Column lifetime:** 50–500 cycles depending on ligand stability; IMAC > Cibacron Blue > antibodies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examination Strategy for Module 3:**
> - Part A (3 marks): Definition + 1 application = 3 sentences minimum.
> - Part B (14 marks): Principle (3) + Diagram (2) + Components (2) + Application in food (4) + Advantages/Limitations (3).
> - Always draw a **labelled block diagram** for full marks.
> - Memorize **at least 3 ligand–target pairs** for fast recall: ConA–glycoprotein, Ni²⁺–His-tag, anti-AFB1–Aflatoxin B1.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Immunochromatography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT527 - SEPARATION PROCESSES IN FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Immunochromatography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Immunochromatography (IC)**, also known as **Lateral Flow Immunoassay (LFIA)**, is a membrane-based, biospecific separation and detection technique that combines the selectivity of antigen–antibody (immunochemical) reactions with the rapid, one-step chromatographic migration of fluids through a porous carrier. It is the cornerstone of **Point-of-Care Testing (POCT)** in food safety, clinical diagnostics, and environmental monitoring.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of **Separation Processes in Food Technology**, immunochromatography is studied as a **rapid bioseparation technique** for detecting chemical, biological, and microbial contaminants in food matrices without requiring sophisticated laboratory instrumentation.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Molecular Checkpoint"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — Imagine a Security Checkpoint at an Airport:**
> - A passenger (the **target analyte**, e.g., *Salmonella* protein, aflatoxin, peanut allergen) walks down a corridor (**the porous membrane strip**).
> - Before entering, they pick up a brightly colored sticker (**the labeled antibody** conjugated to colloidal gold or colored latex).
> - At a designated lane, an **immobilized capture antibody** recognizes the sticker-analyte complex and stops it — forming a visible colored line (the **TEST line**).
> - A second checkpoint always stops any sticker, regardless of passenger — this is the **CONTROL line**, confirming the test worked.
> - Unbound material is wicked away into a sponge (**absorbent pad**).

This single-pass, capillary-driven molecular checkpoint is precisely how an immunochromatographic strip functions.

### 1.3 Classification of Immunochromatographic Formats

| Format Type | Target Analyte Size | Visible Result Interpretation | Typical Food-Safety Application |
|-------------|---------------------|-------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Sandwich (non-competitive)** | Large analytes ($\geq$ 1 kDa), e.g., proteins, pathogens | **Two lines = Positive**, one line (control only) = Negative | *Listeria monocytogenes*, *Salmonella* |
| **Competitive (inhibition)** | Small analytes (< 1 kDa), e.g., toxins, drug residues, pesticides | **One line (control only) = Positive**, two lines = Negative | Aflatoxin B1, ochratoxin, chloramphenicol |
| **Multiplex / Array** | Multiple targets simultaneously | Distinct lines for each target | Simultaneous allergen + pathogen screening |
| **Quantitative (reader-based)** | Any | Line intensity read by optical scanner | Quantitative aflatoxin in groundnuts |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in IC

> [!TIP]
> - **Detection Limit (LOD)** is typically expressed in **ng/mL**, **pg/mL**, or **CFU/mL** (colony-forming units for pathogens).
> - **Assay time:** **5–15 minutes** (qualitative), **15–30 minutes** (semi-quantitative with reader).
> - **Sample volume:** **10–200 μL**.
> - **Storage temperature:** **2 °C – 30 °C** for most commercial kits; **room temperature stable** for lyophilized formats.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Sequence — The Six Logical Phases

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An immunochromatographic test progresses through **six ordered phases**. Mastery of these phases is essential for both theory and KTU board questions.

1. **Sample Introduction & Reconstitution Phase**
   The liquid sample (extract, serum, urine, food homogenate) is deposited on the **sample pad** (often a glass fiber or cellulose membrane pre-treated with buffers, surfactants like Tween 20, and blocking proteins such as BSA). This pad conditions the pH and ionic strength, ensuring the analyte is in an optimal physicochemical state for binding.

2. **Rehydration & Release of Conjugates Phase**
   The fluid front migrates to the **conjugate pad** (glass fiber, polyester, or polyethersulfone), rehydrating **detection antibodies** pre-immobilized there. These antibodies are covalently conjugated to colored labels — most commonly **colloidal gold nanoparticles (AuNPs, 20–40 nm)**, dyed latex microspheres, or luminescent/fluorescent tags.

3. **Immunoreaction (Binding Kinetics) Phase**
   The analyte in the sample and the labeled detection antibody form a soluble **analyte–detection antibody complex** following second-order binding kinetics. This reaction must reach a measurable degree of completion during the brief capillary transit time.

4. **Capillary Transport & Capture Phase**
   The fluid front, carrying the complexes, migrates across the **test membrane** (almost always **nitrocellulose**, pore size **5–15 μm**, capillary flow time **90–180 s per 4 cm**). At the **TEST line**, immobilized **capture antibodies** are pre-deposited in a narrow band. These capture the complex, concentrating the colored label into a visible line.

5. **Control Line Verification Phase**
   Further downstream, a **secondary antibody** (anti-species IgG) is immobilized at the **CONTROL line**. It captures any excess labeled detection antibody, providing an internal validation that fluid flow and reagent release occurred correctly.

6. **Wicking & Termination Phase**
   The **absorbent pad** (cellulose paper, capacity **≥ 5× the sample volume**) maintains the capillary pressure gradient, ensuring the reaction front traverses the entire strip uniformly and stopping the background.

### 2.2 The "Why and How" of Each Phase

> [!NOTE]
> **Why nitrocellulose?** Its high protein-binding capacity (~80–100 μg/cm²) and uniform capillary flow make it the gold-standard membrane. **Why colloidal gold?** It provides an intense, naked-eye-visible red color, is stable, and has a large surface area for antibody conjugation. **Why a control line?** It eliminates false negatives from manufacturing defects or operator error.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Utility |
|---|-------------------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Lucas–Washburn equation:** $L^2 = \dfrac{\gamma \, r \, \cos\theta}{2\eta} \cdot t$ | $L$ = wetted length, $\gamma$ = surface tension, $r$ = pore radius, $\theta$ = contact angle, $\eta$ = viscosity, $t$ = time | Predicts capillary flow front velocity through the nitrocellulose membrane |
| 2 | **Second-order binding kinetics:** $\dfrac{d[AB]}{dt} = k_{on}[Ag][Ab] - k_{off}[AB]$ | $[Ag]$ = analyte, $[Ab]$ = antibody, $k_{on}$ (M⁻¹s⁻¹), $k_{off}$ (s⁻¹) | Determines affinity and required incubation time on the strip |
| 3 | **Equilibrium dissociation constant:** $K_D = \dfrac{k_{off}}{k_{on}}$ | $K_D$ in **mol/L (M)** | Quantifies antibody–antigen affinity; lower $K_D$ = higher sensitivity |
| 4 | **LOD definition (IC):** $LOD = \bar{x}_{blank} + 3 \sigma_{blank}$ | $\bar{x}_{blank}$ = mean blank signal, $\sigma_{blank}$ = standard deviation | Statistical basis for detection limit reporting |
| 5 | **Sensitivity / Specificity:** $Sens = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$, $Spec = \dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | TP, FN, TN, FP from validation studies | Validates IC kit performance against reference (HPLC/ELISA) |
| 6 | **Gold nanoparticle absorption (SPR):** $\lambda_{max} \approx 520 \text{ nm}$ (20–40 nm AuNP) | Surface Plasmon Resonance peak | Optical basis for red color of label at test line |
| 7 | **Beer–Lambert (for quantitative readers):** $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$ | $A$ = absorbance, $\varepsilon$ = molar absorptivity, $c$ = concentration, $l$ = path length | Quantifies test line intensity via strip reader |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not use the vertical pipe `$\vert x \vert$` inside markdown table cells. In your answer sheets, use absolute value bars only in math mode outside tables, or use $\mid$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Food Technology

- **HACCP Programs:** On-site screening of mycotoxins (aflatoxin M1 in milk) at dairy reception points.
- **Cold Chain Logistics:** Pre-shipment verification of *Listeria* and *Salmonella* in ready-to-eat foods.
- **Retailer Compliance:** Rapid gluten, peanut, and tree-nut allergen detection for labeling verification.
- **Export Quality Control:** Screening for veterinary drug residues (chloramphenicol, nitrofurans) in shrimp and honey before export certification.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Capillary Flow Front Velocity (Lucas–Washburn)

The capillary rise in a cylindrical pore of radius $r$ is governed by balancing the **Laplace pressure** driving the fluid against the **viscous drag** (Hagen–Poiseuille flow).

**Step 1:** Laplace pressure driving the fluid column:

$$
\Delta P = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r}
$$

**Step 2:** Hagen–Poiseuille viscous resistance for a cylinder of length $L$:

$$
\Delta P = \frac{8 \eta L}{r^2} \cdot \frac{dL}{dt}
$$

**Step 3:** Equate driving and resisting pressures:

$$
\frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r} = \frac{8 \eta L}{r^2} \cdot \frac{dL}{dt}
$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to isolate the rate of advance:

$$
L \, dL = \frac{\gamma \, r \cos\theta}{4 \eta} \, dt
$$

**Step 5:** Integrate from $L = 0$ at $t = 0$ to $L = L$ at $t = t$:

$$
\int_{0}^{L} L \, dL = \frac{\gamma r \cos\theta}{4 \eta} \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate the integrals:

$$
\frac{L^2}{2} = \frac{\gamma r \cos\theta}{4 \eta} \cdot t
$$

**Step 7:** Final Lucas–Washburn equation:

$$
\boxed{L^2 = \frac{\gamma \, r \cos\theta}{2 \eta} \cdot t}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Doubling the pore radius quadruples the wetted length at a given time. Hence, the choice of **nitrocellulose membrane pore size (8–12 μm)** is a critical design lever for assay kinetics.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Equilibrium Dissociation Constant from Kinetic Constants

Consider the reversible immunoreaction:

$$
Ag + Ab \;\underset{k_{off}}{\overset{k_{on}}{\rightleftharpoons}}\; AgAb
$$

**Step 1:** Rate of complex formation:

$$
v_{fwd} = k_{on} [Ag] [Ab]
$$

**Step 2:** Rate of complex dissociation:

$$
v_{rev} = k_{off} [AgAb]
$$

**Step 3:** At thermodynamic equilibrium, the net rate is zero:

$$
k_{on} [Ag]_{eq} [Ab]_{eq} = k_{off} [AgAb]_{eq}
$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to define the dissociation constant $K_D$:

$$
K_D \equiv \frac{[Ag]_{eq} [Ab]_{eq}}{[AgAb]_{eq}} = \frac{k_{off}}{k_{on}}
$$

$$
\boxed{K_D = \frac{k_{off}}{k_{on}} \quad \text{(units: M or mol/L)}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for KTU:** A high-affinity antibody used in an IC strip typically has $K_D$ in the range **$10^{-9}$ to $10^{-11}$ M (nM to pM)**. Lower $K_D$ → tighter binding → lower detection limit.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Limit of Detection from Blank Statistics

For a quantitative lateral flow reader, the LOD is statistically defined as the smallest analyte concentration that produces a signal distinguishable from the blank.

**Step 1:** Measure the blank (no-analyte) signal $n$ times. Compute:

$$
\bar{x}_{blank} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{i,blank}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the standard deviation of the blank:

$$
\sigma_{blank} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_{i,blank} - \bar{x}_{blank})^2}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the IUPAC-recommended 3-sigma criterion:

$$
LOD = \bar{x}_{blank} + 3 \sigma_{blank}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert the signal LOD to a concentration LOD using the calibration curve slope $m$:

$$
\boxed{[Analyte]_{LOD} = \frac{LOD - \bar{x}_{blank}}{m} = \frac{3 \sigma_{blank}}{m}}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quantitative Comparison of IC vs. Other Food-Safety Methods

| Parameter | Immunochromatography (LFIA) | ELISA | HPLC-MS/MS | PCR |
|-----------|------------------------------|-------|------------|-----|
| **Assay time** | **5–15 min** | 2–6 h | 30–60 min/sample | 3–6 h |
| **LOD (typical)** | ng/mL – μg/mL | pg/mL – ng/mL | fg/mL – pg/mL | 1–10 CFU/mL |
| **Equipment cost** | < ₹10,000 (reader) | ₹2–5 lakh | ₹50 lakh – ₹1.5 Cr | ₹3–10 lakh |
| **Per-test cost** | ₹80–₹500 | ₹200–₹800 | ₹1500–₹5000 | ₹400–₹1500 |
| **Field portability** | **High** | Low (lab) | Very low (lab) | Low (lab) |
| **Quantitative?** | Semi (with reader) | Yes | **Yes (gold standard)** | Semi (Ct value) |
| **Operator skill** | Low | Moderate | High | High |
| **Best use case** | Screening, HACCP | Confirmation routine | Regulatory confirmation | Pathogen ID |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU exam answer strategy:** When asked to "compare IC with ELISA," structure your answer in a 2-column table like the one above, and conclude with a one-sentence statement: *"IC is the preferred first-line screening tool; ELISA/HPLC-MS is used for confirmation."*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Components of a Lateral Flow Strip

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Sample Pad] --> CP[Conjugate Pad with Labeled Ab]
    CP --> TM[Nitrocellulose Membrane]
    TM --> TL[Test Line Capture Ab]
    TL --> CL[Control Line Secondary Ab]
    CL --> AP[Absorbent Pad Wick]
    SAMP[Liquid Food Extract] --> SP
    RES1([Visual Positive Result]) -.-> TL
    RES2([Internal Validity Check]) -.-> CL
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Sandwich Immunochromatographic Assay

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Apply Sample to Sample Pad] --> B[Capillary Flow Activated]
    B --> C[Rehydration of Labeled Ab on Conjugate Pad]
    C --> D[Analyte Binds Labeled Ab to Form Complex]
    D --> E[Complex Migrates to Test Line]
    E --> F{Capture Ab Present at Test Line}
    F -->|Yes| G[Complex Captured Visible Red Line Forms]
    F -->|No| H[No Test Line Negative Result]
    G --> I[Excess Labeled Ab Wicks to Control Line]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Control Line Always Forms if Test Valid]
    J --> K[Final Read by Eye or Strip Reader]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — IC Kit System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input["Sample Preparation Module"]
        S1[Food Homogenization]
        S2[Extraction Buffer Addition]
        S3[Filtration or Centrifugation]
        S1 --> S2 --> S3
    end
    subgraph Strip["Lateral Flow Strip Module"]
        P1[Sample Pad Conditioning]
        P2[Conjugate Release and Labeling]
        P3[Immunoreaction and Capture at Test Line]
        P4[Control Line Validation]
        P5[Absorbent Pad Wicking]
        P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5
    end
    subgraph Output["Readout Module"]
        R1[Visual Naked Eye Read for Qualitative]
        R2[Optical Strip Reader for Semi-Quantitative]
        R3[Smartphone Camera Image Analysis]
    end
    S3 --> P1
    P5 --> R1
    P5 --> R2
    P5 --> R3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation note:** The three subgraphs represent the **three functional subsystems** of any commercial IC kit: (1) upstream sample prep, (2) the consumable strip chemistry, and (3) the downstream readout. In your KTU answer, drawing such a layered architecture is worth **2–3 extra marks** over a generic sketch.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Define immunochromatography. List any four food-safety applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Immunochromatography is a rapid, membrane-based bioseparation technique that uses immobilized antibodies to detect specific analytes as the sample migrates laterally by capillary action through a porous strip, producing a visually observable result typically within 5–15 minutes. *(1 mark for definition)*

Four food-safety applications *(½ mark each)*:
1. Detection of *Salmonella* and *Listeria* pathogens in ready-to-eat foods.
2. Quantification of aflatoxin B1 in cereals, groundnuts, and spices.
3. Screening of peanut, gluten, and tree-nut allergens in packaged foods.
4. Detection of veterinary drug residues (e.g., chloramphenicol in shrimp and honey).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. What is the role of the control line in a lateral flow immunoassay strip?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The control line contains an immobilized secondary antibody (anti-species IgG) that captures any excess labeled detection antibody passing through the membrane. *(1 mark)* Its functions are: *(2 marks)*
- **Validation of fluid flow:** Its appearance confirms the sample front traversed the entire strip.
- **Reagent integrity check:** Confirms the conjugated antibody was released properly from the conjugate pad.
- **Operator confidence:** Absence of the control line invalidates the test, even if a faint test line appears, preventing false-negative reporting.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 3 Model Question]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the **components and working principle of a sandwich-format lateral flow immunoassay** for the detection of *Salmonella* in a food sample. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Lucas–Washburn equation** for capillary flow through the nitrocellulose membrane, and explain how the membrane pore size affects assay kinetics. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Components and working principle of sandwich LFIA for *Salmonella*** *(7 marks)*

The test strip consists of four overlapping components mounted on a backing card: *(1 mark)*

| Component | Material | Function |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| Sample pad | Cellulose/glass fiber | Receives sample, conditions pH |
| Conjugate pad | Glass fiber | Holds anti-*Salmonella* Ab–AuNP conjugate |
| Test membrane | **Nitrocellulose** (8–12 μm pore) | Contains immobilized capture Ab at TEST line and anti-IgG at CONTROL line |
| Absorbent pad | Cellulose | Wicks fluid, maintains capillary pressure |

**Working steps** *(1 mark per key point)*:
1. Food homogenate is enriched in selective broth, then applied to sample pad.
2. Capillary flow rehydrates AuNP-labeled anti-*Salmonella* antibodies on conjugate pad.
3. *Salmonella* cells in the sample bind the labeled antibody, forming a complex.
4. The complex migrates to the **TEST line** where immobilized capture antibody binds the *Salmonella*–AuNP complex — a **red line** appears (positive result).
5. Excess labeled antibody continues to the **CONTROL line**, where it is captured by anti-IgG — a second red line confirms test validity.
6. Positive result = **two lines**; negative = **one line (control only)**.

Neat diagram (must be drawn in the answer sheet — use a horizontal strip layout with the four labeled zones and arrows showing fluid flow). *(1 mark reserved for diagram)*

---

**(b) Derivation of Lucas–Washburn equation and pore-size effect** *(7 marks)*

Full derivation as given in **Section 3.1** above (steps 1–7, worth 5 marks). Conclude with the final boxed equation and engineering interpretation:

The wetted length is proportional to the square root of the pore radius:

$$
L \propto \sqrt{r}
$$

Doubling the pore radius increases the wetted length by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$. For nitrocellulose membranes, an **8–12 μm pore size** is chosen to balance: *(2 marks)*
- **Larger pores (15 μm):** Faster flow → shorter assay time but lower protein binding and lower sensitivity.
- **Smaller pores (5 μm):** Higher protein-binding capacity → better sensitivity but slower flow → longer assay time and risk of non-specific background.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Compare the **sandwich** and **competitive** formats of immunochromatography. State two examples of food contaminants detected by each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A lateral flow assay for aflatoxin B1 in maize is calibrated by spiking blank maize extracts at 0, 2, 5, 10, 20, and 50 ng/g. The strip reader returns a **test line intensity (T/C ratio)** for each level. The blank mean signal is **0.95** and the blank standard deviation is **0.04**. The calibration curve slope is **$-0.018$ per ng/g** (competitive format — higher analyte → lower T/C). Calculate the **Limit of Detection (LOD)** in ng/g. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Sandwich vs Competitive IC** *(7 marks)*

| Feature | Sandwich Format | Competitive Format |
|---------|------------------|---------------------|
| Target analyte size | Large ($\geq$ 1 kDa) | Small (< 1 kDa) |
| Number of antibodies | Two (detection + capture) | One (labeled) + immobilized antigen |
| Positive interpretation | **Two lines** | **One line (control only)** |
| Negative interpretation | One line | Two lines |
| Examples | *Listeria*, *Salmonella* proteins | Aflatoxin B1, ochratoxin A, chloramphenicol |
| Detection at high analyte | Saturation (pro-zone effect) | Decreased signal monotonically |

*(4 marks for tabular comparison; 2 marks for two examples each; 1 mark for concluding remark: sandwich for large antigens, competitive for haptens and small molecules.)*

---

**(b) LOD Calculation** *(7 marks)*

**Given:**
- $\bar{x}_{blank} = 0.95$
- $\sigma_{blank} = 0.04$
- Calibration slope $m = -0.018$ per ng/g (negative because competitive)
- Formula: $LOD_{signal} = \bar{x}_{blank} + 3 \sigma_{blank}$

**Step 1:** Calculate signal LOD:

$$
LOD_{signal} = 0.95 + 3 \times 0.04 = 0.95 + 0.12 = 1.07
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate concentration LOD using the absolute value of the slope:

$$
LOD_{conc} = \frac{\vert LOD_{signal} - \bar{x}_{blank} \vert}{\vert m \vert} = \frac{\vert 1.07 - 0.95 \vert}{\vert -0.018 \vert}
$$

$$
LOD_{conc} = \frac{0.12}{0.018}
$$

**Step 3:**

$$
\boxed{LOD_{conc} = 6.67 \text{ ng/g}}
$$

*(Award: 1 mark each for Step 1, 2, 3; 2 marks for correct formula selection; 1 mark for absolute value usage; 1 mark for correct final units.)*

This means the strip can reliably detect aflatoxin B1 at concentrations $\geq$ **6.67 ng/g** in maize extract, which is **above the EU regulatory limit of 5 ng/g** for total aflatoxins in cereals — *the assay marginally fails compliance*; a more sensitive conjugate or pre-concentration step would be required.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the control line** in diagrams — examiner deducts **1–2 marks** if absent.
> 2. **Confusing sandwich and competitive interpretations** — stating "two lines = negative" for competitive format is a classic error.
> 3. **Incorrect LOD units** — reporting $LOD = 1.07$ without converting to concentration using the calibration slope loses **2 marks**.
> 4. **Not stating the 3-sigma basis** — examiners expect explicit mention of "$\bar{x} + 3\sigma$" or "99.7% confidence" criterion.
> 5. **Missing the membrane material** — always specify "**nitrocellulose**" not just "membrane."
> 6. **Skipping the prozone/hook effect** in sandwich assays for very high analyte concentrations.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> - ✅ Immunochromatography = **antibody + chromatography + capillary flow**.
> - ✅ Four physical zones: **Sample → Conjugate → Membrane (Test + Control) → Absorbent**.
> - ✅ Label of choice: **colloidal gold nanoparticles**, $\lambda_{max} \approx$ **520 nm**, red color.
> - ✅ **Sandwich format** for **large analytes** (proteins, pathogens): *positive = two lines*.
> - ✅ **Competitive format** for **small analytes** (toxins, drugs): *positive = one line (control only)*.
> - ✅ **Nitrocellulose** membrane, pore size **8–12 μm**, protein binding **80–100 μg/cm²**.
> - ✅ **Lucas–Washburn:** $L^2 = \dfrac{\gamma r \cos\theta}{2\eta} \cdot t$ — flow $\propto \sqrt{r}$.
> - ✅ **$K_D = k_{off}/k_{on}$**; high-affinity Ab has $K_D$ in **nM to pM** range.
> - ✅ **LOD** by 3-sigma: $LOD = \bar{x}_{blank} + 3\sigma_{blank}$, then convert via calibration slope.
> - ✅ **Sensitivity = TP / (TP+FN)**; **Specificity = TN / (TN+FP)**.
> - ✅ **Assay time:** **5–15 min**; **Sample volume:** **10–200 μL**.
> - ✅ **Four food-safety superstars:** *Salmonella* detection, aflatoxin screening, allergen testing, veterinary drug residue screening.
> - ✅ **IC vs ELISA vs HPLC-MS:** IC is **screening**, ELISA is **confirmation routine**, HPLC-MS is **regulatory gold standard**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ion exchange chromatography; advantages and disadvantages of chromatographic separations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT527 - SEPARATION PROCESSES IN FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ion exchange chromatography; advantages and disadvantages of chromatographic separations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Chromatography?

**Chromatography** is a versatile laboratory and industrial separation technique used to separate the components of a mixture based on the differential distribution of the components between two immiscible phases — a **stationary phase** (which remains fixed) and a **mobile phase** (which moves through or over the stationary phase carrying the sample).

The word "chromatography" is derived from the Greek words *"chroma"* (meaning **color**) and *"graphein"* (meaning **to write**), coined by the Russian-Italian botanist **Mikhail Tsvet** in **1906**, who used it to separate plant pigments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board Standard):**
> Chromatography is a physical method of separation in which the components of a mixture are separated by their differential distribution between two phases — a stationary phase and a mobile phase that moves in a definite direction.

---

## 1.2 What is Ion Exchange Chromatography (IEC)?

**Ion Exchange Chromatography (IEC)** is a type of liquid chromatography that separates ions and ionizable molecules (such as proteins, amino acids, nucleic acids, and organic acids) based on their **net charge, charge density, and ionic strength of interaction** with a charged stationary phase.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> Ion exchange chromatography is a chromatographic separation technique in which the stationary phase consists of an ion exchange resin bearing fixed ionic groups. Separation is achieved by the **reversible exchange of ions** between the mobile phase (sample solution) and the counter-ions initially bound to the resin matrix.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Magnet and Marble" Model

Imagine a conveyor belt (the **mobile phase**) carrying a mixed bag of marbles, some painted with **positive (+)** charge and some with **negative (−)** charge. Above the belt is a series of **magnetic plates** (the **stationary phase** with fixed charges).

- The **oppositely charged** marbles get "stuck" to the magnetic plates temporarily.
- As the belt moves, you can change the magnetic strength (by changing the **pH** or **salt concentration** in the buffer).
- When the magnetic strength is reduced, the marbles are **released** in a controlled, sequential order — first the weakly held ones, then the strongly held ones.

This is **exactly** how ion exchange chromatography works! The resin acts like the "magnet," and by gradually adjusting buffer conditions, you "elute" the molecules one by one.

---

## 1.3 Types of Ion Exchange Chromatography

| Type | Fixed Charge on Resin | Attracts (from sample) | Typical Use |
|------|----------------------|------------------------|-------------|
| **Cation Exchange Chromatography** | Negative (−) | Cations (positively charged species) | Amino acids, metal ions, basic proteins |
| **Anion Exchange Chromatography** | Positive (+) | Anions (negatively charged species) | Organic acids, nucleic acids, acidic proteins |

Further classification:
- **Strong ion exchangers** — ionize over a wide pH range (e.g., sulfonic acid for cation, quaternary ammonium for anion).
- **Weak ion exchangers** — ionize over a limited pH range (e.g., carboxylic acid for cation, diethylaminoethyl for anion).

> [!TIP]
> **Food Tech Insight:** In sugar refining, **cation exchange resins** are used to remove calcium and magnesium ions (hardness) from cane sugar juice, while **anion exchange resins** remove colored impurities and organic acids — this is called **"ion exchange decolorization."**

---

## 1.4 Common Ion Exchange Resins

The most common resin matrix is based on:
- **Polystyrene-divinylbenzene (PS-DVB)** — synthetic, rigid, durable.
- **Cellulose** or **dextran** (Sephadex, DEAE-Sephadex) — used for protein separation.
- **Agarose** (DEAE-Sepharose) — for large biomolecules.

Functional groups:
- **Cation exchangers:** −SO₃⁻ (strong), −COO⁻ (weak)
- **Anion exchangers:** −N(CH₃)₃⁺ (strong), −NH(CH₂CH₃)₂⁺ (weak)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the *fixed* charge on the resin with the *mobile counter-ion*. Remember — the **functional group on the resin is fixed**, and the **ion that exchanges is the mobile counter-ion** initially bound electrostatically to the resin.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Ion Exchange — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The ion exchange process can be described as a **reversible stoichiometric reaction** between ions in the solution and ions on the resin.

### For Cation Exchange:

$$n R^- A^+ + B^{n+} \rightleftharpoons R_n^- B^{n+} + n A^+$$

Where:
- $R^-$ = Fixed anionic group on the resin (e.g., −SO₃⁻)
- $A^+$ = Mobile counter-ion initially on the resin (e.g., H⁺ or Na⁺)
- $B^{n+}$ = Cation from the sample with valence *n*
- $R_n^- B^{n+}$ = Sample cation now held on the resin
- $A^+$ = Displaced counter-ion released into solution

### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **Equilibration** — The resin is pre-equilibrated with a starting buffer of low ionic strength and appropriate pH, so all binding sites carry the same counter-ion (e.g., Na⁺).
2. **Sample Loading** — The sample solution is introduced. Ions of opposite charge (e.g., proteins at pH below their pI) bind to the resin through electrostatic interaction.
3. **Washing** — Unbound and weakly bound species are washed out with the starting buffer.
4. **Elution** — A gradient of increasing ionic strength (typically by raising NaCl concentration) or changing pH competes with the bound ions, releasing them in order of increasing binding strength.
5. **Regeneration** — After elution, the resin is re-equilibrated with the starting buffer to restore it for the next run.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Elution Order:**
> Ions with **higher charge (valence)** bind more strongly because of greater electrostatic attraction. So **Al³⁺ > Ca²⁺ > Na⁺** in terms of binding strength on a cation exchange resin. During gradient elution, weakly bound monovalent ions elute first, while strongly bound multivalent ions elute last.

---

## 2.2 Selectivity Coefficient — The Heart of Ion Exchange

The **selectivity coefficient** ($K_S$) is a thermodynamic equilibrium constant that quantifies the preference of the resin for one ion over another.

For the general exchange:
$$n A^{z_A^+} + z_A B^{n+} \rightleftharpoons n A^{z_B^+} \cdot z_B B^{n+}$$

Wait, the more standard form is for the binary exchange:
$$z_B A^{z_A} + z_A B^{z_B} \rightleftharpoons z_B \bar{A} + z_A \bar{B}$$

The **selectivity coefficient** is defined as:

$$K_{A}^{B} = \frac{[\bar{B}]^{z_A} \cdot [A]^{z_B}}{[\bar{A}]^{z_B} \cdot [B]^{z_A}}$$

Where:
- $[\bar{B}], [\bar{A}]$ = concentrations of ions B and A on the resin (bar denotes resin phase)
- $[B], [A]$ = concentrations of ions B and A in solution
- $z_A, z_B$ = valences of ions A and B

**Interpretation:**
- If $K_{A}^{B} > 1$ → Resin **prefers** ion B over ion A.
- If $K_{A}^{B} < 1$ → Resin prefers ion A over ion B.

---

## 2.3 Capacity of an Ion Exchange Resin

The **ion exchange capacity** is the number of exchangeable ions per unit mass (or volume) of resin.

**Two types of capacity:**

| Type | Symbol | Definition |
|------|--------|------------|
| **Total Capacity** | $Q_t$ | Total number of functional groups per unit mass (theoretical maximum) |
| **Working / Breakthrough Capacity** | $Q_b$ | Actual usable capacity under dynamic flow conditions |

**Breakthrough Capacity Formula:**

$$Q_b = \frac{C_0 \cdot V_b}{m_{resin}}$$

Where:
- $Q_b$ = Breakthrough capacity (meq/g of dry resin or meq/mL of wet resin)
- $C_0$ = Inlet concentration of the ion being exchanged (meq/mL)
- $V_b$ = Volume of solution treated until breakthrough point (mL)
- $m_{resin}$ = Mass of dry resin (g) or volume of wet resin (mL)

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Relevance:** In the dairy industry, IEC is used for **whey demineralization**. A typical breakthrough capacity of a strong cation exchanger for Na⁺ is about **1.5–2.0 meq/mL**.

---

## 2.4 The Plate Theory — Number of Theoretical Plates

Chromatographic efficiency is quantified by the **number of theoretical plates (N)**, analogous to distillation columns.

$$N = 16 \cdot \left(\frac{t_R}{w}\right)^2 = 5.54 \cdot \left(\frac{t_R}{w_{1/2}}\right)^2$$

Where:
- $t_R$ = Retention time of the peak
- $w$ = Peak width at the base
- $w_{1/2}$ = Peak width at half height

**Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate (HETP):**

$$H = \frac{L}{N}$$

Where $L$ is the column length. Lower $H$ = better column efficiency.

---

## 2.5 The Retention Factor (k)

The **retention factor** describes how strongly a component is retained by the stationary phase:

$$k = \frac{t_R - t_M}{t_M}$$

Where:
- $t_R$ = Retention time of the analyte
- $t_M$ = Dead time (time for an unretained compound to pass through)

- $k < 1$ → Component elutes too quickly (poor separation)
- $1 < k < 10$ → Optimal separation
- $k > 10$ → Component is too strongly retained (broad peaks, long analysis)

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Quick Reference Table

| Formula Name | Equation | Variables | Units | KTU Use Case |
|--------------|----------|-----------|-------|--------------|
| Selectivity Coefficient | $K_{A}^{B} = \dfrac{[\bar{B}]^{z_A} \cdot [A]^{z_B}}{[\bar{A}]^{z_B} \cdot [B]^{z_A}}$ | $[\bar{ }]$ = resin phase conc.; $[\ ]$ = solution conc.; $z$ = valence | Dimensionless | Comparing ion affinity |
| Breakthrough Capacity | $Q_b = \dfrac{C_0 \cdot V_b}{m_{resin}}$ | $C_0$ = inlet conc.; $V_b$ = breakthrough volume; $m$ = resin mass | meq/g or meq/mL | Industrial column design |
| Number of Plates | $N = 16 \cdot \left(\dfrac{t_R}{w}\right)^2$ | $t_R$ = retention time; $w$ = peak width | Dimensionless | Column efficiency |
| HETP | $H = \dfrac{L}{N}$ | $L$ = column length; $N$ = plates | mm or cm | Comparing columns |
| Retention Factor | $k = \dfrac{t_R - t_M}{t_M}$ | $t_R$ = retention time; $t_M$ = dead time | Dimensionless | Method optimization |
| Resolution | $R_s = \dfrac{2(t_{R2} - t_{R1})}{w_1 + w_2}$ | $t_R$ = retention times; $w$ = peak widths | Dimensionless | Quality of separation |
| Selectivity Factor (α) | $\alpha = \dfrac{k_2}{k_1}$ | $k$ = retention factors | Dimensionless | Separation selectivity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU RULE:** Whenever you write $|x|$ (absolute value) in a markdown table, use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\text{vert } x \text{ vert}$ to **avoid breaking the markdown table syntax** with the unescaped vertical pipe symbol $\vert$.

---

## 2.7 Resolution — The Ultimate Measure of Separation Quality

$$R_s = \frac{2(t_{R2} - t_{R1})}{w_1 + w_2} = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k_2}{1 + k_2}$$

This is the famous **Purnell equation**, a high-yield KTU formula.

**Master Equation — Purnell Resolution:**

$$R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k_2}{1 + k_2}$$

- **Efficiency term** $\frac{\sqrt{N}}{4}$ → Increase by lengthening the column or reducing particle size.
- **Selectivity term** $\frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha}$ → Increase by changing stationary phase, pH, temperature.
- **Retention term** $\frac{k_2}{1 + k_2}$ → Maximize by adjusting gradient strength.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Mass Balance for a Breakthrough Curve

In industrial ion exchange columns, the **breakthrough curve** is the plot of outlet concentration ($C$) vs. time ($t$) or volume. The "S-shaped" curve tells the operator when to stop the loading cycle.

### Mass Balance for a Differential Slice of the Column

Consider a column of cross-section $A_c$ and resin bed height $dz$. Over a time interval $dt$, a volume of solution $dV = Q \cdot dt$ (where $Q$ = volumetric flow rate) enters the slice.

**Accumulation in the slice = Inflow − Outflow**

$$\varepsilon \cdot A_c \cdot dz \cdot \frac{\partial C}{\partial t}\bigg|_z + (1 - \varepsilon) \cdot A_c \cdot dz \cdot \rho_p \cdot \frac{\partial q}{\partial t}\bigg|_z = Q \cdot C \big|_{z} \cdot dt - Q \cdot C \big|_{z+dz} \cdot dt$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon$ = bed porosity (void fraction)
- $\rho_p$ = particle density of resin
- $q$ = concentration of adsorbed ion in the resin (meq/g)
- $C$ = concentration in the liquid phase (meq/mL)
- $A_c$ = cross-sectional area of the column
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate

Dividing both sides by $A_c \cdot dz \cdot dt$ and noting that as $dz \to 0$:

$$\varepsilon \cdot \frac{\partial C}{\partial t} + (1 - \varepsilon) \cdot \rho_p \cdot \frac{\partial q}{\partial t} = -u \cdot \frac{\partial C}{\partial z}$$

Where $u = \dfrac{Q}{A_c}$ is the **superficial velocity** (m/s).

> This is the **general mass balance equation** for ion exchange chromatography, also known as the **Thomas / Bohart-Adams framework** for fixed-bed ion exchange.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Retention Time Relationship for IEC

For IEC, the retention of an ion depends on:
1. Its charge ($z$)
2. The ionic strength of the mobile phase ($I$)
3. The pH (which controls the charge on ionizable analytes)

A simplified **ion exchange retention model** is:

$$\log k = \log K_{eq} + z \cdot \log Q - z \cdot \log [Eluent]$$

Where:
- $k$ = retention factor of the analyte ion
- $K_{eq}$ = ion exchange equilibrium constant
- $z$ = charge of the analyte ion
- $Q$ = ion exchange capacity of the resin
- $[Eluent]$ = concentration of the eluent counter-ion

### Step-by-Step Logic:

**Step 1 — Start from equilibrium exchange reaction:**
$$z \cdot R^- E^+ + A^{z+} \rightleftharpoons R^-_z A^{z+} + z \cdot E^+$$

**Step 2 — Write the equilibrium constant:**
$$K_{eq} = \frac{[R_z A] \cdot [E^+]^z}{[R E]^z \cdot [A^{z+}]}$$

**Step 3 — The retention factor $k$ is proportional to the ratio of analyte concentration in the resin phase to that in the mobile phase:**
$$k \propto \frac{[R_z A]}{[A^{z+}]}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting the equilibrium expression:**
$$k \propto \frac{K_{eq} \cdot [R E]^z \cdot [A^{z+}]}{[A^{z+}] \cdot [E^+]^z} = \frac{K_{eq} \cdot [R E]^z}{[E^+]^z}$$

**Step 5 — Since $[RE] = Q$ (resin capacity, constant) and $[E^+] \approx [Eluent]$:**
$$k = \text{const} \cdot \frac{K_{eq} \cdot Q^z}{[Eluent]^z}$$

**Step 6 — Taking the logarithm of both sides:**
$$\log k = \log K_{eq} + z \cdot \log Q - z \cdot \log [Eluent]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** A **doubling of the eluent concentration** decreases the retention factor by a factor of $2^z$ — for a divalent ion ($z=2$), retention drops by **4×**; for a trivalent ion ($z=3$), retention drops by **8×**. This is why multivalent ions elute first when the salt gradient is applied!

---

## 3.3 Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

**Problem (Model KTU Question):**
A cation exchange column has 50 g of resin with a total capacity of 4.5 meq/g. The column is used to remove Ca²⁺ from a hard water sample containing 5 meq/L of Ca²⁺. If the breakthrough occurs when 2.5 L of water has been treated, calculate:
(a) The breakthrough capacity of the resin.
(b) The utilization efficiency of the resin (Working capacity / Total capacity).

### Solution:

**Part (a) — Breakthrough Capacity:**

$$Q_b = \frac{C_0 \cdot V_b}{m_{resin}}$$

$$Q_b = \frac{5 \text{ meq/L} \times 2.5 \text{ L}}{50 \text{ g}} = \frac{12.5 \text{ meq}}{50 \text{ g}} = 0.25 \text{ meq/g}$$

**Part (b) — Utilization Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{Q_b}{Q_t} \times 100\% = \frac{0.25}{4.5} \times 100\% = 5.56\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** A common student mistake is to forget to include the valence of Ca²⁺ in the breakthrough capacity calculation. Because Ca²⁺ contributes 2 equivalents per mole, the **molar capacity** is 0.125 mmol/g, but the **equivalent capacity is 0.25 meq/g** (as calculated above). Always state your units clearly!

---

## 3.4 Comparison of IEC with Other Chromatographic Techniques

| Parameter | Ion Exchange | Gel Filtration | Affinity | Reversed Phase |
|-----------|--------------|----------------|----------|----------------|
| **Separation basis** | Net charge | Molecular size | Specific binding | Hydrophobicity |
| **Typical resin** | DEAE-cellulose, SP-Sephadex | Sephadex, Sephacryl | Ni-NTA, Protein A | C18 silica |
| **Elution method** | Salt gradient or pH change | Isocratic (size-based) | Competitive ligand | Organic solvent gradient |
| **Sample volume** | Moderate | Small to moderate | Moderate | Small |
| **Food industry use** | Demineralization, debittering | Desalting, fractionation | Purification of enzymes | Vitamin, pigment analysis |
| **Cost** | Medium | Low | High | High |
| **Selectivity** | High (charge-specific) | Low (size-based) | Very high (specific) | High (hydrophobicity) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Ion Exchange Chromatography System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Buffer Reservoir] --> A1[Buffer Reservoir]
    A1 --> A2[Solvent Delivery Pump]
    A2 --> A3[Inline Degasser]
    A3 --> A4[Mixing Chamber with Gradient Former]
    A4 --> A5[Sample Injector with Sample Loop]
    A5 --> A6[Ion Exchange Column with Charged Resin Beads]
    A6 --> A7[UV Visible Detector at 280 nm]
    A7 --> A8[Conductivity Monitor]
    A8 --> A9[Data Acquisition System with Computer and Integrator]
    A9 --> A10[Fraction Collector]
    A10 --> A11[Waste Reservoir]
    A11 --> A12[Pure Fraction Collection Tubes]

    subgraph SampleLoadingUnit
        SL1[Sample in Loading Buffer] --> SL2[Injection Valve]
        SL2 --> A5
    end

    subgraph RegenerationUnit
        RG1[Regeneration Buffer High Salt] --> A2
    end

    subgraph DetectionChain
        A6 --> DC1[UV Detector]
        DC1 --> DC2[Conductivity Detector]
        DC2 --> DC9
    end

    A6 -- Eluted Ions in Order of Increasing Affinity --> DC1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:**
> - The **column** is the heart of the system. The resin beads inside are functionalized with charged groups.
> - The **pump + gradient former** delivers a continuously increasing salt concentration (e.g., 0 → 1 M NaCl) to elute bound analytes.
> - The **UV detector** monitors protein elution (A₂₈₀), while the **conductivity monitor** tracks salt gradient simultaneously.
> - The **fraction collector** captures separated fractions for downstream analysis (e.g., SDS-PAGE, mass spectrometry).

---

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Industrial Water Softening by IEC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H0[Hard Water Inlet] --> H1[Hard Water Inlet]
    H1 --> H2[Pre Filter Sand and Carbon]
    H2 --> H3[Cation Exchange Column Na+ Form Resin]
    H3 -- Releases Na+ --> H4[Softened Water Outlet]
    H3 -- Binds Ca2+ and Mg2+ --> H5[Spent Resin After Exhaustion]
    H5 --> H6[Regeneration with 10 percent NaCl Brine]
    H6 -- Replaces Ca2+ with Na+ --> H3
    H6 --> H7[Waste Brine to Drain with High Ca and Mg]

    subgraph ServiceCycle
        H3 --> H4
    end

    subgraph RegenerationCycle
        H6 --> H3
        H6 --> H7
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Note:** This is a continuous **service–regeneration cycle** used in water treatment plants and dairy factories (for boiler feed water and process water). A typical strong cation exchange resin (e.g., Amberlite IR 120) lasts for **3–5 years** before replacement.

---

## 4.3 Mechanism Schematic — Cation Exchange at the Resin Surface

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0[Resin Bead Polystyrene DVB Matrix] --> M1[Functional Group Sulfonate SO3]
    M1 -- Fixed Negative Charge --> M2[Counter Ion Na+ Initially Bound]
    M2 --> M3[Sample Cation Ca2+ Approaches]
    M3 -- Electrostatic Attraction --> M4[Exchange Reaction]
    M4 --> M5[Ca2+ Now Bound to Resin]
    M4 --> M6[Released Na+ in Solution]
    M6 --> M7[Diffuses into Bulk Mobile Phase]
```

---

## 4.4 Breakthrough Curve Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[Concentration of Outlet C] --> B1[Concentration Axis C Ce]
    B1 --> B2[Time Axis t or Volume V]
    B2 --> B3[Breakthrough Curve Sigmoid Shape]
    B3 --> B4[Phase 1 Initial Low Outlet Concentration Service Phase]
    B3 --> B5[Phase 2 Breakthrough Point C Ce at 0.05 to 0.10]
    B3 --> B6[Phase 3 Mass Transfer Zone Sharp Rise]
    B3 --> B7[Phase 4 Exhaustion C Ce approaches 1.0]
    B3 --> B8[Phase 5 Saturation No More Binding]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Operational Practice:** Industrial columns are usually stopped at the **breakthrough point** (Phase 2, where $C/C_e \approx 0.05$–$0.10$) to maintain product quality, and regenerated before reaching exhaustion (Phase 4).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Question]
**Q: What is ion exchange chromatography? Mention the two main types.**

**Model Answer:**

Ion exchange chromatography (IEC) is a liquid chromatographic technique used to separate ions and ionizable molecules based on their reversible exchange with counter-ions on a charged stationary phase (ion exchange resin). The separation is governed by electrostatic interaction between the analyte ions and the oppositely charged functional groups on the resin.

The two main types are:
1. **Cation exchange chromatography** — resin bears fixed negative charges (e.g., −SO₃⁻, −COO⁻) and exchanges cations.
2. **Anion exchange chromatography** — resin bears fixed positive charges (e.g., −N(CH₃)₃⁺) and exchanges anions.

*(3 marks — definition 1, types 1 each = 3)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Model Question]
**Q: List any three advantages of chromatographic separations.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **High resolution** — Chromatography can separate compounds that are structurally very similar (e.g., isomers, homologues) which is difficult by other techniques like filtration or extraction.
2. **Mild operating conditions** — Most chromatographic methods operate at ambient temperature and use non-destructive mobile phases, preserving the biological activity of sensitive biomolecules like proteins and enzymes.
3. **Versatility** — By varying the stationary phase, mobile phase, and operating conditions, chromatography can be applied to a wide range of analytes — from small ions to large macromolecules.

*(3 marks — 1 mark each)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**Q: (a) Explain the principle and mechanism of ion exchange chromatography with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of pH and ionic strength in the separation. (7 marks)**

**Q: (b) A cation exchange column containing 100 g of resin (total capacity 4.0 meq/g) is used to treat a solution containing 8 meq/L of Na⁺ ions. The breakthrough is observed when 12 L of solution has been passed. Calculate (i) the breakthrough capacity, (ii) the percentage utilization of the resin, and (iii) the volume of 2 N HCl required to regenerate the resin completely. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Principle and Mechanism (7 marks):**

**Principle:**
Ion exchange chromatography separates ions based on the **reversible stoichiometric exchange** of ions between a liquid mobile phase and a solid ion exchange resin bearing fixed ionic functional groups. The driving force is **electrostatic attraction** between the analyte ions and the oppositely charged groups on the resin.

**Mechanism — Step-by-Step:**

1. **Equilibration** of the resin with a starting buffer of low ionic strength ensures all exchange sites are occupied by a uniform counter-ion (e.g., H⁺ for cation exchange).
2. **Sample loading** — The sample mixture is introduced. Analyte ions carrying charge opposite to that of the resin's functional groups are electrostatically attracted and bind to the resin. Ions of like charge pass through unretained.
3. **Washing** — Unbound and weakly held species are eluted with the starting buffer.
4. **Elution** — A gradient of increasing ionic strength (typically NaCl) or a change in pH is applied. The competing counter-ions displace the bound analytes, releasing them in order of **increasing binding strength** (i.e., in order of increasing charge, then increasing charge density).
5. **Regeneration** — After elution, the resin is regenerated with a strong acid (HCl) or strong base (NaOH) to restore the original counter-ion form.

**Role of pH:**
- pH determines the **ionization state** of both the analyte and the resin.
- For proteins, pH relative to the **isoelectric point (pI)** governs the net charge: at pH < pI, the protein is positively charged (binds to cation exchanger); at pH > pI, it is negatively charged (binds to anion exchanger).
- Adjusting pH during elution can selectively release bound species.

**Role of Ionic Strength:**
- High salt concentration in the mobile phase provides competing ions that **shield** the electrostatic interaction and **displace** the bound analytes.
- A **gradient of increasing salt** is the most common elution method.
- Salt type and valence affect selectivity: divalent ions (e.g., Ca²⁺, SO₄²⁻) compete more effectively than monovalent ions.

*(7 marks — principle 1, mechanism steps 3, pH/ionic strength 2, neat flow 1)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $m_{resin} = 100$ g
- $Q_t = 4.0$ meq/g
- $C_0 = 8$ meq/L
- $V_b = 12$ L
- Regenerant: 2 N HCl

**(i) Breakthrough Capacity:**

$$Q_b = \frac{C_0 \cdot V_b}{m_{resin}} = \frac{8 \text{ meq/L} \times 12 \text{ L}}{100 \text{ g}} = \frac{96 \text{ meq}}{100 \text{ g}} = 0.96 \text{ meq/g}$$

**(ii) Percentage Utilization:**

$$\eta = \frac{Q_b}{Q_t} \times 100\% = \frac{0.96}{4.0} \times 100\% = 24\%$$

**(iii) Volume of 2 N HCl for Complete Regeneration:**

To regenerate fully, we need to neutralize the total capacity:

$$\text{Total equivalents} = Q_t \times m_{resin} = 4.0 \times 100 = 400 \text{ meq}$$

For 2 N HCl, 1 L contains 2 × 1000 = 2000 meq.

$$\text{Volume of 2 N HCl} = \frac{400 \text{ meq}}{2000 \text{ meq/L}} = 0.2 \text{ L} = 200 \text{ mL}$$

**Practical consideration:** An excess of **1.5× to 2× the stoichiometric amount** is normally used in industry for efficient regeneration. So practical volume = 200 mL × 1.5 = **300 mL** of 2 N HCl.

*(7 marks — i 2, ii 2, iii 3)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Alternative]

**Q: (a) Discuss the various advantages and disadvantages of chromatographic separation techniques used in food technology. (7 marks)**

**Q: (b) With a neat labeled diagram, explain the working of an ion exchange chromatography system. List four food industry applications of ion exchange chromatography. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Advantages and Disadvantages of Chromatographic Separations (7 marks):**

**Advantages:**

1. **High resolution and selectivity** — Chromatography can resolve complex mixtures of compounds with very similar structures (e.g., separating glucose from fructose, or different isomers of an essential oil), which is often impossible by simple physical separations.
2. **Mild operating conditions** — Most chromatographic separations occur at ambient or near-ambient temperatures, preserving heat-sensitive compounds like vitamins, flavors, and bioactive peptides. This is a major advantage in food technology.
3. **Wide applicability** — The same principle (two-phase distribution) can separate gases, liquids, ions, polymers, and biomolecules, simply by changing the stationary and mobile phases.
4. **Both qualitative and quantitative** — Chromatography provides qualitative information (identification by retention time) and quantitative data (peak area or height), making it indispensable for food quality testing.
5. **Scalability** — Methods developed at the analytical scale (mg) can be scaled up to preparative (g) and industrial (kg) levels with the same underlying principle.
6. **Compatible with detection systems** — Can be coupled with UV, MS, RI, ELSD, and other detectors for sensitive and selective analysis.
7. **Selective removal of impurities** — In food processing, ion exchange and affinity chromatography can selectively remove bitter compounds (e.g., naringin from citrus juice), heavy metals, and off-flavors.

**Disadvantages:**

1. **High capital and operating cost** — Chromatographic equipment (HPLC systems, columns, resins) is expensive. Specialty resins for food-grade applications add to the cost.
2. **Time-consuming** — A single chromatographic run can take 10 minutes to several hours, limiting throughput for routine quality control.
3. **Skill-intensive operation** — Requires trained personnel for method development, troubleshooting, and maintenance. Sample preparation and column conditioning demand expertise.
4. **Sample dilution** — Compounds are diluted during elution, requiring concentration steps (e.g., evaporation, freeze-drying) afterward.
5. **Column degradation** — Resins have finite lifetimes, especially when exposed to fouling, microbial contamination, or harsh regeneration chemicals. Replacement costs can be significant.
6. **Solvent consumption and waste** — Especially in normal-phase and reversed-phase HPLC, large volumes of organic solvents are used, creating disposal and environmental concerns.
7. **Complex method development** — Optimizing mobile phase composition, gradient, flow rate, temperature, and detection parameters is iterative and time-consuming.
8. **Limited loading capacity** — The amount of sample that can be loaded in a single run is restricted by the column dimensions, particularly for analytical-scale HPLC.

*(7 marks — 4 advantages + 3 disadvantages, 1 mark each + 1 for coherence)*

---

**Part (b) — Working of an IEC System and Food Applications (7 marks):**

**Working — Component-wise explanation with reference to the diagram in Section 4.1:**

1. **Mobile phase reservoir and pump** — Delivers the buffer (e.g., 20 mM phosphate, pH 7.0) at a constant flow rate.
2. **Sample injector** — Introduces a precise volume (e.g., 20 µL for analytical, up to liters for preparative) of the food extract into the mobile phase stream.
3. **Ion exchange column** — A stainless steel or glass column packed with resin beads (e.g., 5–10 µm particle size for HPLC; 50–100 µm for industrial). Functional groups on the resin (e.g., −SO₃⁻ for cation exchange) bind oppositely charged analytes.
4. **Gradient elution** — The ionic strength of the mobile phase is gradually increased (e.g., 0 → 500 mM NaCl), progressively displacing bound ions in order of increasing affinity.
5. **Detector** — UV-Vis (A₂₈₀ for proteins, A₂₅₄ for nucleic acids, A₂₁₀ for peptides) or conductivity detector records the elution profile in real time as a chromatogram.
6. **Data system** — Peak areas are integrated for quantification; retention times are matched against standards for identification.
7. **Fraction collector** — Collects the eluate in discrete tubes for downstream use (further analysis, drying, formulation).

**Four Food Industry Applications of IEC:**

1. **Water softening and demineralization** in dairy and beverage industries — Removes Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, and other mineral ions that cause scaling in boilers and equipment.
2. **Decolorization of sugar syrups** — Anion exchange resins remove colored phenolic impurities and organic acids from cane sugar and beet sugar juice.
3. **Bitterness removal from citrus juices** — Anion exchange resins adsorb bitter compounds like **naringin** and **limonin** from grapefruit and orange juice without affecting vitamin C or sugars.
4. **Purification of food-grade proteins and enzymes** — IEC is used to isolate and purify proteins like **whey proteins (β-lactoglobulin, α-lactalbumin)**, **soy proteins**, and industrial enzymes such as **glucose isomerase**.
5. **Amino acid analysis** — IEC followed by post-column ninhydrin detection is the gold standard for amino acid profiling of foods.

*(7 marks — working explanation 4, applications 1.5 each = 6; total 7)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the direction of charge exchange:** Students often write that the resin gives up its fixed charge during exchange. **WRONG!** The fixed charge **stays on the resin** — only the **mobile counter-ion** is exchanged.
> 2. **Mixing up breakthrough capacity and total capacity:** Always differentiate — $Q_t$ is the theoretical (static) capacity, $Q_b$ is the practical (dynamic) capacity under actual flow conditions. $Q_b < Q_t$ always.
> 3. **Units confusion in capacity formulas:** $Q$ can be in meq/g (dry resin) or meq/mL (wet resin) — **always state your units**.
> 4. **Resolution vs. Selectivity confusion:** Resolution ($R_s$) is the **measured** quality of separation between two peaks. Selectivity ($\alpha$) is a **property of the chromatographic system** (relative retention). They are related but not the same.
> 5. **Forgetting the valence in numerical problems:** When calculating equivalents, multiply moles by valence (e.g., 1 mole of Ca²⁺ = 2 equivalents). This is a frequent source of mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST:**

- **Chromatography** = separation by differential distribution between two phases (stationary + mobile).
- **Ion Exchange Chromatography (IEC)** = separation of ions based on **reversible electrostatic exchange** with a charged resin.
- **Two types:** Cation exchange (resin is **negative**, attracts **cations**) and Anion exchange (resin is **positive**, attracts **anions**).
- **Strong ion exchangers** (sulfonic acid, quaternary ammonium) work over a wide pH range; **weak exchangers** (carboxylic acid, DEAE) work over a limited range.
- **Selectivity coefficient** $K_{A}^{B} > 1$ means the resin **prefers ion B**.
- **Order of binding strength** on a cation exchanger: $\text{Al}^{3+} > \text{Ca}^{2+} > \text{Na}^{+}$ (higher valence = stronger binding).
- **Elution methods:** Salt gradient (most common), pH change, or specific competing ligand.
- **Breakthrough capacity** $Q_b = \dfrac{C_0 \cdot V_b}{m_{resin}}$ — practical capacity under flow.
- **Total capacity** $Q_t$ = theoretical capacity from functional group density.
- **Retention factor** $k = \dfrac{t_R - t_M}{t_M}$ — optimal between 1 and 10.
- **Purnell Resolution:** $R_s = \dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \dfrac{k_2}{1 + k_2}$.
- **Food applications:** Water softening, sugar decolorization, juice debittering, protein purification, amino acid analysis, enzyme isolation.
- **Advantages of chromatography:** high resolution, mild conditions, versatile, qualitative + quantitative, scalable, mild preservation of bioactives.
- **Disadvantages of chromatography:** costly equipment, slow throughput, skill-intensive, sample dilution, column degradation, solvent waste, complex method development.
- **Always** state units explicitly in numerical answers; always show the three Purnell terms when discussing resolution.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types and choice of adsorbants; adsorptive membranes; adsorption techniques; batch adsorption; stage-type and continuous adsorption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT527 - SEPARATION PROCESSES IN FOOD TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Types and choice of adsorbants; adsorptive membranes; adsorption techniques; batch adsorption; stage-type and continuous adsorption # MODULE 4 — ADSORPTION: ADSORBENTS, MEMBRANES, AND CONTACTING SYSTEMS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Adsorption **Adsorption** is a surface-based unit operation in which molecules of a fluid phase (the *adsorbate*) accumulate on the surface of a solid (the *adsorbent*) through physical (van der Waals) or chemical (covalent/ionic) interactions. The solid itself is never distributed into the fluid — only a thin interfacial layer forms. The reverse process, where molecules leave the surface, is called **desorption**. In food technology, adsorption is widely used for: - Decolorization of sugar syrups, juices, and oils - Removal of off-flavors, toxins (e.g., aflatoxins), and bitter compounds - Deodorization and aroma recovery - Drying of gases and solvent recovery > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 4 treats adsorption as an *equilibrium-stage* and *rate-based* separation, conceptually parallel to absorption/stripping but with a solid phase replacing the liquid solvent. ### 1.2 Adsorbent vs. Adsorbate — The Two Actors | Term | Role | Example in Food Industry | |---|---|---| | **Adsorbent** | The solid that *provides* the surface | Activated carbon, silica gel, zeolite 4A | | **Adsorbate** | The substance being *captured* from the fluid | Color bodies, moisture, caffeine, pigments | | **Adsorption** | The process itself | Sugar syrup passing through a carbon column | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Magnet and Iron Filings Imagine sprinkling iron filings on a table and passing a **magnet** just above it. The filings do not enter the magnet; they only **stick to its surface**. The magnet is your *adsorbent*, the filings are your *adsorbate*, and the table is the *bulk fluid phase*. - A **stronger magnet** (high surface area, high affinity) → more filings stick → higher *capacity*. - A **saturated magnet** (filled surface) cannot hold more filings → *equilibrium is reached*. - To reuse the magnet, you must **wipe the filings off** → *regeneration / desorption*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Two Adsorption Regimes:** *Physisorption* (weak, reversible, multi-layer, low enthalpy of $\Delta H_{ads} \approx 20$ to **40 kJ/mol**) and *Chemisorption* (strong, often irreversible, mono-layer, $\Delta H_{ads} \approx 40$ to **400 kJ/mol**). Most food-industry adsorptions are physisorption. ### 1.4 Why This Module Matters in Food Engineering Adsorption operates at **ambient or mild temperatures**, preserving heat-labile nutrients (vitamins, aromas, probiotics). It requires **no phase change**, hence low energy cost. The standard metric used to grade adsorbents is **specific surface area** expressed in $\mathbf{m^2/g}$, and a good food-grade adsorbent typically has $S_{BET} \geq 500 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}$. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Types of Adsorbents ### 2.1.1 Classification by Chemical Nature Adsorbents used in food processing fall into three families: **(A) Carbonaceous Adsorbents** - **Activated carbon** (powdered — PAC, granular — GAC, extruded — EAC). Derived from coconut shell, wood, lignite, or peat. Pore sizes $0.5$ to **$50$ nm**, $S_{BET} = 500$ to $1500 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}$. Used for decolorization of sugar, removal of chlorination by-products, and aflatoxin binding. **(B) Inorganic Mineral Adsorbents** - **Silica gel** $\left(\text{SiO}_2 \cdot n\text{H}_2\text{O}\right)$: polar surface (Si–OH groups), ideal for drying air and removing water from ethanol. - **Activated alumina** $\left(\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3\right)$: amphoteric, used for fluoride removal and decolorization. - **Zeolites** (e.g., 3A, 4A, 5A, 13X): crystalline aluminosilicates with uniform pore apertures from **0.3 nm to 1 nm**. 4A zeolite is widely used in sugar refining to trap color bodies. - **Activated clay (bentonite, fuller's earth)**: layered silicates used to refine vegetable oils. **(C) Polymeric / Resin Adsorbents** - **Macroreticular resins** (Amberlite XAD-4, XAD-7, Diaion HP-20). Hydrophobic polymer matrices with $S_{BET} = 500$ to $800 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}$. Used for recovering polyphenols, anthocyanins, and aroma compounds. ### 2.1.2 Adsorptive Membranes (Hybrid Sorbents) An **adsorptive membrane** is a hybrid material in which adsorptive particles (zeolites, activated carbon, MOFs) are embedded in a polymeric or inorganic matrix. They combine the **selectivity of a sorbent** with the **permeability of a membrane**. Key types: - **Mixed-matrix membranes (MMM)**: zeolite particles dispersed in PDMS/PVDF/PES. - **Functionalized polymeric membranes**: surface-grafted with affinity ligands. - **Inorganic adsorptive membranes**: $\gamma$-alumina, MFI-type zeolite on $\alpha$-alumina support. > [!IMPORTANT] > In food applications, adsorptive membranes are used for *in-situ* removal of bitter compounds (naringin, limonin) from citrus juices and for *continuous* recovery of aroma volatiles. ## 2.2 Criteria for Choosing an Adsorbent The selection follows a **seven-parameter rule** for KTU problems: 1. **High specific surface area** ($S_{BET}$) 2. **Appropriate pore size distribution** — microporous ($< 2$ nm) for gases; mesoporous ($2$ to $50$ nm) for liquid-phase food color bodies. 3. **Surface chemistry** — polar surface for water/polar molecules; non-polar for oils and aromas. 4. **Mechanical strength** — must withstand column pressure drops. 5. **Regenerability** — thermal swing or pressure swing regeneration cycles. 6. **Food-grade safety** — FDA/EFSA compliance, no toxic leachates. 7. **Cost and availability** — bulk price in ₹/kg. ## 2.3 Adsorption Techniques — An Overview Adsorption contacting is classified by **mode of solid–fluid contact**: | Technique | Description | Industrial Use | |---|---|---| | **Batch (stirred tank)** | Solid + fluid in agitated vessel until equilibrium | Lab-scale, small batch decolorization | | **Stage-type (cross/counter-current)** | Multiple equilibrium stages, solid moves between stages | Sugar decolorization, multistage contactors | | **Continuous (fixed bed / packed column)** | Fluid flows through a stationary solid bed; outlet concentration is monitored | Industrial sugar refining, water treatment | | **Fluidized-bed adsorption** | Solid suspended by upward flow; high mass-transfer rates | Large-scale gas treatment | | **Simulated Moving Bed (SMB)** | Mimics counter-current with periodic port switching | Continuous chromatographic separation of sugars | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead of the vertical pipe character in formulas to keep markdown tables clean. | # | Equation | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $q = \dfrac{x}{m}$ | Loading (adsorbate mass / dry adsorbent mass) | kg/kg | | 2 | $q_e = \dfrac{V \left(C_0 - C_e\right)}{m}$ | Equilibrium loading from batch data | kg/kg | | 3 | $q = \dfrac{q_m K_L C}{1 + K_L C}$ | **Langmuir isotherm** (mono-layer) | kg/kg | | 4 | $q = K_F \, C^{1/n}$ | **Freundlich isotherm**
