$\left[\text{kg/m}^3\right]$ - $q, q_e, q_m$ = instantaneous, equilibrium, and maximum loading $\left[\text{kg/kg}\right]$ - $m$ = dry mass of adsorbent $\left[\text{kg}\right]$; $V$ = fluid volume $\left[\text{m}^3\right]$ - $K_L$ = Langmuir constant $\left[\text{m}^3/\text{kg}\right]$ - $K_F$ = Freundlich constant $\left[\left(\text{kg/kg}\right)\left(\text{m}^3/\text{kg}\right)^{1/n}\right]$ - $k_1, k_2$ = pseudo-first / second-order rate constants - $k_{Th}$ = Thomas constant $\left[\text{m}^3/(\text{kg}\cdot\text{s})\right]$ - $N_0$ = adsorption capacity per unit bed volume $\left[\text{kg/m}^3\right]$ - $Z, L$ = bed depth / length $\left[\text{m}\right]$; $u, u_0$ = interstitial velocity $\left[\text{m/s}\right]$ - $C_b$ = breakthrough concentration; $t_b$ = breakthrough time ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The above equations are used by food engineers to: - Design **column diameter** from superficial velocity $u_0 \leq 0.01 \text{ m/s}$ to avoid bed fluidization. - Predict **regeneration frequency** from breakthrough time $t_b$. - Estimate **carbon dose** for batch decolorization from $q_e$ values. - Optimize **multi-stage counter-current** contactors for color-body removal in cane-sugar refining. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Langmuir Isotherm **Assumptions:** 1. The surface contains a fixed number of identical adsorption sites. 2. Each site holds **one** adsorbate molecule (mono-layer coverage). 3. No interaction between adsorbed molecules. 4. Adsorption is reversible: $A + S \rightleftharpoons AS$. Let: - $\theta$ = fraction of sites occupied = $q / q_m$ - $C$ = fluid-phase concentration in equilibrium with $\theta$ - $k_a, k_d$ = adsorption and desorption rate constants **Step 1** — Rate of adsorption (proportional to bare sites and $C$): $$r_a = k_a \, C \, (1 - \theta)$$ **Step 2** — Rate of desorption (proportional to occupied sites): $$r_d = k_d \, \theta$$ **Step 3** — At equilibrium, $r_a = r_d$: $$k_a \, C \, (1 - \theta) = k_d \, \theta$$ **Step 4** — Solve for $\theta$: $$k_a \, C - k_a \, C \, \theta = k_d \, \theta$$ $$k_a \, C = \theta \left(k_d + k_a \, C\right)$$ $$\theta = \dfrac{k_a \, C}{k_d + k_a \, C} = \dfrac{K_L \, C}{1 + K_L \, C}$$ where $K_L = k_a / k_d$ is the Langmuir equilibrium constant $\left[\text{m}^3/\text{kg}\right]$. **Step 5** — Convert to loading form using $q = \theta \, q_m$: $$q = \dfrac{q_m \, K_L \, C}{1 + K_L \, C}$$ This is the **Langmuir isotherm equation** (Equation 3 in the formula sheet). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Linear form (Row 5 of Formula Sheet):** $\dfrac{C_e}{q_e} = \dfrac{1}{q_m K_L} + \dfrac{C_e}{q_m}$. A plot of $C_e/q_e$ vs $C_e$ gives a straight line of slope $1/q_m$ and intercept $1/(q_m K_L)$. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Freundlich Isotherm **Empirical derivation** from heterogeneous surfaces. The total adsorption energy is assumed to vary logarithmically: $$\theta \propto C^{\beta} \quad \text{where} \quad 0 < \beta < 1$$ **Step 1** — Define loading in terms of $C$ and an empirical constant: $$q = K_F \, C^{1/n}$$ **Step 2** — Apply $\log_{10}$ to both sides: $$\log q = \log K_F + \dfrac{1}{n}\log C$$ **Step 3** — A plot of $\log q$ vs $\log C$ gives a straight line: - **Slope** $= 1/n$ (heterogeneity factor; $n$ between 1 and 10 indicates favourable adsorption). - **Intercept** $= \log K_F$. > [!NOTE] > The Freundlich equation **does not** predict a maximum loading $q_m$. It is best for low-to-moderate concentrations and heterogeneous surfaces (such as activated carbon). ## 3.3 Batch Adsorption — Mass Balance and Kinetics Consider a closed vessel containing volume $V$ of solution (concentration $C$) and mass $m$ of adsorbent (loading $q$). **Step 1** — Total moles of solute in the system is conserved: $$V \, C + m \, q = V \, C_0 + m \, q_0$$ If $q_0 = 0$ at $t = 0$: $$V \, C + m \, q = V \, C_0$$ **Step 2** — Differentiate with respect to time: $$V \, \dfrac{dC}{dt} + m \, \dfrac{dq}{dt} = 0$$ Rearranged: $$V \, \dfrac{dC}{dt} = -m \, \dfrac{dq}{dt}$$ This is the **kinetic mass balance** (Equation 7 in the formula sheet). ### 3.3.1 Pseudo-First-Order Model (Lagergren) Assume $\dfrac{dq}{dt} = k_1 \left(q_e - q_t\right)$: $$\dfrac{dq_t}{q_e - q_t} = k_1 \, dt$$ Integrate from $0$ to $t$: $$\ln \left(q_e - q_t\right) = \ln q_e - k_1 \, t$$ Exponentiating and rearranging: $$q_t = q_e \left(1 - e^{-k_1 t}\right)$$ In concentration form (since $C_t \propto q_t$): $$C_t = C_0 \, e^{-k_1 t}$$ > [!NOTE] > A plot of $\ln(q_e - q_t)$ vs $t$ gives slope $-k_1$. If the regression is poor (R² $< 0.99$), the kinetics are **not** pseudo-first-order. ### 3.3.2 Pseudo-Second-Order Model Assume $\dfrac{dq_t}{dt} = k_2 \left(q_e - q_t\right)^2$: $$\dfrac{dq_t}{\left(q_e - q_t\right)^2} = k_2 \, dt$$ Integrate: $$\dfrac{1}{q_e - q_t} = k_2 \, t + \dfrac{1}{q_e}$$ Multiply both sides by $t$ and rearrange: $$\dfrac{t}{q_t} = \dfrac{1}{k_2 q_e^2} + \dfrac{t}{q_e}$$ This is **Equation 9** of the formula sheet. A plot of $t/q_t$ vs $t$ yields $q_e$ and $k_2$ from slope and intercept. ## 3.4 Stage-Type Adsorption — Cross-Current and Counter-Current ### 3.4.1 Single-Stage Batch Contact For a single equilibrium stage with $V \text{ m}^3$ of solution and $m \text{ kg}$ of fresh adsorbent, the **equilibrium relation** $q_e = f(C_e)$ is combined with the **operating line** $q = \dfrac{V}{m}\left(C_0 - C\right)$. **Step-by-step procedure (KTU board method):** 1. Draw the equilibrium curve $q_e = f(C_e)$. 2. Draw the operating line with slope $-V/m$ passing through $(C_0, 0)$. 3. The intersection gives the equilibrium point $(C_e, q_e)$. 4. Compute the equilibrium loading using Equation 2: $$q_e = \dfrac{V \left(C_0 - C_e\right)}{m}$$ ### 3.4.2 Multi-Stage Cross-Current Fresh adsorbent is added at each stage; the solution moves in series. **Operating line for stage $i$:** $$q_i - q_{i-1} = \dfrac{V_i}{m_i}\left(C_{i-1} - C_i\right)$$ For equal stages, $V_i = V$ and $m_i = m$, leading to the **repeated cross-current relation**: $$C_N = C_0 \left(\dfrac{1}{1 + \frac{m q_m}{V C_0}}\right)^N \quad \text{(Langmuir form)}$$ **Number of stages required:** $$N = \dfrac{\ln \left(C_0 / C_N\right)}{\ln \left(1 + m q_m / V C_0\right)}$$ ### 3.4.3 Multi-Stage Counter-Current Solid moves counter to fluid. The **operating line for each stage** is: $$q_N - q_0 = \dfrac{V}{m}\left(C_{N-1} - C_N\right) \quad \text{or in general} \quad \dfrac{m}{V} = \dfrac{C_{N-1} - C_N}{q_N - q_0}$$ If solid enters fresh ($q_0 = 0$): $$m = \dfrac{V \, C_{N-1} - C_N}{q_N} \quad \text{or} \quad m \, q_N = V \left(C_{N-1} - C_N\right)$$ The **Kremser equation** for counter-current adsorption is: $$\dfrac{C_N - C^{*}}{C_0 - C^{*}} = \dfrac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1} \quad \text{where} \quad A = \dfrac{m \, q_m / V}{C_0 - C_0^{*}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Kremser equation** is the single most important formula for solving KTU counter-current adsorption numericals. It is analogous to the gas-absorption Kremser equation but with the *adsorption factor* $A = L/(m \cdot V)$. ## 3.5 Continuous Adsorption — Fixed-Bed Breakthrough In a packed column, the solid is stationary and the fluid flows continuously. The bed gradually saturates from the inlet end, forming a **mass-transfer zone (MTZ)** that travels along the bed. The outlet concentration versus time is the **breakthrough curve**. **Key definitions:** - $t_b$ = breakthrough time when $C_{out} = C_b$ (typically $0.05 \, C_0$ or $0.10 \, C_0$). - $t_s$ = saturation time when $C_{out} \approx C_0$. - **Bed utilization** $= \dfrac{\text{area above breakthrough curve}}{\text{area of full rectangle to } C_0}$. ### 3.5.1 Thomas Model (Column Kinetics) Assumes plug flow, Langmuir kinetics, no axial dispersion: $$\ln \left(\dfrac{C_0}{C_t} - 1\right) = \dfrac{k_{Th} \, q_0 \, m}{Q} - k_{Th} \, C_0 \, t$$ A plot of $\ln \left(\dfrac{C_0}{C_t} - 1\right)$ vs $t$ yields slope $k_{Th} C_0$ and intercept $\dfrac{k_{Th} q_0 m}{Q}$. ### 3.5.2 BDST (Bed Depth Service Time) Model Linear relation between bed depth $Z$ and service time $t_b$: $$t_b = \dfrac{N_0 Z}{u_0} - \dfrac{1}{k_a C_0}\ln \left(\dfrac{C_0}{C_b} - 1\right)$$ **Critical bed depth** $Z_0$ (where $t_b = 0$): $$Z_0 = \dfrac{u_0}{k_a N_0}\ln \left(\dfrac{C_0}{C_b} - 1\right)$$ If a pilot column of depth $Z_1$ gives breakthrough at $t_{b1}$, the **scale-up depth** $Z_2$ for a new breakthrough time $t_{b2}$ is: $$Z_2 = Z_1 \left[\dfrac{t_{b2}}{t_{b1}}\right]$$ > [!NOTE] > This proportional-depth rule is the standard KTU board question for **fixed-bed adsorber design**. ## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style) **Problem:** A sugar syrup (volume $V = 1 \text{ m}^3$, initial color concentration $C_0 = 100 \text{ mg/L}$) is contacted with $m = 2 \text{ kg}$ of activated carbon in a batch stirred tank. The Langmuir constants are $q_m = 250 \text{ mg/g}$, $K_L = 0.05 \text{ L/mg}$. Find the equilibrium concentration $C_e$ and the equilibrium loading $q_e$. **Step 1** — Convert units: $q_m = 250 \, 000 \text{ mg/kg}$, $K_L = 0.05 \text{ L/mg}$. **Step 2** — Mass balance: $V C_0 = V C_e + m q_e$ $$1 \times 100 = 1 \times C_e + 2 \times q_e$$ $$C_e + 2 q_e = 100 \quad \ldots (i)$$ **Step 3** — Langmuir equilibrium (loading in mg/kg, $C_e$ in mg/L): $$q_e = \dfrac{250\,000 \times 0.05 \times C_e}{1 + 0.05 \, C_e} = \dfrac{12\,500 \, C_e}{1 + 0.05 \, C_e} \quad \ldots (ii)$$ **Step 4** — Substitute (ii) into (i): $$C_e + 2 \times \dfrac{12\,500 \, C_e}{1 + 0.05 \, C_e} = 100$$ $$C_e \left(1 + 0.05 C_e\right) + 25\,000 \, C_e = 100 \left(1 + 0.05 C_e\right)$$ $$C_e + 0.05 C_e^2 + 25\,000 \, C_e = 100 + 5 C_e$$ $$0.05 C_e^2 + 25\,000 \, C_e - 5 C_e - 100 = 0$$ $$0.05 C_e^2 + 24\,995 \, C_e - 100 = 0$$ **Step 5** — Solve the quadratic: $$C_e = \dfrac{-24\,995 \pm \sqrt{24\,995^2 + 4 \times 0.05 \times 100}}{2 \times 0.05}$$ $$C_e = \dfrac{-24\,995 \pm \sqrt{624\,750\,025 + 20}}{0.10}$$ $$C_e = \dfrac{-24\,995 \pm 24\,995.0004}{0.10}$$ Take the **positive** root: $$C_e \approx \dfrac{0.0004}{0.10} = 0.004 \text{ mg/L}$$ **Step 6** — Back-substitute into (i): $$q_e = \dfrac{100 - C_e}{2} = \dfrac{100 - 0.004}{2} = 49.998 \approx 50 \text{ mg/kg}$$ **Step 7** — Interpretation: Activated carbon at $2 \text{ kg}$ per cubic metre removes $> 99.99\%$ of the color bodies. The bed is operating near saturation ($q_e \approx q_m / 5$), confirming efficient utilization. # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Differentiate between physisorption and chemisorption. List two adsorbents used in the food industry for each type.** **Model Answer:** - **Physisorption:** weak van der Waals forces, low enthalpy $\Delta H_{ads} = 20$ to $40 \text{ kJ/mol}$, multi-layer, fully reversible. *Examples:* activated carbon (decolorization of sugar syrup), silica gel (drying of compressed air for aseptic packaging). - **Chemisorption:** strong covalent/ionic bonding, $\Delta H_{ads} = 40$ to $400 \text{ kJ/mol}$, mono-layer, often irreversible. *Examples:* nickel-on-silica for hydrogenation of vegetable oils (technically catalysis but follows the same principle), chemically modified chitosan beads for metal-ion binding in food effluents. ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **What is an adsorptive membrane? Mention two applications in food processing.** **Model Answer:** An **adsorptive membrane** is a hybrid material that combines adsorptive particles (zeolites, activated carbon, MOFs) with a permeable membrane matrix. It allows simultaneous separation by both *sieving* and *adsorption* mechanisms in a single unit. - *Application 1:* **Mixed-matrix PDMS-zeolite membrane** for in-situ removal of naringin and limonin from citrus juices to debitter them. - *Application 2:* **Activated-carbon-filled polymeric hollow-fibre membrane** for continuous recovery of aroma volatiles (esters, aldehydes) from fruit-juice processing streams. ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *adsorption factor* $A$ in the Kremser equation with the *flow ratio* $L/V$ from gas absorption. Always re-derive the operating-line slope for **solid–liquid** adsorption. Another common loss of marks: forgetting to convert $q_m$ from mg/g to mg/kg before substituting into the mass balance. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Batch and Stage-Type Adsorption **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4, Q7(b)]** **(a)** A colour-body solution of volume $V = 0.5 \text{ m}^3$ with $C_0 = 200 \text{ mg/L}$ is treated in a **single-stage batch** with $m = 1 \text{ kg}$ activated carbon. The equilibrium follows the Langmuir isotherm with $q_m = 300 \text{ mg/g}$ and $K_L = 0.02 \text{ L/mg}$. Determine the **equilibrium concentration** $C_e$ and the **percentage removal** of color bodies. **Assume adsorbent is initially free of adsorbate.** **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A sugar refinery needs to reduce the color of a syrup from $C_0 = 200 \text{ mg/L}$ to $C_N = 10 \text{ mg/L}$ using a **three-stage cross-current** adsorption with equal mass of fresh carbon $m = 0.5 \text{ kg}$ per stage and $V = 0.5 \text{ m}^3$ per stage. Using the same Langmuir parameters as in (a), calculate the **final exit concentration** and the **total carbon consumption**. Compare with a **single-stage** system using all $1.5 \text{ kg}$ at once. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a):** Convert units: $q_m = 300 \text{ mg/g} = 300\,000 \text{ mg/kg}$, $K_L = 0.02 \text{ L/mg}$. Mass balance with $q_0 = 0$: $$V C_0 = V C_e + m q_e$$ $$0.5 \times 200 = 0.5 \, C_e + 1 \times q_e$$ $$100 = 0.5 C_e + q_e \quad \ldots (1)$$ Langmuir isotherm: $$q_e = \dfrac{q_m K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e} = \dfrac{300\,000 \times 0.02 \times C_e}{1 + 0.02 C_e} = \dfrac{6\,000 C_e}{1 + 0.02 C_e} \quad \ldots (2)$$ Substitute (2) into (1): $$0.5 C_e + \dfrac{6\,000 C_e}{1 + 0.02 C_e} = 100$$ Multiply by $(1 + 0.02 C_e)$: $$0.5 C_e (1 + 0.02 C_e) + 6\,000 C_e = 100(1 + 0.02 C_e)$$ $$0.5 C_e + 0.01 C_e^2 + 6\,000 C_e = 100 + 2 C_e$$ $$0.01 C_e^2 + 6\,000.5 C_e - 2 C_e - 100 = 0$$ $$0.01 C_e^2 + 5\,998.5 C_e - 100 = 0$$ Solve quadratic (positive root): $$C_e = \dfrac{-5\,998.5 + \sqrt{5\,998.5^2 + 4 \times 0.01 \times 100}}{2 \times 0.01}$$ $$C_e = \dfrac{-5\,998.5 + \sqrt{35\,982\,020.25 + 4}}{0.02}$$ $$C_e = \dfrac{-5\,998.5 + 5\,998.50033}{0.02} \approx 0.0166 \text{ mg/L}$$ From (1): $q_e = 100 - 0.5 \times 0.0166 \approx 99.99 \text{ mg/kg}$ (i.e., $0.0999 \text{ mg/g}$). **Percentage removal:** $$\eta = \dfrac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0} \times 100 = \dfrac{200 - 0.0166}{200} \times 100 \approx 99.99\%$$ --- **Part (b):** For a **single-stage** system with $m_{tot} = 1.5 \text{ kg}$ and $V = 0.5 \text{ m}^3$: $$100 = 0.5 C_e + 1.5 q_e \quad \text{and} \quad q_e = \dfrac{6\,000 C_e}{1 + 0.02 C_e}$$ By the same algebra, the equilibrium concentration $C_e \to 0.011 \text{ mg/L}$ (slightly lower than 1-stage with 1 kg because more carbon is used). **Three-stage cross-current** (equal mass $m = 0.5 \text{ kg}$ per stage): For each stage, the **operating line** slope is $V/m = 0.5/0.5 = 1$: $$C_{i-1} = C_i + \dfrac{m}{V} q_i = C_i + q_i$$ For a *very strongly favourable* Langmuir at low concentrations ($K_L C_e \ll 1$): $q_e \approx q_m K_L C_e = 300\,000 \times 0.02 \times C_e = 6\,000 C_e$. Substitute linearised form into operating line: $$C_{i-1} = C_i + 6\,000 C_i = 6\,001 C_i$$ $$\dfrac{C_{i-1}}{C_i} = 6\,001$$ After three stages: $$C_3 = \dfrac{C_0}{(6\,001)^3} = \dfrac{200}{2.16 \times 10^{11}} \approx 9.25 \times 10^{-10} \text{ mg/L}$$ (essentially complete decolorization). **Total carbon consumption:** $1.5 \text{ kg}$ in both single-stage and three-stage — but the **three-stage system reaches the target $C_N = 10 \text{ mg/L}$ with a much smaller total carbon mass** if we now optimise by reducing the per-stage mass. **Comparison:** Cross-current requires *less carbon for the same removal* because each stage operates against a fresh, high driving force $\left(C_{i-1} - C_i\right)$. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Continuous Fixed-Bed Adsorption **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, Q8(a)]** **(a)** Derive the **Bed Depth Service Time (BDST)** equation. Define all terms and explain the physical significance of the *critical bed depth* $Z_0$. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A pilot column of depth $Z_1 = 0.5 \text{ m}$ gives a breakthrough time $t_{b1} = 8 \text{ h}$ at a superficial velocity $u_0 = 0.005 \text{ m/s}$ and $C_0 = 100 \text{ mg/L}$, with $C_b = 10 \text{ mg/L}$. Estimate the **breakthrough time** for a scaled-up column of depth $Z_2 = 1.5 \text{ m}$ at the same flow conditions. Also calculate the **critical bed depth** if the adsorption rate constant is $k_a = 0.0001 \text{ L/(mg·s)}$ and $N_0 = 50\,000 \text{ mg/L}$. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a):** The BDST model assumes: - Plug flow with negligible axial dispersion. - Constant adsorption capacity $N_0$ (mass of adsorbate per unit bed volume at breakthrough). - The mass-transfer zone (MTZ) moves as a coherent wave with velocity $u_{MTZ}$. **Derivation:** The mass balance on a slice of bed gives the *wave-velocity* relation: $$u_{MTZ} = \dfrac{u_0 \, C_0}{N_0 + \varepsilon \, C_0} \approx \dfrac{u_0 \, C_0}{N_0}$$ (since $N_0 \gg \varepsilon C_0$ in liquid-phase adsorption). The **service time** for a column of depth $Z$ is the time for the MTZ to travel from the inlet to the outlet. The *stoichiometric* time is $t^* = Z/u_{MTZ} = Z N_0 / (u_0 C_0)$. The actual breakthrough is earlier by an amount $\tau$ corresponding to the residual capacity of the MTZ: $$\tau = \dfrac{1}{k_a C_0} \ln \left(\dfrac{C_0}{C_b} - 1\right)$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{t_b = \dfrac{N_0 Z}{u_0} - \dfrac{1}{k_a C_0}\ln \left(\dfrac{C_0}{C_b} - 1\right)}$$ **Physical significance of $Z_0$:** the *critical bed depth* is the minimum bed length for which $t_b \to 0$. Below $Z_0$, the bed saturates *before* the MTZ exits, so no useful service is obtained. Setting $t_b = 0$: $$Z_0 = \dfrac{u_0}{k_a N_0} \ln \left(\dfrac{C_0}{C_b} - 1\right)$$ --- **Part (b):** **Step 1 — Linear BDST proportional-depth rule** (since flow conditions are unchanged): $$\dfrac{t_{b2}}{Z_2} = \dfrac{t_{b1}}{Z_1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_{b2} = t_{b1} \times \dfrac{Z_2}{Z_1} = 8 \times \dfrac{1.5}{0.5} = 24 \text{ h}$$ **Step 2 — Critical bed depth:** $$Z_0 = \dfrac{u_0}{k_a N_0} \ln \left(\dfrac{C_0}{C_b} - 1\right)$$ $$Z_0 = \dfrac{0.005}{0.0001 \times 50\,000} \ln \left(\dfrac{100}{10} - 1\right)$$ $$Z_0 = \dfrac{0.005}{5} \ln (9) = 0.001 \times 2.1972 \approx 0.0022 \text{ m} = 2.2 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 3 — Verification using full BDST equation for column 1:** $$t_{b1} = \dfrac{N_0 Z_1}{u_0} - \dfrac{1}{k_a C_0}\ln \left(\dfrac{C_0}{C_b} - 1\right)$$ $$t_{b1} = \dfrac{50\,000 \times 0.5}{0.005} - \dfrac{1}{0.0001 \times 100} \times 2.1972$$ $$t_{b1} = 5\,000\,000 - 219\,720 \approx 4.78 \times 10^6 \text{ s} \approx 1328 \text{ h}$$ The proportional rule (8 h → 24 h) corresponds to a *different* assumed $N_0$ that absorbs the $\tau$ term. **Both methods are acceptable in KTU board answers**, but the explicit BDST form is preferred for full marks. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Note:** Many students omit units when substituting $k_a$ in the BDST formula. Always convert $k_a$ to L/(mg·s) when $C_0$ is in mg/L, and $N_0$ to mg/L to keep $Z_0$ in metres. A common error is using $C_b/C_0$ instead of $C_0/C_b$ inside the logarithm. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4:** - **Adsorption** is a *surface* phenomenon — never a bulk-dissolution process. - **Physisorption** dominates in food applications (low $\Delta H$, reversible, multi-layer). - **Activated carbon** is the *work-horse* adsorbent (PAC, GAC, EAC). - **Zeolites** give *molecular-sieve* selectivity by precise aperture size (3A, 4A, 5A, 13X). - **Adsorptive membranes** couple selectivity (adsorption) with permeability (membrane) — used for debittering and aroma recovery. - **Langmuir isotherm** assumes mono-layer on identical sites; **Freundlich** handles heterogeneous surfaces but fails at high $C$. - **Pseudo-first-order kinetics** fit loose physical adsorption; **pseudo-second-order** fit chemisorption-dominated systems. - **Single-stage** operating line: $q = (V/m)(C_0 - C)$. - **Kremser equation** (counter-current): $\dfrac{C_N - C^*}{C_0 - C^*} = \dfrac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$, where $A$ is the *adsorption factor* (analogous to $L/V$ in gas absorption). - **Cross-current** uses fresh solid at each stage — high driving force, low solid utilization. - **Counter-current** uses the solid most efficiently but requires solid handling between stages. - **Fixed-bed breakthrough** is described by $t_b$ (when $C_{out} = 0.05 C_0$ or $0.10 C_0$) and $t_s$ (when $C_{out} = C_0$). - **BDST equation** linearly relates bed depth $Z$ to service time $t_b$; *critical bed depth* $Z_0$ is the minimum for any service. - **Thomas model** is the most common model to fit column breakthrough data: $\ln(C_0/C_t - 1)$ vs $t$ is linear. - **Regeneration modes:** Thermal Swing Adsorption (TSA), Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA), and Solvent Elution. - **Dimensionless groups to remember:** - Reynolds number $Re_p = \dfrac{\rho u_0 d_p}{\mu}$ (must be $< 10$ to avoid fluidization). - Schmidt number $Sc = \mu / (\rho D_{AB})$. - Peclet number $Pe = u_0 d_p / D_{ax}$. - **Food-grade compliance** is *non-negotiable* — only FDA- or EFSA-approved adsorbents (activated carbon, food-grade silica, zeolites, food-grade resins) may contact consumables. - **Bed utilization** $\eta = q_{actual}/q_{saturation}$ — typical values 60 to 85% in well-designed columns. > **End of Module 4 Notes — PEFTT527, KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Fixed-bed adsorption; adsorption isotherms; ion exchange resins, properties; binary ion exchange; ion movement theory; controlling factors; electrophoresis; di-electrophoresis; zonal electrophoresis; applications of ion exchange separation in the food industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT527 - SEPARATION PROCESSES IN FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fixed-bed adsorption; adsorption isotherms; ion exchange resins, properties; binary ion exchange; ion movement theory; controlling factors; electrophoresis; di-electrophoresis; zonal electrophoresis; applications of ion exchange separation in the food industry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fixed-Bed Adsorption

**Formal Definition:** Fixed-bed adsorption is a continuous, cyclic separation operation in which a fluid mixture (gas or liquid) is passed through a stationary, packed column of solid adsorbent particles. As the fluid flows through the bed, one or more solutes (adsorbates) preferentially accumulate on the surface of the solid (adsorbent) until the bed becomes saturated and breakthrough occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A fixed-bed is *fixed* in space but operates *continuously in time*. The composition profile inside the bed changes as the adsorption wave moves through the column.

**Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition):**
Imagine a water filter jug at home. As water drips through the carbon block, contaminants stick to the carbon. The top layer of carbon gets "full" first, then the next layer, and so on. A "saturation front" slowly moves down the column. Eventually, water coming out the bottom starts to taste bad again — this is the **breakthrough point**. The fixed-bed adsorption column works exactly like this, but for industrial food streams (e.g., decolorization of sugar syrup, removal of bitter compounds from fruit juice).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Bed capacity is measured in **kg adsorbate / kg adsorbent** or **mg of solute per g of dry resin**. The dimensionless ratio of column length to particle diameter (L/dp) is typically kept > 50 to avoid wall effects and channeling.

---

## 1.2 Adsorption Isotherms

**Formal Definition:** An adsorption isotherm is the equilibrium relationship, at a constant temperature, between the concentration of adsorbate remaining in the fluid phase and the amount adsorbed onto the solid phase. It is plotted as $q_e$ (solid loading) versus $C_e$ (equilibrium fluid concentration).

**Three Classic Models:**

| Isotherm | Use Case |
|----------|----------|
| **Langmuir** | Monolayer adsorption on a homogeneous surface with finite sites |
| **Freundlich** | Heterogeneous surface, multilayer, low-to-intermediate concentrations |
| **BET** | Multilayer adsorption, especially for porous solids |

**Intuition:** A sorption isotherm is essentially a "fingerprint" of how strongly a solute loves to cling to a surface at a given temperature. A steep curve at low concentration means high affinity.

---

## 1.3 Ion Exchange Resins

**Formal Definition:** Ion exchange resins are insoluble, high-molecular-weight, cross-linked polymer matrices (typically polystyrene-divinylbenzene) to which fixed ionic functional groups are chemically attached. Counter-ions of opposite charge are loosely bound and can be stoichiometrically exchanged with ions of like charge from a surrounding solution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification by Ionic Nature:**
> - **Cation exchange resin:** Has fixed anionic groups (e.g., $-\text{SO}_3^-$, $-\text{COO}^-$) that exchange **cations** like $\text{Na}^+$, $\text{Ca}^{2+}$.
> - **Anion exchange resin:** Has fixed cationic groups (e.g., $-\text{N}(\text{CH}_3)_3^+$) that exchange **anions** like $\text{Cl}^-$, $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of ion exchange resin beads as tiny "molecular parking lots." The parking spaces are the fixed ionic groups. Cars (counter-ions) come in, and depending on who wants the spot more, one type parks while another type is pushed out (eluted). The resin is regenerated by flushing with a concentrated solution of the original ion (e.g., brine for water softeners).

---

## 1.4 Binary Ion Exchange

**Formal Definition:** Binary ion exchange involves the exchange of two ionic species (one monovalent and one divalent, or two cations of the same valence) between the resin phase and the solution phase. The equilibrium is governed by selectivity coefficients and equivalent fractions.

**Intuition:** When hard water ($\text{Ca}^{2+}$, $\text{Mg}^{2+}$) flows through a sodium-form cation resin, each $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ ion knocks out two $\text{Na}^+$ ions to maintain electroneutrality. It is a "two-for-one" trade.

---

## 1.5 Ion Movement Theory (Helfferich's Theory)

**Formal Definition:** According to Helfferich's ion movement (or interchange) theory, when a solution of counter-ion B is passed through a resin initially in form A, the exchange is limited by the slower-moving ion. A sharp interchange boundary forms, and the ions of higher mobility (smaller hydrated radius) lead, while the slower ions follow.

**Intuition:** Imagine two runners on a track. The faster runner gets ahead, but the slower one refuses to be left behind, so a "boundary" forms between them. In a resin column, this is the moving exchange front.

---

## 1.6 Controlling Factors in Ion Exchange

The key factors controlling ion exchange performance are:
- **Selectivity** of the resin for the target ion
- **Cross-link density** of the polymer matrix
- **Particle size** of the resin
- **Solution pH** (affects ionization of weak-acid/weak-base groups)
- **Temperature**
- **Flow rate / Contact time**
- **Ionic strength** and concentration of competing ions

---

## 1.7 Electrophoresis

**Formal Definition:** Electrophoresis is an electrokinetic separation technique in which charged particles (ions, proteins, colloids) migrate under the influence of an applied electric field. The migration velocity $v$ is given by:

$$v = \mu_e E$$

where $\mu_e$ is the electrophoretic mobility and $E$ is the electric field strength.

**Intuition:** Drop a battery into salty water. Positive ions swim toward the negative plate; negative ions swim toward the positive plate. Electrophoresis uses this same idea, but to *separate* molecules by their charge-to-size ratio.

---

## 1.8 Dielectrophoresis (DEP)

**Formal Definition:** Dielectrophoresis is the motion of polarizable particles in a **non-uniform** electric field due to the interaction of the particle's induced dipole with the field gradient. Unlike electrophoresis, DEP works on both charged and neutral particles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Difference:** Electrophoresis requires **net charge + uniform field**; Dielectrophoresis requires **field gradient + polarizability** (works on neutrals too).

**Intuition:** A small plastic bead placed in a non-uniform electric field will be pulled toward the region of high field strength (positive DEP) or repelled from it (negative DEP), depending on the dielectric properties of the bead relative to the medium. Used for *separating live vs. dead cells* and *sorting fat droplets in dairy emulsions*.

---

## 1.9 Zonal Electrophoresis

**Formal Definition:** Zonal electrophoresis is a technique in which the sample is applied as a narrow band (zone) in a supporting medium (gel, paper, or capillary) and the components separate into distinct zones based on their electrophoretic mobilities. The most common form uses a **polyacrylamide or agarose gel**.

**Intuition:** Pour colored ink at one end of a strip of wet paper; apply a voltage across the ends. Different dye molecules drift at different speeds and form separate colored "zones." That is zonal electrophoresis in a kitchen.

---

## 1.10 Applications in the Food Industry (Overview)

| Technique | Food Application |
|-----------|------------------|
| Fixed-bed adsorption | Decolorization of sugar syrup, removal of bitter limonoids from citrus juice |
| Ion exchange | Demineralization of whey, water softening in breweries, removal of acidity in fruit juices |
| Electrophoresis | Identification of milk proteins (casein vs. whey), authenticity testing of meat species |
| Dielectrophoresis | Sorting of viable yeast cells, separation of fat globules in dairy |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fixed-Bed Adsorption — The Breakthrough Curve

### Operational Steps
1. **Start of run:** Column is fresh/regenerated; outlet concentration $C_{\text{out}} \approx 0$.
2. **Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ):** A narrow zone in the bed where most of the adsorption takes place. Above it, the bed is saturated; below it, the bed is still fresh.
3. **Breakthrough point ($t_b$):** The time at which $C_{\text{out}}$ reaches a pre-set threshold (often 5 % or 10 % of $C_{\text{in}}$).
4. **Exhaustion point ($t_e$):** The time at which $C_{\text{out}} \approx C_{\text{in}}$ — the bed is fully saturated.
5. **Regeneration:** Hot solvent, steam, or acid/base is passed to desorb the solute and reuse the bed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the MTZ matter?** The steepness of the MTZ determines bed efficiency. A short, sharp MTZ = high utilization of bed capacity. A long, dispersed MTZ = wasted capacity at the top and bottom.

---

## 2.2 The Three Adsorption Isotherms — Theory

### Langmuir Isotherm
**Assumptions:** (i) monolayer coverage, (ii) energetically equivalent sites, (iii) no interaction between adsorbed molecules.

$$q_e = \frac{q_m K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e}$$

- $q_e$ = equilibrium loading (mg/g)
- $q_m$ = maximum monolayer capacity (mg/g)
- $K_L$ = Langmuir affinity constant (L/mg)
- $C_e$ = equilibrium concentration (mg/L)

**Linear form (for plotting):**

$$\frac{C_e}{q_e} = \frac{1}{q_m K_L} + \frac{C_e}{q_m}$$

### Freundlich Isotherm
**Assumption:** Heterogeneous surface, multilayer adsorption. Empirical power-law:

$$q_e = K_F C_e^{1/n}$$

- $K_F$ = capacity constant
- $1/n$ = intensity constant (0 < 1/n < 1 for favorable adsorption)

**Linear form:**

$$\log q_e = \log K_F + \frac{1}{n} \log C_e$$

### BET Isotherm
**Assumption:** Multilayer adsorption; the BET constant $C_B$ relates to the heat of adsorption of the first layer.

$$\frac{1}{q_e\left[(C_s/C_e)-1\right]} = \frac{1}{q_m C_B} + \frac{C_B-1}{q_m C_B}\cdot \frac{C_e}{C_s}$$

- $C_s$ = saturation concentration of solute.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $q_e = \dfrac{q_m K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e}$ | Langmuir isotherm | $q_e$ in mg/g |
| 2 | $R_L = \dfrac{1}{1+K_L C_0}$ | Separation factor; $0<R_L<1$ favorable | dimensionless |
| 3 | $q_e = K_F C_e^{1/n}$ | Freundlich isotherm | $q_e$ in mg/g |
| 4 | $q_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{(C_0-C_e)V}{m}$ | Experimental capacity from batch | mg/g |
| 5 | $v = \mu_e E$ | Electrophoretic migration velocity | m/s |
| 6 | $\mu_e = \dfrac{q}{6\pi \eta r}$ | Mobility (Smoluchowski/Hückel) | m²/(V·s) |
| 7 | $F_{\text{DEP}} = 2\pi r^3 \varepsilon_m \text{Re}[K(\omega)]\nabla \vert E \vert^2$ | DEP force on a sphere | N |
| 8 | $K_d = \dfrac{q_{\text{ion on resin}}}{C_{\text{ion in solution}}}$ | Distribution coefficient | mL/g or L/kg |
| 9 | $\alpha_B^A = \dfrac{y_A x_B}{y_B x_A}$ | Selectivity coefficient (A over B) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $t_b = \dfrac{x}{u}$ | Stoichiometric breakthrough time | s |
| 11 | $\Delta t = (1-f)\dfrac{m_s q_s}{C_0 Q}$ | Unused bed length (UBTL) relation | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where does $R_L$ come from?** $R_L$ is the dimensionless separation factor of the Langmuir isotherm. $0 < R_L < 1$ → **favorable**; $R_L = 0$ → **irreversible**; $R_L = 1$ → **linear**; $R_L > 1$ → **unfavorable**.

---

## 2.4 Ion Exchange Resin — Properties

| Property | Description | Engineering Implication |
|----------|-------------|--------------------------|
| **Matrix** | Polystyrene-DVB or acrylic | Affects hydrophobicity |
| **Cross-link (% DVB)** | 2 %–20 % | Higher DVB = harder, slower, more selective for small ions |
| **Functional group** | $-\text{SO}_3^H$, $-\text{COOH}$, $-\text{NR}_3^+\text{OH}^-$ | Defines cation/anion, strong/weak |
| **Capacity** | Total exchange sites, eq/L or eq/kg | Determines throughput |
| **Particle size** | 0.04 – 1.2 mm | Affects pressure drop and kinetics |
| **Moisture content** | 40 – 70 % | Affects swelling, density |
| **Swelling** | Volume change on ionic form change | Important for column packing |
| **Thermal stability** | Max operating °C | Cation resins 120 °C, anion 60 °C |
| **Selectivity** | $\alpha$ value | Determines separation sharpness |

### Strong-Acid Cation (SAC) Resin Example

$$2\text{R-SO}_3^-\text{Na}^+ + \text{Ca}^{2+} \rightleftharpoons (\text{R-SO}_3^-)_2\text{Ca}^{2+} + 2\text{Na}^+$$

This is the chemistry inside every household water softener.

---

## 2.5 Binary Ion Exchange — Selectivity

For the generic exchange:

$$A^{z_A^+} + z_A \cdot \text{R-B} \rightleftharpoons z_A \cdot \text{R-A} + B^{z_B^+}$$

Selectivity coefficient:

$$\alpha_B^A = \frac{y_A^{z_B} \cdot x_B^{z_A}}{y_B^{z_A} \cdot x_A^{z_B}}$$

where $y$ = equivalent fraction in resin, $x$ = equivalent fraction in solution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Empirical Selectivity Order (cation resin, increasing):** $\text{Li}^+ < \text{H}^+ < \text{Na}^+ < \text{K}^+ < \text{Cs}^+ < \text{Mg}^{2+} < \text{Ca}^{2+} < \text{Sr}^{2+} < \text{Ba}^{2+} < \text{Al}^{3+}$. Divalent ions are generally preferred over monovalent on SAC resins.

---

## 2.6 Electrophoresis & Dielectrophoresis — Controlling Factors

| Factor | Effect |
|--------|--------|
| Field strength $E$ | Higher $E$ → faster migration, but more Joule heating |
| Buffer pH | Affects net charge of proteins |
| Buffer ionic strength | High $I$ → low electric field, low mobility, high heat |
| Gel porosity | Pore size sieves molecules by size (SDS-PAGE) |
| Particle polarizability | Determines sign and strength of DEP force |
| Field frequency $\omega$ | At high freq., Clausius-Mossotti factor $K(\omega)$ becomes negative → negative DEP |

**Clausius-Mossotti factor:**

$$K(\omega) = \frac{\varepsilon_p^* - \varepsilon_m^*}{\varepsilon_p^* + 2\varepsilon_m^*}$$

where $\varepsilon^*$ is the complex permittivity. Sign of $\text{Re}[K(\omega)]$ decides whether DEP is positive (attraction) or negative (repulsion).

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

- **Sugar refining:** Fixed-bed activated carbon removes colour bodies from cane sugar syrup.
- **Whey demineralization:** Ion exchange produces infant formula-grade whey with 90 % mineral removal.
- **Citrus debittering:** Adsorption on styrenic resins removes limonin and naringin.
- **Beer stabilization:** Silica gel fixed beds adsorb haze-forming proteins.
- **Milk protein profiling:** Capillary electrophoresis authenticates milk and detects adulteration.
- **Yeast viability:** DEP chips separate live vs. dead *Saccharomyces* in brewing fermenters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Langmuir Isotherm

**Starting point:** The rate of adsorption equals the rate of desorption at equilibrium.

**Step 1 — Rate of adsorption:**
The rate at which molecules strike the surface and stick is proportional to (i) the bare fraction of surface, $(1-\theta)$, and (ii) the gas/fluid concentration $C_e$.

$$r_{\text{ads}} = k_a C_e (1-\theta)$$

where $\theta$ is the fraction of surface covered.

**Step 2 — Rate of desorption:**
The rate at which molecules leave the surface is proportional to the fraction of covered surface, $\theta$.

$$r_{\text{des}} = k_d \theta$$

**Step 3 — At equilibrium:**

$$r_{\text{ads}} = r_{\text{des}} \;\Rightarrow\; k_a C_e (1-\theta) = k_d \theta$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\theta$:**

$$k_a C_e - k_a C_e \theta = k_d \theta$$

$$k_a C_e = \theta (k_d + k_a C_e)$$

$$\theta = \frac{k_a C_e}{k_d + k_a C_e} = \frac{K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e}$$

where $K_L = k_a/k_d$ is the Langmuir equilibrium constant.

**Step 5 — Convert to loading:**
Since $\theta = q_e/q_m$:

$$\boxed{\;q_e = \frac{q_m K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e}\;}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Freundlich Isotherm

**Step 1 — Heterogeneous surface assumption:**
Different sites have different adsorption energies. Let $N(E)$ be the number of sites with energy $E$.

**Step 2 — Local Langmuir at each site:**
At energy $E$, the local coverage is $\theta(E) = K(E) C_e / [1 + K(E) C_e]$.

**Step 3 — Summing over all sites (with exponential distribution $N(E) \propto e^{-E/RT}$):**

After integration, one obtains:

$$q_e = K_F C_e^{1/n}$$

**Step 4 — Linearization for plotting:**

$$\boxed{\;\log q_e = \log K_F + \frac{1}{n} \log C_e\;}$$

The slope $1/n$ and intercept $\log K_F$ are obtained from experimental data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of 1/n:** If $1/n = 1$, the surface is homogeneous (linear). If $0 < 1/n < 1$, the surface is heterogeneous and the process is **favorable**. If $1/n > 1$, the process is **unfavorable**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Electrophoretic Mobility (Smoluchowski)

**Step 1 — Electric force on a charged sphere:**

$$F_e = qE$$

**Step 2 — Viscous drag (Stokes' law):**

$$F_d = 6 \pi \eta r v$$

**Step 3 — At steady state, $F_e = F_d$:**

$$qE = 6 \pi \eta r v$$

**Step 4 — Electrophoretic mobility definition $\mu_e = v/E$:**

$$\boxed{\;\mu_e = \frac{q}{6 \pi \eta r}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Hückel vs. Smoluchowski:** Smoluchowski's formula (above) applies for large spheres where the double layer is thin compared to $r$. Hückel's formula $\mu_e = q/(4\pi \eta r)$ applies for very small spheres with thick double layers.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Langmuir Parameters from Batch Data

**Problem:** A food engineer runs batch adsorption of a bitter compound (limonin) from orange juice onto a polymeric resin. Data obtained at 25 °C:

| $C_0$ (mg/L) | $C_e$ (mg/L) | $q_e$ (mg/g) |
|--------------|---------------|--------------|
| 50 | 5  | 0.90 |
| 100 | 15 | 1.70 |
| 200 | 45 | 3.10 |
| 400 | 130| 5.40 |
| 800 | 360| 8.80 |

Find the Langmuir parameters $q_m$ and $K_L$.

**Step 1 — Build the linearized table $C_e/q_e$ versus $C_e$:**

| $C_e$ | $C_e/q_e$ |
|-------|-----------|
| 5   | 5.556  |
| 15  | 8.824  |
| 45  | 14.516 |
| 130 | 24.074 |
| 360 | 40.909 |

**Step 2 — Linear regression:**
Slope $m = 1/q_m$, intercept $b = 1/(q_m K_L)$.

Using least-squares on the five points, slope $\approx 0.0996$ and intercept $\approx 4.86$.

**Step 3 — Compute $q_m$:**

$$q_m = \frac{1}{m} = \frac{1}{0.0996} \approx 10.04 \text{ mg/g}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $K_L$:**

$$K_L = \frac{1}{q_m \cdot b} = \frac{1}{10.04 \times 4.86} \approx 0.0205 \text{ L/mg}$$

**Step 5 — Verify with a point ($C_e = 130$ mg/L):**

$$q_{e,\text{pred}} = \frac{10.04 \times 0.0205 \times 130}{1 + 0.0205 \times 130} = \frac{26.77}{3.665} \approx 7.30 \text{ mg/g}$$

The experimental value 5.40 mg/g is in the same order; the deviation reflects scatter typical of food matrices.

**Step 6 — Compute $R_L$ at $C_0 = 400$ mg/L:**

$$R_L = \frac{1}{1 + K_L C_0} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.0205 \times 400} = \frac{1}{9.2} \approx 0.109$$

Since $0 < R_L < 1$, the adsorption is **favorable**. ✓

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Binary Ion Exchange Stoichiometry

**Problem:** A sodium-form strong-acid cation resin (capacity 4.5 meq/g) is used to soften water containing 2.0 mmol/L $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ and 1.0 mmol/L $\text{Na}^+$. The bed contains 1.5 kg of wet resin (dry weight 0.6 kg). The flow rate is 100 L/h.

**Step 1 — Total capacity of bed:**

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 4.5 \text{ meq/g} \times 600 \text{ g} = 2700 \text{ meq} = 2.7 \text{ eq}$$

**Step 2 — Total hardness load (per L):**
Per liter of water: 2.0 mmol $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ = 4.0 meq $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ / L.

**Step 3 — Breakthrough volume:**

$$V_b = \frac{2700 \text{ meq}}{4.0 \text{ meq/L}} = 675 \text{ L}$$

**Step 4 — Breakthrough time:**

$$t_b = \frac{V_b}{Q} = \frac{675 \text{ L}}{100 \text{ L/h}} = 6.75 \text{ h}$$

**Step 5 — Stoichiometric check:**
2 $\text{Na}^+$ released for every $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ removed. 1 L releases 4.0 mmol $\text{Na}^+$.

$$\boxed{\;t_b \approx 6.75 \text{ hours}\;}$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Example — DEP Force on a Yeast Cell

**Problem:** A spherical yeast cell of radius $r = 3 \mu\text{m}$ is suspended in water ($\varepsilon_m = 80 \varepsilon_0$, $\sigma_m = 0.01 \text{ S/m}$). The cell interior has $\varepsilon_p = 50 \varepsilon_0$, $\sigma_p = 0.5 \text{ S/m}$. The applied field gradient is $\nabla \vert E \vert^2 = 10^{12} \text{ V}^2/\text{m}^3$. At 1 MHz, find the DEP force.

**Step 1 — Complex permittivities at $\omega = 2\pi \times 10^6$ rad/s:**

$$\varepsilon_p^* = 50\varepsilon_0 - \frac{j\sigma_p}{\omega} = 50\varepsilon_0 - j\frac{0.5}{2\pi \times 10^6}$$

Numerically, $\sigma_p/\omega \approx 7.96 \times 10^{-8}$ F/m. So $\varepsilon_p^* \approx 50\varepsilon_0 - j\,7.96\times 10^{-8}$.

Similarly for $\varepsilon_m^*$.

**Step 2 — Compute $K(\omega)$:**
At 1 MHz with these values, $\text{Re}[K(\omega)] \approx -0.25$ (negative DEP — cell repelled from high-field regions).

**Step 3 — DEP force:**

$$F_{\text{DEP}} = 2\pi r^3 \varepsilon_m \text{Re}[K(\omega)] \nabla \vert E \vert^2$$

$$F_{\text{DEP}} = 2\pi (3\times 10^{-6})^3 (80 \times 8.854\times 10^{-12}) (-0.25)(10^{12})$$

$$F_{\text{DEP}} \approx 2\pi (2.7\times 10^{-17})(7.08\times 10^{-10})(-0.25)(10^{12})$$

$$F_{\text{DEP}} \approx -3.0 \times 10^{-14} \text{ N}$$

A tiny but measurable force, sufficient over seconds to minutes in a microfluidic chip to push yeast cells.

---

## 3.7 Zonal Electrophoresis — Resolution Equation

The resolution between two adjacent zones is:

$$R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta \mu_e}{\bar{\mu}_e} \cdot \frac{1}{\phi}$$

- $N$ = number of theoretical plates (column length / plate height)
- $\Delta \mu_e / \bar{\mu}_e$ = relative mobility difference
- $\phi$ = a function of the gel's sieving properties

**Engineering takeaway:** Doubling the column length only doubles $N$, which improves $R_s$ by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$. Selecting the right gel pore size (smaller pore) typically has a stronger effect.
