# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]
**Define fixed-bed adsorption and explain the term "breakthrough curve."**

**Model Answer:**
Fixed-bed adsorption is a separation process in which a fluid is continuously passed through a stationary bed of solid adsorbent. As the adsorbent becomes saturated with the solute, a mass transfer zone (MTZ) travels down the column. The breakthrough curve is a plot of the **outlet solute concentration** ($C_{\text{out}}$) versus **time** (or throughput volume). It typically shows:
- A long plateau at $C_{\text{out}} \approx 0$ (fresh bed),
- A steep rise as the MTZ reaches the column exit (breakthrough),
- An asymptote at $C_{\text{out}} \to C_{\text{in}}$ (exhaustion).

The breakthrough point is conventionally taken at $C_{\text{out}} = 0.05\,C_{\text{in}}$ (5 % breakthrough). *(3 marks)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]
**State the Langmuir adsorption isotherm and write its linear form. What does the constant $q_m$ represent?**

**Model Answer:**
The Langmuir isotherm is:

$$q_e = \frac{q_m K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e}$$

**Linear form:**

$$\frac{C_e}{q_e} = \frac{1}{q_m K_L} + \frac{C_e}{q_m}$$

A plot of $C_e/q_e$ versus $C_e$ gives a straight line of slope $1/q_m$ and intercept $1/(q_m K_L)$.

- $q_m$ is the **maximum monolayer adsorption capacity** of the adsorbent (mg of solute per g of adsorbent) — it represents the total number of available adsorption sites.
- $K_L$ is the **Langmuir affinity constant** related to the binding energy. *(3 marks)*

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), 7 marks each. **Internal choice** is provided between two full question sets (Q-A or Q-B).

---

### 🔹 Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]

**Q-A. (a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a fixed-bed adsorption column. Discuss the significance of the mass transfer zone (MTZ) and the breakthrough curve in industrial food processing. *(7 marks)*

**Q-A. (b)** The following data were obtained for the adsorption of a food colour (caramel) on activated carbon at 30 °C. Fit the data to the Langmuir isotherm and determine $q_m$ and $K_L$. Also calculate the dimensionless separation factor $R_L$ at the highest initial concentration. *(7 marks)*

| $C_0$ (mg/L) | $C_e$ (mg/L) |
|---------------|--------------|
| 25  | 2  |
| 50  | 6  |
| 100 | 18 |
| 200 | 55 |
| 400 | 170|

**Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

1. **Construction:** A vertical cylindrical column packed with adsorbent (e.g., activated carbon) supported on a perforated plate or screen. The fluid enters from the top, percolates down through the bed, and exits through a bottom outlet. *(2 marks)*
2. **Working:** As the feed flows in, solutes are adsorbed at the top of the bed, forming a saturated zone. The MTZ moves downward. When the MTZ reaches the column exit, breakthrough occurs. *(2 marks)*
3. **MTZ significance:** Short MTZ = efficient bed utilization; long MTZ = wasted capacity. Industrial columns are designed to keep MTZ short. *(1 mark)*
4. **Breakthrough curve:** Defines operating cycles; breakthrough time $t_b$ determines throughput; exhaustion time $t_e$ determines regeneration schedule. Food examples: sugar decolorization, juice debittering. *(2 marks)*

**Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $q_e$ (assume 1 L solution + 1 g adsorbent):**

$$q_e = (C_0 - C_e) \text{ mg/g}$$

| $C_0$ | $C_e$ | $q_e$ | $C_e/q_e$ |
|-------|-------|-------|-----------|
| 25  | 2  | 23   | 0.087  |
| 50  | 6  | 44   | 0.136  |
| 100 | 18 | 82   | 0.220  |
| 200 | 55 | 145  | 0.379  |
| 400 | 170| 230  | 0.739  |

**Step 2 — Linear regression on $C_e/q_e$ vs $C_e$:**

Using least-squares on 5 points:

- Slope $m \approx 0.00168$ → $q_m = 1/m \approx 595 \text{ mg/g}$
- Intercept $b \approx 0.052$ → $K_L = 1/(q_m \cdot b) = 1/(595 \times 0.052) \approx 0.0323 \text{ L/mg}$

**Step 3 — Compute $R_L$ at $C_0 = 400$ mg/L:**

$$R_L = \frac{1}{1 + K_L C_0} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.0323 \times 400} = \frac{1}{13.92} \approx 0.072$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** Since $0 < R_L < 1$, the adsorption is **favorable** and the carbon has a very high affinity for caramel colour. ✓ *(7 marks: 2 for $q_e$ table, 2 for regression, 2 for $R_L$, 1 for conclusion)*

---

### 🔹 Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]

**Q-B. (a)** Explain the construction, properties, and classification of ion exchange resins. Discuss the factors affecting the ion exchange process in food applications. *(7 marks)*

**Q-B. (b)** A strong-acid cation resin in $\text{Na}^+$ form (capacity 4.5 meq/g, dry mass 0.5 kg) is used to treat 1000 L of water containing 1.5 mmol/L $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ and 1.0 mmol/L $\text{Mg}^{2+}$. Calculate the breakthrough time at a flow rate of 80 L/h. If the resin has 50 % excess capacity, what is the actual operating time before exhaustion? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

1. **Construction:** Cross-linked polystyrene–divinylbenzene matrix. Functional groups are $-\text{SO}_3^H$ (SAC), $-\text{COOH}$ (WAC), $-\text{N}(\text{CH}_3)_3^+\text{OH}^-$ (SBA), $-\text{NR}_2$ (WBA). *(2 marks)*
2. **Properties:** (i) total capacity, (ii) cross-link density, (iii) particle size, (iv) moisture content, (v) thermal stability, (vi) swelling. *(2 marks)*
3. **Classification:** By type (cation/anion) and by strength (strong/weak). *(1 mark)*
4. **Controlling factors in food applications:** (i) selectivity for target ion, (ii) pH, (iii) ionic strength of feed, (iv) flow rate, (v) temperature, (vi) presence of competing ions (e.g., $\text{Na}^+$ vs $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ in milk). Examples: demineralization of whey, removal of acidity from fruit juices, water softening in breweries. *(2 marks)*

**Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Step 1 — Total resin capacity:**

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 4.5 \text{ meq/g} \times 500 \text{ g} = 2250 \text{ meq} = 2.25 \text{ eq}$$

**Step 2 — Total hardness load per liter:**
Per liter of feed:

- $\text{Ca}^{2+}$: 1.5 mmol/L × 2 eq/mol = 3.0 meq/L
- $\text{Mg}^{2+}$: 1.0 mmol/L × 2 eq/mol = 2.0 meq/L
- **Total hardness = 5.0 meq/L**

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric throughput volume:**

$$V_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{2250 \text{ meq}}{5.0 \text{ meq/L}} = 450 \text{ L}$$

**Step 4 — Stoichiometric breakthrough time:**

$$t_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{450 \text{ L}}{80 \text{ L/h}} = 5.625 \text{ h}$$

**Step 5 — Actual operating time with 50 % excess capacity:**

$$t_{\text{actual}} = t_{\text{stoich}} \times (1 + 0.50) = 5.625 \times 1.5 = 8.44 \text{ h}$$

$$\boxed{\;t_{\text{actual}} \approx 8.4 \text{ hours}\;}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for total capacity, 2 for hardness load, 1 for throughput, 1 for stoichiometric time, 1 for excess capacity correction)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign convention confusion:** In ion exchange, never forget that divalent ions release **two** monovalent ions. Writing $\text{Ca}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{Ca}^{2+}$ without tracking charge balance costs full marks.
> 2. **Units of $q_e$:** Always report $q_e$ in mg/g (or mol/g). Mixing units within one calculation (e.g., mg/g with mmol/L) leads to arithmetic errors.
> 3. **Langmuir plot axis confusion:** Many students plot $1/C_e$ versus $1/q_e$. The KTU-approved linear form is $C_e/q_e$ versus $C_e$ with slope $1/q_m$ (not $1/q_m K_L$).
> 4. **DEP force sign:** Forgetting that DEP force depends on $\nabla \vert E \vert^2$ (not $\nabla E$) is a frequent mistake. The gradient is of the **square** of the field magnitude.
> 5. **Zonal vs. moving-boundary electrophoresis:** In the exam, never confuse zonal electrophoresis (where the sample is applied as a band) with isotachophoresis (where the sample moves sandwiched between two leading/terminating electrolytes).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Fixed-bed adsorption:** MTZ moves down; breakthrough at 5 %; exhaustion at 95 %.
- ✅ **Langmuir isotherm:** monolayer, homogeneous sites, finite capacity $q_m$.
- ✅ **Freundlich isotherm:** empirical, heterogeneous; $0 < 1/n < 1$ for favorable adsorption.
- ✅ **BET isotherm:** multilayer; used to estimate surface area.
- ✅ **Separation factor $R_L$:** $0<R_L<1$ → favorable; report this in batch-experiment conclusions.
- ✅ **Ion exchange resins:** SAC (sulphonate), WAC (carboxylate), SBA (quaternary amine), WBA (tertiary amine).
- ✅ **Selectivity order (SAC):** monovalent < divalent < trivalent. Higher valence is preferred.
- ✅ **Helfferich's ion movement theory:** faster ion leads, slower follows; sharp interchange boundary.
- ✅ **Controlling factors:** selectivity, cross-link, particle size, pH, $T$, flow rate, ionic strength.
- ✅ **Electrophoresis:** $v = \mu_e E$; mobility depends on charge, size, viscosity.
- ✅ **Dielectrophoresis (DEP):** works on **neutral** particles too; force $\propto \nabla \vert E \vert^2$.
- ✅ **Clausius-Mossotti factor $K(\omega)$:** sign determines +DEP or −DEP; frequency-dependent.
- ✅ **Zonal electrophoresis:** sample applied as a band; separates into zones by mobility (and size, in gels).
- ✅ **Food applications:** sugar decolorization, whey demineralization, juice debittering, beer stabilization, milk protein profiling, yeast cell sorting.
- ✅ **Breakthrough volume $V_b = Q_{\text{resin}} / C_{\text{fluid}}$** in equivalent units; always check charge balance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Other major techniques: freeze crystallization, pervaporation and permeation techniques for solids, liquids and gases
topics.content:
# Module 4 — Other Major Separation Techniques ## 1.1 Freeze Crystallization (Freeze Concentration) ### Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) **Freeze crystallization** (also termed *freeze concentration* or *progressive freeze concentration*) is a thermal separation process in which a liquid mixture is partially frozen so that the solvent (typically water) is converted into pure ice crystals, thereby concentrating the dissolved solutes (sugars, salts, acids, aroma compounds) in the remaining liquid phase. It is classified as a **phase-change separation** because the separation is achieved by selectively crystallizing one component without reaching the boiling point, making it ideal for **thermolabile food products** such as fruit juices, coffee extracts, milk, and beer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Freeze crystallization is the only commercially used separation technique that operates at temperatures *below* 0 °C while producing a high-purity solvent (ice) phase. It preserves volatile aroma compounds and heat-sensitive nutrients far better than evaporation. ### Conceptual Analogy Think of making **ice cubes from a fruit juice**. When you put orange juice in a freezer, the water in the juice begins to freeze first, leaving behind a thicker, sweeter syrup. If you could carefully remove the ice crystals as they form, you would be left with a highly concentrated, flavor-rich juice — exactly what freeze crystallization does, only in a precisely controlled industrial crystallizer. > [!NOTE] > **Key Idea:** The *freezing point depression* caused by dissolved solutes means ice forms *before* the solutes solidify, naturally yielding a pure solvent phase. --- ## 1.2 Pervaporation (PV) ### Formal Definition **Pervaporation** is a membrane-based separation process in which a liquid feed mixture is contacted with one side of a **non-porous (dense) or microporous membrane**, while the permeate side is maintained under vacuum (or swept by a carrier gas). Components of the feed *selectively* dissolve into the membrane, diffuse through it, and evaporate (desorb) on the downstream side as vapor. Separation is governed by **differential sorption and diffusion** rather than by vapor–liquid equilibrium. ### Conceptual Analogy Imagine placing a thin plastic wrap loosely over a cup of hot tea. Steam (water vapor) escapes through the wrap, but the tea compounds (tannins, theaflavins) move much more slowly. Pervaporation works in a similar way: the membrane acts as a *molecular sieve* that prefers one component to pass through — a process often called **"permeation + evaporation"** = **pervaporation**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Pervaporation is the only membrane process involving a **phase change** (liquid → vapor). It is widely used for **dehydration of ethanol** (breaking the azeotrope) and **aroma recovery** from food streams. --- ## 1.3 Permeation Techniques (Solids, Liquids, and Gases) ### Formal Definition **Permeation** refers to the movement of a species (atoms, molecules, ions) through a *medium* (membrane, solid barrier, or porous matrix) under the influence of a **chemical potential gradient** — concentration, partial pressure, or activity gradient. In food engineering, permeation techniques are categorized as: | **Type** | **Driver** | **Typical Membrane** | **Application** | |---|---|---|---| | Gas Permeation | Partial pressure gradient | Dense polymer (e.g., PDMS) | O₂/N₂ separation, CO₂ removal | | Vapor Permeation | Vapor partial pressure | Hydrophobic/hydrophilic polymer | Solvent dehydration | | Liquid Permeation (Dialysis) | Concentration gradient | Porous membrane | Salt/sugar removal | | Solid Permeation (Leaching) | Concentration gradient | Cell wall/matrix | Sugar extraction from beet | ### Conceptual Analogy Permeation is like a **sponge filter**: a solution sits on one side, and depending on the sponge's pore size and chemistry, certain molecules squeeze through while others are held back. The *driving force* is the difference in "thirst" (concentration) between the two sides. > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition — Permeability Coefficient (P):** The standard metric governing all permeation processes is the **permeability coefficient**, with units typically expressed in **Barrer** (1 Barrer = 3.348 × 10⁻¹⁶ mol·m/m²·s·Pa) or in **cm³ (STP)·cm/cm²·s·cmHg**. # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Freeze Crystallization — Theoretical Framework ### Operational Sequence (Stepwise Logic) 1. **Cooling of feed:** The aqueous feed is cooled below its initial freezing point using a scraped-surface heat exchanger (SSHE) or cooling crystallizer. 2. **Nucleation:** Once supercooling exceeds the *critical radius*, ice nuclei spontaneously form. Heterogeneous nucleation is favored by seeding. 3. **Crystal growth:** Ice crystals grow on the nuclei as water molecules are incorporated into the lattice. Solutes are *rejected* from the crystal face (constitutional supercooling). 4. **Separation:** The ice slurry is sent to a *wash column* or *filter*, where pure ice is separated from the concentrated mother liquor. 5. **Product recovery:** The concentrated liquid is the desired product (e.g., concentrated juice). ### The Underlying Physics The thermodynamic basis is **freezing-point depression** (Raoult's-law analog for solid–liquid equilibria): $$\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$$ where: - $\Delta T_f$ = freezing point depression (°C) - $K_f$ = cryoscopic constant of water = **1.86 °C·kg/mol** - $m$ = molality of solute (mol/kg) The mass of ice formed during equilibrium freeze concentration is governed by an **ice–liquid mass balance**: $$M_{feed} = M_{ice} + M_{liquid}$$ $$x_{feed} \cdot M_{feed} = x_{liquid} \cdot M_{liquid}$$ Combining: $$M_{ice} = M_{feed} \left(1 - \frac{x_{feed}}{x_{liquid}}\right)$$ where $x$ denotes the solute mass fraction. ### The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why partial freezing?** Because only the solvent (water) freezes first; the solute-rich phase remains liquid due to freezing-point depression. - **Why a wash column?** Ice crystals always carry an adhering liquid film containing the solute; gentle counter-current washing with pure water removes this film and restores crystal purity. - **Why slow growth?** Fast growth traps solute inclusions inside the ice, reducing purity. --- ## 2.2 Pervaporation — Theoretical Framework ### Operational Sequence 1. **Feed heating:** Liquid feed is heated (typically 40–80 °C) to enhance vapor pressure of permeants. 2. **Membrane contact:** Feed flows across the upstream face of a dense (or composite) membrane. 3. **Selective sorption:** Component *i* dissolves into the membrane at the upstream face (governed by sorption coefficient $S_i$). 4. **Diffusion through membrane:** Driven by activity gradient, with diffusivity $D_i(x)$. 5. **Desorption as vapor:** Component *i* evaporates into the permeate side (kept at low pressure, typically 1–10 mbar). 6. **Condensation:** Permeate vapor is condensed and collected; non-condensables are removed by vacuum pump. ### The Solution–Diffusion Model The flux of component *i* through a pervaporation membrane is: $$J_i = \frac{P_i}{l}(x_i \gamma_i p_i^{sat} - y_i p_p)$$ where: - $J_i$ = steady-state flux of component *i* (kg/m²·h) - $P_i$ = permeability of *i* in the membrane (kg·m/m²·h·Pa) - $l$ = membrane thickness (m) - $x_i$ = mole fraction of *i* in feed liquid - $\gamma_i$ = activity coefficient of *i* in feed - $p_i^{sat}$ = saturated vapor pressure of *i* at feed temperature (Pa) - $y_i$ = mole fraction of *i* in permeate vapor - $p_p$ = permeate-side total pressure (Pa) ### Selectivity (Separation Factor) The membrane's ability to discriminate between two components (e.g., water and ethanol) is quantified by: $$\alpha_{i/j} = \frac{(y_i / y_j)}{(x_i / x_j)}$$ For the ethanol/water system using a hydrophilic membrane (e.g., NaA zeolite), water is preferentially permeated, and $\alpha_{water/ethanol}$ can exceed **10,000**. ### Why Each Step Matters - **Why heat the feed?** Higher temperature → higher vapor pressure of permeant → larger driving force → higher flux. - **Why vacuum on permeate side?** Maintains the chemical-potential gradient across the membrane. - **Why dense membrane?** Prevents convective flow, ensuring separation is governed by selective sorption–diffusion. --- ## 2.3 Permeation Techniques — Theoretical Framework ### Fick's First Law (Foundation of All Permeation) $$J = -D \frac{dC}{dx}$$ For steady-state permeation through a membrane of thickness *l*: $$J = \frac{D (C_1 - C_2)}{l}$$ ### Permeability (P) and the Solution–Diffusion Mechanism For dense membranes, permeability decomposes into two physical factors: $$P = D \cdot S$$ - $D$ = diffusion coefficient (m²/s) — kinetic factor - $S$ = sorption coefficient (mol/m³·Pa) — thermodynamic factor This is the famous **solution–diffusion mechanism** that unifies gas permeation, vapor permeation, pervaporation, and reverse osmosis. ### Gas Permeation For binary gas mixture (A, B), selectivity is: $$\alpha_{A/B} = \frac{P_A}{P_B} = \frac{D_A S_A}{D_B S_B}$$ For **O₂/N₂** in polysulfone: $P_{O_2} \approx 1.1$ Barrer, $P_{N_2} \approx 0.18$ Barrer → $\alpha_{O_2/N_2} \approx 6.1$. ### Solid Permeation (Leaching) Leaching of sugar from sugar beet follows the **diffusion-limited model**: $$\frac{C(t) - C_\infty}{C_0 - C_\infty} = \frac{6}{\pi^2} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^2} \exp\left(-\frac{D_{eff} n^2 \pi^2 t}{r^2}\right)$$ This is the **Crank equation** for diffusion from a sphere (typical beet geometry). --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Technique** | **Key Equation** | **Variables & Units** | **Typical Range** | |---|---|---|---| | Freeze Crystallization | $\Delta T_f = K_f m$ | $K_f$ = 1.86 °C·kg/mol; $m$ in mol/kg | 0.5 – 5 °C depression | | Freeze Crystallization | $M_{ice} = M_{feed}(1 - x_{feed}/x_{liquid})$ | Mass fractions dimensionless | — | | Pervaporation Flux | $J_i = (P_i/l)(x_i \gamma_i p_i^{sat} - y_i p_p)$ | $P_i$ in kg·m/m²·h·Pa; $l$ in m | $10^{-2}$ to 1 kg/m²·h | | Pervaporation Selectivity | $\alpha_{i/j} = (y_i/y_j) / (x_i/x_j)$ | Dimensionless | 5 – 10,000 | | Fick's Law | $J = -D\, dC/dx$ | $D$ in m²/s; $C$ in mol/m³ | — | | Permeability | $P = D \cdot S$ | $P$ in Barrer; $D$ in m²/s; $S$ in mol/m³·Pa | 0.1 – 1000 Barrer | | Gas Selectivity | $\alpha_{A/B} = P_A / P_B$ | Dimensionless | 2 – 100 | | Crank (sphere leaching) | $\sum (1/n^2)\exp(-D_{eff} n^2 \pi^2 t/r^2)$ | $D_{eff}$ in m²/s; $r$ = particle radius | $D_{eff} = 10^{-10}$ to $10^{-9}$ m²/s | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Tip:** Always quote the **units** of permeability in Barrer in KTU exams — it shows command over the unit system and earns full marks. ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **Freeze crystallization** is used commercially by **Apple & Eve, Inc.** and **Niro Process** for juice concentration up to 50 °Brix without flavor loss. - **Pervaporation** is used in **bioethanol dehydration** to break the 95.6% ethanol–water azeotrope, achieving 99.5%+ fuel-grade ethanol. - **Membrane gas permeation** is the workhorse of **modified atmosphere packaging (MAP)** and **CO₂ removal from natural gas**. - **Solid permeation (leaching)** is the foundational operation in **sugar beet processing** and **tea extraction**. # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Ice Mass in Freeze Concentration (Mass Balance) **Starting Point:** We are given a feed of mass $M_{feed}$ with solute mass fraction $x_{feed}$. We want to find the mass of pure ice that must form to yield a liquid concentrate of mass fraction $x_{liquid}$. **Step 1 — Total Mass Balance (Conservation of Mass):** The total mass entering the crystallizer equals the total mass leaving as ice plus liquid concentrate. $$M_{feed} = M_{ice} + M_{liquid}$$ **Step 2 — Solute Mass Balance (Solute Appears Only in the Liquid Phase):** Solute is rejected by the ice (ice is pure solvent), so all solute reports to the liquid concentrate. $$x_{feed} \cdot M_{feed} = x_{liquid} \cdot M_{liquid}$$ **Step 3 — Solve the Solute Balance for $M_{liquid}$:** $$M_{liquid} = \frac{x_{feed} \cdot M_{feed}}{x_{liquid}}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute into the Total Mass Balance:** $$M_{feed} = M_{ice} + \frac{x_{feed} \cdot M_{feed}}{x_{liquid}}$$ **Step 5 — Isolate $M_{ice}$:** $$M_{ice} = M_{feed} - \frac{x_{feed} \cdot M_{feed}}{x_{liquid}}$$ **Step 6 — Factor out $M_{feed}$:** $$M_{ice} = M_{feed}\left(1 - \frac{x_{feed}}{x_{liquid}}\right)$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{M_{ice} = M_{feed}\left(1 - \frac{x_{feed}}{x_{liquid}}\right)}$$ **Verification of Logic:** If $x_{feed} = x_{liquid}$ (no concentration), then $M_{ice} = 0$ — correct, because no water needs to be removed. If $x_{liquid} \to 1$ (pure solute), then $M_{ice} \to M_{feed}(1-0) = M_{feed}$ — correct, all water is removed as ice. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Pervaporation Flux via the Solution–Diffusion Model **Starting Point:** Consider a homogeneous dense membrane of thickness *l*. Component *i* on the upstream face (liquid feed) has activity $a_i^{(1)}$; on the downstream face (vapor) it has activity $a_i^{(2)}$. **Step 1 — Chemical Potential at the Upstream Face:** The driving force for sorption is the activity of *i* in the liquid feed, $a_i^{(1)} = x_i \gamma_i$. **Step 2 — Sorption Equilibrium at the Upstream Face:** The concentration of *i* dissolved in the membrane at face 1 is given by Henry's-law-like sorption: $$C_i^{(1)} = S_i \cdot p_i^{sat} \cdot a_i^{(1)} = S_i \cdot p_i^{sat} \cdot x_i \gamma_i$$ **Step 3 — Sorption Equilibrium at the Downstream Face:** The concentration of *i* dissolved in the membrane at face 2 is in equilibrium with the vapor-phase partial pressure $p_i^{(2)} = y_i p_p$: $$C_i^{(2)} = S_i \cdot p_i^{(2)} = S_i \cdot y_i p_p$$ **Step 4 — Apply Fick's First Law (Steady State):** $$J_i = \frac{D_i (C_i^{(1)} - C_i^{(2)})}{l}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute the Concentrations:** $$J_i = \frac{D_i S_i}{l}\left(p_i^{sat} x_i \gamma_i - y_i p_p\right)$$ **Step 6 — Recognize that $P_i = D_i S_i$:** $$J_i = \frac{P_i}{l}\left(x_i \gamma_i p_i^{sat} - y_i p_p\right)$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{J_i = \frac{P_i}{l}\left(x_i \gamma_i p_i^{sat} - y_i p_p\right)}$$ **Verification of Logic:** When $p_p \to 0$ (perfect vacuum), $J_i = (P_i/l) \cdot x_i \gamma_i p_i^{sat}$ — maximum flux. When $y_i p_p \to x_i \gamma_i p_i^{sat}$, the driving force vanishes, and $J_i = 0$ — equilibrium. Both limits are physically correct. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Permeability Units and the Barrer **Starting Point:** Permeability is defined as flux per unit driving force per unit membrane thickness. **Step 1 — Definition:** $$P = \frac{J \cdot l}{\Delta p}$$ **Step 2 — SI Unit Analysis:** - $J$ in mol·m/m²·s (or equivalently cm³ (STP)·cm/cm²·s) - $l$ in m (or cm) - $\Delta p$ in Pa (or cmHg) **Step 3 — The Barrer Unit:** 1 Barrer is defined as: $$1 \text{ Barrer} = \frac{10^{-10} \text{ cm}^3(\text{STP}) \cdot \text{cm}}{\text{cm}^2 \cdot \text{s} \cdot \text{cmHg}}$$ **Step 4 — Conversion to SI:** $$1 \text{ Barrer} = 3.348 \times 10^{-16} \frac{\text{mol} \cdot \text{m}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s} \cdot \text{Pa}}$$ **Final Result (Conversion Identity):** $$\boxed{1 \text{ Barrer} = 3.348 \times 10^{-16} \text{ mol·m/(m}^2\text{·s·Pa)}}$$ --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Freeze Concentration of Orange Juice **Problem:** A feed of orange juice ($M_{feed} = 100$ kg) contains 12% soluble solids by mass ($x_{feed} = 0.12$). The freeze crystallizer is operated to produce a concentrate of 35% solids ($x_{liquid} = 0.35$). Calculate: (a) Mass of ice formed (b) Mass of concentrate produced (c) Mass fraction of water removed **Solution:** **(a) Mass of ice formed:** $$M_{ice} = M_{feed}\left(1 - \frac{x_{feed}}{x_{liquid}}\right)$$ $$M_{ice} = 100 \left(1 - \frac{0.12}{0.35}\right)$$ $$M_{ice} = 100 \left(1 - 0.3429\right)$$ $$M_{ice} = 100 \times 0.6571$$ $$M_{ice} = 65.71 \text{ kg}$$ **(b) Mass of concentrate:** $$M_{liquid} = M_{feed} - M_{ice} = 100 - 65.71 = 34.29 \text{ kg}$$ **Check via solute balance:** $M_{liquid} = (0.12 \times 100) / 0.35 = 34.29$ kg ✓ **(c) Mass fraction of water removed:** Initial water = $100 - 12 = 88$ kg Final water (in concentrate) = $34.29 - (0.35 \times 34.29) = 34.29 \times 0.65 = 22.29$ kg Water removed as ice = $88 - 22.29 = 65.71$ kg $$\text{Fraction water removed} = \frac{65.71}{88} = 0.7467 = 74.67\%$$ **Final Answer:** $M_{ice} = 65.71$ kg, $M_{liquid} = 34.29$ kg, and **74.67%** of the feed water is removed as pure ice. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix | **Parameter** | **Freeze Crystallization** | **Pervaporation** | **Permeation (Generic)** | |---|---|---|---| | Driving Force | Temperature gradient (ΔT) | Vapor pressure gradient (Δp) | Concentration / pressure gradient | | Phase Change | Liquid → Solid | Liquid → Vapor | None (often) | | Energy Intensity | **Low** (latent heat of fusion = 334 kJ/kg) | Moderate (latent heat of vaporization + sensible heat) | Low (no phase change) | | Selectivity Source | Freezing point difference | Sorption + diffusion difference | Size exclusion or solubility | | Typical Product Purity | High (volatile retention) | Very high (breaks azeotropes) | High for gases; moderate for liquids | | Operating T (°C) | −5 to −20 | 40 to 80 | 20 to 60 | | Membrane Required? | No | **Yes** | **Yes** | | KTU Mark Weight | High | High | High | # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Freeze Crystallization Process Flow (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Feed Tank
Liquid Food Stream] --> B[Scraped Surface
Heat Exchanger
SSHE Cooler] B --> C[Suspension Crystallizer
Ice Nucleation and Growth] C --> D[Ice Liquid Slurry] D --> E[Wash Column
Counter current Water Rinse] E --> F[Pure Ice Crystals
Reject or Melt] E --> G[Concentrated Product
High Brix Liquid] G --> H[Storage Tank
Finished Concentrate] B -.-> I[Refrigeration Loop
Ammonia or Freon] I -.-> B ``` ## 4.2 Pervaporation Process Flow (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Liquid Feed
e.g. Ethanol Water] --> B[Feed Heater
40 to 80 C] B --> C[Membrane Module
Dense or Composite] C --> D[Permeate Side
Vacuum 1 to 10 mbar] D --> E[Condenser
Cold Trap at minus 20 C] E --> F[Permeate Product
e.g. Pure Water] C --> G[Retentate
Concentrated Feed] G --> H[Recycle or Final Product] D -.-> I[Vacuum Pump] I -.-> J[Non condensable Vent] ``` ## 4.3 Permeation Mechanism Block Architecture (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Upstream Face
High Activity a1] --> B[Membrane Matrix
Solution Diffusion Zone] B --> C[Downstream Face
Low Activity a2] C --> D[Permeate Collection] subgraph Solution_Diffusion E[Sorption
a1 to C1] --> F[Diffusion
Ficks Law] F --> G[Desorption
C2 to a2] end B -.-> Solution_Diffusion ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Block Architecture of Three Techniques (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FC[Freeze Crystallization] FC1[Cooling Stage] --> FC2[Ice Crystal Growth] FC2 --> FC3[Wash and Separation] end subgraph PV[Pervaporation] PV1[Heated Liquid Feed] --> PV2[Membrane Contact] PV2 --> PV3[Vacuum Desorption] PV3 --> PV4[Vapor Condensation] end subgraph PM[Permeation] PM1[High Pressure Side] --> PM2[Membrane Barrier] PM2 --> PM3[Low Pressure Side] end A[Feed Stream] --> FC A --> PV A --> PM FC --> X[Concentrate Plus Ice] PV --> Y[Permeate Vapor and Retentate] PM --> Z[Permeate and Retentate] ``` > [!NOTE] > All diagrams are drawn as block-level functional architecture flows to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs, no markdown formatting inside labels, no reserved keywords). # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Differentiate between **freeze crystallization** and **evaporative concentration** as methods for concentrating fruit juices. Mention one unique advantage of freeze crystallization. **Model Answer:** | **Aspect** | **Freeze Crystallization** | **Evaporative Concentration** | |---|---|---| | Operating principle | Removes water as **ice** | Removes water as **vapor** | | Operating temperature | **Below 0 °C** (–5 to –20 °C) | **Above 60 °C** typically | | Energy requirement | Lower (latent heat of fusion = 334 kJ/kg) | Higher (latent heat of vaporization ≈ 2260 kJ/kg) | | Effect on aroma/volatile compounds | **Preserved** | Partially lost with vapor | | Phase change | Liquid → Solid | Liquid → Vapor | **Unique Advantage:** Freeze crystallization retains volatile aroma compounds, vitamins, and heat-sensitive nutrients that would otherwise be lost during evaporation, yielding a **higher-quality, fresher-tasting concentrate**. (3 marks) --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** State the **solution–diffusion model** for membrane permeation and write the expression for permeability. **Model Answer:** The **solution–diffusion model** describes transport of a species through a dense membrane as a three-step process: 1. **Sorption** of the penetrant into the upstream face of the membrane. 2. **Diffusion** of the penetrant through the membrane under a chemical potential gradient. 3. **Desorption** of the penetrant from the downstream face. The permeability $P$ is the product of diffusivity $D$ and sorption coefficient $S$: $$P = D \cdot S$$ Units: Barrer, where 1 Barrer = 3.348 × 10⁻¹⁶ mol·m/(m²·s·Pa). (3 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]** (a) Explain the **principle and working of pervaporation** with a neat flow diagram. Discuss its application in the **dehydration of bioethanol** from the ethanol–water azeotrope. (7 marks) (b) Derive the **solution–diffusion flux equation** for a single component pervaporating through a dense membrane. (7 marks) #### Model Solution **(a) Principle, Working & Application (7 marks):** **Principle (2 marks):** Pervaporation combines **permeation** (selective transport through a membrane) with **evaporation** (vaporization on the permeate side). A liquid feed is contacted with a membrane; one or more components preferentially dissolve into and diffuse through the membrane, then evaporate as vapor on the downstream (vacuum) side. **Working (3 marks):** The feed (e.g., 95 wt% ethanol) is heated to 60–80 °C and circulated across the upstream face of a **hydrophilic membrane** (e.g., NaA zeolite, PVA, or chitosan). Water preferentially sorbs into the membrane due to its strong interaction with the hydrophilic sites. It diffuses through the membrane thickness, evaporates into the permeate side (held at 1–10 mbar by a vacuum pump), and is condensed in a cold trap. The retentate exits as anhydrous ethanol (>99.5 wt%). **Process Flow:** Feed → Heater → Membrane Module → Condenser → Permeate (water) + Retentate (dry ethanol) **Application to Bioethanol (2 marks):** The ethanol–water system forms an **azeotrope at 95.6 wt% ethanol**, which cannot be separated by simple distillation. Pervaporation breaks this azeotrope in a single stage because the membrane's selectivity is not governed by vapor–liquid equilibrium. Using a NaA zeolite membrane, water/ethanol selectivity exceeds 10,000, enabling production of fuel-grade ethanol (>99.5%). **(b) Derivation of the Solution–Diffusion Flux Equation (7 marks):** Refer to the complete derivation in **Section 3.2** of these notes. Key steps: **Step 1:** At the upstream face, sorption equilibrium gives: $$C_i^{(1)} = S_i \cdot p_i^{sat} \cdot x_i \gamma_i$$ **Step 2:** At the downstream face: $$C_i^{(2)} = S_i \cdot y_i p_p$$ **Step 3:** Steady-state Fick's law through membrane of thickness *l*: $$J_i = \frac{D_i (C_i^{(1)} - C_i^{(2)})}{l}$$ **Step 4:** Substituting and using $P_i = D_i S_i$: $$\boxed{J_i = \frac{P_i}{l}\left(x_i \gamma_i p_i^{sat} - y_i p_p\right)}$$ Final equation shown above. (7 marks) --- ### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]** (a) Describe the **process of freeze crystallization** for concentration of fruit juice. With the help of suitable equations, explain the **ice–liquid mass balance**. (7 marks) (b) A 1000 kg batch of apple juice containing 11% total soluble solids (TSS) is concentrated by freeze crystallization to a final TSS of 40%. Calculate the **mass of ice removed** and the **mass of concentrate obtained**. What percentage of water in the feed is removed? (7 marks) #### Model Solution **(a) Description & Mass Balance (7 marks):** **Description (4 marks):** Freeze crystallization (also called freeze concentration) is a low-temperature phase-change separation in which water is removed as pure ice, leaving behind a concentrated liquid. The process involves: 1. **Pre-cooling** the juice in a **scraped-surface heat exchanger (SSHE)** to just above its freezing point. 2. **Crystallization** in a stirred vessel where ice nucleates and grows on the surface of cooled walls; the suspension contains 10–30% ice crystals. 3. **Separation** in a **wash column** (or *Bergles column*), where ice crystals are gently melted and counter-currently washed with pure water to remove the adhering concentrated mother liquor. 4. **Recovery** of the concentrated juice from the bottom of the wash column. **Advantages:** Retention of aroma, color, and nutrients; low energy use (334 kJ/kg for ice vs. 2260 kJ/kg for vapor). **Mass Balance Equations (3 marks):** - Total mass balance: $M_{feed} = M_{ice} + M_{liquid}$ - Solute balance: $x_{feed} \cdot M_{feed} = x_{liquid} \cdot M_{liquid}$ - Combined: $M_{ice} = M_{feed}\left(1 - x_{feed}/x_{liquid}\right)$ **(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Given:** - $M_{feed} = 1000$ kg - $x_{feed} = 0.11$ - $x_{liquid} = 0.40$ **Step 1: Mass of ice removed:** $$M_{ice} = 1000 \left(1 - \frac{0.11}{0.40}\right) = 1000(1 - 0.275) = 1000 \times 0.725 = 725 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 2: Mass of concentrate:** $$M_{liquid} = 1000 - 725 = 275 \text{ kg}$$ **Verification by solute balance:** $M_{liquid} = (0.11 \times 1000)/0.40 = 110/0.40 = 275$ kg ✓ **Step 3: Percentage of water removed:** Initial water = $1000 - 110 = 890$ kg Final water in concentrate = $275 - (0.40 \times 275) = 275 - 110 = 165$ kg Water removed as ice = $890 - 165 = 725$ kg $$\% \text{ water removed} = \frac{725}{890} \times 100 = 81.46\%$$ **Final Answer:** $M_{ice} = 725$ kg, $M_{liquid} = 275$ kg, **81.46% of water removed.** (7 marks) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark Losers):** > 1. **Forgetting to quote units** for permeability (Barrer) or flux (kg/m²·h) — loses 1 mark per occurrence. > 2. **Confusing pervaporation with vapor permeation** — they differ: PV starts with a *liquid* feed, VP starts with a *vapor* feed. > 3. **Skipping the solute balance** in freeze concentration derivations — KTU requires *both* mass and solute balances explicitly. > 4. **Omitting the azeotrope concept** when discussing bioethanol — examiners specifically check for the 95.6% azeotrope as justification for using pervaporation. > 5. **Using $P$ for permeability vs. $P$ for pressure** in the same equation — use distinct symbols or specify clearly. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Freeze crystallization** is a **sub-zero, phase-change (liquid → solid)** separation; ideal for **thermolabile** foods. - ✅ **Energy of ice formation (334 kJ/kg)** is ~7× lower than vaporization (2260 kJ/kg). - ✅ **Freezing-point depression** follows $\Delta T_f = K_f m$, with $K_f = 1.86$ °C·kg/mol for water. - ✅ **Wash column (Bergles column)** is essential for removing adhering mother liquor from ice. - ✅ **Pervaporation** = **permeation + evaporation**; involves **liquid-to-vapor phase change**. - ✅ **Solution–Diffusion Model** is the unifying theory for membrane separations: $J_i = (P_i/l)(x_i \gamma_i p_i^{sat} - y_i p_p)$. - ✅ **Permeability** $P = D \cdot S$; units in **Barrer** ($1$ Barrer $= 3.348 \times 10^{-16}$ SI units). - ✅ **Selectivity** $\alpha_{i/j} = (y_i/y_j)/(x_i/x_j)$ is dimensionless and indicates membrane performance. - ✅ **Bioethanol dehydration** uses **hydrophilic membranes** (NaA zeolite) to break the **95.6% ethanol–water azeotrope**. - ✅ **Gas permeation** selectivity for O₂/N₂ in polysulfone ≈ **6.1**; for CO₂/CH₄ in cellulose acetate ≈ **15–20**. - ✅ **Crank equation** governs leaching (solid permeation) from spherical particles. - ✅ **Driving forces:** Freeze (ΔT), Pervaporation (Δp vapor), Permeation (Δc or Δp). - ✅ **Commercial examples:** Niro Process freeze concentrators; Sulzer / GFT pervaporation modules; Praxair O₂/N₂ membrane separators.

topics.title: Osmotic distillation; zone melting: equilibrium diagrams, controlling factors, apparatus and applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT527 - SEPARATION PROCESSES IN FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Osmotic distillation; zone melting: equilibrium diagrams, controlling factors, apparatus and applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — OSMOTIC DISTILLATION & ZONE MELTING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Osmotic Distillation (OD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **Osmotic Distillation (OD)**, also termed *Osmotic Evaporation* or *Isothermal Membrane Distillation*, is a membrane-based concentration process in which water vapor transfers across a **hydrophobic (water-repellent), microporous membrane** from a dilute aqueous feed (high water activity) to a hypertonic brine/stripper solution (low water activity). The driving force is the **gradient in water activity (Δaᵥ)**, NOT a temperature gradient, making it an inherently **isothermal** process ideal for heat-sensitive biological/food matrices.

**Key Thermodynamic Anchor:**
- Pure liquid water has water activity **aᵥ = 1.000**
- Most fruit juices and liquid foods have **0.95 ≤ aᵥ ≤ 0.99**
- Concentrated brines (CaCl₂, NaCl, MgSO₄, glycerol) used as strippers have **aᵥ ≈ 0.20 – 0.75**

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Two-Room Sweating Wall":**
> Imagine two sealed rooms separated by a wall that is *waterproof to liquid* but *breathable to water vapor* (like a Gore-Tex fabric). One room is warm-humid (juice), the other is dry-saline (salt). Water does NOT flow as a liquid — only as vapor — slowly migrating from the humid side to the dry side. The wall stays dry (hydrophobic pores prevent liquid intrusion), and the juice gradually thickens without ever being boiled. **This is osmotic distillation.**

**Why a Hydrophobic Membrane?**
- Hydrophobicity (typically PP, PTFE, PVDF) prevents capillary penetration of liquid water into the pores.
- Liquid Entry Pressure (LEP) must exceed the hydraulic pressure differential — usually **LEP > 200 kPa** for OD modules.
- Pore size range: **0.1 – 0.45 μm** (larger than MD, since thermal gradient is absent).

---

### 1.2 Zone Melting (Zone Refining)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **Zone Melting (ZM)** is a **purification/separation technique** in which a narrow, mobile molten zone is passed one or more times along a solid bar (ingot). Solute (impurity) is redistributed between the liquid zone and the adjacent solid according to the **equilibrium distribution coefficient**, k = Cₛ/C_L. Repeated traversal progressively sweeps soluble impurities toward one end of the bar, enabling ultra-high-purity solidification refining.

**Distribution Coefficient — the Heart of Zone Melting:**

$$k = \frac{C_{S}}{C_{L}} \bigg|_{interface}$$

where:
- **C_S** = concentration of solute in the *solidifying* solid at the solid–liquid interface
- **C_L** = concentration of solute in the *molten* liquid at the same interface
- **k < 1** → solute is rejected by the solid → sweeps along with zone (most purification cases)
- **k > 1** → solute is preferentially incorporated into the solid (used for controlled *doping* of crystals)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Sweeping Window":**
> Picture a long chocolate bar with raisins scattered inside. You pass a small heat-torch along the bar, melting only a 2-cm window. As the window slides forward, the solid re-freezes behind it. If the raisins dislike the solid chocolate (k < 1), they get pushed ahead of the window like snow by a snowplow. After many passes, all raisins accumulate at the far end, leaving ultra-clean chocolate behind. **That is zone refining.**

**Historical Footnote:** Invented by **William Gardner Pfann (Bell Labs, 1952)** for purifying germanium and silicon for the early transistor industry — still the bedrock of semiconductor purification today.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Osmotic Distillation — Transport Mechanism & Step Logic

**Step-by-step mass-transfer sequence:**

1. **Water evaporation** at the feed-side membrane surface (driven by aᵥ,feed).
2. **Vapor diffusion** through the air-filled, hydrophobic membrane pores (Knudsen + molecular diffusion in series).
3. **Vapor condensation** at the stripper-side membrane surface (driven by low aᵥ,stripper).
4. **Sweep-away** of condensate into the bulk brine via mixing/flow.

**The "Why" Behind Each Step:**
- Step 1: Only *evaporated* water can cross — liquid entry is thermodynamically forbidden below LEP.
- Step 2: Pore is dry air; vapor traverses by combined Knudsen and molecular diffusion (no convective flow).
- Step 3: Lower aᵥ on stripper side = lower vapor pressure = condensation sink.
- Step 4: Without mixing, the stripper surface would saturate, killing the gradient.

**Membrane Material Choices (KTU-Favorite Comparison):**

| Membrane | Hydrophobicity | Typical Pore Size | LEP (kPa) | Food-Grade Use |
|----------|----------------|-------------------|-----------|----------------|
| **PTFE (Teflon)** | Excellent | 0.1 – 0.45 μm | 300 – 500 | Juice, dairy |
| **PP (Polypropylene)** | Good | 0.1 – 0.2 μm | 200 – 350 | Aqueous extracts |
| **PVDF** | Moderate | 0.1 – 0.45 μm | 150 – 300 | Limited aqueous |
| **PE** | Moderate | 0.2 – 0.5 μm | 100 – 200 | Rare |

---

### 2.2 Zone Melting — Theoretical Framework

**Why Zone Melting Works — Solute Redistribution Logic:**

When a molten zone of length **ℓ** moves by a small distance **dx**:
- A thin layer of liquid at the *trailing* (re-freezing) interface solidifies → traps solute with concentration C_S = k·C_L.
- A thin layer of solid at the *leading* (melting) interface dissolves → adds solute to the zone.
- Net effect: solute either concentrates at the moving front (k < 1) or is captured in the solid (k > 1).

**Controlling Factors (KTU High-Yield List):**
1. **Distribution coefficient (k)** — primary parameter determining separation direction.
2. **Zone length (ℓ)** — short zones → better purification per pass.
3. **Number of zone passes (n)** — Pfann showed n ≥ 5 typically achieves plateau purity.
4. **Zone travel speed (v)** — slow speed → near-equilibrium, better separation; fast speed → banding.
5. **Initial concentration (C₀)** — dictates total impurity load.
6. **Bar length (L)** — affects final distribution profile.
7. **Stirring/convection in zone** — improves mixing, raises effective k toward equilibrium value.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TABLE A — Osmotic Distillation Equations**

| # | Equation | Description / Units |
|---|----------|--------------------|
| 1 | $J_w = B \cdot \Delta P_w = B \cdot (a_{w,f} - a_{w,s}) \cdot P_w^{sat}$ | Water flux (kg m⁻² h⁻¹); B = membrane permeability (kg m⁻² h⁻¹ Pa⁻¹) |
| 2 | $P_w = a_w \cdot P_w^{sat}(T)$ | Partial vapor pressure of water (Pa) |
| 3 | $\Delta a_w = a_{w,feed} - a_{w,stripper}$ | Driving force (dimensionless) |
| 4 | $C_{f,t} = C_{f,0} \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{B \cdot A \cdot \Delta a_w \cdot P_w^{sat}}{m_f} \cdot t\right)$ | Feed concentration vs. time |
| 5 | $LEP = \dfrac{-2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r_{max}}$ | Liquid Entry Pressure; γ = surface tension, θ = contact angle, r_max = max pore radius |
| 6 | $SF = \dfrac{C_{feed,initial}}{C_{feed,final}}$ | Solid (concentration) Factor, must be > 1 for viable OD |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TABLE B — Zone Melting Equations**

| # | Equation | Description / Units |
|---|----------|--------------------|
| 1 | $k = \dfrac{C_S}{C_L}$ | Equilibrium distribution coefficient (dimensionless) |
| 2 | $C(x) = C_0 \left[1 - (1-k) \exp\left(-\dfrac{k x}{\ell}\right)\right]$ | **Pfann's equation** — concentration profile after *one* pass |
| 3 | $C_n(x) = k \cdot C_0 \cdot \left[\dfrac{(L-x)(k-1)\ell^{-1}}{1 - \exp(-(k-1)\ell^{-1} (L-x))}\right]^{n-1}$ | Concentration after *n* passes (Pfann's n-pass form) |
| 4 | $k_{eff} = \dfrac{k}{k + (1-k)e^{-v\delta/D}}$ | Effective k at finite growth rate v (η = 1 for full mixing) |
| 5 | $g = \dfrac{x}{L}$ | Fraction solidified at position x (0 ≤ g ≤ 1) |
| 6 | $C = \dfrac{C_0}{(1-g)}$ (for k = 0) | Ultimate concentration limit at the swept end |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall — Use \vert, not |, in exam formulas.**
> Always write \vert aᵥ,1 - aᵥ,2 \vert in your answer scripts, never \vert aᵥ,1 - aᵥ,2 \vert, to avoid markdown table breakage and to follow LaTeX best practice in LaTeX-submitted assignments.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Osmotic Distillation** is the *only* membrane process capable of concentrating **high-viscosity, heat-sensitive, high-solid foods** (e.g., 60–70 °Brix fruit juice concentrates, aroma-rich essences) without aroma loss or Maillard browning.
- **Zone Melting** is the *only* technique routinely achieving **> 99.9999% (6N) purity** in metals, alloys, and semiconductor-grade crystals — fundamental to microelectronics, photonics, and pharmaceutical-grade inorganics.

# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — EQUILIBRIUM, KINETICS & PROCESS DESIGN

## 2.5 Equilibrium Diagram for Osmotic Distillation — The aᵥ–Composition Curve

Osmotic distillation has **no true vapor–liquid equilibrium (VLE) curve** in the classical thermodynamic sense, since no phase change of bulk liquid occurs. However, the **water activity vs. solute concentration diagram** is its functional "equilibrium diagram."

**Shape and Significance:**

- For food solutes (sugars, organic acids, salts), aᵥ drops **non-linearly** with concentration.
- **aᵥ = 1.0** for infinitely dilute solutions.
- As concentration increases, aᵥ follows the **Gibbs–Duhem / NRTL or UNIQUAC-derived curves**.
- Concentrated brines (e.g., CaCl₂) drop aᵥ to **< 0.30** — providing huge Δaᵥ for juice.

$$\ln a_w = -\dfrac{\nu \cdot m \cdot M_s \cdot \phi}{1000} \quad \text{(Raoult's law, dilute ideal)}$$

For concentrated real food solutions, the **Norrish equation** or **Pitzer model** is used:

$$a_w = X_w \cdot \exp(-K \cdot X_s^2)$$

where X_w, X_s are mole fractions of water and solute, K is empirical Norrish constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Question Trigger:**
> "Sketch the aᵥ vs. °Brix curve for sucrose solution and identify the region where osmotic distillation flux is maximum."
> **Model Answer:** Flux is maximum at low °Brix (high aᵥ) on the feed side, and falls exponentially as the feed concentrates. Practically, OD is *most economical* in the early concentration stage (10 → 50 °Brix); final polishing to 70 °Brix is often done by a coupled RO + OD system.

---

## 2.6 Equilibrium Diagram for Zone Melting — Phase Diagram in (T, C) Space

Zone melting is governed by the **solid–liquid phase diagram** (analogous to binary melting/freezing diagrams).

**Key Features of a Typical Binary Eutectic Phase Diagram Used in ZM:**

- **Liquidus line** (upper boundary) — temperature at which a solution of composition C_L begins to freeze.
- **Solidus line** (lower boundary) — temperature at which a solid of composition C_S begins to melt.
- For dilute solutes with k < 1, the liquidus slope is **dT/dC > 0** (melting point *decreases* with solute addition).
- The **tie-line at any temperature T** between solidus and liquidus gives the equilibrium partition coefficient k.

**Local Equilibrium Form (lever rule):**

At the solid–liquid interface at temperature T_i:

$$k = \frac{C_S(T_i)}{C_L(T_i)}$$

This k is a *function of temperature and composition* (not strictly constant), but for dilute solutes (C < 1 at%), it is treated as a constant — **k₀** — the *equilibrium* distribution coefficient.

**Departure from Equilibrium — Scheil Equation:**

When growth rate is finite and solid-state diffusion is negligible:

$$C_S = k \cdot C_0 (1 - g)^{k-1}$$

where g = fraction solidified.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **If k < 1** → C_S > C₀ for any g > 0 → **microsegregation (coring)**.
> **If k > 1** → C_S < C₀ → solid is purer than melt (zone-melting produces purification in this regime too, but direction is reversed).
> **For k = 1** → no separation possible; zone melting is useless.

---

## 2.7 Controlling Factors — Consolidated Engineering View

### A. Osmotic Distillation — Controlling Factors

| Factor | Effect on Flux J_w | Engineering Implication |
|--------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| Δaᵥ (feed – stripper) | Linear ↑ | Choose stripper with lowest possible aᵥ |
| Membrane permeability B | Linear ↑ | Select thin, highly porous, hydrophobic |
| Temperature | ↑ P_w^sat → ↑ J_w exponentially | Mild warming (35–40 °C) helps but must not damage food |
| Cross-flow velocity | ↑ surface renewal → ↑ J_w | Optimize to avoid pressure-induced wetting |
| Feed viscosity | ↓ diffusion → ↓ J_w | Worst at end of concentration (60+ °Brix) |
| Stripper concentration | ↑ Δaᵥ → ↑ J_w | Recirculate/replace brine regularly |
| Membrane fouling | ↓ permeability → ↓ J_w | Pretreat juice (pectinase, filtration) |

### B. Zone Melting — Controlling Factors

| Factor | Effect on Separation | Engineering Implication |
|--------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| k value | Direction & magnitude of solute sweep | Select process for the right k regime |
| Zone length ℓ | Shorter ℓ → sharper gradient | Use ℓ ≈ 0.05–0.1 L for best refining |
| Number of passes n | Exponential improvement, plateau at n ≈ 5–10 | Multi-pass is essential |
| Zone travel speed v | Slow v → near-equilibrium | Typical v = 1–10 cm/h for laboratory work |
| Initial concentration C₀ | Sets total impurity load | Multi-pass required for high C₀ |
| Convection in zone | ↑ k_eff toward k₀ | Use electromagnetic stirring |
| Bar geometry | Affects heat transfer, zone stability | Thin bars preferred for uniform zone |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Utility Recap

- **OD in Industry:** Commercial OD plants (e.g., *Osmotek/SUEZ* in Israel) concentrate **orange, apple, cranberry, pomegranate juices** to 60–70 °Brix, retaining volatile aroma esters (>90% retention vs. ~30% for evaporative concentration). Also used for **ethanol removal from beer**, **dealcoholization of wine**, and **aroma recovery from coffee extract**.
- **ZM in Industry:** **Silicon** (semi-conductor grade, 11N purity), **germanium**, **gallium arsenide (GaAs)**, **aluminum**, **copper**, **zinc**, and ultra-pure organic compounds (*p*-terphenyl, naphthalene, benzoic acid) for scintillation detectors. ZM is the method of choice for **metals where distillation/vacuum refining is impractical** (low vapor pressure metals like Fe, Ni, Ti).

# STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Water Flux in Osmotic Distillation (J_w)

Starting from the membrane transport equation for vapor diffusion:

$$J_w = \frac{B}{P_w^{sat}} \cdot (P_{w,feed}^{vap} - P_{w,stripper}^{vap})$$

**Step 1:** Express partial vapor pressures in terms of water activity:

$$P_{w,feed}^{vap} = a_{w,feed} \cdot P_w^{sat}(T)$$

$$P_{w,stripper}^{vap} = a_{w,stripper} \cdot P_w^{sat}(T)$$

**Step 2:** Subtract:

$$P_{w,feed}^{vap} - P_{w,stripper}^{vap} = (a_{w,feed} - a_{w,stripper}) \cdot P_w^{sat}(T)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute back into flux equation:

$$J_w = B \cdot P_w^{sat}(T) \cdot (a_{w,feed} - a_{w,stripper})$$

**Step 4:** Define driving force explicitly:

$$\Delta a_w = a_{w,feed} - a_{w,stripper}$$

**Step 5:** Final compact form:

$$\boxed{J_w = B \cdot P_w^{sat}(T) \cdot \Delta a_w}$$

**Conversion logic (text):** The flux is the product of three terms — membrane permeability (material property), saturation vapor pressure (a strong function of temperature per Clausius–Clapeyron), and the water-activity gradient (the actual "OD driving force"). This is why OD is temperature-sensitive even though it is *isothermal by design* — P_w^sat(T) appears explicitly.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pfann's Equation for One-Pass Zone Refining

Consider a bar of length L, total solute mass = C₀·L, and a molten zone of length ℓ moving from x = 0 to x = L.

**Step 1:** Mass balance on the zone at position x:

When the zone advances by dx, a length ℓ of liquid "sheds" a solid layer of thickness dx at the trailing interface. The solute mass in this newly formed solid is C_S·dx = k·C_L·dx. The leading interface dissolves a length dx of the original solid, contributing C₀·dx of solute (assuming uniform initial concentration).

**Step 2:** Change in solute mass in zone:

$$d(M) = C_0 \, dx - k \, C_L \, dx$$

But M = C_L · ℓ, so dM = ℓ · dC_L.

**Step 3:** Equate:

$$\ell \cdot dC_L = (C_0 - k \cdot C_L) \, dx$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange as first-order ODE:

$$\frac{dC_L}{C_0 - k \cdot C_L} = \frac{dx}{\ell}$$

**Step 5:** Integrate from x = 0 (C_L = C₀/k at start) to position x (C_L = ?):

$$-\frac{1}{k} \ln(C_0 - k \cdot C_L) \Big|_{C_0/k}^{C_L} = \frac{x}{\ell}$$

**Step 6:** Apply limits and simplify:

$$\frac{1}{k} \ln\left(\frac{C_0 - k \cdot C_0/k}{C_0 - k \cdot C_L}\right) = \frac{x}{\ell}$$

$$\frac{1}{k} \ln\left(\frac{0}{C_0 - k \cdot C_L}\right) = ?$$

**Correction (resolving the singularity):** At x = 0, the leading interface of the zone meets the original solid of concentration C₀, so the *initial* liquid composition is exactly C₀ (no pre-existing liquid). Boundary condition is **C_L(0) = C₀**. Re-integration:

$$\int_{C_0}^{C_L} \frac{dC_L}{C_0 - k \cdot C_L} = \int_0^x \frac{dx}{\ell}$$

$$-\frac{1}{k} \ln\left(\frac{C_0 - k \cdot C_L}{C_0 - k \cdot C_0}\right) = \frac{x}{\ell}$$

**Step 7:** Simplify denominator (C₀ − k·C₀ = C₀(1−k)):

$$\ln\left(\frac{C_0 - k \cdot C_L}{C_0(1-k)}\right) = -\frac{k \cdot x}{\ell}$$

**Step 8:** Exponentiate:

$$\frac{C_0 - k \cdot C_L}{C_0(1-k)} = \exp\left(-\frac{k \cdot x}{\ell}\right)$$

**Step 9:** Solve for C_L:

$$C_L = \frac{C_0}{k} \left[1 - (1-k) \exp\left(-\frac{k \cdot x}{\ell}\right)\right]$$

**Step 10:** The solid composition (the desired "purified bar") is C_S = k·C_L:

$$\boxed{C_S(x) = C_0 \left[1 - (1-k) \exp\left(-\frac{k \cdot x}{\ell}\right)\right]}$$

**Conversion logic (text):** This is **Pfann's equation** — the master equation of zone refining. It shows that for k < 1, the trailing portion of the bar (large x) approaches C₀ (no purification in this simplified form), and the leading portion (small x) has the *original* concentration. In multi-pass refining, the leading "clean" end is the product.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Concentration Factor and Solid Recovery in OD

**Step 1:** Mass balance on the feed tank (volume V_f, mass m_f, solute mass m_s):

$$\frac{dm_s}{dt} = 0 \quad (\text{solute does not leave feed})$$

$$m_s = C_f(t) \cdot m_f(t) = C_{f,0} \cdot m_{f,0}$$

**Step 2:** Water removal rate equals flux × area:

$$-\frac{dm_f}{dt} = J_w \cdot A = B \cdot P_w^{sat} \cdot \Delta a_w \cdot A$$

**Step 3:** Express m_f in terms of initial and concentration change:

$$m_f(t) = m_{f,0} \cdot \frac{C_{f,0}}{C_f(t)}$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate:

$$-\frac{dm_f}{dt} = m_{f,0} \cdot C_{f,0} \cdot \frac{1}{C_f^2} \cdot \frac{dC_f}{dt}$$

**Step 5:** Equate and rearrange:

$$m_{f,0} \cdot C_{f,0} \cdot \frac{1}{C_f^2} \cdot \frac{dC_f}{dt} = B \cdot P_w^{sat} \cdot \Delta a_w \cdot A$$

**Step 6:** Separate variables:

$$\frac{dC_f}{C_f^2} = \frac{B \cdot P_w^{sat} \cdot \Delta a_w \cdot A}{m_{f,0} \cdot C_{f,0}} \, dt$$

**Step 7:** Integrate from C_{f,0} to C_f(t):

$$\left[-\frac{1}{C_f}\right]_{C_{f,0}}^{C_f(t)} = K \cdot t$$

where $K = \dfrac{B \cdot P_w^{sat} \cdot \Delta a_w \cdot A}{m_{f,0} \cdot C_{f,0}}$

**Step 8:** Solve:

$$\frac{1}{C_{f,0}} - \frac{1}{C_f(t)} = K \cdot t$$

**Step 9:** Final form:

$$\boxed{C_f(t) = \frac{C_{f,0}}{1 - C_{f,0} \cdot K \cdot t}}$$

**Step 10:** Solids (concentration) Factor:

$$SF = \frac{C_f(t)}{C_{f,0}} = \frac{1}{1 - C_{f,0} \cdot K \cdot t}$$

**Conversion logic (text):** This is the *concentration polarization-free* model of batch OD. SF grows hyperbolically with time; the process is bounded by the membrane getting wetted or the feed becoming too viscous for pumping. The reciprocal relationship tells you that the *last 10%* of water removal takes longer than the first 50%, an important process economics insight.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — OD vs. Zone Melting Comparison

| Parameter | Osmotic Distillation | Zone Melting |
|-----------|----------------------|--------------|
| **Phase state of feed** | Liquid (aqueous) | Solid + thin liquid zone |
| **Driving force** | Δaᵥ (water activity gradient) | ΔT + distribution coefficient k |
| **Operating temperature** | 25–40 °C (isothermal) | Above melting point (e.g., 1414 °C for Si) |
| **Purity/separation achievable** | 60–70 °Brix, >90% aroma retention | 6N – 11N purity (>99.9999%) |
| **Membrane/crucible required** | Hydrophobic porous membrane | Inert boat/crucible, RF heater |
| **Energy form** | Chemical potential (concentration) | Thermal (latent heat + sensible) |
| **Scale-up** | Modular membrane area | Limited by bar length uniformity |
| **Continuous operation** | Yes (with recirculation) | Semi-batch (per bar) |
| **KTU module** | Module 4 (advanced membrane) | Module 4 (advanced thermal) |

# STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Osmotic Distillation — Membrane Module Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FeedTank[FEED TANK<br/>Fruit Juice<br/>a_v approx 0.98]
    FeedPump[FEED PUMP]
    FeedIn[FEED INLET]
    MemTop[MEMBRANE<br/>FEED CHANNEL]
    MemCore[HYDROPHOBIC<br/>MICROPOROUS<br/>MEMBRANE<br/>Pore 0.2 micron]
    MemBot[MEMBRANE<br/>STRIPPER CHANNEL]
    StripIn[STRIPPER INLET]
    StripPump[BRINE PUMP]
    StripTank[BRINE TANK<br/>CaCl2 solution<br/>a_v approx 0.25]
    VaporPath[VAPOR DIFFUSION<br/>Knudsen plus Molecular]
    FeedOut[FEED OUTLET<br/>Concentrated Juice]
    StripOut[STRIPPER OUTLET<br/>Diluted Brine]
    Conc[CONCENTRATED<br/>PRODUCT]

    FeedTank --> FeedPump --> FeedIn --> MemTop
    StripTank --> StripPump --> StripIn --> MemBot
    MemTop --- MemCore
    MemCore --- MemBot
    MemCore -. VaporPath .-> MemBot
    MemTop --> FeedOut --> Conc
    MemBot --> StripOut --> StripTank
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Two independent flow loops (feed + stripper) separated by the hydrophobic membrane.
- Vapor diffuses *across* the membrane (dashed arrow) — liquid never crosses.
- Stripper is recycled to maintain constant aᵥ in the stripper loop.
