---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Define osmotic distillation. How does it differ from conventional membrane distillation?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) Definition:** Osmotic distillation is a membrane-based concentration process where water vapor transfers across a hydrophobic, microporous membrane from a feed solution (high water activity) to a hypertonic stripper solution (low water activity).
- **(1 mark) Driving force:** The driving force is the **gradient in water activity (Δaᵥ)**, *not* a temperature gradient.
- **(1 mark) Key difference from MD:** Conventional membrane distillation uses a **temperature gradient (ΔT)** to create a vapor pressure difference across the membrane. OD is **isothermal**, MD is **non-isothermal**. Hence OD is preferred for heat-sensitive foods and aromatics.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"State the distribution coefficient in zone melting. What does k < 1 signify?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) Definition:** The distribution coefficient **k = C_S / C_L** is the ratio of solute concentration in the *solidifying solid* (C_S) to that in the *molten liquid* (C_L) at the solid–liquid interface.
- **(1 mark) k < 1 meaning:** The solid rejects the solute; the solute is enriched in the liquid zone. As the molten zone traverses the bar, impurities are **swept toward one end**, leaving a purified region behind.
- **(1 mark) Consequence:** This is the *purification regime* of zone refining — the most common industrial application.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question is 14 marks, sub-divided as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]

**"With the help of a neat sketch, explain the principle, equilibrium aspects, controlling factors, and applications of osmotic distillation in the food industry."**

#### Part (a) — Principle, Equilibrium & Sketch (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(1) Definition (1 mark):** Osmotic distillation is an **isothermal membrane process** that uses a hydrophobic, microporous membrane to selectively remove water as vapor from aqueous food streams, with a hypertonic brine as the receiving (stripping) phase.

**(2) Working Principle (2 marks):**
- A **hydrophobic microporous membrane** (PTFE/PP, pore size 0.1–0.45 μm) separates two aqueous streams.
- The **feed** (e.g., fruit juice) has high water activity (aᵥ ≈ 0.95–0.99).
- The **stripper** (e.g., CaCl₂ brine) has low water activity (aᵥ ≈ 0.20–0.40).
- Water evaporates at the feed-membrane interface, diffuses as vapor through dry pores, and condenses at the stripper side.
- No phase change of bulk liquid; no temperature gradient; the membrane pores remain dry (gas-filled) because hydrophobicity and LEP prevent liquid entry.

**(3) Equilibrium Aspect — aᵥ vs. Composition Diagram (2 marks):**
- Plot **aᵥ (y-axis) vs. solute concentration / °Brix (x-axis)**.
- Curve descends non-linearly (Norrish or Pitzer type).
- The **vertical gap** between feed and stripper curves at any operating point represents Δaᵥ — the driving force.
- Maximum flux occurs at the **largest Δaᵥ**, which is at the start of concentration (dilute feed + concentrated brine).
- As feed concentrates, aᵥ,feed drops, reducing Δaᵥ, hence flux decays exponentially with time.

**(4) Sketch (2 marks):** See Mermaid Figure in Section 4.1 (membrane module architecture) — re-draw on answer script for full marks.

#### Part (b) — Controlling Factors & Applications (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(1) Controlling Factors (4 marks):**
- **Δaᵥ (water activity gradient):** Higher Δaᵥ → higher flux. Use very concentrated brine (CaCl₂) for maximum gradient.
- **Membrane permeability (B):** Function of porosity, thickness, hydrophobicity. Thin, porous PTFE gives high B.
- **Temperature:** Increases P_w^sat(T) exponentially (Clausius–Clapeyron), so mild warming raises flux; but > 40 °C may damage food.
- **Cross-flow velocity:** Higher velocity reduces concentration polarization on both sides, sustaining flux.
- **Stripper concentration maintenance:** Stripper is diluted with time; periodic replacement or membrane contactor regeneration is needed.
- **Membrane fouling:** Pectin/protein deposition blocks pores → flux decline. Pretreatment (pectinase, microfiltration) is essential.

**(2) Applications in Food Industry (3 marks):**
- **Fruit juice concentration:** Orange, apple, cranberry, pomegranate concentrated to 60–70 °Brix with >90% aroma retention.
- **Aroma recovery:** Coupling OD with aroma trapping on the stripper side yields natural essences.
- **Dealcoholization of beverages:** Ethanol removal from beer/wine at ambient temperature.
- **Concentration of heat-sensitive bioactives:** Probiotics, enzymes, nutraceutical extracts.
- **Pre-concentration step before evaporation/RO:** Reduces thermal load on downstream evaporators.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]

**"Discuss the principle of zone melting. Derive the Pfann equation for the concentration profile after one pass. List the controlling factors and applications."**

#### Part (a) — Principle, Pfann's Equation Derivation & Equilibrium Diagram (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(1) Principle of Zone Melting (2 marks):**
- A **narrow molten zone** (length ℓ, much less than total bar length L) is established and moved along a solid bar.
- As the zone advances, the **trailing interface solidifies** (freezing) and the **leading interface melts** (dissolution).
- Solute (impurity) is distributed between liquid and solidifying solid according to **k = C_S / C_L**.
- For **k < 1**, the solid rejects the solute; the solute accumulates in the liquid zone and is *swept* along with it.
- Multi-pass operation progressively pushes all soluble impurities to one end of the bar.

**(2) Equilibrium Diagram (Binary Phase Diagram) (2 marks):**
- Refer to a typical **binary solid–liquid phase diagram** with axes T (vertical) and composition C (horizontal).
- **Liquidus** and **solidus** lines enclose a two-phase (solid + liquid) region.
- The **distribution coefficient k** is given by the ratio of solidus composition to liquidus composition at the interface temperature: $k = C_S^{solidus} / C_L^{liquidus}$.
- For **k < 1** (typical), the melting point *decreases* with increasing solute → liquidus slope is positive.
- Equilibrium tie-line at interface temperature gives local compositions.

**(3) Derivation of Pfann's Equation (3 marks):** *(See Section 3.2 for full derivation. Recap the final result:)*

$$C_S(x) = C_0 \left[1 - (1-k) \exp\left(-\frac{k x}{\ell}\right)\right]$$

- At x = 0 (start of zone): C_S = C₀ (initial concentration).
- At x = L (far end): C_S approaches $C_0 [1 - (1-k) e^{-kL/\ell}]$.
- For k < 1, as L/ℓ → ∞, C_S → C₀ (no purification in the *single-pass, equal-length* simple model).
- True purification requires **multi-pass refinement** and/or **ingot cropping**.

#### Part (b) — Controlling Factors & Applications (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(1) Controlling Factors (4 marks):**
- **Distribution coefficient (k):** Direction and magnitude of separation; k close to 0 = best purification; k = 1 = no separation.
- **Zone length (ℓ):** Shorter zones yield sharper separation gradients; ℓ ≈ 0.05–0.1·L is typical.
- **Number of zone passes (n):** Per Pfann's n-pass formula, purity improves exponentially with n, plateauing at n ≈ 5–10.
- **Zone travel speed (v):** Slower speed → near-equilibrium freezing → effective k → equilibrium k₀. Fast speed → banding / solute trapping.
- **Initial concentration C₀:** Higher C₀ requires more passes for the same final purity.
- **Convection in the molten zone:** Electromagnetic stirring raises k_eff toward k₀.
- **Bar/crucible material:** Must be chemically inert (quartz, graphite) to avoid contamination.

**(2) Applications (3 marks):**
- **Semiconductor industry:** Purification of **silicon** and **germanium** to 11N (99.999999999%) purity for transistors, integrated circuits, solar cells.
- **Metallurgy:** Refining of **aluminum, copper, zinc, lead, tin** for high-conductivity applications.
- **Photonics:** **GaAs, InP, CdTe** single crystals with controlled dopant distribution.
- **Organic compound purification:** *p*-terphenyl, naphthalene, anthracene, benzoic acid for scintillation detectors and analytical standards.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Ultra-pure inorganics (Ti, Zr) for implants and drug manufacture.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Penalties Applied by KTU Valuators):**
> 1. **Confusing "membrane distillation" with "osmotic distillation":** Mentioning ΔT as the OD driving force = 0 marks for the "driving force" sub-part. The correct answer is **Δaᵥ**.
> 2. **Forgetting the "hydrophobic" qualifier:** The membrane MUST be hydrophobic + microporous. Generic "membrane" answers lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Mismeasuring k:** Writing k = C_L/C_S (inverted) = full mark loss for definition.
> 4. **Skipping the "why" in the Pfann derivation:** Showing only the final equation without boundary conditions and integration steps = max 1/3 marks for that sub-part.
> 5. **Not drawing/labeling the membrane module:** KTU mandates "neat sketch" — a labeled diagram is worth 2 marks.
> 6. **Confusing the *zone length* ℓ with *bar length* L:** This is a classic trap; L is the total ingot, ℓ is the molten zone.
> 7. **Stating applications without specifying purity achieved:** Always cite a *number* (e.g., "6N purity for Si") to show engineering depth.

---

## 5.4 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

### Osmotic Distillation — Quick Recall
- ✅ **OD = isothermal membrane process, hydrophobic, microporous membrane**
- ✅ **Driving force = Δaᵥ (water activity gradient)**, NOT ΔT
- ✅ **Stripper options:** CaCl₂ (aᵥ ≈ 0.25), MgSO₄, glycerol, concentrated NaCl
- ✅ **Membrane materials:** PTFE, PP, PVDF
- ✅ **Flux equation:** $J_w = B \cdot P_w^{sat}(T) \cdot \Delta a_w$
- ✅ **LEP equation:** $LEP = -2\gamma \cos\theta / r_{max}$
- ✅ **Flux decays with time** as feed concentrates
- ✅ **Applications:** Juice concentration, aroma recovery, ethanol removal, bioactives
- ✅ **Advantage over evaporation:** >90% aroma retention, no Maillard browning
- ✅ **Advantage over MD:** Isothermal → no thermal damage

### Zone Melting — Quick Recall
- ✅ **ZM = solid–liquid zone refining, mobile molten zone**
- ✅ **Distribution coefficient:** $k = C_S / C_L$ at the interface
- ✅ **k < 1 → purification regime** (solute swept with zone)
- ✅ **k > 1 → doping regime** (solute captured in solid)
- ✅ **k = 1 → no separation possible**
- ✅ **Pfann's equation (one pass):** $C_S(x) = C_0[1 - (1-k) e^{-kx/\ell}]$
- ✅ **Controlling factors:** k, ℓ, n, v, C₀, convection, geometry
- ✅ **Multi-pass is essential** for high purity (n ≈ 5–10)
- ✅ **Equilibrium diagram:** Binary solid–liquid phase diagram (liquidus + solidus)
- ✅ **Applications:** Si, Ge, GaAs purification; metals refining; organic compounds
- ✅ **Inventor:** William G. Pfann, Bell Labs, 1952
- ✅ **Scheil equation (non-equilibrium):** $C_S = k C_0 (1-g)^{k-1}$

### Cross-Topic Comparison Lock
- ✅ OD is a **membrane process** for **liquid foods**; ZM is a **thermal process** for **solid metals/crystals**.
- ✅ OD's "equilibrium diagram" is **aᵥ vs. concentration**; ZM's is **T vs. concentration (phase diagram)**.
- ✅ OD driving force = **Δaᵥ**; ZM driving force = **k (and ΔT for melting).**

---

*End of Module 4 Notes — Osmotic Distillation & Zone Melting*
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) — B.Tech Food Technology*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Foam separation: surface adsorption, nature of foams, apparatus, applications in food processing, and controlling factors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT527 - SEPARATION PROCESSES IN FOOD TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Foam separation: surface adsorption, nature of foams, apparatus, applications in food processing, and controlling factors # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Foam Separation (Foam Fractionation)** is an **adsorptive bubble separation technique** in which surface-active solutes (surfactants, proteins, lipids, bitter compounds, colour pigments) are preferentially concentrated at a **gas–liquid interface** and are subsequently removed from the bulk liquid as a **foam phase** rising above the solution. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, foam separation is classified as an **equilibrium-stage based adsorptive separation process** under the broader domain of **surfactant-based separations**. The driving force for the separation is the **reduction in surface free energy (Gibbs free energy) of the system** when surface-active molecules migrate from the bulk to the air–water interface. The mathematical characterization uses the **Gibbs Adsorption Isotherm**, which quantitatively relates the surface excess concentration $(\Gamma)$ to the bulk concentration and the surface tension gradient: $$\Gamma = -\frac{C}{RT}\left(\frac{d\gamma}{dC}\right)$$ Where: - $\Gamma$ = Surface excess concentration (mol/m²) — standard metric: **mol·m⁻²** - $C$ = Bulk concentration of the solute (mol/m³) - $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** - $T$ = Absolute temperature in **Kelvin (K)** - $\gamma$ = Surface tension of the solution (N/m) ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Soap Bubble Cleaning Trick" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a bathtub filled with greasy water. When you blow bubbles through a straw, the bubbles rise and carry grease droplets on their surface. The collected bubbles (foam) are far "dirtier" than the water you started with — the grease has migrated from the bulk water *onto* the bubble surface. This is exactly how **foam separation** works in food processing: bubbles scrub surface-active compounds out of a liquid. Just as a sponge soaked in oil floats on water (because oil lowers the surface energy at the sponge–air interface), surface-active food molecules such as **saponins, hop bitter acids, milk proteins (casein, whey), and anthocyanin pigments** "prefer" to sit at the air–water interface. Foam separation exploits this natural preference to concentrate them. ## 1.3 Why Foam Separation Matters in Food Technology - **Mild Operating Conditions** — conducted at **ambient or low temperatures (5–50 °C)** and **atmospheric pressure**, preserving heat-labile nutrients. - **Low Energy Footprint** — no phase change, no evaporation; only gas sparging is required. - **Selective for Surface-Active Species** — ideal for **proteins, bitter compounds (limonin, naringin in citrus), color bodies, and surfactants** in dilute streams. - **Continuous Operation Possible** — easily scaled for industrial food processing lines. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Foam separation is grouped with other **adsorptive separation methods** (membrane separation, ion exchange, chromatography) but is unique because the *adsorbing surface is created in situ* — namely, the rising bubbles themselves. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Foam Separation ### 2.1.1 Surface Adsorption Mechanism — The "Why" Surface-active molecules (surfactants) possess an **amphiphilic molecular architecture** — a hydrophilic (polar) head and a hydrophobic (non-polar) tail. When dissolved in water: 1. **Bulk Phase Repulsion:** The hydrophobic tail disrupts the hydrogen-bonded water network, increasing the system's free energy. 2. **Interface Stabilization:** Migration to the gas–liquid interface orients the tail toward the gas phase (a non-polar medium) and the head toward the water, **lowering the interfacial free energy**. 3. **Net Result:** The system achieves a thermodynamically favorable state when solute molecules are *adsorbed* at the interface — this is the **thermodynamic driving force** for foam separation. ### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Process Logic - **Step 1 — Sparging:** Gas (typically **air, nitrogen, or CO₂**) is dispersed through a **porous sparger / frit / Venturi injector** at the column base, generating a swarm of fine bubbles (radius **0.1–3 mm**). - **Step 2 — Adsorption at the Bubble Surface:** As bubbles rise through the liquid, surface-active solute molecules adsorb onto the gas–liquid interface according to the **Gibbs Adsorption Isotherm**. - **Step 3 — Foam Formation and Rise:** Bubbles laden with surfactant reach the surface, forming a **polyhedral foam** that rises above the bulk liquid. - **Step 4 — Drainage and Coalescence:** Liquid in the foam lamellae drains back into the bulk, **concentrating the surface-active solute** in the foam phase. - **Step 5 — Foam Collection and Collapse:** The dry foam is collected at the top and collapsed (mechanically or thermally) to yield a **concentrate (foamate)** with enrichment ratios of **2 to 100+** depending on operating conditions. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The following equations form the analytical core of any numerical/problem-solving question on foam separation. Memorize the symbols, units, and the Gibbs equation especially — it is asked almost every KTU cycle. | # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Description | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\Gamma = -\dfrac{C}{RT}\left(\dfrac{d\gamma}{dC}\right)$ | Gibbs Adsorption Isotherm: surface excess $\Gamma$ (mol/m²), bulk concentration $C$ (mol/m³), surface tension $\gamma$ (N/m) | Quantifies adsorption density; used to design sparger systems | | 2 | $E = \dfrac{C_f}{C_i}$ | Enrichment Ratio: $C_f$ = foamate concentration, $C_i$ = feed concentration | Performance indicator — higher $E$ = better separation | | 3 | $R = \dfrac{C_f \cdot V_f}{C_i \cdot V_i}$ | Recovery Fraction: $V_f$ = foamate volume, $V_i$ = feed volume | Mass balance check for continuous operation | | 4 | $\Pi = \gamma_0 - \gamma$ | Surface Pressure: $\gamma_0$ = pure solvent surface tension (N/m) | Measures surfactant activity at the interface | | 5 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma}{r}$ | Young–Laplace Pressure: pressure difference across bubble of radius $r$ | Predicts bubble stability and coalescence tendency | | 6 | $v_t = \dfrac{2r^2 g (\rho_l - \rho_g)}{9\mu_l}$ | Stokes Terminal Velocity of a bubble | Used to size column height for adequate contact time | | 7 | $\tau = \dfrac{H}{v_t}$ | Bubble Residence Time: column height $H$ (m) | Sets minimum column height for target $\Gamma$ | **Boundary Conditions & Notes:** - Valid for **dilute surfactant solutions** ($C < \text{CMC}$, Critical Micelle Concentration). - At $C = \text{CMC}$, $\Gamma$ plateaus — *no further adsorption occurs above CMC*. This is a **critical design limit** in foam separation. - Stokes' law valid for $\text{Re} < 1$ (laminar bubble rise). ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility In **industrial food plants**, foam separation is employed in: - **Citrus juice debittering** (removing limonin and naringin). - **Whey protein fractionation** (β-lactoglobulin enrichment). - **Enzyme recovery** from fermentation broths (e.g., protease concentration). - **Color and off-flavor removal** from sugar syrups and brewing streams. The **Gibbs isotherm** informs the design of the bubble generator — to maximize $\Gamma$, one must generate a **large total interfacial area**, which requires **small bubble radius** and **high gas flow rate**. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Gibbs Adsorption Isotherm The Gibbs Adsorption Equation is the **cornerstone relation** for any foam separation problem. We derive it from fundamental thermodynamic principles. ### Starting Point: Differential of Interfacial Free Energy For a system with an interface of area $A$ and bulk solution of concentration $C$, the change in Gibbs free energy at constant $T$ and $P$ is: $$dG = \gamma \, dA + A \, d\gamma + \mu \, dn_s$$ Where: - $\mu$ = chemical potential of the surface-active solute (J/mol) - $n_s$ = number of moles of solute adsorbed at the interface (mol) ### Step 1 — Define the Surface Excess The **surface excess concentration** $\Gamma$ (mol/m²) is defined as: $$\Gamma = \frac{n_s}{A}$$ Therefore: $$n_s = \Gamma A \quad \Rightarrow \quad dn_s = A \, d\Gamma + \Gamma \, dA$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute into the Free Energy Expression Replacing $dn_s$ in the energy equation: $$dG = \gamma \, dA + A \, d\gamma + \mu \left(A \, d\Gamma + \Gamma \, dA\right)$$ Grouping terms by $dA$ and $d\Gamma$: $$dG = A\left(d\gamma + \mu \, d\Gamma\right) + dA\left(\gamma + \mu \Gamma\right)$$ ### Step 3 — Apply the Equilibrium Condition At equilibrium, the chemical potential of the solute at the interface must equal that in the bulk ($\mu_i$). For a reversible change at constant $T$ and $P$: $$\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial A}\right)_{T,P,\Gamma} = \gamma + \mu \Gamma = \gamma_0 \quad \text{(interfacial tension of pure solvent)}$$ The term $\gamma_0$ represents the **reference state** (pure solvent–gas interface). Subtracting this baseline: $$d\gamma = -\Gamma \, d\mu$$ ### Step 4 — Relate Chemical Potential to Concentration For an ideal dilute solution: $$\mu = \mu^\circ + RT \ln C \quad \Rightarrow \quad d\mu = \frac{RT}{C} \, dC$$ ### Step 5 — Final Form of the Gibbs Isotherm Substituting $d\mu$ into $d\gamma$: $$d\gamma = -\Gamma \cdot \frac{RT}{C} \, dC$$ Rearranging to solve for $\Gamma$: $$\Gamma = -\frac{C}{RT} \left(\frac{d\gamma}{dC}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation:** If $\dfrac{d\gamma}{dC} < 0$ (surfactant *lowers* surface tension), then $\Gamma > 0$, confirming **positive adsorption** at the interface. This is the condition required for foam separation to work. ## 3.2 Worked Analytical Problem — KTU Numerical Style > **Problem:** A foam fractionation column is used to recover a protein from a dilute aqueous solution. The bulk concentration of the protein is $C_i = 0.05 \text{ mol/m}^3$, and the surface tension of the solution varies with concentration according to $\gamma = 0.072 - 0.015 C$ (N/m), where $C$ is in mol/m³. Calculate the surface excess concentration $\Gamma$ at $T = 298$ K. Given: $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). ### Solution: **Step 1 — Evaluate the surface tension gradient** $$\frac{d\gamma}{dC} = \frac{d}{dC}(0.072 - 0.015C) = -0.015 \text{ N·m}^2/\text{mol}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute into the Gibbs Isotherm** $$\Gamma = -\frac{C}{RT}\left(\frac{d\gamma}{dC}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Plug in numerical values** $$\Gamma = -\frac{(0.05)}{(8.314)(298)} \cdot (-0.015)$$ **Step 4 — Compute the denominator** $$RT = 8.314 \times 298 = 2477.572 \text{ J/mol}$$ **Step 5 — Final calculation** $$\Gamma = -\frac{0.05}{2477.572} \times (-0.015)$$ $$\Gamma = \frac{0.05 \times 0.015}{2477.572} = \frac{7.5 \times 10^{-4}}{2477.572}$$ $$\boxed{\Gamma \approx 3.027 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol/m}^2}$$ ### Interpretation: The surface excess of protein is **~0.3 μmol/m²**, indicating a moderately surface-active molecule. In practice, this means the rising bubbles will carry a measurable quantity of protein to the foam phase, making **recovery economically viable** even from dilute streams. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Performance Relationships > [!IMPORTANT] > This matrix links each operating parameter to the **Enrichment Ratio (E)** and **Recovery (R)** — the two key performance metrics for any foam separation problem. | Operating Parameter | Effect on Enrichment (E) | Effect on Recovery (R) | Design Trade-off | |---|---|---|---| | ↑ Gas flow rate | ↓ (more water in foam) | ↑ (more bubbles generated) | Tune for $E \cdot R$ optimum | | ↓ Bubble size | ↑ (more interfacial area) | ↑ | Requires fine-pore sparger (ΔP penalty) | | ↑ Column height | ↑ (more drainage time) | ↓ (drainage losses) | Tall column → drier foam | | ↑ Feed concentration | ↓ (E typically) | ↑ (more solute available) | Dilute feeds preferred for high E | | ↑ Temperature | ↓ (lower surface tension) | ↑ (lower viscosity) | Compromise on protein denaturation | | ↓ pH toward pI | ↑ (max surface activity for proteins) | ↑ | Critical for protein separations | | ↑ Ionic strength | Variable (electrostatic screening) | ↑ (reduces repulsion) | Excess salt can collapse foam | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Foam Separation Column The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of a continuous foam fractionation apparatus — the standard configuration for industrial food applications. This is the type of diagram KTU examiners expect students to reproduce in board exams. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Feed Reservoir
Dilute Surfactant Solution] --> B[Feed Pump
Metered Inflow] B --> C[Liquid Pool
Equilibration Zone] D[Gas Supply
Air or N2] --> E[Sparger Frit
Bubble Generator] E --> C C --> F[Ascending Bubble Swarm
Adsorption Zone] F --> G[Polyhedral Foam Layer
Drainage Zone] G --> H[Collection Hood
Foam Outlet] H --> I[Mechanical Foam Breaker
Rotating Blade] I --> J[Concentrate Foamate
Product Stream] G -.Drainage.-> C C --> K[Depleted Bottom Liquid
Refined Outlet] J --> L[Surfactant-Rich Liquid
Further Processing] ``` ## 4.2 Schematic of a Batch Foam Fractionation Apparatus ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Compressed Air Inlet] --> R1[Pressure Regulator] R1 --> R2[Flow Meter] R2 --> S1[Porous Glass Frit
Sparger] S1 --> CL1[Foam Column
Glass Tube 50 mm dia] CL1 --> FM1[Top Foam Collection Cup] CL1 --> BL1[Bottom Liquid Sampling Port] BL1 --> BL2[Residual Liquid Drain] FM1 --> FC1[Foam Collapse Chamber] FC1 --> PS1[Product Sampling Port] ``` ## 4.3 Apparatus Components — Functional Description Matrix > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked to "describe the apparatus for foam separation," cover *at least* the following six components: (1) gas supply, (2) sparger, (3) column body, (4) foam collection zone, (5) foam breaker, and (6) feed/outlet ports. The table below provides a board-ready summary. | Component | Typical Material | Functional Role | Operating Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Gas Supply | Compressed air / N₂ cylinder | Provides bubble-generating gas | Food-grade gas; flow rate **0.5–5 L/min** | | Sparger | Porous glass, sintered stainless steel | Disperses gas into fine bubbles | Pore size **10–100 μm** controls bubble radius | | Column | Borosilicate glass / SS 316 | Houses the adsorption zone | Height **0.5–2 m**, diameter **2–15 cm** | | Liquid Reservoir | Glass / food-grade SS | Holds feed and bottom liquid | Jacketed for temperature control | | Foam Collector | Stainless steel cone | Captures rising foam | Angled baffle improves yield | | Foam Breaker | Mechanical (rotating blade) or thermal | Collapses foam to liquid concentrate | Mechanical preferred to avoid heat damage | ## 4.4 Process Flow of Industrial-Scale Continuous Foam Separation ```mermaid flowchart TD Raw[Raw Food Stream
e.g. Citrus Juice] --> S1[Screening
Remove Pulp] S1 --> EQ[pH Adjustment
to Target Value] EQ --> CL1[Foam Column 1
Primary Separation] CL1 --> CL2[Foam Column 2
Polishing Stage] CL2 --> P1[Polished Juice
Product] CL1 -.Foamate.-> COL[Foam Collapse Unit] CL2 -.Foamate.-> COL COL --> BC[Byproduct Recovery
Bitter Compounds / Protein] CL1 --> RS[Refined Liquid Out] RS --> CL2 ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define foam separation. What is the role of the Gibbs Adsorption Equation in this process? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (1 Mark):** Foam separation is an adsorptive separation technique in which surface-active solutes are concentrated at a gas–liquid interface and removed as a foam rising above the bulk liquid. - **Role of Gibbs Equation (2 Marks):** The Gibbs Adsorption Equation, $\Gamma = -\dfrac{C}{RT}\left(\dfrac{d\gamma}{dC}\right)$, quantitatively relates the surface excess concentration $(\Gamma)$ of the adsorbed solute to the bulk concentration $(C)$ and the rate of change of surface tension with concentration. It helps engineers predict *how much* solute will adsorb at the bubble surface, which in turn determines the **enrichment ratio (E)** achievable in the column. A steeper negative slope of $\gamma$ vs. $C$ implies stronger surface activity and a more efficient separation. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** List the four main factors controlling foam separation efficiency and briefly explain any two. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **List of four factors (1 Mark):** (i) pH of the solution, (ii) gas flow rate, (iii) bubble size, and (iv) temperature. - **Explanation of two factors (2 Marks — 1 each):** - **pH:** For protein separations, the surface activity is maximum at the **isoelectric point (pI)**, where the protein has zero net charge and minimum electrostatic repulsion. Operating near pI gives the highest foam stability and recovery. - **Bubble size:** Smaller bubbles provide a **larger total interfacial area per unit gas volume**, increasing $\Gamma$ accumulation and improving separation efficiency. However, very small bubbles may not drain efficiently, lowering the enrichment ratio. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]* > **"With a neat schematic, describe the apparatus used for foam separation. Discuss the working principle and enumerate the applications of foam separation in food processing."** **Model Answer — Sub-Part (a) — 7 Marks: Apparatus and Working Principle** **(i) Apparatus Description (4 Marks):** A typical foam separation apparatus consists of: - **Gas supply unit:** A cylinder of compressed air or nitrogen connected through a pressure regulator and rotameter. - **Sparger / bubble generator:** A sintered glass frit (pore size 10–100 μm) at the column base that disperses gas into fine, uniform bubbles. - **Column body:** A vertical glass or stainless steel column (typically 0.5–2 m tall, 2–15 cm internal diameter) holding the feed liquid. - **Foam collection zone:** The top section of the column where the polyhedral foam accumulates and is channeled to the outlet. - **Foam breaker:** A mechanical device (rotating blade) that collapses the foam into a liquid concentrate. - **Feed inlet and product outlets:** Metered feed line and two outlets — one for the foamate (concentrate) and one for the depleted bottom liquid. **(ii) Working Principle (3 Marks):** - The feed solution containing surface-active solutes is introduced into the column. - Gas is sparged from the bottom, generating a swarm of fine bubbles. As the bubbles rise through the liquid pool, surface-active molecules **adsorb onto the gas–liquid interface** following the Gibbs Adsorption Isotherm. - The bubbles carry the adsorbed solute to the top, forming a **polyhedral foam** that rises above the bulk liquid. Liquid drains back from the foam lamellae, **concentrating the solute** in the foam phase. - The dry foam is collected and mechanically broken to yield a **concentrated liquid product (foamate)** with an enrichment ratio of typically 2 to 100+. --- **Model Answer — Sub-Part (b) — 7 Marks: Applications in Food Processing** | Application | Target Compound Removed/Recovered | Typical Industry | |---|---|---| | Citrus juice debittering | Limonin, naringin (bitter flavonoids) | Juice processing | | Whey protein fractionation | β-lactoglobulin, α-lactalbumin | Dairy industry | | Enzyme concentration | Proteases, amylases from fermentation broth | Bioprocess / food enzymes | | Color removal | Anthocyanins, caramel color bodies | Sugar refining, beverage industry | | Hop bitter acid removal | Iso-α-acids (in over-hopped beer) | Brewing industry | | Phytic acid reduction | Phytic acid (anti-nutrient) in legume extracts | Plant protein processing | | Surfactant removal | Cleaning-agent residues | Post-CIP rinse water | > **Conclusion (1 Mark):** Foam separation is a versatile, energy-efficient technique for selectively concentrating surface-active food components under mild conditions, making it highly suitable for **heat-sensitive and biologically active** food streams. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 — Alternative Choice]* > **"Explain the nature and classification of foams. Discuss in detail the various controlling factors that affect the efficiency of foam separation in food systems."** **Model Answer — Sub-Part (a) — 7 Marks: Nature and Classification of Foams** **(i) Nature of Foams (3 Marks):** A foam is a **two-phase colloidal dispersion** in which **gas bubbles are dispersed in a continuous liquid or solid matrix**. In foam separation, we are concerned with **liquid foams**, which are thermodynamically metastable systems. The stability of the foam is governed by: - **Surface viscosity** of the adsorbed film, - **Surface elasticity** (Marangoni effect), - **Disjoining pressure** in thin films, and - **Rate of liquid drainage** from the lamellae. **(ii) Classification of Foams (4 Marks):** Foams are classified based on **liquid content and bubble geometry**: - **Type 1 — Spherical (Wet) Foams:** Liquid fraction > 5%, bubbles are roughly spherical, separated by thick liquid films. Forms at low gas flow rates or in early stages of foam rise. - **Type 2 — Polyhedral (Dry) Foams:** Liquid fraction < 5%, bubbles are deformed into polyhedra (typically **pentagonal dodecahedra**) separated by thin **Plateau borders**. Forms at high gas flow rates and is the *preferred foam state* in foam separation because the thin films maximize solute concentration. - **Type 3 — Kugelschaum (Foam with mobile bubbles):** Intermediate between wet and dry, characterized by free bubble movement. - **Type 4 — Polyederschaum (Rigid polyhedral foam):** Highly stable, used in food products like mousses and meringues. --- **Model Answer — Sub-Part (b) — 7 Marks: Controlling Factors** The efficiency of foam separation (quantified by **Enrichment Ratio $E$** and **Recovery $R$**) depends on: 1. **pH of the Solution (1 Mark):** For proteinaceous systems, surface activity is maximum at the **isoelectric point (pI)**. Operating pH must be tuned accordingly. 2. **Temperature (1 Mark):** Higher temperature reduces viscosity (improving drainage) but may denature proteins and lower surface tension. **Optimum range: 20–40 °C** for most food applications. 3. **Gas Flow Rate (1 Mark):** Higher flow increases recovery (more bubbles) but reduces enrichment (wetter foam). **Typical range: 0.5–5 L/min** for laboratory columns. 4. **Bubble Size (1 Mark):** Smaller bubbles (10–100 μm radius) provide higher interfacial area per unit gas volume, increasing $\Gamma$. Achieved using **fine-pore frits or Venturi-type spargers**. 5. **Ionic Strength (1 Mark):** Adding neutral salts screens electrostatic repulsion between adsorbed molecules, stabilizing the foam. However, multivalent cations (e.g., Ca²⁺) can destabilize the foam. 6. **Column Height and Feed Concentration (1 Mark):** Taller columns allow more drainage (higher E, lower R). Dilute feeds (typically < 1% w/w) yield higher enrichment ratios. 7. **Surfactant Concentration Relative to CMC (1 Mark):** Separation is effective only when $C < \text{CMC}$; above CMC, micelles form in the bulk, reducing interfacial accumulation. > **Conclusion (1 Mark):** A careful balance of all seven factors is required to achieve an optimum product of $E \times R$, with typical industrial values of $E = 5$–$50$ and $R = 60$–$95\%$. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing Enrichment Ratio with Recovery** — $E$ measures *concentration* while $R$ measures *mass captured*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for interchanging them. > 2. **Omitting the Gibbs Isotherm in apparatus questions** — Any 14-mark apparatus question *must* mention the Gibbs equation or a quantitative expression of adsorption. > 3. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** — $\Gamma$ in mol/m², $\gamma$ in N/m, $C$ in mol/m³, $T$ in **Kelvin** (not °C). This is the single most common arithmetic error. > 4. **Listing applications without specifying the target compound** — "Used in food industry" earns 0 marks; "Removal of limonin from orange juice" earns full credit. > 5. **Skipping the diagram in long-answer questions** — A neat schematic is worth **at least 2 marks** out of 14. Always draw and label the apparatus. > 6. **Ignoring the CMC limit** — Foam separation fails above CMC. Examiners specifically test this conceptual boundary. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Definition:** Foam separation = adsorptive bubble separation using gas–liquid interface. - **Driving Force:** Reduction of interfacial free energy (Gibbs free energy minimization). - **Governing Equation:** Gibbs Adsorption Isotherm $\Gamma = -\dfrac{C}{RT}\left(\dfrac{d\gamma}{dC}\right)$. - **Apparatus Components:** Gas supply → sparger → column → foam collector → foam breaker → outlets. - **Nature of Foams:** Two-phase colloidal systems; classified as **spherical (wet)** or **polyhedral (dry)**. - **Dry Polyhedral Foams** are preferred for separation (drier = higher enrichment). - **Plateau borders** are the thin liquid channels where drainage occurs. - **Performance Metrics:** Enrichment Ratio $E = C_f/C_i$ and Recovery $R = C_f V_f / (C_i V_i)$. - **Controlling Factors:** pH, temperature, gas flow rate, bubble size, ionic strength, column height, feed concentration, CMC threshold. - **Critical Design Limit:** Operate at $C < \text{CMC}$ — above CMC, no further interfacial adsorption occurs. - **Food Applications:** Citrus debittering (limonin/naringin), whey protein recovery, enzyme concentration, anthocyanin removal, phytic acid reduction. - **Industrial Advantages:** Low energy, ambient operation, no phase change, continuous operation, ideal for heat-sensitive food streams. - **Young–Laplace Pressure:** $\Delta P = 2\gamma/r$ — predicts bubble stability; smaller bubbles have higher internal pressure and tend to coalesce. - **Stokes Terminal Velocity:** $v_t = \dfrac{2r^2 g(\rho_l - \rho_g)}{9\mu_l}$ — determines column height for adequate bubble–liquid contact time. - **Surface Pressure:** $\Pi = \gamma_0 - \gamma$ — measures surfactant activity; positive $\Pi$ confirms surface activity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Study Tip:** For the **Module 4** exam, focus 60% of your preparation on the **Gibbs Isotherm derivation** and the **factor-effect matrix**. These two topics account for the majority of marks across Part A and Part B questions in past KTU cycles. Always pair the *theory* with a *food-industry example* to earn full credit on application-based questions.
